Introduce the traditions of Chinese New Year to your children with our 'Show and Tell' educational video, perfect for children ages 5-11.

Children can join the Chinese dragon as he enters his celebratory scrap book and shows the children all about how the event is celebrated in his country.

Perfect for use in the classroom for teaching EYFS, KS1, and KS2, our video explores the celebration of Lunar New Year and how it's celebrated.

Children will learn about the customs surrounding red envelopes, Chinese lanterns, and the significance of the Year of the Rabbit.

Watch as we learn how Chinese New Year is celebrated, and how to understand Chinese culture and traditions.

This video is a perfect tool for teaching Chinese New Year and offers a great learning resource.

It is a great classroom video that offers a fun way to educate your students about Chinese Celebrations and culture.

To support the video, we have a whole host of supporting resources are available to download - ranging from writing and drawing activities, to colouring and discussion cards.

We suggest using the video before using any of the supporting activities, to increase learning opportunities and open discussions in your classroom.

title: What Chinese New Year Is Really About - Origins, Legends & 30 Days of Traditions | Spring Festival
description:
🎉 What is Chinese New Year really about? Is it just firecrackers, red envelopes, and dumplings? Or is there something deeper behind Chinese New Year traditions — something ancient, mystical, and just a little terrifying?

In this video, we’ll explore what Chinese New Year is really about, from its origin in star-gazing rituals to a modern month-long extravaganza of ancestor worship, kitchen gods, fireworks, and cosmic accounting. This isn’t just another “how to celebrate Chinese New Year” video — it’s a full-blown crash course in Chinese New Year mythology, Lunar New Year customs, and the spiritual logic behind why your mom becomes a cleaning warlord in January.

🌕 We begin with the origin of Chinese New Year, tracing how ancient Chinese people observed the Big Dipper to decide when a new year begins. We'll uncover the astrological roots of the Chinese zodiac and how the changing sky signaled a time for renewal — or judgment.

Then comes the legend of Nian, the punctual demon of New Year’s Eve. With its glowing eyes, sharp claws, and taste for humans, it wasn’t just a story — it was the myth behind Chinese firecrackers, red paper, and staying up all night. This Nian monster legend explains why Lunar New Year began as a monster-repellent strategy, not a celebration.

And let’s not forget the Kitchen God, the divine tattletale who reports your family’s behavior to the heavens. That sticky candy? Not just a snack — it’s hush money. This video dives into Kitchen God rituals, revealing how incense offerings, malt candy, and a clean stove might just secure you some extra celestial brownie points.

💥 From there, we go day by day, decoding all the Chinese New Year rituals from Laba Festival to the Lantern Festival. Learn about:

Ancestor worship customs (yes, ghosts are part of the guest list)

Why people burn incense to call home great-grandma’s spirit

The meaning behind red decorations, dumplings, and fish dishes

How to properly “sweep out bad luck” without offending the divine

The day you honor rocks. (Seriously. It’s called Stone Day.)

And when Lantern Festival finally arrives on the 15th day of the first lunar month, you’ll know why people eat tangyuan, guess lantern riddles, and watch dragon dances — not just because they’re pretty, but because they close the ritual loop started weeks earlier.

🔍 Featured Topics in This Video:

Chinese New Year origin and history

Nian monster legend

Kitchen God ritual explained

Chinese ancestor worship traditions

Day-by-day Chinese New Year ritual calendar

Meaning behind Chinese red decorations and firecrackers

What tangyuan symbolizes during Lantern Festival

Why Lunar New Year isn’t just one day

How the Chinese zodiac ties into the calendar

The secret meaning of sweeping and cleaning

Stone Day, Human Day, and other hidden traditions

Red envelopes and their spiritual roots

🧧 Whether you're curious about the history of Chinese red envelopes, looking to understand Spring Festival etiquette, or want to impress your relatives with obscure facts about Tofu Day, Pig-Slaughtering Day, or why sons-in-law get treated like emperors on the 2nd day — this is your guide.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Legend Of Nian -Lunar New Year
description:
Chinese New Year, also known as the Spring Festival or Lunar New Year, is the most important traditional Chinese holiday. It celebrates the beginning of a new year on the traditional lunisolar Chinese calendar.

According to Eastern mythology there was a horrible beast called Nian which lived on the mountains.
Towards the end of Winter when there was nothing to eat, Nian would come on the first day of New Year to the villages to eat livestock, crops, and even villagers, especially children.
The villagers would live in terror over the Winter, but over time they learned that the ferocious Nian was afraid of three things: fire, and noise and  the colour red.  So when the New Year was about to come, the villagers would hang red lanterns and red spring scrolls on windows and doors.  They also used firecrackers to frighten away the Nian. From then on, Nian never came to the village again.

That is the story of #chineseNewyear .

Thank you for watching.

description:
This year's Spring Festival Gala, an annual celebration produced and broadcast by China Media Group, commenced on Feb 16, the eve of the Chinese New Year, as Chinese people worldwide celebrated the Spring Festival, the most important Chinese festival centering on family reunions.

Known as "Chunwan," the gala, the first since China's Spring Festival was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, has not only continued its tradition as the must-watch show for Chinese families on Chinese New Year's Eve since 1983 but also reached new heights in showcasing human warmth and cultural heritage.

title: Dramatic horse-themed dance sequence lights up Spring Festival Gala
description:
The China Media Group (CMG)'s Spring Festival Gala delivered a spectacular dramatic dance performance themed on galloping horses, as audiences welcomed the arrival of the Year of the Horse on Tuesday.

Echoing the gala's main theme of "Galloping Steed, Onward Unstoppable," the dance sequence was led by an acrobatic dancer dressed in white depicting a horse, supported by others delivering powerful movements amid the sound of drumbeats, representing the perseverance and free spirit of a horse that enables it to channel its raw power into an enduring spirit of progress.

Through innovative, narrative-driven choreography, the act brought a sense of the elegance, strength and vitality of horse imagery to life.

description:
🐎中国中央广播电视总台发布春晚全球推介片，为这场即将到来的新春盛会拉开帷幕。

春节，又称中国新年，是中国人一年之中最隆重的传统佳节。2025年，春节成功入选联合国教科文组织人类非物质文化遗产代表作名录，成为全人类的文化瑰宝。

总台春晚自1983年首次播出，是被吉尼斯世界纪录认定的全球观看人数最多的年度电视盛会。农历除夕，全家人围坐在电视机前共赏春晚，已成为中国亿万家庭镌刻于心的年俗仪式。

歌舞传情、科技添翼、传统焕新，总台马年春晚将以“骐骥驰骋、势不可挡”的昂扬之姿，与全球受众一起迎接生机勃勃、吉祥美满的马年新春！

title: CMG unveils 2026 Spring Festival Gala promo
description:
China Media Group has released a short video promoting its Spring Festival Gala, which will be broadcast live around the world on the evening of February 16.

Spring Festival, or Chinese New Year, is the most important traditional holiday for Chinese people, and watching the Spring Festival Gala on television is a cherished part of the celebrations in hundreds of millions of Chinese homes.

Since first being broadcast in 1983, the gala has been recognized by Guinness World Records as the most-watched annual television program on the planet. Just over a year ago, the Spring Festival itself was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

So, get ready for a joyous spectacle of music and dance, high tech and shared traditions to mark the Year of the Horse!

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Spring Festival, Lunar New Year in China
description:
To celebrate Earth’s complete one-year rotation around the sun, people around the world celebrate the new year with fireworks, family, and festivities. One of the most famous of the world’s New Year’s festivals is the Spring Festival in China, where celebrations begin on New Year’s Eve of the lunar New Year and lasts for fifteen days. The lantern festival takes place on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month, and it marks the end of the New Year celebration. 

The Spring Festival period is also an important holiday for families. People traveling back to their hometowns within China to visit their family make New Year’s one of the world’s largest annual migrations, known as chunyun or spring travel. Family meals are prepared by elders. One mealtime tradition is to place a small item, usually a coin or jujube, into one of the dumplings. If someone gets the coin, it means that person will have good financial luck in the New Year, whereas a jujube would signify romantic luck. The ancient Chinese technology of fireworks was used as a way to scare off evil spirits during the Spring Festival. The tradition continues today as a symbol for a joyful time of year.

Learn more about Spring Festival: https://s.si.edu/2V6Bb9k
Learn more about Lantern Festival: https://s.si.edu/34zRWNg

Editing: Jackson Harvey

Description: In China, the spring festival marks the beginning of the new year. It falls on the first day of the Chinese calendar and involves a variety of social practices to usher in the new year, pray for good fortune, celebrate family reunions and promote community harmony. This process of celebration is known as ‘guonian’ (crossing the year). In addition to participating in the public festivities, people make offerings and extend greetings to elders, relatives, friends and neighbours.

🐎中国中央广播电视总台发布春晚全球推介片，为这场即将到来的新春盛会拉开帷幕。

春节，又称中国新年，是中国人一年之中最隆重的传统佳节。2025年，春节成功入选联合国教科文组织人类非物质文化遗产代表作名录，成为全人类的文化瑰宝。

总台春晚自1983年首次播出，是被吉尼斯世界纪录认定的全球观看人数最多的年度电视盛会。农历除夕，全家人围坐在电视机前共赏春晚，已成为中国亿万家庭镌刻于心的年俗仪式。

歌舞传情、科技添翼、传统焕新，总台马年春晚将以“骐骥驰骋、势不可挡”的昂扬之姿，与全球受众一起迎接生机勃勃、吉祥美满的马年新春！

title: CMG Spring Festival Gala celebrates the artistry of calligraphy, martial arts
description:
For  more:
https://news.cgtn.com/news/2025-01-28/Spring-Festival-Gala-celebrates-artistry-of-calligraphy-martial-arts-1Axr2DOdP6E/p.html

China takes pride in its profound cultural heritage, where calligraphy and martial arts stand as timeless symbols of its artistic and philosophical depth. During China Media Group's 2025 Spring Festival Gala, "The Pen Writes the Dragon and Snake" brings these two traditions together in a mesmerizing performance.

Inspired by Li Bai's poem "A Ballad of Cursive Script," the performance celebrates the fluid beauty of calligraphy, likening its strokes to the movements of a dragon and a snake. The show opens with martial artist Donnie Yen appreciating a piece of cursive script, setting the stage for a stunning fusion of martial arts and calligraphy. A rotating table transforms into an ink stone while a tai chi performer embodies the ink, their movements reflecting the grace and dynamism of cursive writing. #springfestival2025

title: How To Plan A Chinese Tea Ceremony
description:
In Chinese culture, newlyweds often include a traditional Chinese tea ceremony, called “敬茶” (ging3 caa4) in Chinese, as a part of their wedding day festivities. The Chinese tea ceremony is a way for you to show gratitude to your parents and elders while addressing them by their new titles for the first time. This also gives the elders an opportunity to personally bless your marriage and shower you with gifts.

If you’re thinking of including a Chinese tea ceremony on your wedding day, we’ve put together this video with a list of everything you need to know.

title: Hong Kong teamaker revives ancient Chinese tea ceremony
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here: 
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

Janice Zhu, a co-founder and tutor of the Hong Kong tea shop Yuentea, holds regular public classes on performing a Chinese tea ceremony. The process includes a special technique for rapidly whipping tea after water is added and how to paint on its surface in a manner reminiscent of the Japanese practice of chado, which has a surprising resemblance to modern latte art. The ancient tea ceremony known as dian cha was originally established in China during the Song dynasty. Dian cha requires significantly more time and effort than modern tea brewing methods and involves complex cultural traditions.

Discover the timeless art of gongfu (or kung fu) tea in Chaozhou, where tea is more than just a drink – it's a way of life. This traditional ritual, recognized as part of China's intangible cultural heritage since 2008, is a journey of patience, precision and passion. title: [ASMR] Traditional Chinese Tea Ceremony
description:
Sound design & Foley while having Chinese Tea Ceremony using Audio Technica at4053b recorded into Zoom f8n Pro.

Traditional Chinese Tea Ceremony popularized in the Tang dynasty has a long history and culture that ties to its spiritual and nourishing elements. 

Many credit Shen Nong as the first person to discover the effects of tea and spread the tea culture. 

There are many forms of Chinese tea ceremony. Here, I am practicing what my grandma has taught me and referred to it as Lao Ren Cha 老人茶 (old person's tea). It is now more commonly referred to as Gong Fu Tea.

For me, there's always a certain calmness and enjoyment in brewing Lao Ren Cha. The process lets the tea brew to its fullness and allows the full potential of tea tasting 品茶.

Different type of tea have different brewing process. Here, I am brewing Alishan tea, which is a type of Oolong tea, from Taiwan.


Filmed on Panasonic GH5s with Canon FD 50mm f1.2, Canon FD 24mm f2.8

title: How to Brew Chinese Tea the Right Way
description:
How do you make tea? Chances are you’ve been doing it wrong.

For many people in China, brewing tea is a ritual akin to meditation. And there is a proper ceremony called gongfu cha.

Gongfu means skill, and cha means tea. It’s a form of Chinese tea service that dates back to the 14th century in Fujian. It places emphasis on the tea’s taste, temperature, and quality.

In this video, we’ll run you through how to do the tea ceremony correctly.

title: The history of tea - Shunan Teng
description:
Trace the origins of tea and find out how it became the second most consumed beverage in the world.

--

Tea is the second most consumed beverage in the world after water — and from sugary Turkish Rize tea to salty Tibetan butter tea, there are almost as many ways of preparing the beverage as there are cultures on the globe. Where did this beverage originate, and how did it become so popular? Shunan Teng details tea’s long history.

title: Tai Chi Walking for Balance: Step-by-Step Beginner Tutorial by Dr Paul Lam
description:
Learn *Tai Chi Walking with Dr Paul Lam,* creator of the Tai Chi for Health programs. This foundational movement from the *Tai Chi for Beginners* program teaches mindful weight transfer and balance through slow, deliberate stepping.

*What is Tai Chi Walking?*

Tai Chi Walking is walking with mindfulness of Tai Chi principles: mindful weight transfer, maintaining balance, upright posture, and awareness. Instead of using momentum, you shift your weight to one leg and establish good balance before moving the other foot. As Dr Lam explains: 

_"Tai Chi Walking is walking with mindfulness of Tai Chi principles: mindful weight transfer, maintaining balance, upright posture, and awareness."_

*What will I learn in this video?*

This video covers forward walking (Movement One: Stepping Forward). You'll learn the complete technique including proper knee alignment, the weight shift sequence, adding the turn, and how to practice both left and right sides in a flowing sequence. Backward walking is taught in the full Tai Chi for Beginners program.

0:00 Introduction and full demonstration
1:08 Movement One: Stepping Forward explained
2:48 Proper knee alignment
3:55 Building strength gradually
5:02 Adding the turn
6:30 Why balance matters (physical and mental)
8:01 Right side practice
9:11 Flowing sequence: left and right together
10:29 Practice with Dr Lam

*Who is this video for?*

Anyone wanting to improve balance and body awareness. No prior Tai Chi experience needed. This is Movement One: Stepping Forward from the Tai Chi for Beginners program.

*Is Tai Chi Walking good for balance?*

Yes. Research shows Tai Chi reduces recurring falls by nearly 70% and can reduce arthritis pain by 35%. While Tai Chi Walking on its own offers real benefits, complete Tai Chi sets provide deeper health benefits because they train the whole body and mind together. Think of this as a foundation for the full Tai Chi for Beginners program.

*About Dr Paul Lam*
Dr Paul Lam is a retired family physician, tai chi expert, and founder of the Tai Chi for Health Institute. He created the Tai Chi for Health programs, practised by millions of people worldwide. His programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

Unlock the power of Tai Chi for everyone over 50 and discover how this ancient practice can transform your modern approach to healthy aging, including for seniors. In this session designed specifically for Tai Chi over 50, we focus on gentle exercises that prioritize balance, coordination, and joint health. Whether you are a total beginner or looking to refine your form, these slow, intentional movements provide a safe and effective way to build functional strength while reducing the risk of falls.

This moving meditation is more than just a workout; it is a tool for mental clarity and stress relief. By integrating deep breathing with fluid motion, you will learn to soothe the nervous system and cultivate mindful movement that carries over into your daily life. title: Tai Chi for Beginners Video | Dr Paul Lam | Free Lesson and Introduction
description:
Learn Tai Chi from the beginning with *Dr Paul Lam* . This is the free first lesson from the *Tai Chi for Beginners* program, including introduction, how to use the program, staying safe, warm-up exercises, your first movements, and cool-down.

*What is Tai Chi for Beginners?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is Dr Paul Lam's program suitable for beginners, consisting of fluid, gentle and relaxed movements suitable for anyone and can be practised almost anywhere. It includes the Six Easy Steps, including four Qigong exercises, five foundational movements (including Tai Chi Walking), and the Tai Chi for Beginners set based on the 24 Forms, the world's most popular tai chi set. Starting with warm-up exercises, Dr Lam guides you steadily through each step as though you were in his class. This program improves muscular strength, flexibility, balance, and confidence.

*What will I learn in this free lesson?*

0:00 Introduction

5:35 What is Tai Chi?
8:57 Welcome
10:59 Warm Up Exercises
22:47 Learn New Forms
36:47 Run Through Lesson One
38:19 Cool Down Exercises
39:42 Message

*Who is this program for?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is suitable for all ages and fitness levels. You can practise seated or standing, and adjust the depth of your movements to what feels comfortable.

*Is Tai Chi good for health?*

Yes. Medical studies have shown that tai chi improves muscular strength, flexibility, balance, and mental wellbeing. Dr Lam's Tai Chi for Health programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

Dr Paul Lam is a retired family physician, tai chi expert, and founder of the Tai Chi for Health Institute. He created the Tai Chi for Health programs, practised by millions of people worldwide. His programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 
This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.

description:

Dr. Yang's tai chi lineage can be traced back to the Yang family through Grandmaster Kao, Tao (高濤) and his teacher Yue, Huanzhi (樂奐之), an indoor disciple of Yang, Chengfu (楊澄甫).
Taijiquan (Tai Chi Chuan) is an ancient internal Chinese martial art which has gained widespread popularity for its many health benefits. Today, most people practice taiji slowly to develop their balance, strength, and vitality, and the martial applications of the art are often ignored. Taijiquan, or 'Grand Ultimate Fist', is a highly effective form of combat specializing in short and middle-range fighting.
Dr. Yang, Jwing-Ming offers practical martial applications for each of the 37-postures of traditional Taiji, based on the forms passed down by Yang, Ban-Hou. Once the viewer has a basic understanding of these universal principles, you may use them to devise further applications for every movement no matter which style of Taiji you practice.
Learn the Basic Concepts of Taiji and Taijiquan.
Understand the purpose of every Taiji movement.
Over 50 effective applications for any Taijiquan style.

title: Tai Chi BEAT DOWN - Chen Style Taijiquan Fight Commentary
description:
In this video I'm giving some fight commentary and analysis on an informal tai chi push hands session, that turns in to what I can might be best described as a 'beat down'!

As I understand it the chap handing it out is Chen Village Taijiquan Combat Captain Wang Yan [王岩].

I have read comment on this suggesting that what is being demonstrated is ''not real tai chi chuan" as it is using "only external mechanics". That's not a debate I feel inclined to get into, BUT I do think there are a lot of good tai chi principles evident in Wang Yan's work that are missing from the work of his challenger. This is, I believe, a significant factor in the very one sided outcome.

For more videos on Tai CHi as a combat art, please do Subscribe to Flowboxer.

James Tripp

Original Video: https://youtu.be/CWcH-puvyic

title: Taichi 4 basic moves
description:
Specific exercise improve symptoms, daily full-body exercises remove root causes.

https://taichizidong.com/products/daily-routine-1


Taichi 4 basic moves
1. 掤 peng(Warding off)
2. 挼 Lv(Rolling back)
3. 挤 Ji(Pressing)
4. 按 An(Pushing)


These are four of the eight most basic movements of Tai Chi, which express the four ways of using power. 
Peng is the outward spread and attack of power; 
Lv is the retraction and defense of force; 
Ji is the concentration of power in a point and burst; 
An is the absorption and conversion of force.

title: Tai Chi For Beginners: 7-Minute Routine For A New You
description:
When you consider your happiness and wellbeing, aside from all the reasons that have held back, and inside all that you have thought about what you want to accomplish, you can begin to realize, who are you, that is capable of more joy and more well-being?

And as you begin to feel more like your happiest and healthiest self, would you like that, for your life? Would it be worth 7 minutes of your day?

Because Tai Chi is completely transformative, and when you know the secret, totally easy. And what is the secret? 

The secret is to let go. Let go of your thoughts about doing it right or wrong. Let go of being perfect, of being critical, of self doubting. Let go and that means you can just float. Like a leaf in the wind, like a boat in a gentle stream, like a being of your highest   spirit, floating in a serene sea of blissful energy.

Tai chi for beginners is the best place to begin when learning a tai chi form or tai chi quan. The Yang Style is suitable for all ages. The great thing about Master Wong’s training method is it can be transferred in to combat too or even self defence. Tai Chi chuan relies on technique, balance and softness. 

Tai Chi Health
This daily practice of Tai chi will enhance your mental capacity to deal with the stresses of every day life. Qigong or chi gong is practiced for this reason.
The movements will strengthen your muscles and joints and improve your balance and co-ordination externally. As you master breathing you will become more aware of your bodies natural systems and begin to balance and control your respiration and circulation internally. 
Meditating while balancing your body internally and externally will lead to a balanced mind. Your memory will be better, you will pick things up quicker and the decisions you make in your life will be easier. Researchers have found that Tai Chi practice shows favorable effects on the promotion of balance control, flexibility, and cardiovascular fitness. Tai Chi has stood the test of time, millions upon millions of people practice Tai Chi to keep themselves young in body and mind and have been doing so for thousands of years. 

The slower and longer you practice the more beneficial it is. Play around with the short form like the 8 step to begin with to build strength and understanding, before doing the full 24 yang form.

How do I know I’m doing it right?
Your hands and body should feel warmer if not hotter when you have done the movements for a while. You may feel achy the day after training when you first begin. Later you will feel less achy as you progress.

What is Tai Chi? (Also known as Taiji)
There are 5 styles of Tai Chi forms. Yang, Chen, Sun, Wu, Ng.
Tai chi chuan is a slow motion, moving meditative exercise for relaxation, health and self-defence. Now a days most people practice the Yang style for the health benefits. Originally from China and designed for combat, Tai Chi has gained enormous popularity in America and throughout the rest of the world for its health benefits and competitions. However many few are able to Master the combat side and rear to see Masters who can apply it in real time.

Tai Chi chuan or also known as Taijiquan was originated by a Shaolin monk ‘Chan San Feng’, while studying in the Taoist monasteries on Wudang Mountain. For more info on the history please visit our website: http://taichicombat.com/

Yin and Yang
According to Chinese philosophy, yin and yang are the two forces that generate chi. They are seen as opposites: yin is feminine and yang is masculine. But neither can exist without the other. And together they represent the perfect harmony that underpins all creation. The balance between yin and yang is never static.

✩ FAQ

▹Why is there no fighting? 
This is a performance of a taiji sword form in modern wushu. This is meant to be a form and not actual combat, although the movements all derive from real techniques. All Asian martial arts had a heavy emphasis on forms training, and it was the equivalent of shadowboxing or padwork in boxing. Traditionally, forms were really used to train and demonstrate techniques, and you would practice the form and actual fighting. 

For wushu's adaption to sport, there is a forms division (taolu), and a fighting division (sanda). Today there's no need to fight, so many people learn the basic applications, but focus only on taolu. Wushu taolu take a style's martial techniques and builds them into aesthetically pleasing routines that epitomize the ideals of a style's philosophy. These forms showcase techniques at the highest level, but are stylized and composed for performance in a sport setting. They are meant to be entertaining and pleasing to watch, just like a high-level gymnastics or ice skating routines. Wushu taolu is judged on proper technique, power, agility, technical difficulty, and martial intent.

If you're interested in actual Chinese martial arts in combat, take a look at Sanda (Chinese kickboxing): https://youtu.be/dwM0gEpD9QY

▹Why is her sword floppy?
Since this is adapted for sport and health, you want to change the weapons to be safer for practice and competition as a sport. Not only that, Taiji is regularly practiced by over a billion people, the last thing you need is these practitioners showing up to parks and gyms with real swords or anything close to it. Note, although these are made to be purposely floppy at the ends, they are counterbalanced at the hilt to be as closer to the balance weight as traditional Chinese swords. 

If you this seems to odd to you, think about some other sports that came from combat, such as fencing, archery, and javelin throwing . These sports use heavily modified weapons that are safer for sport practice as well. In fact, fencing swords doesn't even look like swords anymore, and are even more flexible than wushu swords.

title: Early Yang style Taijiquan demonstrated by Niu Chunming [with English Captions]
description:
The footage from 1960, the first documentary promoting Taijiquan and focusing on one person: Niu Chunming, one of four earliest disciples of Yang Chengfu. Niu followed Yang Chengfu and Yang Jianhou, Chengfu's father, for 17 years.

Niu Chunming (1881-1961) of Manchu nationality, was born in Beijing, first studied the art of gardening and osteopathy, but his interest in martial arts lead him to study Yang Family Taijiquan. Although he became the disciple of Yang Chengfu in 1902 it was Yang's father, Yang Jianhou, who taught Niu. The relation became very close in 1907 when Niu took the post of the doctor for the Capital Fire Brigade, which Yang Jianhou was the honorary coach of martial arts at.  In 1914 Niu became the assistant instructor at the Yang family martial arts school in Beijing, also supporting Yang Chengfu when he taught in Hangzhou in 1928. When Yang Chengfu moved further to teach in Shanghai and Guangzhou, Niu continued to teach in Hangzhou, and soon became famous for his skill in pushing hands. In 1956 Niu took part in all-China Wushu Competition, and called by the young athletes who crossed hands with him "Niu the Strongman". According to his disciples Niu's power came from daily practice of dou dagan - shaking the long pole - which he learnt from Yangs. Another skill Niu learnt from Yang Jianhou was point striking - apparently Niu's fingers were so hard he could open a steel can with them. Niu was also a successful TCM doctor, although would often proudly say that he preferred to recommend Taijiquan to cure diseases instead of medicine. 

In 1960 Chen Yun, then vice-chairman of the CPC and one of the most powerful people in the country (in the 1980s and 1990s second only to Deng Xiaoping), as well as Teng Daiyuan, the head of the Ministry of Railways, were recuperating in Hangzhou and asked Niu to teach them Taijiquan. Soon Chen arranged a set of still photos of Niu to be taken, and not longer later a documentary featuring Niu and called "Forever Young" was shot in Hangzhou. Only 9 minutes of the documentary have survived relatively intact to these days - this is the video presented here.

For more information about traditional martial arts of China please visit: www.ChinaFromInside.com

description:

URL: https://ich.unesco.org/en/RL/01608
Description: The Ong Chun ceremony and related practices are rooted in folk customs of worshipping Ong Yah, a deity believed to protect people and their lands from disasters. The element is centered in coastal communities in China’s Minnan region and in Melaka, Malaysia. The ceremony includes welcoming Ong Yah to temples or clan halls, delivering 'good brothers' (people lost at sea) from torment, and honoring the connection between man and the ocean. Performances during the procession include different types of dancing.

title: Tradisi Song Wang Chuan atau mengantar Kapal Raja. #wangchuan #shortsvideo #tiongkok #malaka
description:
Tradisi Upacara Song Wang Chuan atau mengantar Kapal Raja adalah upacara tradisional Tionghoa untuk menghormati Dewa Ong Yah atau Dewa Pelindung agar masyarakat terhindar dari bencana dan malapetaka. 

Berakar pada tradisi rakyat untuk memuliakan Wang Ye, dewa penjaga yang diyakini berpatroli atas nama surga, mengusir wabah, dan melindungi masyarakat pesisir dari bencana. Upacara ini melambangkan harapan masyarakat akan perdamaian dan keberuntungan di laut. 

Diadakan setiap tiga tahun sekali di daerah pesisir Fujian, serta di komunitas Tionghoa di Melaka, Malaysia, dan Taiwan.

title: Send-off of the Royal Ship
description: Zhangzhou’s "Wangchuan Ceremony" (Send-off of the Royal Ship) is a jointly inscribed UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity between China and Malaysia. Since the Ming Dynasty, coastal ancestors have built ships, held rituals, and burned the "Royal Ships" to ward off pestilence and disasters. Today, it has become a cross-border cultural bond, weaving together the maritime memories and visual heritage of Chinese communities along the Maritime Silk Road. The ceremony is held annually in October.

title: 忘川 Wang Chuan 艾晓明 Ai Xiaoming (2010)
description:
(English follows.) 该片为艾晓明导演的四川地震纪录片系列的第四部，记录了地震一年后，政府、媒体以及遇难学生家长的态度和境况。该片聚焦于北川中学的祭奠仪式，记录了被警察跟踪控制的学生家长，和各地涌来旅游、凑热闹的游客。方兴未艾的川震旅游业鼓吹着抗震救灾、重建家园的伟业，与遇难学生家长还有依然坚持调查的志愿者们形成了鲜明的对比。

艾晓明在推特上写道，“忘川，是阴阳两界之河，亡灵投胎转世，在这里把前尘往事尽数遗忘。谭作人被捕之后，5•12周年祭前夕，我和志愿者谢贻卉再次来到北川。我们探访了以前采访过的部分遇难学生家长，寻找着北川中学施工图知情人。次日，我们见证了新北川中学奠基仪式前的热闹以及20万人涌入北川的奇观。灾难似乎过去，记忆难抵遗忘……继《我们的娃娃》《公民调查》和《花儿为什么这样红》之后，这部纪录片以略微疏离的态度，呈现了地震游的兴起，记录了志愿者、遇难家长和观光客在周年祭的心理状态。影片中穿插了北川中学遇难学生家长2008年拍摄的垮塌教学楼取样视频史料、建筑师朱涛对施工图和教学楼质量的分析、坚持寻找真相的母亲、老师、摄影师的观感……”

科学研究部门和权威机构一致表示地震是造成这次学校及其他公共设施受损的主要原因。为此，四川省建设厅已明确规定，对5.12地震垮塌房屋一律不进行质量鉴定。而北川中学至纪录片拍摄时依旧没有公布遇难学生确切人数、名单。垮塌教学楼残余废墟已被禁止拍摄。

创作者简介：
艾晓明（1953— ），女权学者、写作者、纪录片工作者。艾晓明关注公共事务，持续参与社会倡导，并在这个过程中接触到纪录片，拍摄了多部记录中国公民维权运动的作品。因其倡导行动，艾晓明长期面临政府打压，但依旧持续抗争至今，记录真实的历史。


This film is the fourth installment in director Ai Xiaoming’s documentary series on the Sichuan earthquake. It documents the attitudes and circumstances of the government, media, and parents of deceased students one year after the disaster. The film focuses particularly on the memorial ceremony held for Beichuan Middle School, capturing the stark contrast between grieving parents, who were monitored and restrained by police as they attempted to mourn, and the floods of tourists arriving from all over, treating the occasion as a spectacle.

The booming “earthquake tourism” industry glorified narratives of disaster relief and heroic reconstruction, sharply contrasting with the enduring pain of bereaved parents and the persistence of volunteers still investigating the causes of school collapses.

As Ai wrote on Twitter: “Wangchuan is the river between the realms of the living and the dead—a place where spirits forget their past lives before reincarnation. After Tan Zuoren’s arrest, in the days leading up to the first anniversary of 5.12, I returned to Beichuan with volunteer Xie Yihui. We revisited several bereaved parents we had previously interviewed and searched for those familiar with Beichuan Middle School’s construction blueprints. The next day, we witnessed the grand spectacle of the groundbreaking ceremony for the new Beichuan Middle School and the astonishing sight of 200,000 people pouring into Beichuan. The disaster seemed to have passed, and memory struggled against oblivion…”

Following Our Children, Citizen Investigation, and Why Are the Flowers So Red, this documentary adopts a slightly more distanced tone to depict the rise of 'earthquake tourism.' It captures the psychological states of volunteers, grieving parents, and sightseers during the anniversary. Interwoven throughout the film are archival materials: footage from 2008 showing samples taken from the collapsed school building by victims' parents; architect Zhu Tao’s analysis of the construction drawings and building quality; and reflections from mothers, teachers, and photographers who continue to seek the truth.”

Scientific research departments and official institutions uniformly claimed that the earthquake was the primary cause of damage to schools and other public facilities. On that basis, the Sichuan Provincial Department of Construction issued a directive stating that no structural quality assessments were to be conducted for buildings that collapsed during the 5.12 earthquake.

As of the time of filming, Beichuan Middle School had still not released the exact number or full list of deceased students. The rubble from the collapsed classrooms had also been designated off-limits for photography.

About the Creator:
Ai Xiaoming (1953- ), feminist scholar, writer, and documentary filmmaker. Ai follows public affairs and participates in social advocacy, during which she encountered documentary films and began to shoot her own films documenting Chinese citizens’ rights protection struggles. Due to her advocacy, Ai has faced various forms of repression, but continues to document history.

title: The Ong Chun Procession
description:
Sang Ong Cun ceremony, a ritual perform by local Chinese and Peranakan chinese, in Tanah Merah, Kelantan. The Sang Ong Cun ritual is a procession that gathers wandering souls, evil energy and negativity into the dragon boat and to be burn and send off away. Chinese community believe that this effort will bring peace, prosperity and protection to the land and people.It takes a whole village to work together until completion.

description: Mr Wang Chuan-fu is the founder, chairman, executive director and president of the China-based BYD Co Ltd. The company manufactures batteries for mobile devices, grid-tied energy storage and electric vehicles. It also produces electric vehicles, LEDs and other IT components. Mr Wang founded the company in 1995 and grew it to 170,000 employees; it is now worth $11.2bn. Through its manufacture of electric cars for taxi fleets, electric buses, over 100MWh of grid-tied energy storage systems, and 850MW of PV panels annually, it has avoided over 40,000 tons of CO2 emissions. BYD is the third largest lithium ion battery producer and its grid-scale energy storage systems account for 23% of globally commissioned installed capacity (not including pumped hydro or compressed air systems). Mr Wang and BYD have received thousands of patents and numerous awards around the world.

title: Wangkang in Melaka
description: Wangkang, a celebration held by the Fujian or Hokkien Chinese of Melaka, honours the deity Ong Yah. Five clans await their turn to lead the parade according to Ong Yah’s proclamation. The event includes a royal barge that travels across the state to gather wandering spirits before being burned in a symbolic cremation ritual. This video goes behind-the-scenes of the 2017 ceremony arranged by the Choo clan and the 188-year-old Cheng Wah Keong Temple. Wangkang was declared an intangible cultural heritage in Malaysia in 2013, and has been submitted for UNESCO’s world cultural heritage status.

至于勇全殿创建之年代，也因文献的缺乏而无法确定。就勇全殿现存的文物，年代最古老者是刻有志年“嘉庆二十三年戊寅荔业朔日吉置”(1818年)的池府王爷神x。其次则为志年“道光十七年岁次丁酉茘月谷旦”(1837年)的“昭明有融”之匾额。若以1818年计算，则勇全殿之创立，迄今至少有186年的历史。

勇全殿 （English: Yong Chuan Tian) known as Tee Ong Yah Temple is located at 195 Jalan Parameswara (Bandar Hilir), 75000 Melaka. Malaysia.

An engraved date is shown on the wooden shrine of Tee Ong Yah that reads “wuyin” and the 23 rd year of Jiaqing’s reign corresponding to 1818 A.D. An oldest temple in Melaka.

title: 電話民調｜蔡旺詮誓師重返議會 陳亭妃現身力挺衝民調
description:
電話民調｜蔡旺詮誓師重返議會 陳亭妃現身力挺衝民調
民進黨台南市議員初選進入白熱化，深耕東區多年的市議員參選人蔡旺詮，正式舉行旺詮之友會暨挺妃後援會成立大會。現場湧入大批熱情支持群眾，氣氛熱烈。

中嘉新聞／三冠王．雙子星／台南報導

旺詮之友會暨挺妃後援會成立大會，26日晚間在東區和平里活動中心登場，由議員陳怡珍擔任主持人，蔡旺詮也在小提琴家林晴薇的演奏下唱出他自己作詞的歌曲，唱出對這個城市的愛意感情。大會這天，立委陳亭妃、議員陳秋萍、周麗津、李偉智、陳碧玉也都出席力挺，大聲呼籲電話民調唯一支持蔡旺詮。

陳亭妃強調，蔡旺詮長期聚焦教育、文化及民生議題，問政精準且具前瞻性，與她的市政願景高度契合。陳亭妃也說，未來的台南需要一位懂政策、有經驗、且溝通能力強的議員在議會把關，「蔡旺詮就是那位能與我並肩作戰的『戰友』。面對鄉親的熱情支持，蔡旺詮感性表示在關鍵時刻看到這麼多支持者挺身而出，是他持續奮鬥的最大動力。

最後，陳亭妃、蔡旺詮、妃妃鐵馬隊20多位成員及後援會幹部歐政輝、胡富文、郭世芳醫生等人共同推薦，呼籲鄉親在民調期間務必「在家顧電話」。並回答「唯一支持蔡旺詮」。

title: 东篱下的菊语：方寸砂器，归隐桃源 #午茶時光 #teatime#teapot #tea
description:
一捧温润的紫砂，在匠心和文思的共塑下，化作承载千古归隐之梦的方舟。执一把怀隐，沏一盏清茶，摩挲其圆融的壶身，凝视其含蓄的流把，仿佛便能听见东篱下的菊语，看见辋川中的云起，触摸到那份穿越时空的宁静与淡泊。它不仅是茶席上的雅器，更是安放现代人疲惫灵魂的“心隐”之所，于喧嚣尘世中，筑起一座精神的桃源。
A handful of warm purple clay, shaped by ingenuity and literary thought, transforms into an ark carrying the dream of eternal seclusion. Holding a hidden embrace, brewing a cup of clear tea, caressing its smooth pot body, gazing at its subtle flowing handle, as if one can hear the chrysanthemum language under the eastern fence, see the clouds rising in the Wangchuan, and touch the tranquility and indifference that transcends time and space. It is not only an elegant utensil on the tea table, but also a place for modern people's tired souls to hide, building a spiritual paradise in the hustle and bustle of the world.

title: 館長晚間到廈門參加廈港送王船活動　現場邀請在廈門的朋友快來看 #館長 #廈港送王船 #廈門
description:
館長表示，今晚將前往廈門參與廈港送王船民俗活動，並在行程中向粉絲發出即時邀請，直說「在廈門的趕緊來看」。他形容，送王船不只是熱鬧儀式，更是融合信仰、社群與傳承的重要時刻。
他也提到，這類民俗活動只有到現場，才能感受到人潮、鼓陣與儀式氛圍交織而成的震撼感。對於已在廈門的朋友，他呼籲把握機會親眼見證，感受同源文化的力量。
訊息曝光後，彈幕立刻熱絡起來：「今晚就衝」、「難得一見」、「現場一定很壯觀」。不少觀眾表示，臨時通知反而更有即時感與參與感。

館長預告今晚到廈門參加廈港送王船活動，並邀請在廈門的朋友到現場一同感受民俗盛事。

title: Tibetan Medicine warm oil massage - Contemplation By Design Summit 2017, Stanford University
description: Self-care skills have always been a fundamental component of contemplative practice traditions. This practical workshop will impart self-care skills from Tibetan Medicine that help prepare the mind and body for meditation. Techniques presented include Hor-mey, or warm oil therapy, which promotes calmness, relaxation, and rejuvenation. Skills for recognizing imbalances and restoring balance in your body and mind also will be presented. Tibetan Medicine, which has been practiced for more than 4,000 years, is called "Sowa Rigpa" in Tibetan. "Sowa" means to heal that which is imbalanced and "rigpa" means the knowledge and science of a particular subject. Sowa Rigpa is regarded as one of the five most important and essential sciences in Tibetan traditional education.

title: The Benefits of Cloves According to Traditional Tibetan Medicine
description:
In this vlog Menpa Phuntsog Wangmo, the director of the international Shang Shung Institute School of Tibetan Medicine, teaches about the versatile benefits of Cloves. Leave your questions in the comments! For more information visit: www.TibetanMedicineSchool.org

Menpa Phuntsog Wangmo

Khewang (Tibetan for Honored Scholar) Phuntsog Wangmo (Ping Cuowangmu) received her advanced degree from the Lhasa University School of Traditional Medicine in 1988 after completing her five-year training program and two-year residency. During that time she studied with the Khenpos Troru Tsenam and Gyaltsen, two of Tibet’s foremost Tibetan Medicine doctors.

Menpa Phuntsog Wangmo had the exceptional opportunity of extensive clinical training under Khenpo Troru Tsenam for four years. Thereafter, she dedicated many years of work as a doctor of Tibetan Medicine in Eastern Tibet where she collaborated with and directed the implementation of A.S.I.A. the non-profit organization founded by Professor Namkhai Norbu. After that, she worked on behalf of A.S.I.A. setting up hospitals and training centers in the remote regions of Sichuan Province and Chamdo Prefecture.

From 1997-2000, she has been the A.S.I.A. project coordinator in Tibet for the development of Gamthog Hospital in collaboration with expatriate personnel as well as the overall health coordinator and practitioner of traditional Tibetan medicine supervising health activities throughout the surrounding region of Chamdo Prefecture.
In 2007 she co-founded the American Tibetan Medical Association (ATMA), a national organization representing the Tibetan medical profession within the United States. Its mission is to preserve, protect, improve, and promote the philosophy, knowledge, science and practice of Tibetan medicine for the benefit of humanity.

In 2012  Menpa Phuntsog Wagmo was appointed the International Director of the School of Tibetan Medicine
She is currently in residence at the Shang Shung Institute of America, the international seat of the School of Tibetan Medicine, where she continues in her leadership as Director and International Director of the Institute’s national and international programs in U.S.A., Russia and Tenerife Spain.

project_name_en: Lum medicinal bathing of Sowa Rigpa (Tibetan people)
title: China's "Tibetan Medicine bath Law" has been included in the world intangible heritage
description: The Lum Medicinal Bathing of Sowa Rigpa (Tibetan Medicine) has been developed by the Tibetan people within a life view based on Jungwa-nga (five elements) and a view about health and illness based on Nyepa-sum (three dynamics). In Tibetan, “Lum” indicates the traditional knowledge and practices of bathing in natural hot springs, herbal water or steam to adjust the balance of mind and body, to ensure health and treat illnesses. The element is centered in the Tibetan agricultural and pastoral areas along the Yarlung Valley and the Tsongkha Mountain Range. As a main component of Sowa Rigpa, the element has been transmitted and widely practiced for generations in relevant communities of Xizang (Tibet) Autonomous Region. This includes Lhokha, Lhasa, Shigatse, Nagchu, Chamdo, Ngari, and Nyingtri. Through the initiative of and with the active participation of the communities, groups and individuals concerned, the element was included in the National List of Intangible Cultural Heritage

project_name_en: Lum medicinal bathing of Sowa Rigpa (Tibetan people)
title: Tibetan medicine bathing
description:
Tibetan medicine bathing, also known as the "Five - Flavor Ambrosia Bath," is first recorded in the classic Tibetan medical text "Gyud - bzhi" (The Four Tantras) . It has functions such as treatment, rehabilitation, and prevention. On November 28, 2018, UNESCO inscribed China's "Tibetan medicine bathing" on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

Tibetan medicine bathing is one of the external treatment methods in Tibetan medicine. It involves immersing the whole body or specific parts like legs and feet in a liquid made from Tibetan medicinal herbs. Under the combined effects of the water's heat and the medicinal properties, the pores of the human body are opened and the meridians are unblocked. The active ingredients of the medicine penetrate through the skin pores, are absorbed into the body by the capillaries, and quickly reach the affected areas, taking effect rapidly. It has functions such as inducing sweating, anti - inflammation, pain - relief, balancing the body's humors, and promoting blood circulation. It is used for various skin diseases, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, hemiplegia, post - partum diseases in women, and soft tissue injuries.

With the development of Tibetan medicine, Tibetan medicine bathing has evolved into a specialized treatment field and has become a distinctive feature of the integration of internal and external treatments in Tibetan medicine.

title: Tibetan Medicine Focuses on Prevention, Not Just Treatment | Dr. Tenzin Gelek
description:
In a world dominated by modern medicine, scans, and expensive treatments, Tibetan medicine offers a different path, healing the root cause, not just the symptoms.
In this in-depth conversation, Dr. Tenzin Gelek, a respected Tibetan physician from the Men-Tsee-Khang (Tibetan Medical & Astrological Institute), explains how ancient Tibetan healing principles remain deeply relevant in today’s world of stress, anxiety, and lifestyle-related illnesses.
What you will learn in this video:
What Tibetan medicine really is (beyond myths and stereotypes)
* The concept of balance of three energies (Wind, Bile, Phlegm)
* How Tibetan medicine treats stress, anxiety, depression & mental health issues
* Why modern lifestyles are causing more psychosomatic disorders
* How Tibetan medicine focuses on prevention, not just treatment
* The importance of diet, lifestyle & body type in healing
* How Tibetan medicine works alongside modern treatments like chemotherapy
* Why Tibetan medicines feel more like food than drugs
* The role of herbs, natural elements & holistic care
This interview was recorded at the Tibetan Medical Clinic, Guwahati.Like, share and subscribe our channel.

title: Journey to Enlightenment: Exploring Dharma and Tibetan Medicine
description:
Contemplating the concept of dharma as a form of medicine presents an opportunity for spiritual growth, potentially leading towards enlightenment.

The importance of health on one's spiritual path cannot be understated.

A primary aspect of dharma practice involves working with one's attachments, and Tibetan medicine offers a unique perspective on understanding this.

It introduces the concept of lung, one of three principles governing physical function and health, representing the body's circulatory and movement principle.

Delving deeper into lung reveals its significance, as it closely connects to the functioning of the mind and consciousness, highlighting the interdependence of body and mind.

However, disturbances in lung can lead to issues such as nervous system disorders, anxiety, and depression.

This brings forth an essential principle of Tibetan medicine - the practice of self-care and the importance of creating balance.

Eliot Tokar shares valuable insights on this topic.

How does traditional Tibetan medicine, also known as Sowa Rigpa in Xizang, thrive in the modern world? Described as both "nourishment" and a "knowledge system," Sowa Rigpa is a unique healing practice developed in Xizang's high-altitude environment and remains a vital part of China's traditional medicine. Dr Mikyi Tsomo, a medical doctor and professor, serves as both a guardian of this ancient tradition and a bridge connecting centuries-old Tibetan healing with today's healthcare challenges. Don't miss the chance to learn how this rich tradition continues to thrive and evolve!

title: Tibetan Medicine in Practice: Self Care & Practical Applications with Dr. Nashalla Nyinda
description:
Tibetan medicine may indeed derive from thousands of years of Tibetan and Buddhist tradition, yet its essence is timeless. Learn what makes this unbroken Buddhist healing system work with grace, ease, and power to effect lasting change.

Join Menpa (Doctor of Tibetan Medicine) Nashalla G. Nyinda as she shares techniques that demystify and bring forward these profound practices. You need only look to the kitchen cabinet, your garden, and within to begin to apply basic principles of Tibetan Medicine into applicable and practical healing wisdom.

ABOUT DR NASHALLA
Dr Nashalla began to study Tibetan Medicine in 1999 earning an Interdisciplinary Studies BA from Naropa University in 2001. At the urging of her root lama Venerable Thrangu Rinpoche, she continued pursuing Tibetan medical studies in India, eventually earning her Menpa degree (Doctor of Tibetan Medicine) conferred by Qinghai Tibetan Medical College, Tibet (China) and The Shang Shung Institute of Tibetan Medicine in 2009. Her Master of Acupuncture in the Five Element lineage is from the Institute of Taoist Education & Acupuncture. Nashalla teaches worldwide and is Associate Adjunct faculty at Naropa University. Founding The Sowa Birthing Method™ for women in the late stages of pregnancy or postpartum, she aims to prevent delivery complications and postpartum depression. Nashalla is the clinical director at the Nyinda Clinic of Tibetan Medicine & Holistic Healing in Boulder, Colorado USA.

title: Webinar: Tibetan Medicine: Self-Care for Mind and Body
description:
All of us want to be happy and avoid suffering. Yet too often we make choices that sabotage us. Tibetan medicine, Tibet’s ancient science of healing, teaches that the purpose of life is to be happy, and that after our basic needs are met, happiness results primarily from our own thinking.

Tibetan medicine describes the intricate relationship between mind and body. In this webinar, Dr. Miriam Cameron and Dr. Tenzin Namdul will explain how to use Tibetan medicine for self-care and as a complement to modern medicine. Tibetan medicine empowers us to make mindful, informed choices that create health and happiness for ourselves, others, and our planet.

At the end of the webinar, participants will be able to explain these statements about Tibetan medicine:

1. The purpose of life is to be happy.
2. Health = balance; dis-ease = imbalance.
3. Mental poisons sabotage health and happiness.
4. Live and die in harmony with your own unique, inborn constitution.

title: Mind, Wind, and Heart: Tibetan Notions of Wind in Mental Health and Illness, Susannah Deane
description:
This presentation explores how Tibetan Buddhist and medical notions of the relationship between heart, wind and mind come together to explain the (dys)functioning of the mind, and how this is understood to lead to various forms of 'mental illness' through incorrect Tantric practice and other factors including an individual's behaviors and/or emotional states. I use examples from interview material with lay Tibetans and Tibetan Buddhist and Sowa Rigpa medical specialists, alongside some material from Tibetan medical texts, to explore the causes, symptoms, and treatments of various forms of 'wind illness' in Tibetan perspective.

Contents:
0:00 Introduction
0:44 Rlung
3:05 Wind in Sowa Rigpa
5:28 Wind in Tibetan Buddhist Tantra
7:46 Wind illnesses
15:27 Treating wind illness with medicine
17:30 Concluding remarks

See additional resources at http://www.jivaka.net/buddhism-and-breath/mind-wind-heart/ . 

Susannah Deane is a British Academy Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Department of Religion and Theology, University of Bristol. Susannah does research on Tibetan perspectives on mental health, illness, and healing in both exile communities and Tibetan areas of the People’s Republic of China.

title: Festive China: 24 Solar Terms
description:
The traditional Chinese lunar calendar divides the year into 24 solar terms. More than 2,000 years ago, ancient Chinese people created this overall framework to mark the annual passage of time based on observations of the sun’s motion. Nowadays, the 24 solar terms not only apply to farming, but also guide Chinese people in everyday life.

In 2016, the 24 solar terms were included in UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. Watch this episode of Festive China to find out more.

Festive China is a series of short clips that focus on traditional Chinese festivals and festivities, the cultural connotations of traditional holidays, their development and changes, and how they are manifested in today's China. #ChinaCulture #24solarterms

description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2016
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00647
Description: To better understand the seasons, astronomy and other natural phenomena the ancient Chinese looked at the sun’s circular motion and divided it into 24 segments called Solar Terms. The terms such as First Frost, based on observations of the environment, have been integrated in calendars as a timeframe for daily routines and production, being particularly important for farmers. Some folk festivities are associated with the terms, which have contributed to community cultural identity. Knowledge is transmitted in families and schools.

title: Chinese 24 Solar Terms
description:
This video shows you what the Chinese 24 Solar Terms is.
24 solar terms in Chinese is called 24 Jie Qi.
Jie means each term in a year.
Qi means the climate and climate changing.
Jie and Qi putting together means the climate changes in each term of a year.

The 24 solar terms cleverly combine astronomy, agriculture, phenology and folklore to produce a large number of related seasonal cultures.
Today,Chinese still use it to guide their Agricultural production and daily life.

title: 24 Solar Terms that help you align your energy with nature’s rhythm 🌸👇🏼
description:
24 Solar Terms that help you align your energy with nature’s rhythm 🌸👇🏼

The 24 Solar Terms in Taoism divide the year into phases, helping us align our lives with nature’s rhythms. Each term offers guidance for our body, mind, and energy. Here's a brief look at all 24 and how they can guide your wellness:

🌱 Spring (New Beginnings):

1. Lichun (Start of Spring) – Cleanse your energy, set new intentions.
2. Yushui (Rain Water) – Nourish your body like the earth nourishes seeds.
3. Jingzhe (Awakening of Insects) – Awaken your potential.
4. Chunfen (Spring Equinox) – Seek balance in day and night.
5. Qingming (Clear and Bright) – Clear your mind; embrace clarity.
6. Guyu (Grain Rain) – Cultivate growth, just like crops in the field.

🌞 Summer (Energy and Growth):

7. Lixia (Start of Summer) – Embrace action and expansion.
8. Xiaoman (Grain Full) – Nourish yourself as life flourishes.
9. Mangzhong (Grain in Ear) – Focus on projects bearing fruit.
10. Xiazhi (Summer Solstice) – Peak energy—stay active!
11. Xiaoshu (Lesser Heat) – Manage heat, avoid burnout.
12. Dashu (Greater Heat) – Keep your body cool and mind calm.

🍁 Autumn (Reflection and Harvest):

13. Liqiu (Start of Autumn) – Reflect on your progress.
14. Chushu (End of Heat) – Slow down, enjoy the cooling air.
15. Bailu (White Dew) – Time for self-care and replenishment.
16. Qiufen (Autumn Equinox) – Balance reflection with action.
17. Hanlu (Cold Dew) – Focus on building internal strength.
18. Shuangjiang (Frost Descent) – Prepare for colder times; reflect on the year.

❄️ Winter (Rest and Renewal):

19. Lidong (Start of Winter) – Conserve energy, slow down.
20. Xiaoxue (Lesser Snow) – Focus on inner warmth and mindfulness.
21. Daxue (Greater Snow) – Embrace rest and rejuvenation.
22. Dongzhi (Winter Solstice) – The longest night invites deep rest.
23. Xiaohan (Lesser Cold) – Stay grounded and prepare for renewal.
24. Dahan (Greater Cold) – Reflect on the cycle ending, and get ready for the next.

By living in harmony with these Solar Terms, you’ll flow with nature’s rhythm, improving your energy, health, and mindset all year long. 🌿

Save this and follow us for the best in Taoism. 

How do you align with the seasons in your life? Let us know in the comments!

title: Seasons of China| Chinese 24 Solar Terms-Xiazhi (summer solstice)
description: Traditional Chinese lunar calendar divides the year into 24 solar terms. Xiazhi, the tenth solar term of the year, and the fourth of summer, marks the time when the sun is ultimately perpendicular to the Tropic of Cancer. Wheat becomes ripe around Xiazhi in China. Therefore, since Zhou Dynasty, ancient Chinese emperors have worshiped Earth on that day to wish for peace and abundant harvest. Besides the festivals, Xiazhi was once a public holiday. Before Qing Dynasty (1644-1911), people had a one-day holiday; and in Song Dynasty (960-1279), it was recorded that officials could have three days off during Xiazhi.

title: 24 solar terms TAI JI--Summer Solstice 2023 Dao Yin Exercise
description:
Summer Solstice xià zhì夏至 Jun 21st 2023
Summer Solstice is the fourth solar term in summer. On this day, the sun shines directly on the Tropic of Cancer, making it the longest day of the year in the northern hemisphere.
The lotus is in full bloom. The cicadas begin to chirp. Abundant rainfall makes it the golden period of farming in a year.
After this day, daylight hours get shorter and temperatures become higher.
The Summer Solstice is the day when Yang qi is at its extreme and the Yin qi begins to grow. Therefore, people should protect Yang qi to correspond to the season.
The exercise of Summer Solstice improves the heart meridian sinew of
hand-Shaoyin.
It originates from the inner side of the little finger, goes up into the armpit along the inside of the elbow, then extends to the chest, runs down the diaphragm, and connects to the navel.
When inhaling, step back with one foot and sit back. Meanwhile, push forward your hands with fingers crossed.
Retract the back foot and put down your hands as you exhale.
Repeat several times according to your own conditions.
Summer belongs to fire in the five elements and represents bitterness in the five flavors, so eating bitter food can clear summer heat and increase appetite.


ΘΕΡΙΝΟ ΗΛΙΟΣΤΑΣΙΟ

Το θερινό ηλιοστάσιο είναι η τέταρτη ηλιακή περίοδος του καλοκαιριού. Την πρώτη μέρα, ο ήλιος λάμπει ακριβώς πάνω από τον Τροπικό του Καρκίνου, καθιστώντας την τη μεγαλύτερη ημέρα του χρόνου στο το βόρειο ημισφαίριο. 
Ο λωτός είναι ολάνθιστος. Οι τζίτζικες αρχίζουν να τραγουδούν. Η άφθονη βροχή που πέφτει σηματοδοτεί τη Χρυσή Εποχή της γεωργίας για τη χρονιά.
Κατόπιν αυτής της ημέρας, οι ώρες με ήλιο λιγοστεύουν και η θερμοκρασία όλο και ανεβαίνει. 
Το θερινό ηλιοστάσιο είναι η μέρα που η ενέργεια του Γιανγκ φτάνει στην κλιμάκωσή της και αρχίζει να αναδύεται η ενέργεια του Γιν. Οπότε οι άνθρωποι οφείλουν να προστατέψουν την ενέργεια του Γιανγκ, για να ανταπεξέλθουν στη θερινή περίοδο. 
Η άσκηση κατά το θερινό ηλιοστάσιο βελτιώνει το μεσημβρινό (ενεργειακό κανάλι βελονισμού) Shao Yin. 
Ξεκινά από το εσωτερικό του μικρού δακτύλου του χεριού, ανεβαίνει από το εσωτερικό του αγκώνα έως τη μασχάλη και επεκτείνεται στο στέρνο, κατεβαίνει στο διάφραγμα και καταλήγει στον ομφαλό.   
Όταν εισπνέετε, τραβήξτε το ένα πόδι πίσω και σταθείτε με το βάρος στο πίσω πόδι. Παράλληλα, σπρώξτε εμπρός τα χέρια σας με τα δάκτυλα σταυρωμένα. Επαναφέρετε το πίσω πόδι μπροστά και κατεβάστε τα χέρια σας καθώς εκπνέετε. 
Επαναλάβετε όσες φορές σας είναι απαραίτητο. 
Το καλοκαίρι ανήκει στη φωτιά, ανάμεσα στα πέντε στοιχεία της φύσης και αντιπροσωπεύει την πικρή γεύση, μια από τις 5 γεύσεις συνολικά. Οπότε τρώγοντας πικρές γεύσεις, μπορούμε να αποβάλουμε την καλοκαιρινή κάψα και να διεγείρουμε την όρεξή μας.

title: The History of the Chinese Abacus  中国算盘史
description:
7th grade Social Studies Fair project: The History of the Chinese Abacus

There is no record of who invented the abacus, nor the time when the abacus made its first appearance. The first mention of the abacus appears in the book, “Shushu Jiyi”(Notes on Arithmetic Methods), written over 1,700 years ago, by a Chinese mathematician, Xu Yue. 

Cheng Dawei, a master of the abacus, wrote a book, “Suanfa Tongzong” (General Source of Computational Methods) in 1592. This book made the largest contribution to the promotion of abacus development. 

The Chinese abacus comes in different shapes and sizes. Square, octagonal, and round abaci are normally kept in museums. Rectangle abacus is the common one. The materials for the abacus also have a wide variety including wood, gold, silver, copper, iron, bone, jade, agate and crystal. 

Thanks to the emphasis on traditional culture, in 2008, the abacus was listed as a Chinese national intangible cultural heritage. In 2013, the Chinese abacus was listed as a UNESCO (United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization) intangible cultural heritage. 

In the late 20th century Chinese people started to teach children abacus mental math. The abacus mental math is a system where users mentally visualize an abacus to do calculations. It is believed that the abacus mental math could improve mental capability, speed of response, memory power, and concentration power. 

By tracing the abacus back to its origins, we can see the ancient wisdom behind it. After thousands of years of continuous use, the abacus certainly has become a symbol of the Chinese civilization. 

Although the abacus is not used as often, it still plays an important role in mathematics research, mental math, intelligence development, architecture, art work, literature, and even some areas of the world in modern society.

title: Abacus Multiplication
description:
This video is a tutorial for multiplying two double digit numbers using an Abacus. 

Tips and Tricks:
When determining the number of values in the tens place look back at the original two numbers. Using the first example we have 56*48. Then,
5*4=20 has two digits in the tens place since the values 5 and 4 are located in the tens place in the original numbers.
5*8=40 has one digit in the tens place because only 5 is in the tens position in the original numbers. 


When multiplying the two single digit numbers the resulting product much be written in a two digit format. For example the multiplication should be written in the following format 2*3=06. Failing to write the product as a two digit number will cause the place values to be off and the final answer will be wrong.

title: China’s Abacus The 2,000-Year-Old Supercomputer
description:
The abacus 🧮 is more than just a counting tool—it is a living symbol of China’s intangible cultural heritage. First developed during the Spring and Autumn Period and refined through the Han, Tang, Song, and Yuan Dynasties, the abacus became the world’s earliest “calculator,” revolutionizing trade, mathematics, and education.

This video explores the origins, evolution, and cultural importance of the Chinese abacus. We’ll uncover how merchants, scholars, and mathematicians relied on its ingenious bead-based design for centuries, long before modern computers existed.

Recognized today as a key part of intangible heritage, the abacus is more than a tool—it represents human ingenuity, tradition, and continuity across generations.

title: Chinese Zhusuan, knowledge and practices of mathematical calculation through the abacus
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2013
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00853
Description: Chinese Zhusuan is a time-honoured traditional method of performing mathematical calculations with an abacus. By moving beads along rods, practitioners can perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponential multiplication, root and more complicated equations. Zhusuan has been handed down through the generations, using traditional models of oral teaching and self-learning. Beginners can make quick calculations after some fairly basic training, while proficient practitioners develop an agile mind. Zhusuan is widely used in Chinese life and is an important symbol of traditional Chinese culture and identity.
Country(ies): China

title: Finger Abacus | Learn to count 0-9 | Finger counting | Mental Math
description:
The **finger abacus** is a mental math technique that uses your fingers to simulate the workings of a traditional abacus. It is often used in abacus-based mental arithmetic training programs, especially for children, as an introductory step before moving to a physical abacus. The fingers represent the beads of an abacus, and by moving or "counting" on your fingers in specific ways, you can perform arithmetic calculations.

### Key Features:
1. **Representation**: Each finger represents a certain value, similar to how the beads on an abacus are positioned.
   - For example, the fingers of one hand may represent numbers 1–5, while the other hand may represent higher values like 10s or 100s.
   
2. **Movements**: By "raising" or "folding" your fingers, you simulate the movement of abacus beads, which represent addition or subtraction.

3. **Benefits**:
   - Improves mental arithmetic skills.
   - Enhances concentration and memory.
   - Serves as a stepping stone to mastering a physical abacus.

### Example:
- A common method might have:
  - Thumb = 5
  - Each other finger = 1
  So, raising two fingers and the thumb represents 7 (5 + 1 + 1).

Description: Fujian Puppetry is a Chinese performing art consisting of string and hand puppetry. Faced by new threats to its transmission the local communities formulated the 2008--2020 Strategy for the Training of Coming Generations of Fujian Puppetry Practitioners. This strategy has witnessed a wide participation of practitioners, local people and educational institutions to enhance its sustainability through professional training, compilation of teaching materials, setting up of performing venues, training institutes and exhibition halls, sensitization through formal and non-formal education, regional and international cooperation and artistic exchange.

title: The puppetry of southern Fujian
description:
2018 Outstanding Award

Hello there! My name is Kathryn, I am a B. Communication student at Western Sydney University, majoring in journalism. 
The reason I LOVE journalism is because I am constantly getting to explore and learn about new things. Recently, I was given the incredible opportunity to visit Xiamen University, China for just one week. In this one week, I well and truly got to practice this passion I have for learning, and whilst there I made my video Kat Does China - Puppet Style, with some filming and language-barrier help (the puppet museum workers only knew Chinese!!) from my two new Chinese friends Faye and Cathy.
I had a great time making this video, from learning about Chinese puppetry right through to the editing process (had a blast re-familiarising myself with premier pro and audition), and it was great to investigate something so unique and different. Before making this video, when I thought of China, I had thoughts of tea, food, the Great wall and pandas...so it was great to really expand my horizons and learn about something with such a deep and rich history in Chinese culture. Anyway, I hope you enjoy my snippet into the world of Chinese puppetry!!

title: Fujian artisan fuses puppetry with tea ceremony
description:
In Fuding City, China's Fujian Province, intangible cultural heritage inheritor Zhong Changgan has ingeniously combined the She ethnic group's traditional string puppetry with the refined art of Fuding's renowned white tea. His innovative creation, the puppet tea ceremony show, features a puppet making and serving tea with intricate movements and gestures controlled by dozens of strings.

To bring this unique tea art performance to life, Zhong crafted tea utensils designed specifically for puppet tea ceremonies. During the 2024 "Xinjiang is a Wonderful Land" intangible cultural heritage exhibition held in Korla, northwest China's Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region, he showcased this extraordinary project, captivating visitors with his remarkable blend of cultural artistry.

title: Young budaixi puppet master attempts to revive traditional art
description: Have you ever seen a Chinese opera performed with glove puppets? The art of "budaixi" puppet theatre was once one of the most popular forms of entertainment in Taiwan. But since the 1960s, its traditional orchestra and lengthy narration have lost out against TV and the internet. One young artist is bucking the trend, trying to revive the traditional art. His new budaixi troupe has incorporated a number of innovations into the familiar old traditions, attracting new audiences in the process. Our Sunday special report. In the courtyard of a large building, a young man skips about, puppets in hand, narrating a play. Twenty-seven-year-old KuoChien-fu is doing everything he can to revive the 267-year-old art of budaixipuppet theatre.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterFifty years ago there was almost no entertainment, so of course there were loads of people going to watch budaixi. But with the changing times, there are more and more kinds of entertainment on offer so of course the audience has gotten smaller.Budaixi was brought to Taiwan in the Qing dynasty with the influx of Min Chinese immigrants from Fujian and Guangdong. At first, the plays were based on classic books and historical novels, and were accompanied by nanguan orchestral music. But by the 1920s, martial arts novels had become much more popular themes, along with the derivate “Jinguang” style, emphasizing sound and light effects while using popular music. With the spread of modern media on the TV and internet, the audience for traditional budaixi performances collapsed.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterWhy can’t we develop new traditions, or new “classic” styles from “traditional” budaixi? I believe this is also an avenue open to us.KuoChien-fufelt that in order to find new ways to attract modern audiences to budaixi, he had to start from the basics. So he took an apprenticeship under Chen Hsi-huang, son of the great budaixi master Li Tien-lu.Kuo’s first move was to introduce more detailed movements into the puppet’s repertoire, as well as tiny lifelike props, to help audiences understand the plot. In traditional budaixi, many plot points are explained in long and rambling dialogue and then skipped over in small, abstract gestures.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterIn the old story of Wu Song slaying the tiger, traditionally it was all symbolic, so when the waiter enters, he takes out the drink, puts in on the table, and now see, there’s nothing at all on the table. When he’s put it down, Wu Song does the same, he picks up the ‘air’ and drinks it, just these two movements. In the new version, he can pour the drink, look at it, drink it. It’s finished.He also realized that while the traditional art emphasized the unity of the puppet master and his puppet, nowadays most puppeteers don’t know how to paint their own puppets’ heads, part of the traditional job description. So he conducted his own research into the features of each kind of character, and started to paint his own puppets.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterIt makes it easier for our emotions, feelings, our spirit, to enter the figure. Because you have to like how it looks first, in order to give the audience a better performance.But his innovations have also attracted criticism from many of budaixi’s older fans.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterSome audiences prefer the old style, and they think I’m messing it up. Sometimes they even take me to task in public. We reduce the spoken parts so that more audiences can watch and understand. We don’t want to tell the story through the words. But lots of older viewers think “You obviously aren’t good enough, that’s why you don’t do the speeches, you can’t read the texts so you cut them out.”The audience for traditional budaixi is aging, and Kuo hopes to attract new viewers to the genre. He has even experimented with bringing the puppeteer out from behind the curtain to become an onstage actor.Lai Yung-tingPuppeteerIn the performance, it’s almost as if you’re a street performer. It makes children think, “Oh, so THIS is what Taiwanese budaixi is like! You can also do it like this!”As a child, Kuo loved to watch Peking opera with his elders. The path he’s taken, of learning budaixi and founding his own troupe, has been driven completely by his own passion. But with no family background in budaixi, he’s sometimes lacked resources. And even his own teacher is pessimistic about the future of budaixi.KuoChien-fuBudaixi troupe masterI don’t have any of the experience or history of established troupes, or the physical resources. Everything started from zero.Chen Hsi-huangMaster puppeteer and teacher of KuoChien-fuIt’s a terrible moment. Budaixi appears to be about to disappear. I also worry about what they’re going to do after they’ve learned the skills.Hsu Shu-huiMother of KuoChien-fuBut Chien-fuonce said, “Don’t worry, Mum, I’m not going to starve. But I’m not going to...

project_name_en: Strategy for training Fujian puppetry practitioners
title: Budaixi glove puppets from ‘Pili’ series become magnificent sand art
description:
Are you a fan of "Pili" puppetry? This budaixi glove puppet saga is a cornerstone of Taiwanese entertainment. The TV show has been a favorite among young and old ever since its genesis in the ''80s. Combining glove puppets with CGI, the show has a special Taiwanese flavor. Now, for the first time, Pili puppetry is carved into Taiwan''s sand! A sand art exhibition gives fans the chance to meet characters up close and personal on the beach.

Su Huan-jen the glove puppet meets with a live-action Su Huan-jen. Lots of us grew up watching this puppet hero on his adventures. But we’ve never before seen a sand art Su Huan-jen.

Tai Juan-hung
Hotel owner
The hardest part is the incredible complexity of all the details on the puppet. The design team had many discussions with the “Pili” team, to simplify some details. We want this version to show consumers our commitment to Taiwanese culture and our collaboration.

A sand artist carves out the sculpture, cut by painstaking cut. With all the folds in the clothes and reflected light to consider, creating this one sculpture takes two and a half months. In a test of mastery, the team is making 36 separate sculptures of budaixi puppets. The result is a testament to two art forms in one.

Chiu Cheng-sheng
Pili International Multimedia
We can acquaint more Taiwanese people, young people, with Taiwanese culture, through their tourism. You can meet the “Pili” heroes, and get a greater understanding of our homegrown Taiwanese media entertainment, and get more interested in it. Together we can all enjoy the “Pili” budaixi puppets.

Budaixi is not always a major stop on a relaxing vacation, but this spring, you can enjoy a cultural immersion along with a refreshing trip to the beach.

title: Assignment Asia: Saving the Art of Hand Puppetry in Taiwan
description:
Hand puppetry or budaixi is a traditional art form whereby puppeteers move small glove dolls on wooden stages to tell historical and martial arts tales accompanied by live folk music. Once hugely popular in Taiwan, it had become a dying art in recent years. But as Barnaby Lo discovered, some groups and individuals on the island are not just trying to revive and promote, but also to breathe new life into the old tradition.

title: Budaixi (Chinese hand puppets) master puppeteer demonstration
description:
Recently Z and I attended a Budaixi workshop (Chinese hand puppetry) taught by master puppeteer Yanhong Zhuang, from a renowned puppet family of the Xiamen Hongyanzhuang Inheritance Center of Puppetry and Shadow Play in Fujian Province, China. Her 87 year-old father, renowned as the best Chou puppet performer in China, dazzled us with a demonstration of his incredible skills. Her daughter Raven was on hand to translate.

In addition to showing off different typical characters you might see in a Budaixi play, traditionally trained Beijing Opera performers from Chinese Theatre Works joined in with brief demonstrations of how that character might move on stage in an opera. Budaixi, which is 400 years old, mimics the movement of opera performers. Yanhong told us that lately there is at least one opera troupe in China whose performers' movements mimic the puppets moves which is very interesting to see how the forms influence each other.
