title: Traditional glove puppetry (Budaixi)
description:
掌中乾坤 傳統布袋戲
Glove puppetry is also known as Budaixi, and can usually be seen as part of temple activities for festivals or deity birthdays.

There are two parts to a glove puppetry (Budaixi) performance: the puppeteer performs on stage, while musicians play backstage, using music to control the atmosphere.

Although many new types of puppet theater have emerged, including large scale, flamboyant glove puppetry, which has an army of loyal fans, the small and exquisite form of Traditional glove puppetry (Budaixi) is still one of the most representative forms of Taiwanese folk art.

title: The Epic Drama in Your Palm: Glove Puppetry (Budaixi) Performance  #culturalheritage #Storytelling
description:
One puppeteer. One hand. An entire epic. Experience the breathtaking speed and skill of traditional Glove Puppetry (Budaixi), where ancient legends of gods, knights, and demons unfold in a whirlwind of cloth and wood.

Watch as the puppeteer brings a cast of characters to life with nothing but their fingers. A warrior somersaults into battle, a maiden elegantly sways her sleeves, and a mythical beast roars—all controlled by a single master's hand. The action is synchronized with clashing cymbals, dramatic narration, and poetic dialogue.

This is more than a puppet show; it's a high-octane storytelling tradition, a martial art for the hands, and a living piece of cultural heritage that continues to captivate audiences.

title: Where Legends Come Alive: The Art of Glove Puppetry (Budaixi) | #PerformanceArt #asianculture
description:
In the dim glow of the stage, a universe of heroes, villains, and legends comes to life in the palms of masters. This is Glove Puppetry (Budaixi) , a captivating Chinese performing art where intricate puppets enact epic tales of loyalty, betrayal, and magic.

Watch as the puppeteer's hands give soul to the puppet. Each subtle movement conveys emotion—a general's commanding presence, a maiden's delicate grace, or a deity's soaring power. The performance is synchronized with live narration and traditional music, creating an immersive theatrical experience that blends storytelling, audio, and precise manipulation.

The stories often draw from beloved Chinese classics, preserving and sharing these cultural narratives with each performance. This is more than a puppet show; it is a vibrant form of theater that has been passed down through generations.

project_name_en: Strategy for training Fujian puppetry practitioners
title: The art of Taiwanese glove puppetry (Budaixi)
description:
臺灣布袋戲工藝之美
Glove puppetry, or Taiwanese puppetry (Budaixi) was an important part of early folk activities in Taiwan. It is a combination of traditional wood-carving, embroidery, music and literature. The superb craftsmanship of the carved puppet heads, the costumes’ embroidery and accessories make all the puppets works of art.

The puppet heads are carved from camphor wood. The difficult process starts with marking the positions of the facial features, such as eyes and nose. Next is the carving of the features, then laminating and covering with loess. The laminating and loess-coating process needs to be repeated 5 to 10 times to get rid of blemishes and to make painting easier. The painting stage is done with Chinese calligraphy brushes. The last stage to complete the heads is to apply a coat of clear paint and then add the wig, head accessories and beard.

description:
Footage of Taiwanese glove puppetry (Potehi or Budaixi) filmed in Yunlin, Taiwan for PuppetVision: The Movie, a feature length documentary about puppeteers and puppetry around the world that is scheduled to be released in 2015.

Potehi is a traditional form of glove puppetry that is performed throughout Taiwan. The Taiwanese artists who operate these puppets are remarkably skilled technical puppeteers who perform in a style that is unlike anything seen in the West.

The Taiwanese puppeteers who appear in this video are (in order of appearance):

Master Chong Jen-pi (鍾任壁)
"A-Zon" Chen Han-zon (阿-忠)
Master Huang Shih-chin (黃世志)

Special thanks to the volunteers and staff of The Yunlin International Puppetry Festival and Yunlin Storyhouse in Huwei, Yunlin, Taiwan for all their assistance, support and friendship.

project_name_en: Strategy for training Fujian puppetry practitioners
title: The ‘puppet hospital’: budaixi restoration at Chiayi Performing Arts Center
description:
Did you know that the puppets of Taiwan’s traditional puppet theater sometimes need to go to the hospital? One of Taiwan’s budaixi puppet makers provides a restoration service in Chiayi that’s become known as the puppet hospital. SamadhiTang Creative Puppet Troupe exhibits many puppets at Chiayi Performing Arts Center. It’s also where Wang Wen-chih welcomes patients to the clinic. He was happy to show us around this unique cultural institution. 

The puppet’s hair is knotted and its lavish clothing stained. Wang Wen-chih takes it apart while gently explaining the diagnosis to its owner.

This Taichung budaixi puppet enthusiast has brought along another patient. She likes to take her puppet out for photo shoots, and is dismayed that it has grazed its nose on the ground, scratching some paint off. Luckily she’s chosen a good doctor. Before long, the puppet’s back to his normal degree of refinement.

Puppet theater fan
After the restoration, you can’t see any scars at all. The face is exactly as before. I’m extremely satisfied with how beautifully he’s repaired it for me.

Wang Wen-chih
SamadhiTang Creative Puppet Troupe
It seems like when I can restore a puppet well, it makes them happy, and I feel happy too, because I’m also someone who loves puppets.

Wang grew up in a budaixi troupe family. He’s been playing with these puppets since he was a child. The talented puppet designer makes clothes, headdresses and accessories. When patients come in with minor injuries, he does everything he can to nurse them back to good health, only charging at cost. So far he has restored almost 2,000 puppets.

Wang Wen-chih
SamadhiTang Creative Puppet Troupe
The most expensive ones are perhaps NT$3,000 or more. I might have to repair the entire puppet, and it kind of becomes a puppet hospital. It’s very unexpected for me too.

Many of Wang’s exquisite budaixi puppets are exhibited here at Chiayi Performing Arts Center. The SamadhiTang Creative Puppet Troupe allows other fans to enjoy these cherished puppets. Meanwhile, the puppet hospital is a place where Wang can use his special skills to nurse sick puppets back to life.

project_name_en: Strategy for training Fujian puppetry practitioners
title: Lukang enthusiast collects treasure trove of over 200 budaixi puppets
description:
Turning now to an incredible private collection of traditional budaixi theater puppets. Over more than 10 years, a true budaixi fan has amassed hundreds of exquisite items of the uniquely Taiwanese art. The head of Lukang Township, Hsu Chih-hung, went down to admire the collection.

The chief of Lukang Township is here with puppet theater enthusiast Huang Chin-ying, admiring his 200-strong puppet collection in awe. It features dolls, accessories, heads and costumes: there’s no end of stories to tell.

Hsu Chih-hung
Lukang Township head
This costume was embroidered stitch by stitch by the master dollmaker. It was an enormous undertaking to complete a piece like this.

Huang Chin-ying
Puppet collector
If you turn the eyes a little, it seems to give you a sideways glance.

Huang runs a plumbing and electrical supplies company. But his passion for traditional Taiwanese puppet theater, budaixi, propelled him to start collecting more than a decade ago. Puppets are normally dismantled for safe keeping in boxes and then lovingly reassembled when taken out and put on display.

Huang Chin-ying
Puppet enthusiast
There’s the doll’s costume, its head and its hat. Every time you put it together it’s a slightly different feeling.

The collection of over 200 items set Huang back more than NT$2 million. As the distillation of a traditional art form, they’re worth it, says Huang. Even giving the puppets’ hair a go-over takes real skill.

Huang Chin-ying
Puppet enthusiast
Getting the hair combed costs about NT$6,000. It’s made with real human hair.

Budaixi puppets are meticulously carved and painted. To connoisseurs, the traditional arts are irreplaceable and reward reverent examination. They reflect something unique about the spirit of Taiwan.

title: Budaixi fan opens museum of unique collection, altered puppets｜Taiwan News
description:
Bilingual text here: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2025701W04EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description
雙語文字版: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2025701W04EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description

Our official website: ➔ https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/

Follow us on
Facebook: ➔ https://www.facebook.com/ftvformosanews
X (Twitter): ➔ https://x.com/FTV_Taiwan_News
After retiring, a former typewriter salesman in Hsinchu’s Zhubei City threw himself into his childhood love of glove puppetry, collecting a wide range of puppets. As his collection grew, he turned the first floor of his home into a museum for people to appreciate budaixi as much as he does. Apart from collecting, he also alters puppets so they can change faces or play ball, each with their own special ability.

Have you ever seen a puppet that can dribble? Even juggling or changing faces are no problem. These puppets were all created by avid budaixi fan Tseng Wen-teng.

Needle and thread in hand, he attentively sews an outfit for one of his puppets, using pliers to adjust the area where the thread passed through. Apart from making them look more lifelike, he also makes them easier to manipulate.

As soon as you bring up budaixi, Tseng’s eyes light up. When he was a young man, he sold typewriters for a living. Bu after retiring, he turned back to his childhood passion and started collecting puppets. The longer it’s been, the larger his collection has grown. He also alters puppets to give them special abilities, and even turned the first floor of his home into a budaixi museum. 

Tseng Wen-teng
Budaixi museum founder
I want to preserve this culture. I’m interested in preserving this culture. Otherwise it’ll die off. I’m passionate about it. Everyone is welcome to come check it out.

Whenever you get him talking about budaixi, Tseng can talk for hours telling endless stories. For him, it’s more than an interest. It’s a cultural pastime and a cherished lifelong passion.

title: The Epic Drama on Your Hand: Unveiling Budaixi, China's Glove Puppetry Art #Puppetry #PerformanceArt
description:
Step into the enthralling world of Budaixi (布袋戏), a traditional Chinese glove puppetry where elaborate puppets become vessels of epic tales, heroic legends, and timeless philosophy, all brought to life by the dexterous hands of master puppeteers.

This performance captures the dynamic essence of Budaixi. Witness the stage come alive as intricately crafted puppets, each with distinct personalities, engage in dramatic dialogues and breathtaking combat. The puppeteer's hands are the heart of the action—with precise finger movements, a general can command an army, a deity can soar across the skies, and a heroine can convey deep emotion.

The experience is amplified by a powerful narration that voices multiple characters and an immersive soundtrack driven by traditional percussion, creating a complete and captivating theatrical spectacle.

More than a puppet show, this is a vibrant form of storytelling that has been passed down through generations, preserving the rich tapestry of Chinese folklore and historical narratives in the most imaginative way.

#Puppetry #PerformanceArt #AsianCulture #ChineseTradition #GlovePuppetry #Budaixi #Theatre #Storytelling #VisualArt #CulturalHeritage #ChineseOpera #ArtForm

heritage_item: 福建木偶戏后继人才培养计划
project_name_en: Strategy for training Fujian puppetry practitioners
title: Bringing Wood to Life: The Magic of Chinese Glove Puppetry | #TraditionalArt #PuppetShow
description:
In the palm of a master puppeteer, a carved wooden head and embroidered robes transform into a living character. This is the ancient art of Chinese Glove Puppetry (布袋戏), where legends walk on miniature stages and epic tales unfold at fingertip control.

The magic begins in the workshop. Artisans carve expressive faces from Chinese parasol wood, each features capturing a role's personality—heroes with sharp brows, jesters with comical grins. Then begins the delicate costuming: tiny silk robes embroidered with dragons, hairpieces with miniature beads, and articulated hands that can hold miniature weapons.

On the traditional gold-and-red stage, a single puppeteer brings the figure to life. One hand manipulates the puppet's movements—walking, fighting, even delicate gestures like stroking a beard. The other hand controls the puppet's emotions through subtle turns and nods, while the puppeteer's voice provides all the characters, from heroic generals to mischievous spirits.

This is more than puppetry—it's a one-person opera, a portable theater of imagination where history, martial arts, and folk wisdom meet in the palm of a hand.

#GlovePuppetry #ChinesePuppet #TraditionalPuppet #FolkArt #IntangibleHeritage #ChineseCulture #TaiwanPuppet #PuppetMaster #HandPuppet #CulturalHeritage #AsianTradition

title: Hezhen Yimakan storytelling
description:
UNESCO: List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding - 2011
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00530
Description: Narrated in the language of the Hezhen people of north-east China, and taking both verse and prose forms, Yimakan storytelling consists of many independent episodes depicting tribal alliances and battles, including the defeat of monsters and invaders by Hezhen heroes. Yimakan performers improvise stories without instrumental accompaniment, alternating between singing and speaking, and make use of different melodies to represent different characters and plots. Yimakan plays a key role in preserving the Hezhen mother tongue, religion, beliefs, folklore and customs.
Country(ies): China
© 2010 by Center for Safeguarding ICH of Heilongjiang Province, China
Duration: 00:08:47 - Support: DVD (0053000004)

title: China's Hezhen Yimakan storytelling added to UNESCO intangible cultural heritage list
description:
The Chinese folk tradition of "Hezhen Yimakan storytelling" has been added to the UNESCO intangible cultural heritage list, a recognition that inheritors and researchers say will help to better preserve and promote these age-old artistic customs.

title: Echoes of Yimakan: stories of living heritage
description: The global erosion of oral traditions continues not for lack of value, but because modern life leaves little room for slow, spoken memory. As languages vanish and elders pass on, entire ways of understanding the world disappear with them. Yet, the revival of Yimakan, from near-extinction to international recognition, demonstrates a powerful alternative. It shows that cultural heritage can survive not by being frozen in the past, but by finding a vital place in the present. / The joy and benefit of being bad at something (17:27). On the show: Niu Honglin, Steve & Fei Fei

title: Traditional fish-skin clothing of China’s ‘mermaid’ Hezhen people may hook global fashion
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here: 
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

You Wenfeng is one of only a few people who know how to create clothing from fish skin, a skill of her ethnic Hezhen minority people –who were once so skilled plying the waters of the nearby Heilong River that they are said to be “descended from mermaids”. The 68-year-old woman from Tongjiang, a city in China’s northeastern Heilongjiang province near the border with Russia, fears the loss of the ancient tradition from her people. But that might change she started teaching others her craft, and because the exotic, aquatic leather caught the eyes of international fashion designers working for the likes of Dior and Prada.

title: The UNESCO adds 11 traditions to its intangible heritage list
description:
In cultural news, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization adds 11 traditions to its intangible heritage list. These include Indonesia's traditional Saman dance; Aulacese (Vietnamese) Xoan singing; a healing prayer by Peru's Huachipaire people; a traditional weaving by Bedouin women in the United Arab Emirates; Mongolian folk songs on ancient flutes; Yimakan oral story telling by China's Hezhen people; Yaokwa, an indigenous Brazilian ritual to overcome drought by the Enawene Nawe people; Iran's dramatic story-telling, Naqqa-li and the skills of building and sailing traditional boats called Lenjes; Mauritania's Moorish epic T'heydinn poems; and the Secret Society of Kôrêdugaw, the rite of wisdom in Mali. 

Our thankfulness, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization, for safeguarding precious traditions, artistry, and skills of cultures around the world. May we all appreciate the incredible heritages which enrich our lives on Earth.

title: ACIJ Jerk
description:
The African Caribbean Institute of Jamaica (ACIJ) and the Jamacia Memory Bank (JMB) have been documenting and archiving aspects of Jamaica's Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) since 1973 and 1981, respectively.

Our research has garnered a wealth of information on Jamaica's folk culture such as storytelling, religious beliefs, traditional craft including straw craft and woodworking, in addition to oral history testimonies from our senior citizens.

We invite you to contribute to our storytelling tradition and share your tales with the ACIJ/JMB; pass on your oral history testimonies to us; visit our office to learn about the influence of kumina on our religious beliefs; and keep our traditional craft alive by contacting us to learn these skills.

Regards.

title: Cultura 2025 en China: Patrimonio de la humanidad
description:
En julio de 2025, los mausoleos de la dinastía Xia Occidental fueron inscritos en la Lista del Patrimonio Mundial, con lo que China alcanzó los 60 sitios reconocidos por la UNESCO. Durante el mismo año, el Comité Intergubernamental de la UNESCO decidió trasladar el arte narrativo Yimakan de la minoría étnica hezhen de la Lista de Salvaguardia Urgente a la Lista Representativa del Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial de la Humanidad. Además, su programa de protección fue incluido en el Registro de Buenas Prácticas por su eficacia.  #China #CulturaChina #TurismoCultural #wh #PantallaYTintaChina

title: Ong Chun/Wangchuan/Wangkang ceremony
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2020
URL: https://ich.unesco.org/en/RL/01608
Description: The Ong Chun ceremony and related practices are rooted in folk customs of worshipping Ong Yah, a deity believed to protect people and their lands from disasters. The element is centered in coastal communities in China’s Minnan region and in Melaka, Malaysia. The ceremony includes welcoming Ong Yah to temples or clan halls, delivering 'good brothers' (people lost at sea) from torment, and honoring the connection between man and the ocean. Performances during the procession include different types of dancing.
Country(ies): China; Malaysia
© Ministry of Culture and Tourism of the People's Republic of China and Ministry of Tourism, Arts and Culture of Malaysia, 2019

title: How To Become a Chinese Shadow Puppet Master
description:
Chinese shadow puppetry virtuoso Annie Katsura Rollins shows you a few of the steps that make shadow puppets so unique. 

»Subscribe to CBC Arts to watch more videos: http://bit.ly/CBCArtsSubscribe

Montreal-based artist, Annie Katsura Rollins, is trying to save Chinese shadow puppetry from disappearing. It took years for Rollins to master the craft of making and manipulating the puppets, and those years involved many mistakes and a ton of bandages.

Now, Rollins is showing you some of the steps for you to try and create your own shadow puppets, from cutting the stubborn material to deciding just where an elbow or knee should bend. It's fascinating stuff, captured by filmmaker Ashley Duong.

title: Chinese shadow puppetry
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2011
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00421
Description: Chinese shadow puppetry is a form of theatre whereby colourful silhouette figures perform traditional plays against a back-lit cloth screen, accompanied by music and singing. Puppeteers make the figures from leather or paper and manipulate them by means of rods to create the illusion of moving images. The puppeteers' skills of simultaneously manipulating several puppets, improvisational singing, and playing various musical instruments are handed down in families and troupes, passing from master to pupil. Puppetry spreads knowledge, promotes cultural values and entertains the community, especially the youth.

title: The Master of Shadow Puppets
description:
Fan Zheng’an is keeping the tradition of Mount Tai shadow puppetry alive in Tai’an, China. The art form was created more than 2,000 years ago. Zheng’an, who turns 76 this year, has been at it for 68 years, and he’s basically a one-man puppeteering band. He operates anywhere from four to eight (and sometimes dozens!) of puppets when he performs. Zheng’an also does the voices, writes the stories and plays the music (with his feet, of course). Join us as we pull back the curtain on this master puppeteer’s shows.

title: Carving Life into Leather—The Art of Chinese Shadow Puppets
description:
Step into the world of Chinese intangible cultural heritage: the art of shadow puppetry.
Watch as raw cowhide is dehaired, dried, polished smooth, and intricately carved into expressive figures. Each puppet is hand-painted with vivid colors, sealed with tung oil, and finally stitched together with rods for animation.
This ancient craft isn’t just about puppets—it’s about breathing life into leather and letting stories unfold in light and shadow.

Chinese shadow puppetry is a form of theatre acted by colourful silhouette figures made from leather or paper, accompanied by music and singing.

title: Chinese shadow puppetry: The legend of light and shadow
description:
Shadow puppetry is a traditional Chinese folk art with a history of more than 2,000 years.

Puppeteers hold puppets made of animal hides, project them on a rear-illuminated white curtain, and create bodily movements and facial expressions by moving the bamboo sticks with their dexterous hands. 

To stage a play, members of a shadow play troupe need seamless cooperation in operating puppets, playing music and singing.    

Shadow puppetry has taken on a variety of styles around China by mixing with local traditions. Chinese shadow puppetry artists have been working hard to preserve and innovate this ancient art form to help it shine once again in modern society.   

Watch this video to discover the stories behind shadow puppetry.

📜 The Story: 
This unusual shadow play will melt your heart. you see a pregnant woman struggling with her back pain. Then it shows her gently rocking her little baby to sleep, singing her favorite song. See the baby now became a kid, sharing playtime together. Time flies, and she is now in her teens. Her mom is helping her get ready for school and proudly waving her off. And the day she had been waiting for finally came. Her daughter's wedding. She helps her get ready for her big day, giving her a tight hug and standing next to each other. title: VIP Puppets Shadow Puppet Show: The legend of the Chinese Zodiac: Narrated by: Dave Ward
description:
VIP Puppets Shadow Puppet Show: Legend of the Chinese Zodiac
The Great Wheel of Time:

This Family Friendly show, commissioned by Liverpool City Council, is aimed at Kids and Adults of all ages. It is an epic representation of the How The Legend of the Chinese Zodiac was born. This accessible, cultural show engages children of all ages in the ancient Buddhist story of how the Chinese Calendar came about, in Vibrant Shadow Puppet form.

How did the Great Emperor choose which animal would represent the first year of the Chinese Calendar? Watch the 13 animals race across mountains and rivers; running, hopping, leaping, swimming and plotting their way to victory. All the while learning about their individual personalities and character traits, and how they relate to your own!

Audience members are transported into a magical world, where a storyteller narrates this ancient and epic, traditional tale in just ten minutes, to the backdrop of a magnificent, live shadow puppetry performance, performed live, by three professional puppeteers.

title: Meshrep
description:
UNESCO: List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00304
Description: Found among the Uygur people concentrated largely in China's Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region, Meshrep constitutes the most important cultural carrier of Uygur traditions. A complete Meshrep event includes a rich collection of traditions and performance arts, such as music, dance, drama, folk arts, acrobatics, oral literature, foodways and games. Uygur muqam is the most comprehensive art form included in the event, integrating song, dance and entertainment. Meshrep functions both as a 'court', where the host mediates conflicts and ensures the preservation of moral standards, and as a 'classroom', where people can learn about their traditional customs. Meshrep is mainly transmitted and inherited by hosts who understand its customs and cultural connotations, by the virtuoso performers who participate, and by all the Uygur people who attend. However, there are numerous factors endangering its viability, such as social changes resulting from urbanization and industrialization, the influence of national and foreign cultures, and the migration of young Uygur to cities for work. Frequency of occurrence and the number of participants are progressively diminishing, while the number of transmitters who understand the traditional rules and rich content of the event has sharply decreased from hundreds to tens.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 by ICH Protection and Research Centre, Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of China
Duration: 00:09:26 - Support: DVD (0030400024)

بىللە ئويناپ چوڭ بولغان.
ئايرىلمايمىز مەڭگۈ دەپ،
ۋەدىنى ئۆزەڭ قىلغان.
مەن خاماندا سەن باغدا،

title: Meshrep Yoq Yerde | Where there is no meshrep
description:
Meshrep Yoq Yerde | Where there is no meshrep

London Uyghur Ensemble's live concert at Brunei Gallery Theatre, SOAS. October  6, 2016, London. Performed by the members of the London Uyghur Ensemble: Rahimä Mahmut (singer), Rachel Harris (dutar), Nizamidin Sametov (tämbur), Iskender Ding (Rawap/dap), Dilzat Mahmut Turdi (singer/dap), Patigul Arken (dancer). 

Recorded & produced by Aziz Isa Elkun. London Uyghur Ensemble. www.uyghurensemble.co.uk

_________________________________


Where there is no meshrep 

Translated by Aziz Isa Elkun

(Uyghur folk music & song, Meshrep*)

Are you playing in the garden
Are you singing like a nightingale
You have loved me before
And now are you going to abandon me?!

I don’t want to go there
I don’t want to play music
Where there is no meshrep!

We came here to throw you an apple
We came here to give a bunch of flowers
Like today’s meshrep -
We came here to play happily.

I don’t want to go there
I don’t want to play music
Where there is no meshrep!

www.azizisa.org

_________________________________

Meshrep - Uyghur meshrep is a traditional community musical and dancing gathering. It’s like a musical party.  In 2010 Uyghur meshrep became a UNESCO cultural heritage of the Uyghurs.

- http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/en/USL/meshrep-00304  

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meshrep  

- http://www.meshrep.com/what_is_meshrep.htm 

- http://www.azizisa.org/en/where-there-is-no-meshrep

URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00321
Description: Takes note that China has nominated the watertight-bulkhead technology of Chinese junks for inscription on the List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding, described as follows:
Developed in South China's Fujian Province, the watertight-bulkhead technology of Chinese junks permits the construction of ocean-going vessels with watertight compartments. If one or two cabins are accidentally damaged in the course of navigation, seawater will not flood the other cabins and the vessel will remain afloat. The junks are made mainly of camphor, pine and fir timber, and assembled through use of traditional carpenters' tools. They are built by applying the key technologies of rabbet-jointing planks together and caulking the seams between the planks with ramie, lime and tung oil. The construction is directed by a master craftsman who oversees a large number of craftsmen, working in close coordination. Local communities participate by holding solemn ceremonies to pray for peace and safety during construction and before the launch of the completed vessel. The experience and working methods of watertight-bulkhead technology are transmitted orally from master to apprentices. However, the need for Chinese junks has decreased sharply as wooden vessels are replaced by steel-hulled ships, and today only three masters can claim full command of this technology. Associated building costs have also increased owing to a shortage in raw materials. As a result, transmission of this heritage is decreasing and transmitters are forced to seek alternative employment.

Country(ies): China

A bulkhead is an upright wall within the hull of a ship or within the fuselage of an aeroplane. Other kinds of partition elements within a ship are decks and deckheads. The word bulki meant "cargo" in Old Norse. Sometime in the 15th century sailors and builders in Europe realized that walls within a vessel would prevent cargo from shifting during passage. In shipbuilding, any vertical panel was called a "head". So walls installed abeam (side-to-side) in a vessel's hull were called "bulkheads". Now, the term bulkhead applies to every vertical panel aboard a ship, except for the hull itself. Bulkhead partitions are considered to have been a feature of Chinese junks, a type of ship. Song Dynasty author Zhu Yu (fl. 12th century) wrote in his book of 1119 that the hulls of Chinese ships had a bulkhead build. The 5th-century book Garden of Strange Things by Liu Jingshu mentioned that a ship could allow water to enter the bottom without sinking. Archaeological evidence of bulkhead partitions has been found on a 24 m (78 ft) long Song Dynasty ship dredged from the waters off the southern coast of China in 1973, the hull of the ship divided into twelve walled compartmental sections built watertight, dated to about 1277.
(Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bulkhead)

title: Coin Highlight: (1933-1949) China Republic Sun Yat-sen 'Junk' Dollar | Hidden Numismatist
description:
Obverse: Bust of Sun Yat-sen facing left with Chinese ideograms above
                 Script: Chinese (traditional, regular script)
                 Lettering: 年二十二國民華中
                 Translation: Year 22 of the Republic of China

Reverse: Two-masted ship (a junk) sailing right with one Chinese ideogram on either side.
                 Script: Chinese (traditional, regular script)
                 Lettering: 圓壹
                 Translation: 1 Yuan

Edge: Reeded

Mints:  United States Mint of Denver, United States (1906-date)
             United States Mint of Philadelphia, United States (1792-date)
             United States Mint of San Francisco, United States (1854-date)

  A junk is a type of Chinese sailing ship characterized by a central rudder, an overhanging flat transom, watertight bulkheads and a flat-bottomed design. They are also characteristically built using iron nails and clamps. The term applies to many types of small coastal or river ships, usually serving as cargo ships, pleasure boats or houseboats, but also going up in size up to large ocean-going vessels. There can be significant regional variations in the type of rig and the layout of the vessel.
  The English word "junk" comes from Portuguese junco from Malay jong. The word originally referred to the Javanese djong, very large trading ships that the Portuguese first encountered in Southeast Asia. It later also included the smaller flat-bottomed Chinese chuán, even though the two were markedly different vessels. After the disappearance of the djong in the 17th century, the meaning of "junk" (and other similar words in European languages) came to refer exclusively to the Chinese ship.
  The development of the sea-going Chinese chuán (the "junk" in modern usage) in the Song dynasty (c. 960 to 1279) is believed to have been influenced by regular contacts with sea-going Southeast Asian ships (the k'un-lun po of Chinese records) in trading ports in southern China from the 1st millennium CE onward, particularly in terms of the rigging, multiple sails, and the multiple hull sheaths. However, the chuán also incorporates distinctly Chinese innovations from their indigenous river and coastal vessels (namely watertight compartments and the central rudders). "Hybrid" ships (referred to as the "South China Sea tradition") integrating technologies from both the chuán and the djong also started to appear by the 15th century.

  (Sources: Numista, https://en.numista.com/16370;
                    Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Junk_(ship))

title: Wooden movable-type printing of China
description:
UNESCO: List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00322
Description: One of the world's oldest printing techniques, wooden movable-type printing is maintained in Rui'an County, Zhejiang Province, where it is used in compiling and printing clan genealogies. Men are trained to draw and engrave Chinese characters, which are then set into a type-page and printed. This requires abundant historical knowledge and mastery of ancient Chinese grammar. Women then undertake the work of paper cutting and binding, until the printed genealogies are finished. The movable characters can be used time and again after the type-page is dismantled. Throughout the year, craftspeople carry sets of wooden characters and printing equipment to ancestral halls in local communities. There, they compile and print the clan genealogy by hand. A ceremony marks the completion of the genealogy, and the printers place it into a locked box to be preserved. The techniques of wooden movable-type printing are transmitted through families by rote and word of mouth. However, the intensive training required, the low income generated, popularization of computer printing technology and diminishing enthusiasm for compiling genealogies have all contributed to a rapid decrease in the number of craftspeople. At present, only eleven people over 50 years of age remain who have mastered the whole set of techniques. If not safeguarded, this traditional practice will soon disappear.
Country(ies): China

title: Movable-Type Printing | The Timeless Innovation of Ancient China
description:
✨ Movable-type printing, one of China's Four Great Inventions, revolutionized traditional printing methods and opened new avenues for knowledge dissemination. Invented by Bi Sheng during the 11th century in the Northern Song Dynasty, this groundbreaking technique laid the foundation for modern printing and left an indelible mark on human civilization.

Unique Features of Movable-Type Printing

🌟 Diverse Materials
Early types were made of ceramic, later evolving into wood and metal, catering to various printing needs.

🌟 Modular Design
Each character was carved on individual blocks, allowing flexibility in arrangement and reuse, enhancing efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

🌟 Efficiency
Compared to block printing, movable type dramatically increased production speed and reduced costs, fostering widespread access to literature.

🌟 Versatile Applications
From classic texts to business documents, movable type provided a practical solution for mass text reproduction.

Cultural and Historical Significance
🌟 Knowledge Accessibility
By making books cheaper and faster to produce, movable-type printing enabled broader access to knowledge and culture.

🌟 Global Influence
This invention served as a vital medium for spreading ideas, technologies, and cultural values worldwide.

🌟 A Foundational Contribution
Movable-type printing provided the technical groundwork for modern printing, continuing to inspire innovation.

Modern-Day Relevance
Despite the rise of advanced printing technologies, movable-type printing remains a cherished intangible cultural heritage, celebrated for its:

✨ Cultural Showcase: As an educational tool, it offers hands-on experiences in traditional craftsmanship.
✨ Skill Preservation: Teaching and demonstration ensure that this ancient art is passed down to future generations.
✨ Artistic Value: Many artists incorporate movable type into contemporary designs, breathing new life into this timeless craft.

A Legacy of Innovation
Movable-type printing is not just a technique but a legacy of creativity, intellectual pursuit, and cultural exchange. It continues to inspire us to value knowledge, cherish tradition, and innovate for the future.

title: Living Heritage: Woodblock Printing
description:
Delicate strokes, carved onto wood, with ultimate precision. This is #woodblockprinting, an ancient #printing technique that propelled human civilization.

As Buddhism prevailed in China during the Tang Dynasty (618-907), there was a strong need to produce a large amount of Buddhist scriptures, and copying by hand could not meet the rising demand. Ancient Chinese craftsmen thus came up with a novel way to mass produce printed works. Hence came the prototype of woodblock printing.

Traditional woodblock printing can be divided into four major steps: writing, engraving, printing, and binding. With each step then sub-divided into several procedures, it takes roughly 30 steps to produce a woodblock print.

Carving lies at the center of woodblock printing as this arduous step can make or break the final print. Characters and images are carved to produce raised areas or lines that will eventually apply ink to paper. It calls for a pair of skilled hands.

A five-meter-scroll of the Buddhist scripture Diamond Sutra, printed in 868, is the "earliest dated printed book" in the words of the British Library where it is stored. It is just one example of ancient works of art that not only tell the wisdoms of our predecessors, but also is a testimony to the pursuit of beauty by Chinese craftsmen throughout centuries.

The invention of writing gave life to great thoughts, but it is the invention of printing that made knowledge a shareable fruit for all humankind. #Chineseculture

title: The Hard Part of Inventing The Printing Press
description:
The invention of the printing press in the 15th century revolutionized the world by enabling mass production of written material. Its impact on communication, education, and the dissemination of knowledge was profound. However, the development of this groundbreaking technology was not without its challenges.

One of the major hurdles was the creation of movable type, a system where individual characters could be arranged and rearranged to form words and sentences. Metal typefaces had to be crafted with precision and uniformity, allowing for efficient printing. This required the mastery of metalworking techniques and the creation of durable alloys that could withstand the pressure of the printing process.

In addition to the technological aspects, chemical advancements were essential. The invention of oil-based inks with the right viscosity and drying properties was crucial. Printers had to experiment with various pigments, binders, and drying agents to achieve optimal results.  Another hurdle was developing the. right metal to be used for the typeblocks. The metal needed to be soft enough to be easily melted and molted, but durable enough to withstand the pressures of the printing process.

Overcoming these challenges required the ingenuity and collaboration of inventors, craftsmen, chemists, and scholars.

title: Woodblock & Movable Type: How China Invented the Printing Revolution
description:
🖨️ Printing in Ancient China — From Woodblock to Movable Type
The written word scaled through innovation — how Chinese printing changed human history.

📝 Blog-Style Overview
Long before Gutenberg revolutionized printing in Europe, China had already pioneered printing technology — first with woodblock printing, and later with movable type. These early innovations made the mass production of texts possible and dramatically increased the spread of knowledge, culture, and governance in East Asia.

Both woodblock printing (7th century CE) and movable type printing (11th century CE) laid the foundation for modern publishing and global literacy.

🪵 Woodblock Printing (Tang Dynasty, 7th Century)
In woodblock printing:

Entire pages were carved into wooden blocks (mirror-image)

Ink was applied, and the block was pressed onto paper, silk, or fabric

It was used for books, religious texts, and government documents

🔖 Oldest known example:
The Diamond Sutra, printed in 868 CE, is the world’s oldest surviving printed book with a date.

Advantages:

Durable and reusable blocks

Efficient for mass-copying texts
Limitations:

Labor-intensive to carve each page

Time-consuming to revise or correct errors

🧱 Movable Type Printing (Song Dynasty, c. 1040 CE)
Invented by Bi Sheng, movable type printing used individual characters (initially clay, later metal or wood) that could be rearranged to print multiple pages.

Advantages:

Faster typesetting for lengthy works

Reusable characters

Easier error correction
Challenges:

Thousands of Chinese characters made organizing and using type difficult

Less efficient in China than in alphabet-based systems like Latin

Still, movable type printing flourished in Korea (where metal movable type was perfected) and had a significant influence on later global developments in print technology.

🌍 Global Impact
Laid groundwork for the mass production of books

Enabled wider literacy, education, and bureaucratic administration in East Asia

Spread to the Islamic world and eventually influenced European printing

Became a key precursor to the Gutenberg press (1440s)

description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: www.bilibili.com/video/BV1HG4y1Z7A6/?spm_id_from=333.337.search-card.all.click

The most well-known work of the famous opera master Mei Lanfang, The Drunken Concubine is more of a one-person performance than an opera that follows a long plot. It is a one-act opera that explores themes of the fragility of life, power and sorrow, and requires high skill from the performer. 

This opera is about Concubine Yang, a real person who was one of the Four Ancient Beauties of China. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Beauties

I hope you enjoy~


Cultural notes:

Chang'e is the chinese goddess of the moon who is accompanies by the jade rabbit. They live in the Guanghan palace on the moon.

Meng Haigong (farmer general) – Xue Yongkang

title: Peking opera
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00418
Description: Peking opera is a performance art incorporating singing, reciting, acting, martial arts. Although widely practised throughout China, its performance centres on Beijing, Tianjin and Shanghai. Peking opera is sung and recited using primarily Beijing dialect, and its librettos are composed according to a strict set of rules that prize form and rhyme. They tell stories of history, politics, society and daily life and aspire to inform as they entertain. The music of Peking opera plays a key role in setting the pace of the show, creating a particular atmosphere, shaping the characters, and guiding the progress of the stories. 'Civilian plays' emphasize string and wind instruments such as the thin, high-pitched ''jinghu ''and the flute ''dizi, ''while 'military plays' feature percussion instruments like the ''bangu ''or ''daluo. ''Performance is characterized by a formulaic and symbolic style with actors and actresses following established choreography for movements of hands, eyes, torsos, and feet. Traditionally, stage settings and props are kept to a minimum. Costumes are flamboyant and the exaggerated facial make-up uses concise symbols, colours and patterns to portray characters' personalities and social identities. Peking opera is transmitted largely through master-student training with trainees learning basic skills through oral instruction, observation and imitation. It is regarded as an expression of the aesthetic ideal of opera in traditional Chinese society and remains a widely recognized element of the country's cultural heritage.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 by Han wen / Beijing Bureau of Culture

京剧 《铡美案》 片段 【中英文唱词字幕】
Part from Beijing Opera “Execution of Chen Shimei” (Chinese Lyrics + English Translation + Pinyin)

In Song (宋) dynasty (960-1279), Cheng Shimei (陈世美) won the 1st seed in the entrance exam for central government officials. Appreciating his abilities, Princess married him. 
The truth is Shimei already married Qin Xianglian (秦香莲) at his hometown. Years later, Xianglian and their kids came to Kaifeng (开封), the capital of Song, to find him. To hide the truth, Shimei ordered Han Qi (韩琦) to assassinate Xianglian and the kids. After knowing the truth, Han Qi let them go. Han Qi was forced to suicide for this.  
After escaping the assassination, Xianglian files lawsuit against her husband.
Bao Zheng (包拯), or Bao Longtu (包龙图), is the judge of the Supreme Court of Kaifeng.

Having already proven himself a master of kinetic action with Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain, legendary director Tsui Hark would add lashings of comedy and frenzied expressionism to spectacular effect with 1986’s Peking Opera Blues.

Beijing, 1913. In a time of violent turmoil when local tyrants vie for supremacy, three women’s lives converge in the local Peking Opera house. Together they hatch a revolutionary plan to stop president Yuan Shikai from reinstating the monarchy and proclaiming himself emperor. With the help of a righteous freedom-fighter and a disillusioned palace guard, the rebels must use all their wits and brawn to protect their democratic principles and save the emerging Chinese republic.

Showcasing all of Tsui’s talent for visual spectacle and featuring captivating performances from Brigitte Lin, Sally Yeh and Cherie Chung in the three leading roles, Peking Opera Blues is an explosive thrill ride presented here in a stunning 4K restoration and packed with extras.

title: The Legendary Life of Mei Lanfang - How Peking Opera Conquered the World
description:
Discover the captivating story of Peking opera and the legendary Mei Lanfeng, the master who transformed Chinese performing arts forever.

Dive deep into the rich history and evolution of Peking opera, China’s most iconic theatrical tradition. This video unpacks how Peking opera emerged as the nation’s cultural treasure, outshining hundreds of local operatic forms. You’ll learn why it’s called “Peking” opera, explore its roots in Beijing, and see how the genre blends singing, acting, martial arts, and intricate stagecraft. The spotlight is on Mei Lanfeng, the visionary artist who overcame early setbacks and skepticism to revolutionize Peking opera with his creativity, discipline, and courage.

Follow Mei Lanfeng’s journey from his challenging childhood and rigorous training to his rise as a superstar on China’s grandest stages. Witness his relentless drive for innovation - from modernizing costumes and choreography to introducing new themes that resonated with contemporary audiences. The video also reveals how Mei Lanfeng became an international cultural ambassador, dazzling audiences from Shanghai to Broadway and forging connections between East and West. Even during turbulent times, his artistry and integrity shone, leaving a legacy that continues to inspire new generations.

Whether you’re a fan of Chinese opera, fascinated by world music and theater, or curious about the life of one of China’s greatest artists, this story will leave you in awe of the artistry, perseverance, and global impact of Peking opera and Mei Lanfeng.

title: Decoding Peking Opera Masks: From Colors to Characters
description:
#chineseopera #chineseculture #facepainting #painting #funfacts

Did you know the colors on Peking Opera face paint have a secret code? Unlike in the West where blue means sadness, in China, a character's personality is revealed by the colors on their face. We're breaking down the full guide, from heroes to villains! Red shows loyalty and courage, black stands for justice, and blue represents rebellion but also kindness. You'll see gold and silver on mythical figures. On the flip side, villains wear white for cunning, yellow for cruelty, and green is often used for lower-level demons. After this video, you'll be able to guess a character's personality just by their face!

title: Peking Opera:《Hongniang》京剧: 红娘 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: tv.cctv.com/2019/09/26/VIDEgMn4SmeuyHJYaQazrqvS190926.shtml?spm=C89386.P7wa09jwQaQR.EVc74vQbb7Ue.260
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Hongniang is a classic romantic comedy, telling the story of how the clever maidservant Hongniang united her Lady, Yingying and the scholar Zhang Junrui together in love.
Hongniang (红娘) literally meaning "red maiden, " is nowadays the common term for a female matchmaker, speaking to the opera's renown.

I hope you Enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

2:35 I don't know what he means by "smelt" tranquility either

3:49 Imperial examinations are exams that determine bureaucracy based on merit. If passed one may become an official and rise on the social ladder. It's a pretty big deal. wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_examination

7:22 Yingying's concern with the parrot is that it might copy what she says and other people might hear it.

10:11 Sometimes called ancestor worship, Chinese culture has a custom of offering food and incense to deceased relatives as a sign of respect and thanks.

The dates the characters speak of are of the traditional Chinese lunar calendar, but I've just used Gregorian terms to avoid confusion

When characters talk about respect and honour they are usually referring to li (礼) which can be roughly translated to courtesy or etiquette; rules of what one can do or say in certain situations.

12:29 A courtesy name is a name given to usually men when they teach adulthood at 20 as it was believed to be rude for others to address an adult man by their given name.

24:34 When everyone refers to Sun Feihu's troops as bandits, it is more metaphorical as they call his troops something more like "thieving troops"

24:55 When Madam Cui refers to family values (门风) it can be roughly translated as family principles or honour

26:34 When Zhang suggests Yingying is still in mourning, he is actually saying she's still donning xiao robes (孝服), literally "filial piety clothes." These were worn during periods of mourning where people would abstain from events perceived as joyful out of respect for the deceased.
Filial Piety was an important part of Chinese culture due to Confucian influences.

38:00 Traditional Chinese weddings feature wedding wines from both families, wine a family ferments until the child is married. The White Horse General means he will not be able to stay to drink their wedding wines.

41:16 The 'flowery candles' are a reference to marriage, a reference to the candles that are found in traditional bridal chambers when a couple is married

46:52 Traditional Chinese marriages have the bride and groom first bow to the heavens and the earth, second bow to their parents, and third bow to each other.

49:07 "Quickly sweep the smoke to the pureland, the sun and moon shine together" is a proverb meaning two people will be united together. Hongniang finishes saying the proverb for Yingying, showing they're on the same page.

52:05 I've translated silence from duoko (多口) which directly translates to too much mouths, meaning something more like you speak too much but that doesn't convey the same emotion 多口 usually implies.

54:14 Hongniang's original words "男女有别" is a Chinese proverb translating to "men and women have their differences." This proverb is used by Hongniang to tell Zhang not to touch her so casually.

56:32 The story of Sima Xiangru and Zhao Wenjun is a classical love story of two poets eloping together. 

57:27 Hongniang makes a reference to their wedding wines as winter melon soup as melons tend to symbolise family abundance. Hongniang's saying they'll have a happy family whilst poking fun at how silly the two are by calling their wedding wines melon soup.

title: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00425
Description: Acupuncture and moxibustion are forms of traditional Chinese medicine widely practised in China and also found in regions of south-east Asia, Europe and the Americas. The theories of acupuncture and moxibustion hold that the human body acts as a small universe connected by channels, and that by physically stimulating these channels the practitioner can promote the human body's self-regulating functions and bring health to the patient. This stimulation involves the burning of moxa (mugwort) or the insertion of needles into points on these channels, with the aim to restore the body's balance and prevent and treat disease. In acupuncture, needles are selected according to the individual condition and used to puncture and stimulate the chosen points. Moxibustion is usually divided into direct and indirect moxibustion, in which either moxa cones are placed directly on points or moxa sticks are held and kept at some distance from the body surface to warm the chosen area. Moxa cones and sticks are made of dried mugwort leaves. Acupuncture and moxibustion are taught through verbal instruction and demonstration, transmitted through master-disciple relations or through members of a clan. Currently, acupuncture and moxibustion are also transmitted through formal academic education.
Country(ies): China

Dr Yoann Birling left France at the age of 18 years old to learn the wisdom of Chinese medicine in Beijing, China. He obtained a Bachelor of Medicine and a Master of Medicine from Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, arguably the most reputed Chinese medicine university. During his studies, Yoann followed many TCM experts including Pr Dong Zhenhua, Pr Liu Qingguo, Pr Hao Wanshan and Pr Wang Zhaoyang. In 2022, he completed a PhD at Western Sydney University, exploring the treatment of insomnia with Chinese medicine.

Yoann has practiced Chinese medicine in China, France and Australia for more than 8 years. His clinical focus is on the treatment of mental health issues with Chinese medicine and psychotherapy. Yoann has produced 17 peer-reviewed publications in the field of mental health and one clinical manual on the treatment of insomnia with Chinese medicine. He is also undergoing important work on clinical reasoning in Chinese medicine and the improvement of Chinese medicine education.

title: Moxa Therapy for Breech Baby #alternativemedicine
description:
Moxa therapy for breech baby! 
Acupuncturist can help turn a breech baby, with a combination of acupuncture and moxabustion. Moxabustion is a healing method that’s falls under the umbrella of Chinese medicine. It’s an age-old way of applying heat and can be used as a stand-alone treatment or in combination with acupuncture or other treatments. 

Moxibustion involves warming points on your body by burning the herb, artemesia (also called mugwort or Ai Ye) that’s been compressed and rolled into a long stick. It’s considered to be both warming and drying. This kind of heat is also very moving, which is why it’s a common way to turn an improperly positioned baby in Chinese medicine.

Researchers who have studied the use of moxibustion for correcting breech presentations found that, when combined with acupuncture, it resulted in fewer births by caesarean section.

Using moxibustion on this point is a well-known treatment for breech presentations, and causes your baby to increase its movements and in many cases change position entirely.

title: Moxibustion - What Can It Do For Your Health?
description:
Moxibustion is an essential part of Oriental medicine, which includes traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) as well as Japanese medicine. Moxibustion is an ancient heat treatment, which complements acupuncture and herbal medicine. 

Moxibustion involves the burning of mugwort, a particular tinder of dried herbs, on or near the skin to facilitate healing. It has been used throughout Asia for more than 3,000 years and was first practised in cold climates to generate warmth for the body and to reduce pain. The purpose of moxibustion, as with most forms of Traditional Oriental medicine, is to build and strengthen Blood, improve circulation by stimulating the flow of Qi (energy) and obtain or maintain health.

Ondol Oriental Medicine Clinic is a holistic medical practice based in Toowong Brisbane, Australia.

For more information visit: www.ondol.com.au

_ what is moxa?
-Ling Shu, Guan Neng says that where needle does not work, moxibustion does. 
-TCM theory holds that moxibustion has a dual effect of tonification and purgation. 
-Different from needles and drugs, characteristics of moxibustion in materials and using fire determine that its efficacy is inclined to warming and nourishing. 
-So, moxibustion is often applied in deficiency-cold syndrome, though some excess-heat syndrome can also use it. 
-The roles of moxibustion can be broadly grouped into warm nourishing, warm dredging, and warm melting. 
-Warm nourishing refers to the benefits of warming Yang, tonifying qi, nurturing blood, and relieving depletion; warm dredging refers to the functions of activating blood, dissolving stasis, promoting qi, dredging channels, and relieving pain; 
-warm melting refers to the roles of reducing phlegm, eliminating stagnation, removing wind, dispelling dampness, drawing out poison, and purging heat. 
.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: Moxibustion - Haynes Acupuncture Gold Coast
description:
Moxibustion is a traditional Chinese medicine technique that involves the burning of mugwort, a dried herb, to promote healing and alleviate various health conditions. This ancient practice is often used with acupuncture but can be performed independently. During moxibustion, a small cone or stick of dried mugwort is ignited and held over specific acupuncture points or body areas. The radiant heat generated by the burning mugwort penetrates the skin, stimulating circulation and warming the underlying tissues. This process is believed to balance the flow of vital energy, known as qi, within the body and restore health.

There are two primary types of moxibustion: direct and indirect. In direct moxibustion, a small moxa cone is placed directly on the skin at the acupuncture point and burned until it smoulders, producing a localized warming sensation. Indirect moxibustion involves holding the burning moxa stick above the skin, typically about an inch away, to avoid direct contact with the skin. The heat from the burning moxa penetrates the skin without causing burns, making it a gentler option for sensitive individuals. Both direct and indirect moxibustion can effectively address a wide range of health concerns, including pain, digestive disorders, menstrual irregularities, and immune system imbalances.

Moxibustion is often used in traditional Chinese medicine to complement acupuncture treatments and enhance their therapeutic effects. It is believed to strengthen the body's vital energy, boost the immune system, and promote overall well-being.

title: Turn a Breech Baby using Moxibustion & Acupuncture
description:
Breech Baby? 🤰🌀 
Here’s how Chinese Medicine can gently support fetal turning — naturally. 

🌿 Why It Happens (TCM View): 
• Qi & Blood Stagnation – Energy is stuck in the lower abdomen, restricting movement. 
• Cold in the Uterus – A cold internal environment slows down energy and fetal movement. 
• Emotional Stress – Stress and worry can block Qi, affecting the baby's position. 

 🌿 How We Support Fetal Turning 
• Moxibustion:  
We gently warm Bladder 67 (至陰穴) on the pinky toe using a moxa stick. 
→ This helps activate movement, promote circulation, and bring warmth to the womb. 

• Acupuncture:
We use key points like Bladder 67, Stomach 36, and others 
tailored to your individual needs. 

→ In addition to supporting fetal turning, 
we may include points to help your body feel more balanced 

— addressing issues such as: 
• Back pain • Poor sleep • Stress or anxiety • Edema (swelling) • Digestive concerns 

📅 When to Start? 
✔️ Ideally between 32–34 weeks 
✔️ Each session is personalized — we may adjust techniques or add points week by week 
✔️ Includes lifestyle advice and self-care tips for better results at home 



🌿中醫觀點：胎位不正原因
• 氣血瘀滯 —— 下腹部氣血運行不暢，影響胎兒活動空間
• 子宮受寒 —— 子宮內環境偏寒，影響氣血運行與胎動能力
• 情緒壓力 —— 情緒緊張、焦慮易導致氣機鬱結，影響胎位

🌿中醫如何協助胎兒轉正？
• 艾灸：艾條溫和地灸在小腳趾的至陰穴
→ 有助於溫暖子宮、促進氣血流動與胎動
• 針灸：會根據個人體質選取如至陰穴、足三里等穴位
→ 除了幫助胎位轉正，也會加入其他穴位，幫助以下問題：
• 腰背痠痛、睡眠不佳、焦慮、水腫、腸胃不適

📅 何時開始療程？
✔️ 建議在32至34週之間開始為佳
✔️ 每次療程依體質調整，逐週可能加入不同技法與穴位
✔️ 包含生活建議與居家保健方法，提升整體效果

heritage_item: 黎族传统纺染织绣技艺
title: Traditional Li textile techniques: spinning, dyeing, weaving and embroidering
description:
UNESCO: Urgent Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00302
Description: The traditional Li textile techniques of spinning, dyeing, weaving and embroidering are employed by women of the Li ethnic group of Hainan Province, China, to make cotton, hemp and other fibres into clothing and other daily necessities. The techniques involved, including warp ikat, double-face embroidery, and single-face jacquard weaving, are passed down from mothers to daughters from early childhood through verbal instruction and personal demonstration. Li women design the textile patterns using only their imagination and knowledge of traditional styles. In the absence of a written language, these patterns record the history and legends of Li culture as well as aspects of worship, taboos, beliefs, traditions and folkways. The patterns also distinguish the five major spoken dialects of Hainan Island. The textiles form an indispensable part of important social and cultural occasions such as religious rituals and festivals, and in particular weddings, for which Li women design their own dresses. As carriers of Li culture, traditional Li textile techniques are an indispensable part of the cultural heritage of the Li ethnic group. However, in recent decades the numbers of women with the weaving and embroidery skills at their command has severely declined to the extent that traditional Li textile techniques are exposed to the risk of extinction and are in urgent need of protection.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 by Hainan Provincial Mass Art Center

description:
In this video, I’m slow stitching little fabric landscapes and showing how I’m building the fabric book that holds them all. Each page is layered with bits of thread, texture, and imagination.

It’s not about perfection — it’s about taking a quiet moment to create something small and beautiful from scraps. I’ll share how I choose my fabrics, how I layer and secure them, and some of my favorite stitches using pearl cotton and DMC embroidery floss.

Whether you’re new to slow stitching, an experienced textile artist, or just love seeing how fabric scraps can come together into something special, I hope this inspires you to pick up a needle and start your own tiny landscape.

title: What Is A ‘Fabric Pil’ And How Do You Remove Them?  | A Drycleaner’s Guide #laundry
description:
Fabric pils show up as little fabric balls that sit ontop of your garments. They are most commonly seen on knit garments and only pose a visual hindrance. 

They can be removed pretty easily with disposable razors, pumice based depilling combs, and electric buzzers (the most fun way in my opinion and also not pictures :L). Simply brush/scrap away and collect the removed pills with your hands or a lint brush / roller. 

P&G claims that fabric softener can prevent pilling.

title: The Elaborate Art of Weaving Nanjing Yunjin Silk Brocade
description:
It takes skill, patience and a massive loom to make Nanjing yunjin. The luxurious silk brocade dates back nearly 2,000 years in China. Designer Liu Junzheng crafts the traditional fabric with a team of artisans. They weave silk, gold, peacock feathers and other materials into intricate works of textile art. It can take years to finish a piece. But each and every one is worth the wait.

heritage_item: 黎族传统纺染织绣技艺
title: Li brocade: Weaving preservation with tradition
description:
Every time Liu Xianglan sits gracefully on the ground with her back straight, fastens a handloom around her waist, and begins to weave, a steady stream of questions flows from the curious visitors to her hometown of Wuzhishan city in South China's Hainan province.

They are mostly eager to learn how the colorful threads move back and forth on a set of anachronistic-looking tools and transform into the delicate patterns of Li brocade, says the woman in her 50s.

The loom, an ancient yet ingenious device, stretches before her, with one end tied to a wooden post secured by her feet, while the other is wrapped around her lower back, allowing her to control the tension of the threads through her posture and movements.

"This is warp thread and that is weft thread. These small wooden rods have different functions. Some are for pulling the threads or arranging the warp, and some are for securing the threads," says Liu from Fanmao village in Wuzhishan.

"The size of the fabric varies, so the number of threads required also changes. For example, a brocade that is 20 centimeters wide needs 50 to 60 bundles of threads, but for a 30-cm-wide piece, you need 150 bundles of threads."

The explanation has been part of her routine to promote Li brocade for over 20 years.

title: Looking China: Li Brocade, a tradition that never fades
description:
Living in a world where traditions are disappearing and knowledge is lost, a group of Li people, the ethnic minority group that mostly lives on Hainan Island, have never changed the lifeline of their culture and ancestors.
Fifty-year-old Fu Baiyin spends most of her leisure time creating a unique traditional handicraft - Li Brocade - along with the other Li women and girls, to keep the tradition alive. 
Their lives are actually like a Li Brocade. The clothes they make mirror their lifestyles, morals and customs, beliefs and taboos. This is the charm of the Li Brocade. 

Looking China is a short film series offering a glimpse of China through the lens of 20 foreign filmmakers. We will be sharing their stories in the coming days and weeks.

As the Li ethnic group does not have a written language, its traditions, myths and legends have been passed down through the generations by word of mouth. The Li people also record their stories in their brocade designs. The oldest of these stories features in the “Shan Hai Jing” (The Classic of Mountains and Seas). Black, red, yellow and blue – the dominant colors in the brocades – are still extracted from plants, just as they were in ancient times. Li brocade is considered a cultural gem of Hainan Island.

For over 3,000 years, the Li people of Hainan have woven cotton, bark fibers, and natural dyes into vibrant brocade—an art often called “an encyclopedia worn on the body.” Each motif reflects Li history, beliefs, and daily life.

heritage_item: 黎族传统纺染织绣技艺
title: Yunjin Brocade Master 1 (720p)
description:
Yunjin Brocade Master features Master Zhou Shuangxi, the "Heir to Yunjin Brocade". Master Zhou explains how the ancient art form has been preserved and passed down from generation to generation. Yunjin Brocade was once only fit for a Chinese emperor, weaved with fine silks and threads made from peacock feathers and real gold. Today, Yunjin Brocade is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage List and continues to be preserved, cherished, and modernized by the Nanjing Brocade Research Institute in Nanjing, China.

The attention to detail and time consuming processes to make a Yunjin Brocade garment is a collaborative effort. The reproduction of ancient designs and the creation of new ones are all done by hand, from the making of the silk, gold and peacock threads to the knotting and weaving process. The weaver's hands are the last to touch the creation. One slip up will ruin a garment worth thousands of dollars. How does Master Zhou and other Yunjin Brocade weavers do it? Watch and see!

heritage_item: 黎族传统纺染织绣技艺
title: Culture Identity and Innovation Based on Hainan Li Brocade - Future Patch of Sustainability of ICH
description:
CIVINEDU 2021

Abstract: 
Based on the field investigation of Li Brocade weaving skills and the interview with the skill inheritors, the author found that the core concept of the inheritance and protection of Li Brocade skills needs to be clarified and improved, and the link between the government and the craftsman needs to be further improved to form a complete chain. Based on the benign operation of the ecological system under the concept of ecological culture, this paper discusses the five elements needed for the ICH sustainability taking Li Brocade weaving skills as an example. By analyzing the organic correlation among various elements, we can promote the formation of the closed loop of ICH sustainability, to explore the sustainable development path of ICH.

Author：
Yue WANG, Associate Professor of Acade0my of Arts & Design, Tsinghua University
Yang LIU, Doctoral Candidate of Academy of Arts & Design, Tsinghua University
Yuke MA, Doctoral Candidate of Academy of Arts & Design, Tsinghua University

title: Ancient Interlocking Wood Bridge Explained #TraditionalArchitecture #Shorts
description:
Discover the ancient engineering technique of building a wooden bridge using only nine interlocking pieces of timber—no nails, no metal supports.
This traditional construction method appears in historic manuscripts and reflects the remarkable craftsmanship passed down through generations.
Each wooden beam is precisely carved to fit seamlessly with the others, forming a strong arched structure capable of withstanding heavy loads.
This timeless method highlights the brilliance of ancient architectural design and the ingenuity of early builders who relied solely on skill, balance, and natural materials.
Watch how this unique bridge takes shape through perfect interlocking geometry and traditional craftsmanship.

heritage_item: 中国木拱桥传统营造技艺
title: Traditional design and practices for building Chinese wooden arch bridges
description:
UNESCO: Urgent Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00303
Description: Wooden arch bridges are found in Fujian Province and Zhejiang Province, along Chinas south-east coast. The traditional design and practices for building these bridges combine the use of wood, traditional architectural tools, craftsmanship, the core technologies of beam-weaving and mortise and tenon joints, and an experienced woodworkers understanding of different environments and the necessary structural mechanics. The carpentry is directed by a woodworking master and implemented by other woodworkers. The craftsmanship is passed on orally and through personal demonstration, or from one generation to another by masters teaching apprentices or relatives within a clan in accordance with strict procedures. These clans play an irreplaceable role in building, maintaining and protecting the bridges. As carriers of traditional craftsmanship the arch bridges function as both communication tools and venues. They are important gathering places for local residents to exchange information, entertain, worship and deepen relationships and cultural identity. The cultural space created by traditional Chinese arch bridges has provided an environment for encouraging communication, understanding and respect among human beings. The tradition has declined however in recent years due to rapid urbanization, scarcity of timber and lack of available construction space, all of which combine to threaten its transmission and survival.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 by Yuzhou Zhang

heritage_item: 中国木拱桥传统营造技艺
title: Ancient bridge-building techniques, craftsman spirit passed down from generation to generation
description:
As one of the representatives of traditional bridge structures, stone arch bridges have remarkable characteristics such as ingenious structure, beautiful shape and durability:

🧱 Structural and technological characteristics of stone arch bridges

1. Strong structural stability (clever mechanical principles)

The arch bridge relies on the arch structure to convert the vertical load into lateral thrust, which is then transmitted to the foundation by the bridge pier.

The fit of the stones and the gravity compression form a "force closed loop".

The "locking stone" in the middle is the key to the arch, locking the structure as a whole and not easy to collapse.

2. Local materials and craftsmanship rely on experience

Local stones (such as granite, sandstone, etc.) are usually used, which have good weather resistance.

Craftsmen use "knocking, listening, measuring, chiseling" and other processes to manually repair, relying entirely on experience for precise docking.

3. Beautiful shape, integrated into nature

The arch bridge has an elegant arc and smooth lines, reflecting the aesthetic idea of ​​"harmony between man and nature".

It forms a complete arc with the reflection in the water, which is called "perfect bridge" or "mirror bridge". 

4. Strong durability, standing for thousands of years

Many ancient Chinese stone arch bridges (such as the Zhaozhou Bridge) have a history of thousands of years and are still strong.

Stone is not easy to corrode, is not afraid of water, wind and snow, and is suitable for mountain and water environment.

This ancient method (similar to a woven timber arch or Chinese “lu ban” bridge) has been used for centuries to cross narrow streams. The finished bridge flexes slightly but holds his weight. title: Chinese Wooden Arch Bridges Added To UNESCO Intangible Heritage List
description:
The traditional design and craftsmanship of Chinese wooden arch bridges were officially included in UNESCO's list of intangible cultural heritage on Thursday. This recognition came during the 19th session of the Intergovernmental Committee in Asunción, Paraguay, where the item was removed from the urgent safeguarding list. Found primarily in Fujian and Zhejiang provinces, these 1,000-year-old structures exemplify sustainable practices, blending woodcraft, "beam-weaving" techniques, and mortise-and-tenon joints. They reflect the harmony between human ingenuity and natural resource management, addressing both community needs and environmental preservation. Watch the video to know more.

heritage_item: 中国木拱桥传统营造技艺
title: China Timber Arch Bridge (From Zhang Zeduan's painting: "Qingming Shanghe Tu")
description:
China Bridge 2 - CAD demonstration of the construction of the "Rainbow Bridge" depicted in the Qingming Shanghe Tu painting by Zhang Zeduan (1085-1145 A.D.) using the connector described by patent US10053854 Beam Connector for Arch Structure. (Chinese Patent # ZL 201780032653X)

This bridge was originally built in 1000 A.D.  It was one of a few dozen bridges built using the same design in the middle of the Song Dynasty.  Government officials at the time required that the bridges be built without obstructing the canals in any way.  Stone or wooden bridge designs of the time required scaffolding which always blocked the waterway for months or years.  

The architect of this design figured out a way to cantilever the ribs of the bridge and connect them mid-stream, creating a series of supporting arches without needing any scaffolding. No record of how he did it has survived. All the bridges he built had disappeared when another wooden bridge design appeared about 400 years later ("Lounge Bridges" https://ich.unesco.org/en/USL/traditional-design-and-practices-for-building-chinese-wooden-arch-bridges-00303).

NOVA sponsored the construction of a 1/2 size replica of the "Rainbow Bridge" in 1999 ( https://youtu.be/RwIFZKd04EM ).  The NOVA team, which included Tang Huan Cheng, the most famous Chinese architect who had studied the bridge for decades, had to use scaffolding and medieval European iron spikes to complete the project, suggesting that they had not discovered how the original bridge was built.

All the materials needed to construct the bridge as shown in this CAD video were commonly available in 1000 A.D. and the required steel casting and forming technology had been in use for making military equipment for more than a century.

title: What is Qiang New Year In China
description:
Qiang New Year is a traditional festival of the Qiang people in Sichuan Province, China. The Qiang, an ancient nation with a history of three thousand years, have their own unique traditional culture, and the Qiang New Year is a typical representative of it.
According to the Qiang calendar, a year consists of 365 days, which are divided into 10 months. The celebration takes place on the first day of the tenth lunar month every year. During the festival, the Qiang people worship the heavenly gods and pray for prosperity. Villagers, led by a shaman known as "Sibi," dance the Pi Gu dance and the Salang dance.

In 2009, at the fourth meeting of the UNESCO Intergovernmental Committee for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage held in the United Arab Emirates, the Qiang New Year was officially approved and included in the first batch of the "Urgently Needed to be Protected Intangible Cultural Heritage List."
