title: How Qiang Ethnic Group Celebrate New Year?
description:
Qiang New Year, a mysterious celebration from the remote mountains of Sichuan, China. just earned a spot on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list.

Join me as I venture into the heart of the Qiang people’s world—a group so enigmatic that they’ve kept their traditions alive without a written language! The Qiang New Year is filled with ancient rituals, mysterious ceremonies, and celebrations you’ll never believe existed! 

From ancient prayer chants in the dead of night to the unique liquor rituals, you’ll be transported to a village that feels like a step back in time. But that’s not all—the food, the dances, and the vibrant totems are unlike anything you’ve ever seen.

Whether you're a culture enthusiast or simply love exploring new traditions, this video will open your eyes to the richness of Qiang culture. Don’t forget to like, share, and subscribe for more adventures like this!

title: Qiang New Year festival
description:
UNESCO: Urgent Safeguarding List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00305
Description: The Qiang New Year Festival, held on the first day of the tenth lunar month, is an occasion for the Qiang people of Chinas Sichuan Province to offer thanks and worship to heaven for prosperity, reaffirm their harmonious and respectful relationship with nature, and promote social and family harmony. The solemn ritual sacrifice of a goat to the mountain is performed by villagers clad in their finest ceremonial dress, under the careful direction of a shibi (priest). This is followed by the communal sheepskin-drum and salang dances, led by the shibi. The ensuing festivities combine merrymaking with the chanting of traditional Qiang epics by the shibi, singing and the drinking of wine. At the end of the day the heads of families preside over family worship during which sacrifices and offerings are made. Through the festival, Qiang traditions distilling history and cultural information are renewed and diffused, and social behaviours are reinforced, the community expressing respect and worship towards all creatures, the motherland and their ancestors. Participation in the festival has declined in recent years due to migration, declining interest in Qiang heritage among the young and the impact of outside cultures, but the 2008 Sichuan earthquake that destroyed many of the Qiang villages and devastated the region put the New Year festival at grave risk.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 Culture Department of Sichuan Province

title: Visiting a Minority Village in Sichuan, China - a STONE FORTRESS | Qiang Minority Village
description:
Ah, so you're looking for a minority village in China ? This minority, the Qiang, live up in the mountains of northern Sichuan. ALL my social media: https://www.linktr.ee/austininchina This minority is almost exclusively in Sichuan, and their villages are made of stone! Yeah, a stone village - a fortress village! This is a really cool place - watch till the end! Chinese minorities are something special!

This was an interesting one! Tim and I went on another trip - this time up into northern Sichuan - the Ngawa Tibetan and Qiang Autonomous Region - called A'ba in Chinese. We went there to visit this place - the 桃坪羌寨 - Taoping Qiang Village - a mountain stronghold built to protect against Tibetan invaders (among others). It has a history of over 2,000 years, so it's a real, legitimate, historic, and authentic ancient town / village in China. They're getting harder and harder to find nowadays. Ancient towns and villages are disappearing and being lost to "progress." 

After this, we headed up to Bipenggou (Bipeng Valley) - you'll see more in the next video! It's a beautiful place!

Feel free to subscribe to see more videos about what it is like to be an American (or any kind of foreigner) in China! All are welcome!

title: Qiang ethnic group celebrates New Year in SW China
description:
Beichuan Qiang Autonomous County, southwest China's Sichuan ProvinceThe Qiang New Year Festival, held on the first day of the tenth lunar month, is a joyous occasion for the Qiang people to celebrate the harvest and express gratitude for prosperity.
Various activities are held during the festival. Normally, people butcher pigs, offer sacrifices to ancestors, and enjoy pork dishes together.
The festival, which involves sacrificial ritual, singing and dancing, was inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2009 for its cultural value and significance.

title: Three Chinese items added to the UNESCO heritage list
description:
Three Chinese items - the Li ethnic group's traditional textile techniques of spinning, dyeing, weaving, and embroidering; the Qiang New Year festival, a traditional annual event of the Qiang ethnic group in China; and traditional design and practices for building Chinese wooden arch bridges - were added to the UNESCO heritage list during the 19th session of the Intergovernmental Committee for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage held in Asuncion, Paraguay. After the meeting, Bangladesh NGO representative Lubna Marium said, "China has so many minority ethnic groups. The fact that it is giving importance to these ethnic groups, giving them the same status as all others shows that China has the good will to maintain plurality and cultural diversity. I very much appreciate that."

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00202
Description: A popular saying among the Dong people in Guizhou Province in southern China has it that rice nourishes the body and songs nourish the soul. Their tradition of passing on culture and knowledge in music is exemplified in the Grand Song of the Dong ethnic group, multi-part singing performed without instrumental accompaniment or a leader. The repertoire includes a range of genres such as ballads, childrens songs, songs of greeting and imitative songs that test performers virtuosity at mimicking the sounds of animals. Taught by masters to choirs of disciples, Grand Songs are performed formally in the drum-tower, the landmark venue for rituals, entertainment and meetings in a Dong village, or more spontaneously in homes or public places. They constitute a Dong encyclopaedia, narrating the peoples history, extolling their belief in the unity of humans and nature, preserving scientific knowledge, expressing feelings of romantic love, and promoting moral values such as respect for ones elders and neighbours. Grand Song is performed widely today, with each village boasting various choirs divided by age and sometimes gender. In addition to disseminating their lifestyle and wisdom, it remains a crucial symbol of Dong ethnic identity and cultural heritage.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 Ministry of Culture

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: How China's Dong ethnic group saved their culture through music
description:
The Dong people in southwest China’s Guizhou Province believe rice nourishes the body and songs nourish the soul. Their traditions are best embodied in the “Grand Song”, a multi-part singing feat without instrumental accompaniments or a conductor. The polyphonic music that has been sung for over 2,500 years tells stories from history, extols nature and romance, and promotes moral customs. It joined UNESCO’s Representative List of the Intangible Culture Heritage of Humanity in 2009.

title: Grand song of Dong ethnic group| CCTV English
description:
The Grand Song of Dong Ethnic Minority – or Grand Chorus, is the generally accepted title used by outsiders to describe folk-song traditions of China's Dong ethnic minority. It was inscribed by UNESCO as an intangible cultural heritage in 2009. Originated in the Spring and Autumn period, it has a history of more than 2,500 years. Performed without aid from musical instruments and a conductor, it is a form of folk chorus with polyphonic parts. Watch to enjoy the beautiful voices sung by these beautiful Dong woman.

An intangible cultural heritage certified by UNESCO, the Grand Song of Dong Ethnic Minority is a vocal chorus using alternating breaths and no conductor or accompaniment, displaying the harmonious coexistence of humankind and nature. The love songs of the Grand Song, combined with elements of the Yi ethnicity and the resonance of distinct voice parts, is a display of human feelings and perceptions of life. project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Grand Songs of the Dong Ethnic Group
description:
On the second day in the village, the four of us “found” the grand songs in the art troupe of Mrs. Zhong Shengli.

For those who have little knowledge about the Grand Songs of the Dong Ethnic Group, let me explain first. Grand Songs of the Dong Ethnic Group, is known as “ga lao” in Dong language, where “ga” means song and “lao” means grand. Dating back to the Spring and Autumn and the Warring States Periods, it has a history of over 2.500 years. As China’s earliest-discovered and internationally recognized polyphonic, multi-part singing performed without instrumental accompaniment or a conductor, the Grand Songs has been regarded as one of the representative works of the Intangible Cultural Heritage by the UNESCO.

When we arrived, the first song – Singing the Dong Ballads, just began.

Without instrumental accompaniment, the 24 singers made the whole room tremble with their beautiful singing.

Now, let’s enjoy the performance on the spot with my exclusive broadcast. #VivirenGuizhou

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Grand Songs of the Dong Ethnic Group: Visit the Dong village and look for the grand songs
description:
Unlike Singing the Dong Ballads, the next grand song is more like a cup of tea with its melodious tune.

A girl played Dong pipa (a plucked string instrument) and sang the song Lovesickness after Long Separation, whispering her love and longing for a boy. “Trees on hills turn green in spring, swallows fly back to nestle sooner or later; my dear love, don’t make me spend day and night missing you, we should be together like the birds sharing a nestle and the fish sharing a pond”.

The single teammates were moved.

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Dong Grand Song: A Living Polyphonic Tradition
description:
#hmong #tradition #folksong Dong Grand Song (Kam Big Song) is a traditional form of polyphonic singing practiced by the Dong people in southern China.
It is performed without instruments and without a conductor, relying entirely on memory, listening, and collective experience.
Each voice enters naturally, forming complex harmonies that are learned through daily life rather than written notation.
More than a performance, Dong Grand Song is a living tradition—one that carries history, community, and time through human voices alone.

title: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
description: The Kam people, officially known in China as Dong people (Chinese: 侗族; pinyin: Dòngzú; endonym: Gaeml, [kɐ́m]), a Kam–Sui people of Southern China, are one of the 56 ethnic groups officially recognized by the People's Republic of China. They are famed for their native-bred Kam Sweet Rice (Chinese: 香禾糯), carpentry skills and unique architecture, in particular a form of covered bridge known as the "wind and rain bridge" (Chinese: 风雨桥). The Kam people live mostly in Eastern Guizhou, Western Hunan and Northern Guangxi in China. Small pockets of Kam speakers are found in Tuyên Quang Province in Vietnam. The Kam people call themselves Kam, Geml, Jeml or Gaelm.

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Episode 6: Kam Grand Choir 第六集：侗族大歌
description:
In the past, the Dong People had no written language of their own, so they had been using songs to record their culture, history, production knowledge and social custom. Their singing is called Kgal Laox in their own language, which can be literally translated as Kam Grand Choir in English. It is a polyphonic choir without a conductor or musical accompaniment. The songs are performed by chorus made up of three people or more with at least one leading, one for alt and several for bass. Most Dong villages have one chorus and some have a dozen. The chorus is built up by people of the same gender and generation or from the same clan, and the Dong children usually join the chorus when they are very young. Singers are trained by singing masters of their village in leisure time until they are qualified for competing in the Drum Tower. Songs are considered knowledge and culture by the Dong. The singing master is the successor, disseminator and creator of the Dong culture and traditions, who is of high superiority and authority. The more songs one can sing, the more educated he is. Rich contents and multiple ways of singing are closely related to the Dong’s social traditions, life philosophy and way of life.
历史上，由于侗族没有自己的文字，侗族的民族历史、社会知识、生产生活、伦理道德等都是用歌来记录与传承。侗族大歌简称大歌，侗语称“嘎老”，“嘎”就是歌，“老”具有宏大和古老的意思。侗族大歌以“众低独高”，复调式多声部合唱为主要演唱方式。侗族大歌需要3人以上的歌班(队)才能演唱，每个歌班包括至少一个领唱，一个高音和若干低音。一般侗族孩子很小便进入歌班学习，几乎每个侗寨都有歌班，有的侗寨多达10来个；歌班的组建一般遵循同族、同性、同辈的原则，由本族或本寨经验丰富的歌师在农闲时间专门教授，直到他（她）们能独立参加鼓楼对歌为止。歌师在侗寨拥有一种超然的权威，他们是一切传统的继承者、传播者与缔造者。因此，侗家人视歌为宝，他们认为歌就是知识，就是文化，谁掌握的歌多，谁就是有知识的人。他们的演唱内容、表现形式，无不与侗人的习俗、性格、心理以及生活环境息息相关，是对侗族历史的真实记录，是侗族文化的直接表现。

title: 侗族大歌   Yandong Grand Singers (China) - Lotus Music Festival-Bloomington, Indiana - September 2019
description:
Lotus Music Fesival - Bloomington Indiana - Sept 2019 - 
 侗族大歌
*from Wikipedia*:
The Kam people are internationally renowned for their polyphonic choir singing, called Kgal Laox in the Kam language (Chinese: 侗族大歌), which can be literally translated as Kam Grand Choir or Grand song in English. The Kam Grand Choir has been listed by UNESCO as a world-class intangible cultural heritage since 2009. Kam choral songs include nature songs, narratives, and children's songs.

One-part songs (as opposed to polyphonic, or many-part, songs) can be sung by one or many people.[2] They include:

Duo Ye songs
Love songs - accompanied by the pipa or niutuiqin
Drinking songs
Bride's songs
Mourning songs
Pipa songs
*also from Wikipedia*
iThe Dong people, also known as Kam people (Chinese: 侗族; pinyin: Dòngzú; endonym: Gaeml [kɐ́m]), a Kam–Sui people of southern China, are one of the 56 ethnic groups officially recognized by the People's Republic of China. They are famed for their native-bred Kam Sweet Rice (Chinese: 香禾糯), carpentry skills, and unique architecture, in particular a form of covered bridge known as the "wind and rain bridge" (Chinese: 风雨桥). The Kam people live mostly in eastern Guizhou, western Hunan, and northern Guangxi in China. Small pockets of Kam speakers are found in Tuyên Quang Province in Vietnam.[1]

project_name_en: Grand song of the Dong ethnic group
title: Discovering the Enchanting Beauty of China's Dong Villages with Aerial Footage
description:
The Dong ethnic minority in China is renowned for their unique culture and picturesque village architecture. The Dong villages, also known as "Dongzhai," are typically built along the contours of the mountains and rivers, with layouts that vary depending on the terrain. The four main types of Dong villages are the flatland type, mountain foothill type, mountain ridge type, and mountain valley type.

A typical Dong village is comprised of various elements such as wind and rain bridges, drum towers, pavilions, gateways, hanging houses, well pavilions, drying racks, and sacrificial temples. These structures are not only functional but also aesthetically pleasing, and they represent the Dong people's profound understanding of their natural environment.

Dong villages are mainly found in Guangxi, Guizhou, and Hunan provinces in China. They are a perfect example of how human civilization can be harmoniously integrated with the natural landscape. A visit to a Dong village is an opportunity to witness the Dong people's way of life, learn about their unique customs and traditions, and experience the beauty of their architecture and natural surroundings.

title: 第四屆兩岸非物質文化遺產月系列活動：2017多彩貴州
description:
「霓裳華冠～多彩貴州苗族服飾展覽」精選苗族各支系盛裝服飾與多套巧奪天工的珍貴銀飾來台展出；並邀七位大陸「國家級」與省級非物質文化遺產代表性傳承人於展場演示，透過傳承人精湛的技藝，觀眾們可以充分領略貴州非物質文化遺產的迷人風貌。展覽自9月8日起至24日止，於高雄佛光山佛陀紀念館火熱展開。此外，9月16日、23日將開設兩場工作坊，提供民眾實作的機會，零距離接觸、感受貴州民俗工藝的魅力。另將於9月18日於佛陀紀念館舉辦教育推廣課程；9月19日於高雄第一科技大學舉辦「傳統與現代工藝的對話～苗族銀飾鍛造藝術」講座活動，教育推廣課程與講座活動均為免費參加。活動詳情請持續關注活動專頁（www.ichmae.com）與沈春池文教基金會臉書粉絲團！

「根與魂～多彩貴州非物質文化遺產演出」內容特別安排了享譽中外、被聯合國列入「人類非物質文化遺產」的「侗族大歌」、侗族青年浪漫的「行歌坐月」與牛腿琴。「侗族琵琶歌」、「苗族飛歌」以及奔放的「反排木鼓舞」、「錦雞舞」、「苗族蘆笙舞」（滾山珠）等多項被列入大陸「非物質文化遺產名錄」的項目，介紹貴州非物質文化遺產豐富而獨特的風貌。首場演出將於9月9日於佛光山佛陀紀念館大覺堂隆重開演；9月10日則將於佛館園區內以定時不定點的廣場演出，展現貴州絢麗多彩的文化魅力，也讓觀眾親身領會「非物質文化遺產就在你我生活之中」的樂趣！活動詳情請持續關注活動專頁（www.ichmae.com）與沈春池文教基金會臉書，或洽詢電話（02-8787-2787）。

description:

Lyrics:
叹晦明苦短
生老病苦 皆归于死
仙骸有终空叹惋
神枝莫莫 庶卉群生
木实唪唪遍南山

Rough Translation:
The darkness and the light are short.
Life, old age, sickness and suffering all end in death.
It's a pity to see the end of an immortal's life.
The branches of the gods are inexhaustible, and the flowers are all in abundance.
The fruits of the trees are recited all over the southern mountains.

The lyrics are in Dong Language (also known as Kam language), spoken by the people of Kam/Dong ethnic group (primarily from south west China). The music incorporates the ethnic group's Grand Song (kgal laox) which is a type of polyphonic folk choir, listed by UNESCO as a world-class intangible cultural heritage. Coincidentally, the Dong people have a traditional festival 'Lin Kuan Day' (couldn't find a better translation but it's 林王节 in mandarin), that celebrates hero Lin Kuan and often involves placing offerings beneath maple trees. When the maple leaves turn red in autumn (when Lin Kuan was captured), it is known as Dan Feng (cinnabar maple, basically).

title: 侗族大歌 Kam Grand Choirs | Traditional Music of Dong Nationality 侗族歌曲 无伴奏/无指挥/人声大合唱 侗族民族文化
description:
Kam Grand Choirs | Traditional Music of Dong Nationality 侗族传统民族文化 侗族歌曲

Kam Grand Choirs, or Gal Laox in the Dong language, is an indispensable part of Dong people s culture. Dong songs can be traced back more than 2,500 years and tell stories and express emotions. The Kam Grand Choir is a type of polyphonic folk singing performed without musical instruments or a conductor. The imitation of natural sounds is an integral part of Dong songs. The Kam Grand Choir was included in the World Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2009.

侗族大歌是侗族文化不可或缺的组成部分。侗族歌曲可以追溯到2500多年前，用以讲述故事和表达情感。侗族大歌由多人演唱不同的和声，不需要指挥或音乐伴奏。对自然声音的模仿是侗族歌曲的重要组成部分。2009年被联合国教科文组织列入世界非物质文化遗产名录。

title: Kam Grand Choir passing down
description:
#artisticguizhou Xiaohuang Village(小黄村) of Congjiang County(从江县) under the administration of Qiandongnan Miao and Dong Autonomous Prefecture is one of the birthplace of Kam Grand Choir. Dong ethnic group don’t have their own language, yet they have passed on their history and culture by means of kam grand choir. At Xiaohuang Village, almost everyone can sing a line or two.
Kam Grand Choir now has a history of over 2,500 years. It’s a multipart polyphonic choir. In 2009, it was included by the UNESCO as a world-class intangible cultural heritage. It is the priceless treasure not only to Dong people but also to the whole country.

title: "Living Fossil" of Chinese classical music -- Xi'an Drum Music
description:
According to analyses of historical materials, Xi'an drum music is said to probably be originated from the Tang Dynasty (618-907). It has been developed by the following dynasties. Great vitality was added to Xi'an drum music through continuous practice and developments, especially being influenced by opera music of the Ming and Qing dynasties (1364-1912), so that it was gradually formed into a complete grand folk music style. 
During the period of the An Shi Rebellion (755-763) in the Tang Dynasty, musicians escaped from the imperial palace, and that's why drum music was heard and became known by common people. The drum music from the palace was greatly different from folk music at that time; it was solemn, plentiful, complete and rhythmical.
From 1951, Chinese scholars began to collect and classify the historical materials of Xi'an drum music, in order to make people aware of the wonderful cultural heritage of China and to make protection a priority.
A great number of famous musicians were involved in the protection of Xi'an drum music, and great achievements have been made so far. High praises have been given by musicians from all over the world.
In2011, the "Xi'an Drum Music" was list as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The rich features of drum music have provided precious evidence in the study of Chinese ancient music, and will play an important part in the further development of Chinese folk music culture.

title: Xi'an Guyue 西安鼓乐 from Shaanxi, China: "Ba Wang Bian"《霸王鞭》
description:
"Ba Wang Bian"《霸王鞭》, a piece from the tradition of Xi'an Guyue (西安鼓乐).

Performed by the Dongcang Drum Music Society (东仓鼓乐社). Filmed at the Tang Paradise (大唐芙蓉园), a 165-acre theme park located near Big Wild Goose Pagoda (Da Yan Ta, 大雁塔) in Beilin District (碑林区), Xi'an, south-central Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China, c. January 2010. Tang Paradise opened in 2005.

Xi'an Guyue (西安鼓乐) is a genre of traditional ensemble music used primarily for ceremonial purposes in Xi'an (西安市), south-central Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China. It is believed to be of great antiquity and is often called a "living fossil of Chinese music" (音乐活化石). It is written down using an archaic system of musical notation and the repertoire comprises about 3,000 pieces. The tradition was nearly lost during the Cultural Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s.

In 2006 Xi'an Guyue was included in the First Batch of China's National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage List (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

In 2007 Xi'an guyue, centering on the Shaanxi Institute of Arts (陕西省艺术研究所) in Xi'an, was further included as the first of 145 traditions in the First Batch of the Shaanxi Province Intangible Cultural Heritage List (陕西省第一批非物质文化遗产名录). In 2009 it was inscribed by UNESCO on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

More information about this ensemble and performance:
有幸在大唐芙蓉园观看到东仓鼓乐的演出。 西安鼓乐是隋唐皇室宫廷音乐的延伸，被称之为“音乐活化石”，是世界非物质文化遗产。一千多年来因种种原因，皇家宫廷音乐被流传于长安城内外，由无数的艺人口授心传保留至今，成为一千多年来罕见的承传文化工程。东仓鼓乐社是西安鼓乐著名的乐社之一，后成为数家鼓乐社中的佼佼者，并培养出梁振源、程天向、赵庚辰等鼓乐大师。“文革”后，赵庚辰先生的大弟子范炳南协助赵庚辰等人全力以赴抢救东仓鼓乐社，并促使鼓乐走出仓门，将神秘的宫廷皇室音乐让中外旅客亲耳聆听。

title: Xi'an Guyue 西安鼓乐 from Shaanxi, northwest China: "Ba Wang Bian"《霸王鞭》
description:
A performance "Ba Wang Bian"《霸王鞭》, a piece from the tradition of Xi'an Guyue (西安鼓乐).

Performed by the Cleveland Chinese Music Ensemble of Cleveland, Ohio, United States, with guest musician Jarrelle Barton (guzheng), who is based in Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States.

This performance was part of the ensemble's "Spring Comes to the Snowy Land":  A concert for the Chinese New Year 新年音樂會《雪地春来》, held at the Hudson Library & Historical Society in Hudson, Ohio, United States on Sunday, February 3, 2019 (their eighth annual concert at the library). 

Recorded by Mike Hovancsek.

Personnel:
● Jay Xiao - dizi (笛子, transverse bamboo flute with buzzing membrane)
● David Badagnani - sheng (笙, mouth organ)
● Yan Ping Ye - pipa (琵琶, pear-shaped lute)
● Jarrelle Barton - guzheng (古筝, 21-string bridge zither, guest artist)
● Rob Hassing - percussion
● Courtney Lambert - percussion

Photos by James Knight (photos 1 and 2) and Gerry Simon (photos 3 and 4).

Xi'an Guyue (西安鼓乐) is a genre of traditional ensemble music used primarily for ceremonial purposes in Xi'an (西安市), south-central Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China. It is believed to be of great antiquity and is often called a "living fossil of Chinese music" (音乐活化石). It is written down using an archaic system of musical notation and the repertoire comprises about 3,000 pieces. The tradition was nearly lost during the Cultural Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s.

In 2006 Xi'an Guyue was included in the First Batch of China's National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage List (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

Additionally, in 2007 the tradition of Xi'an guyue, centering on the Shaanxi Institute of Arts (陕西省艺术研究所) in Xi'an, was included as the first of 145 traditions in the First Batch of the Shaanxi Province Intangible Cultural Heritage List (陕西省第一批非物质文化遗产名录).

title: Xi'an guyue 西安鼓乐 music from Xi'an 西安市, Shaanxi, northwest China
description:
A performance of Xi'an guyue (西安鼓乐). The title of the piece being performed in this video is unknown.

Performed by the Dongcang Drum Music Society (东仓鼓乐社). Probably filmed at the Tang Paradise (大唐芙蓉园), a 165-acre theme park located near Big Wild Goose Pagoda (Da Yan Ta, 大雁塔) in Beilin District (碑林区), Xi'an (西安市), Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China, c. November 26, 2015.

Xi'an guyue (西安鼓乐) is a genre of traditional ensemble music used primarily for ceremonial purposes in Xi'an, Shaanxi province, northwest China. It is believed to be of great antiquity and is often called a "living fossil of Chinese music" (音乐活化石). It is written down using an archaic system of musical notation and the repertoire comprises about 3,000 pieces. The tradition was nearly lost during the Cultural Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s.

In 2006 Xi'an guyue was included in the First Batch of China's National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage List (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

Additionally, in 2007 the tradition of Xi'an guyue, centering on the Shaanxi Institute of Arts (陕西省艺术研究所) in Xi'an, was included as the first of 145 traditions in the First Batch of the Shaanxi Province Intangible Cultural Heritage List (陕西省第一批非物质文化遗产名录).

In 2009 Xi'an guyue was inscribed by UNESCO on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

东仓鼓乐社演出精彩片段

More information about this ensemble:
有幸在大唐芙蓉园观看到东仓鼓乐的演出。 西安鼓乐是隋唐皇室宫廷音乐的延伸，被称之为“音乐活化石”，是世界非物质文化遗产。一千多年来因种种原因，皇家宫廷音乐被流传于长安城内外，由无数的艺人口授心传保留至今，成为一千多年来罕见的承传文化工程。东仓鼓乐社是西安鼓乐著名的乐社之一，后成为数家鼓乐社中的佼佼者，并培养出梁振源、程天向、赵庚辰等鼓乐大师。“文革”后，赵庚辰先生的大弟子范炳南协助赵庚辰等人全力以赴抢救东仓鼓乐社，并促使鼓乐走出仓门，将神秘的宫廷皇室音乐让中外旅客亲耳聆听。

title: Sultans Of Swing (Dire Straits); drum cover by Sina
description:
For this drum cover I played along to a drumless track of the original studio version but I like the energetic Alchemy Live version better. So this is my tribute to Terry Williams, the great Dire Straits drummer throughout the 80s, I'm a fan! However, I did not cover his Live drum part note for note but I played in the style of Terry Williams.

Edit 2021, Oct. 28th
This video launched 5 years ago today and it seems (I think..?) with 51,066,299 views, it's the most-watched drum cover on YouTube. At least until now... Thanks to everyone who watched this!!

title: Xi'an guyue 西安鼓乐 music from Xi'an 西安市, Shaanxi, northwest China
description:
A rare field recording of Xi'an guyue (西安鼓乐), a genre of traditional ensemble music used primarily for ceremonial purposes in Xi'an, south-central Shaanxi province, northwest China. It is believed to be of great antiquity and is often called a "living fossil of Chinese music" (音乐活化石). It is written down using an archaic system of musical notation and the repertoire comprises about 3,000 pieces. The tradition was nearly lost during the Cultural Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s.

The performing group is called the Zhouzhi Nan Ji Xian Xi Cun Guyueshe (周至南集贤西村鼓乐社), also known as the Nan Ji Xian Xi Cun Yueshe (南集贤西村乐社), of Xi Village (西村), Jixian Town (集贤镇), Zhouzhi County (周至县), Xi'an (西安市), south-central Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China, whose musical scores, notated in gongche notation (gongche pu, 工尺谱), have been handed down over 46 generations. The piece they are playing is entitled "Jiangjun Ling"《将军令》(The General's Command).

Filmed in Xi Village (西村), Jixian Town (集贤镇), Zhouzhi County (周至县), Xi'an (西安市), Shaanxi province (陕西省), northwest China, 1991 (although the video caption gives the erroneous date of 1999). The exact location and date of filming, as well as the name of the videographer, is, unfortunately, unknown. Present during the filming is the Chinese ethnomusicologist Li Shigen (李石根, 1919-2010), whose pioneering achievements in the field of the protection, inheritance, and research of Xi'an guyue over the period of five decades have received widespread attention.

Jixian Town's ensemble tradition is said to have originated in the Sui and Tang dynasties.

Zhouzhi County is the most spacious but least densely populated county-level division of Xi'an, and also contains the city's southernmost and westernmost points. Two famous temples are located there: Louguantai Daoist temple (楼观台), where the Chinese philosopher Laozi is said to have composed his "Dao De Jing"; and Xianyou Buddhist temple (仙游寺).

In 2006 Xi'an guyue was included in the First Batch of China's National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage List (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

Additionally, in 2007 the tradition of Xi'an guyue, centering on the Shaanxi Institute of Arts (陕西省艺术研究所) in Xi'an, was included as the first of 145 traditions in the First Batch of the Shaanxi Province Intangible Cultural Heritage List (陕西省第一批非物质文化遗产名录).

In 2009 Xi'an guyue was inscribed by UNESCO on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

On January 9, 2019, by virtue of its rich heritage of Xi'an Guyue, Jixian Town was named a "Chinese Folk Culture Art Village" (中国民间文化艺术之乡) by China's Ministry of Culture and Tourism; as of 2018, 964 areas held this designation.

title: How SILK Is Made From MILLIONS of SILKWORMS? Amazing Cocoon Harvesting Process!
description:
Discover How SILK Is Made From MILLIONS of SILKWORMS🐛| Cocoon Harvesting

Ever since human being invented clothes, a variety of fibres  have been discovered or invented to suit changes in time climate and fashion. Silk is one out of many fabrics that have been invoked over millennia despite drastic changes in our taste. Since it was discovered fine strong lustrous silk has been wooing the world.  

The cultivation of silk and the craft of silk weaving are even more fascinating we thought it would be interesting to get an insight into the story of a silk thread, let's begin with the backbone of sericulture the silkworm. Commercial silk is obtained by cultivating different species of silkworm the most widely and most commercially used species is Bombyx Mori native to Asia. Silkworm are soft bodied and slow-moving and relatively fast growing insects like other insects silkworm to go through four stages of development egg, Larva, pupa and adult. The larva is a caterpillar, the pupa is what the silkworm changes into after spinning its cocoon before emerging as a month.

title: How Mountains Of Worm Cocoons Are Turned Into Expensive Silk In Vietnam | Big Business
description:
Silk craft villages have dotted Vietnam for hundreds of years. They make silk by hand in a tedious 30-step process. Craftspeople feed the silkworms every four hours so they spin brilliantly yellow cocoons. And turning those cocoons into silk is even more delicate work. 

In the 2010s, as cheaper, fake silk flooded the market and young textile workers moved to the big cities, this local craft started dying out. But one village turned things around, banding together into one cooperative called HanhSilk.

We head to northern Vietnam to learn how Nam Cao village saved its big business of silk.

title: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00197
Description: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China, based in Zhejiang and Jiangsu Provinces near Shanghai and Chengdu in Sichuan Province, have an ancient history. Traditionally an important role for women in the economy of rural regions, silk-making encompasses planting mulberry, raising silkworms, unreeling silk, making thread, and designing and weaving fabric. It has been handed down within families and through apprenticeship, with techniques often spreading within local groups. The life cycle of the silkworm was seen as representing the life, death and rebirth of human beings. In the ponds that dot the villages, silkworm waste is fed to fishes, while mud from the ponds fertilizes the mulberry trees, and the leaves in turn feed the silkworms. Near the beginning of the lunar year, silkworm farmers invite artisans into their homes to perform the story of the Goddess of the Silkworm, to ward off evil and ensure a bountiful harvest. Every April, female silkworm farmers adorn themselves with colourful flowers made of silk or paper and make harvest offerings as part of the Silkworm Flower festival. Silk touches the lives of rural Chinese in more material ways, too, in the form of the silk clothes, quilts, umbrellas, fans and flowers that punctuate everyday life.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 by China National Silk Museum

title: A Venetian spin 🌀on ancient Chinese knowledge
description:
Sericulture, the cultivation of silkworms to produce silk, was a closely guarded Chinese secret for thousands of years. From the 6th century, silkworms - and their main food source - the white mulberry tree were moved along the Silk Roads to Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire. 
 
By the 12th century Italian cities like Venice, Lucca, and Florence developed their own thriving silk industries. Today CREE-AA, Europe's largest facility for silkworm research, is exploring new uses for silk, from cosmetics to pharmaceuticals.

title: 30,000 Silkworms! Short Documentary of Rural Sericulture in Zhenze
description:
Keywords: Zhenze, silkworm, sericulture, cottage industry

From egg incubation on April 28, to silk reeling on June 2nd, this is a short documentary of the full sericulture process in a certain village in the town of Zhenze. Silkworm breeders could also purchase leftover silkworm pupae from the reelers...yes purchase, w/ money, I've always thought they get to take home the pupae for free.
从4月28孵卵，到6月2缫丝，这是今年震泽某村子里养蚕的全过程。随后，可以把蚕蛹从缫丝作坊手里收购回来，但这需要给钱，此前以为蚕蛹是蚕农们自给自足的呢。


When my buddy Ting took us to his place in Zhenze to see waxberry plantation and sericulture last year, we were stunned! Not just me, but also a knowledgeable elder who knows his way w/ farming (grew up hoeing lands, then worked his way into senior management at ABC), who exclaimed his excitement and said he would take his granddaughter here. I for one never had the chance to grow silkworms like many comrades, cuz I left my hometown China Hong Kong when I was young. The experience was eye-opening and impressive, and right there and then I thought I must film this next year. Next year has arrived.
去年挺哥带着我们到震泽看他家里的桑蚕，大开眼界，超级震撼。我十岁离开了老家中国香港，后来在新加坡、美国生活读书，一直没有过养蚕的经历，这种规模的养殖更不用提，完全无法想象。其中一位同行的长者，以前从亲自耕地做到农行高管，算是在农务方面比较博学多闻的了，他也说没见识过，以后还要带孙女来看看呢，所以当时就萌生了拍片的念头。等了一年，终于等到机会了。

Spoke w/ another Zhenze homie about sericulture sometime ago. He said no one does this in his village any more (and villagers don't treat guests to "smoked bean tea" no more). Apparently, the Zhenze-based public company Taihu Snow Silk has a museum that showcases all aspects of sericulture year-round. Does that mean technology has circumvented natural, seasonal limitations? Dunno how long the sericulture cottage industry will last, so I am filming this process as a record.之前跟另一位震泽的朋友聊起蚕桑，他说小时候村里也有，但现在没人干了（也很少以熏豆茶招待客人了）。被誉为北交所“新国货丝绸第一股”的太湖雪就来自震泽，据说其太湖雪桑蚕博物馆常设植桑、养蚕、丝织各步骤的展览，不知道是否意味着工业化、科技化养蚕已经突破传统季节性的约束，也不知道朋友家里这种“不时不丝”的家庭蚕农模式还会流传多久，故而特此拍片记录，留存乡村民间记忆。

title: The Secret Origin of Ancient Chinese Silk: How Hidden Army of Women Controlled a Global Monopoly.
description:
Silk was more than just a fabric; it was a state-guarded biological technology that fueled the longest monopoly in human history. For 3,000 years, the secret to engineering the Bombyx mori. A flightless, blind "biological machine" and extracting its 1,000-meter continuous thread was held by a decentralized network of women in rural Chinese villages. While the West viewed silk as a luxury, the Chinese Empire treated it as a strategic asset, protecting the "software" of its production with a draconian legal code and female-only workshops. In this episode of SilkRoadDiaries, we perform a forensic autopsy on the chemistry of mordant dyeing, the physics of silk-lined ballistic armor, and the 552 AD industrial heist that proved the hardware of an invention is worthless without the generational knowledge required to run it.

title: The Sericulture and Silk Craftsmanship of China
description:
Spanning at least 5,000 years, 
        silk-weaving craftsmanship has woven out delicate fabrics as well as splendid and profound Chinese culture, with warps and wefts.
        Sericulture originated in China.
        Legend has it that over 5,000 years ago,
        Empress Leizu, wife of the ancient Yellow Emperor,
        managed to domesticate silkworms 
        and produce silk thread from the filaments.
        The knowledge of sericulture then became widespread.
        As a cultural symbol of China, silk craftsmanship
        has exerted significant influence on human civilization.
        Silk-making encompasses various processes, including mulberry planting, silkworm raising, silk unreeling, dyeing and weaving.
        Silk-making has been handed down and developed for thousands of years, 
        with various techniques spreading within local groups, 
        each demonstrating unique characteristics.
        The Yuhang Rinsing Silk Wadding in east China’s Zhejiang Province managed to develop white and pure silk; 
        The Hangluo silk is soft, smooth and tenacious；
        The Shuanglin silk damask is as thin as a cicada's wings and as light as the mist,
        whereas silk from north China’s Shanxi Province is bright and colorful, a tribute to the imperial court.
        All local styles constitute the unique silk culture of the Chinese nation.
        The skills of silk-making eventually spread beyond China. 
        Goods and thoughts from different civilizations
        travelled the ancient Silk Road, 
        which connected China and the Far East with the Middle East and Europe.
        Fine silk sketches the splendid image that is exclusive to the Chinese nation.

title: Silkworm making Cocoon | Bombyx Mori | Sericulture
description:
The cocoon construction by a silkworm involves several stages, typically over two to three days:

1.Spinning Silk : Once the silkworm reaches the mature larval stage, it secretes a fluid silk protein from its silk glands. This fluid hardens when exposed to air, forming silk threads.

2. Forming the Cocoon : The silkworm attaches these silk threads to a surface and begins spinning a continuous thread around itself. It uses figure-eight movements of its head to form the cocoon, which is made of a single, long, unbroken silk fiber.

3. Complete Cocoon : After about two to three days, the silkworm completes the construction, fully enclosing itself inside the cocoon. This cocoon serves as a protective covering as the silkworm undergoes metamorphosis, transforming into a moth. 

Once the process is complete, the cocoon provides a source of raw silk, which is harvested in silk production.

title: How China Turned Silkworms into Worth of Gold #shorts #silk #craft
description:
Silkworm cocoons are the magical starting point of silk production, crafted naturally by silkworms as they prepare to transform into moths. These delicate, silky capsules have been treasured for centuries for their smooth texture and fine fibers.

🇨🇳 China – Sericulture Farms
The best place to find silkworm cocoons is on traditional sericulture farms in China, especially in provinces like Zhejiang, Jiangsu, and Sichuan, where raising silkworms is a centuries-old craft.

🌿 Mulberry Fields
Silkworms feed exclusively on mulberry leaves, so farms growing mulberries often harvest the cocoons once the worms complete spinning.

🛍️ Markets & Craft Stores
Silkworm cocoons can also be found in local markets, craft stores, and online specialty shops, often sold for silk-making, beauty treatments, or educational purposes.

These tiny natural wonders are not only the source of luxurious silk but also a fascinating glimpse into ancient craftsmanship and biology.

title: Traditional Silk Reeling Process: From Cocoon to Raw Silk Thread by Hand
description:
Watch this incredible video showcasing the traditional process of making raw silk thread from silkworm cocoons. This ancient craft involves several precise steps done entirely by hand.

The process begins with cooking the cocoons in hot water to soften the sericin—the natural gum that binds the silk filament. The loose filament ends are then collected and skillfully reeled onto a Charkha reeling machine to form a continuous thread. The reeler carefully adds more cocoons to ensure the silk thread maintains a consistent denier (thickness).

Finally, the raw silk is boiled again with an alkali (soda) in a process called degumming to completely remove the sericin, leaving the fiber soft, lustrous, and ready for weaving. This is a beautiful glimpse into the authentic handicraft and sericulture traditions of silk production.

title: Zhuang Lifen, Award Winning Nanyin Artist of Nanyin Heritage Center
description:
Zhuang Lifen｜庄丽芬｜莊麗芬
Nanyin is a traditional genre of music that’s central to the culture of people from Minnan on China’s south-east coast. This “living fossil” is one of the four oldest forms of Chinese music that’s still preserved in its original state. 

The slow, simple and elegant melodies are performed on a mixture of traditional and more commonplace instruments. These include a bamboo flute called a dongxiao, and a pipa – a crook-necked lute played horizontally – as well as various wind, string and percussion instruments.

Nanyin’s rich repertoire of songs and scores is a repository of the ancient
folk music and poems of the Minnan region, where it has influenced opera,
puppet theatre and other performing art traditions. 

Nanyin is deeply rooted in the social life of the Minnan region. It’s performed during ceremonies in spring and autumn to honor Meng Chang, the god of music, as well as at weddings and funerals. At these and other celebrations, it’s played in courtyards, markets and the streets. For people in China and across Southeast Asia whose families hail from Minnan, Nanyin is the sound of home.

title: Nanyin
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00199
Description: Nanyin is a musical performing art central to the culture of the people of Minnan in southern Fujian Province along Chinas south-eastern coast, and to Minnan populations overseas. The slow, simple and elegant melodies are performed on distinctive instruments such as a bamboo flute called the ''dongxiao'' and a crooked-neck lute played horizontally called the ''pipa,'' as well as more common wind, string and percussion instruments. Of nanyins three components, the first is purely instrumental, the second includes voice, and the third consists of ballads accompanied by the ensemble and sung in Quanzhou dialect, either by a sole singer who also plays clappers or by a group of four who perform in turn. The rich repertoire of songs and scores preserves ancient folk music and poems and has influenced opera, puppet theatre and other performing art traditions. Nanyin is deeply rooted in the social life of the Minnan region. It is performed during spring and autumn ceremonies to worship Meng Chang, the god of music, at weddings and funerals, and during joyful festivities in courtyards, markets and the streets. It is the sound of the motherland for Minnan people in China and throughout South-East Asia.

description:
While succession planning remains an uphill challenge in the traditional Chinese music scene, Siong Leng Musical Association stands out as one of the few organisations that is predominantly managed by a younger generation of practitioners. Despite the uncertain future of traditional Chinese music in Singapore, the younger members of Siong Leng valiantly continue to find ways of sharing their passion for Nanyin with their peers.

The Forefathers Project was initiated by TENG in 2016 to uncover and explore the music of Singapore’s early Chinese migrants. Over the course of four years, TENG interviewed, documented and learnt from a community of first-generation masters, luthiers, instrument dealers and proteges from local Hokkien, Cantonese, Teochew and Hakka traditions to shed light on the lesser-known elements of Singapore’s Chinese music heritage.

The TENG Ensemble is a critically acclaimed Singaporean Chinese fusion music group that bridges between cultures East and West, boasting musical influences from both the traditional and the contemporary. 

As a collective of music educators, scholars and award-winning instrumentalists, the Ensemble’s performances have been a staple in Singapore’s arts scene since 2009. At the 2015 National Day Rally, The TENG Ensemble was described by Prime Minister Lee Hsien Loong as ‘creating something uniquely Singapore’.  

The group has performed at events such as the ASEAN Tourism Forum, TEDxPetaling Street, Youth Olympic Games, Shanghai World Expo, Singapore Day in Melbourne, George Town Festival and Singapore International Jazz Festival.  

The Ensemble's first music album titled Eight (Ba) was released in 2013. This was followed by Stories From an Island City in 2016 and Heirlooms in 2019 - both of which were awarded 4.5 out of 5 stars by The Straits Times and highly regarded by several other media outlets, while Stories From an Island City was ranked number 1 on Hong Kong’s iTunes World Music chart.

title: Siong Leng Musical Association Revives Nanyin: the ‘Living Fossil’ of Chinese Music
description:
Nanyin, which literally translates as ‘Music of the South’, has been around for over 2,000 years. With origins from the Han Dynasty (206 B.C. – 220 A.D.), nanyin is a form of liyue (ritual and music) that was traditionally performed only in the royal court. 

According to the Classic of Rites and Music, ritual and music forms like nanyin were practiced by ancient Chinese rulers to improve themselves spiritually. “Through ritual, human nature is preserved, through music, harmony is upheld.”

Later, civil turbulence forced court musicians to migrate south, where they spread nanyin to the common folk. Nanyin became an inseparable part of folk culture, particularly in Fujian’s Quanzhou region. 

Nanyin, which is also called xianguan or nanguan, is written in the ancient Chinese music notation system gongchepu - one of the world’s most well-preserved notation systems. This ancient music genre was listed on the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2009.

“It is regarded as the ‘living fossil’ of ancient Chinese music,” says Lim Ming Yi, an administrative manager and principal musician of Siong Leng Musical Association.

title: Quanzhou Nanyin 郑明明
description:
Zheng Mingming｜心水南音 郑明明｜心水南音 鄭明明
Nanyin is a traditional genre of music that’s central to the culture of people from Minnan on China’s south-east coast. This “living fossil” is one of the four oldest forms of Chinese music that’s still preserved in its original state. 

The slow, simple and elegant melodies are performed on a mixture of traditional and more commonplace instruments. These include a bamboo flute called a dongxiao, and a pipa – a crook-necked lute played horizontally – as well as various wind, string and percussion instruments.

Nanyin’s rich repertoire of songs and scores is a repository of the ancient
folk music and poems of the Minnan region, where it has influenced opera,
puppet theatre and other performing art traditions. 

Nanyin is deeply rooted in the social life of the Minnan region. It’s performed during ceremonies in spring and autumn to honor Meng Chang, the god of music, as well as at weddings and funerals. At these and other celebrations, it’s played in courtyards, markets and the streets. For people in China and across Southeast Asia whose families hail from Minnan, Nanyin is the sound of home.

Watch the Nanyin episode here: https://youtu.be/yGD-xNLMRW8

description:
Nanyin (南音) musicians from Siong Leng Musical Association facilitated a workshop for students from NAFA's School of Music in this academic collaboration for workshops and sectional practices for the 'Music in Context' and 'Professional Studies (Ensemble)' modules.

A traditional musical performance genre of the Minnan community in Southern China, Nanyin was inscribed in 2009 on UNESCO’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. It is an important cultural heritage for descendants of Southern Chinese immigrants to Nanyang (South Seas) and the Southeast Asia region.

The study and research of Southeast Asian arts has positively influenced the multidisciplinary nature of art studies here at NAFA, accumulating a growing resource of ideas and innovations that include outcomes from 3D Design, Design & Media, Fine Art, Arts Management, Fashion, Theatre, Dance, and Music. ISEAA adopts the role as catalyst and motivator to bring the best of ideas and innovations articulating NAFA’s SEA dimension to a world stage. Apart from the annual SEA Arts Forum held at NAFA, ISEAA gathers exciting new opportunities, avenues and platforms for NAFA’s faculty, students and network affiliates to showcase their research and output featuring SEA content unique to our artistic spectrum in visual and performing arts. 

Our Art Galleries strive to cultivate a high level of aesthetic standards in exhibition presentation and take the lead in materialising important artistic projects with both conventional and new media. title: The craftsmanship of Nanjing Yunjin brocade
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00200
Description: In the Chinese tradition of weaving Nanjing Yunjin brocade, two craftspeople operate the upper and lower parts of a large, complicated loom to produce textiles incorporating fine materials such as silk, gold and peacock feather yarn. The technique was once used to produce royal garments such as the dragon robe and crown costume; today, it is still used to make high-end attire and souvenirs. Preserved primarily in Jiangsu province in eastern China, the method comprises more than a hundred procedures, including manufacturing looms, drafting patterns, the creation of jacquard cards for programming weaving patterns, dressing the loom and the many stages of weaving itself. As they pass the warp and split the weft, the weavers sing mnemonic ballads that remind them of the techniques they employ and enhance the cooperative, artistic atmosphere at the loom. The workers view their craft as part of a historical mission since, in addition to creating fabrics for contemporary use, yunjin is used to replicate ancient silk fabrics for researchers and museums. Named for the cloud-like splendour of the fabrics, yunjin remains popular throughout the country.
Country(ies): China

title: The Cream of Nanjing Yunjin Brocade
description:
In Chinese, the character for brocade, ‘锦’,  consists of two parts: ‘金’ which means gold, and ‘帛’ which means silk. Therefore brocade is intricately tied to the most luxurious and valuable fabrics in ancient China. Nanjing brocade is famous for its cloud-like patterns and picturesque landscapes, reflecting the profound culture of this ancient Chinese city. 

In 2006, Nanjing Yunjin Brocade was added to the China National Intangible Cultural Heritage list, demonstrating its importance in Chinese culture. In ‘The Cream of Nanjing Yunjin Brocade’, fine examples of this beautiful craft are revealed. 

Produced by the Beijing Wangtu Films & TV Production Consulting Co., Ltd. Production supervised by the Center of International Cultural Exchange. Presented by the Bureau for External Cultural Relations, Ministry of Culture and Tourism of China.

title: Nanjing Yunjin Brocade | China’s Intangible Cultural Heritage 🧵✨ #ChineseTextile #Yunjin #shorts
description:
Step into the world of Nanjing Yunjin (Cloud Brocade), one of the most treasured forms of traditional Chinese silk weaving. Recognized as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, Yunjin has a history spanning more than 1,500 years.

The name “Yunjin” literally means “cloud brocade”, reflecting its shimmering, colorful patterns that resemble floating clouds. Woven with silk and gold threads, Yunjin was once exclusively crafted for imperial robes, court garments, and ceremonial attire, representing the highest level of textile art in ancient China.

Every piece of Yunjin is woven on a massive traditional loom, often requiring two artisans working together: one on top lifting the warp threads, and another below controlling the shuttle. The process is so intricate that only a few centimeters of fabric can be produced in a whole day.

Today, Nanjing Yunjin is admired not only as fabric but also as cultural heritage and artistic expression. Its patterns carry auspicious meanings — from dragons and phoenixes symbolizing power and harmony, to floral motifs representing prosperity and elegance.

✨ Owning Yunjin is like holding a piece of history, a bridge between past and present.

title: Yunjin Brocade Master 1 (720p)
description:
Yunjin Brocade Master features Master Zhou Shuangxi, the "Heir to Yunjin Brocade". Master Zhou explains how the ancient art form has been preserved and passed down from generation to generation. Yunjin Brocade was once only fit for a Chinese emperor, weaved with fine silks and threads made from peacock feathers and real gold. Today, Yunjin Brocade is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage List and continues to be preserved, cherished, and modernized by the Nanjing Brocade Research Institute in Nanjing, China.

The attention to detail and time consuming processes to make a Yunjin Brocade garment is a collaborative effort. The reproduction of ancient designs and the creation of new ones are all done by hand, from the making of the silk, gold and peacock threads to the knotting and weaving process. The weaver's hands are the last to touch the creation. One slip up will ruin a garment worth thousands of dollars. How does Master Zhou and other Yunjin Brocade weavers do it? Watch and see!

title: OMG | China’s intangible treasure: Nanjing Yunjin brocade
description: Nanjing Yunjin is an exquisite kind of silk brocade from Nanjing, China. “Yunjin”, meaning “beautiful as clouds at sunset”, is a more than thousand-year-old craft incorporating fine materials such as silk, gold and peacock feather yarn. In ancient times, Yunjin brocade was only produced for Chinese emperors. Today, Yunjin brocade is on UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage List and continues to be preserved in Nanjing, China. title: The old loom clicks, and every inch of brocade is worth its weight in gold.
description:
Purely Handmade Intangible Cultural Heritage Yunjin:
​​① The loom is like a piano, the warp and weft like strings.
​​The master craftsman pushes a wooden rod with one hand to control the upper and lower warp threads, while using the other to toss the shuttle. The alternating warp and weft threads are like playing a piano, creating a pattern one string at a time. As the old saying goes: "The warp is as straight as a piano string, and the weft is as dense and airtight!"
​​② Color matching relies on mental calculation, with each color assigned a unique shuttle.
​​Yunjin is woven with silk and gold thread, with a separate shuttle for each color. The motto is: "The gold shuttle weaves clouds, the silver shuttle weaves waves, and the colorful shuttle weaves a hundred flowers in bloom!"
​​③ The patterns are "prescribed," the designs are hidden within the artist's mind. Complex designs are not sketched; they are memorized by the craftsman. Weaving requires two people: one weaving the pattern on top, the other weaving below. The master craftsman says: "Dragon scales and phoenix tails depend on feel; one wrong weave and the whole thing is a mess!"
​

title: Timeless Mastery: Discovering Nanjing Yunjin Brocade at the Museum – UNESCO Heritage
description:
Location: Nanjing Yunjin Brocade Museum, China
Handmade Nanjing Yunjin is one of the most precious brocades in the world – the pinnacle of ancient Chinese weaving, still thriving today.
Unique techniques like "make-up and weave as one," full-length warps with spliced wefts, swivel coiled weaving, and flower-by-flower color variation are exclusive to China and rare globally.
He Guanyi, Cultural Promotion Manager at Nanjing Yunjin Brocade Museum:
"This is a centuries-old precision loom, still in active use today.
It's 4 meters high, 5.6 meters long, 1.4 meters wide, and assembled from 1,924 parts.
One loom requires two skilled masters working in perfect sync.
Look up – there's another master on top! They coordinate flawlessly to operate it.
The upper master interprets the pattern book, sending coded signals to the warps.
The lower master weaves the threads in, row by row.
Eight hours of work produces just 5-6 centimeters – that's why we say: 'An inch of brocade is worth an inch of gold.'
From traditional auspicious patterns to today's exquisite artistic designs, Yunjin continues to evolve and inspire."
Discover the breathtaking beauty of this UNESCO-listed intangible cultural heritage!

title: Amazing Chinese Art- Chinese Yunjin(Cloud Brocade) in Nanjing
description:
Yunjin, also called cloud brocade, is a traditional Chinese silk brocade made in Jiangsu since the end of the Song dynasty, and based on weft-weaving techniques from both the Song and Tang dynasties. To see more history about yunjin, please check this link: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yunjin

In this video, you would see the wood machine that produce Yunjin. And it is a slow work but produce precious artworks, as they just can weave out 1 issun (3.33333..cm) each day. And it has to be two people working together at the same time on the 4-meter high machine. Yunjin was used to make imperial robe for kings in Chinese history. And now, this artwork is still playing its important role, which is used to make decoration on clothes, on pictures, on bags and so on. 
It is also a good choice to enshrine them, as there is not many people making it in this world. In China, there are just about 100 people knowing how to weave Yunjin, but lots of them are not living for it. And with the workers getting older, and when they are old enough, they won't be able to work anymore, as it is necessary that the workers should have good eyesight for the work.

If you want to know more of Yunjin, or you have the intention to enshrine some, please feel free to contact me.

Our website is still under construction, and later we will upload some Yunjin pictures (and other kind of Chinese artworks) on it. So if you are interested, please follow us and we will let you know when our website is ready.

title: The traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00201
Description: The unique water quality and mild climate of Jing County in Anhui Province in eastern China are two of the key ingredients in the craft of making Xuan paper that thrives there. Handmade from the tough bark of the Tara Wing-Celtis or Blue Sandalwood tree and rice straw, Xuan paper is known for its strong, smooth surface, its ability to absorb water and moisten ink, and fold repeatedly without breaking. It has been widely used in calligraphy, painting and book printing. The traditional process passed down orally over generations and still followed today proceeds strictly by hand through more than a hundred steps such as steeping, washing, fermenting, bleaching, pulping, sunning and cutting  all of which lasts more than two years. The production of the Paper of Ages or King of Papers is a major part of the economy in Jing County, where the industry directly or indirectly employs one in nine locals and the craft is taught in local schools. True mastery of the entire complicated process is won only by a lifetime of dedicated work. Xuan paper has become synonymous with the region, where a score of artisans still keep the craft alive.
Country(ies): China

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Living Heritage: Xuan Paper
description:
Smooth yet tensile. Versatile and durable. With these qualities, Xuan paper is known as the "Paper of Ages".

The term Xuan paper first appeared in On Famous Paintings through the Ages, a book written by Tang Dynasty (618-907) scholar Zhang Yanyuan, in which he described Xuan paper as an ideal carrier for calligraphy and painting. Handmade Xuan paper from Jingxian county, East China's Anhui province, was listed as a tribute to the imperial court for its supreme quality during the period.

The traditional #craft of making Xuan paper is extremely demanding. Sandalwood bark, a plant native to southern China, goes through 108 procedures together with rice straw over the course of three years before it can transform into a batch of fine Xuan paper. The entire procedure is so complex that even the most skilled craftsman can only master limited number of steps.

The flow of ink, both guided and resisted by water, determines the output of Chinese art, and Xuan paper outshines others with its excellent ability to give full play to ink. Different proportions of bark to straw during the papermaking process can create different canvases best suited for artistic expression in freehand ink paintings or calligraphy. 

Xuan paper has accompanied the passionate brushstrokes of the Chinese literati for thousands of years. Unlike other forms of paper, it is very resistant to damage brought by time. It is this durability that has made the preservations of many valuable works from ancient China possible.

Papermaking is a crystallization of wisdom of the ancient Chinese, and Xuan paper is the zenith of papermaking. #LivingHeritage #XuanPaper

title: Chinese Rice Paper Explained | Sheng & Shu Xuan Paper
description:
We no longer stock this product, however we will leave this video up as it is a valuable resource for anyone getting into the artform!

Xuan Paper otherwise known as Rice Paper or Shuen Paper, is renowned for being soft and finely textured appearing as a thin, semi-opaque surface. Its delicate nature (40gsm approx.) renders it wholly suitable for both Chinese calligraphy, Chinese brush painting and printmaking. The characteristic of the paper and how pigment reacts to it is largely down to its absorbency - unsized (Raw) paper is porous and absorbent whilst lightly sized paper (Ripe) is less absorbent and more forgiving.

This is also both parts 1 and 2 of our Sheng and Shu paper Shorts videos.

title: How to Make Paper from Bamboo
description:
In this video, we showcase the fascinating process of making paper from bamboo, a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative to traditional paper-making. Bamboo, known for its fast growth and strength, is an excellent material for paper production. This process begins by harvesting bamboo, which is then shredded into fibers, boiled, and pulped to create the foundation for paper. The pulp is then spread onto a screen to form sheets, which are left to dry naturally. The resulting bamboo paper is strong, durable, and has a unique texture, making it ideal for writing, drawing, and even eco-conscious packaging.

The process of making bamboo paper is an example of how ancient techniques can be adapted to modern sustainability needs. Bamboo is an incredibly versatile material, and by using it to create paper, we can help reduce the environmental impact of traditional paper production. This method not only provides a unique, high-quality product but also showcases the endless possibilities of using natural, renewable resources in everyday life.

title: Rice paper omelette✨

▪️3 Eggs▪️Salt & Pepper▪️1 Scallion, sliced▪️1 Tbsp Olive Oil▪️1 Rice Paper▪️2 tsp Chili Oil▪️
.
1️⃣ Beat eggs with salt and pepper and set aside
2️⃣ Heat a pan over medium high and spread on a layer of oil; place a dry rice paper on the pan and top with scallions, salt and pepper
3️⃣ Slowly pour on the egg (if it seeps outside the rice paper perimeter push it back towards the rice paper with a spatula) and cook until egg is almost set.. drizzle with chili oil and then fold in half
4️⃣ Cut and eat like a quesadilla with more chili oil drizzled on top - enjoy!
.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Traditional Chinese Paper Trimming with a Half-Moon Knife
description:
A Little Wisdom Helps You Become Smarter !

Cutting a Stack of Xuan Paper with a Curved Hand Blade

In the video, a skilled artisan is using a traditional half-moon-shaped paper knife to trim a large stack of Xuan paper. The paper is tightly pressed under a simple wooden frame to keep it aligned and secure. With precise and steady hands, the worker pulls the sharp curved blade along the edge of the wooden guide, cutting smoothly through the entire stack in one motion. This traditional method is used in Chinese papermaking and bookbinding workshops to neatly trim the rough edges of handmade paper. The curved knife allows long, continuous cuts without damaging the delicate paper fibers. This technique ensures every sheet is uniformly sized with clean edges, ready for calligraphy, painting, or printing. The process highlights the efficiency and elegance of handmade paper finishing. Using simple tools, experienced crafters can achieve results that are difficult to replicate even with modern machines. This video offers a glimpse into a time-honored craft that values precision, patience, and practicality. Viewers can appreciate the seamless blend of tool, material, and human skill in this delicate yet decisive act of cutting.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Xuan paper production process
description:
A little tip helps you become smarter!

The production process of Xuan paper mainly includes the following aspects:
 
Raw Material Selection
The main raw materials are the bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree and Shatian straw. The bark should be taken from the phloem of Pteroceltis tatarinowii trees that have grown for 6 to 8 years. As for Shatian straw, it is preferably produced in Jingxian County and its surrounding areas in Anhui Province. The straw is required to have long, slender, uniform fibers with few impurities.
 
Soaking and Retting
Soak the bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree and the straw separately in water to make them fully absorb water and soften. The soaking times vary. Generally, the bark needs to be soaked for 1 to 2 months, and the straw for about 1 week. After that, they are retted. During retting, natural microorganisms are used to decompose and wash away the impurities in the raw materials.
 
Cooking and Bleaching
Put the soaked and retted raw materials together with alkaline substances such as lime into a steamer for cooking. The bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree is cooked for about 8 to 12 hours, and the straw for about 4 to 6 hours. After cooking, bleaching is carried out. Traditionally, sun bleaching is often used to make the raw material fibers whiter and purer.
 
Pulping and Beating
Wash the raw materials that have been cooked and bleached to remove residual alkaline substances and other impurities. Then, beat the materials to make the fibers fibrillated and separated, turning them into a pulp suitable for papermaking, laying the foundation for the subsequent paper - forming process.
 
Paper - Scooping and Forming
Put the pulp into a paper trough, add water and stir it into a uniform pulp suspension. The paper - scooping worker holds a bamboo curtain and gently scoops in the pulp suspension, making the pulp evenly adhere to the bamboo curtain to form a wet paper sheet. This step plays a crucial role in determining the thickness and evenness of the paper.
 
Pressing and Dehydration
Transfer the wet paper sheet from the bamboo curtain to a wooden board, and apply a certain pressure through a pressing device to squeeze out a large amount of water from the wet paper sheet, making the paper take an initial shape and getting it ready for the subsequent drying process.
 
Drying and Finishing
Stick the pressed paper onto a drying wall made of adobe bricks and dry it with charcoal fire or steam heating. After drying, cut the Xuan paper according to the specified requirements to remove the rough edges. Finally, check each sheet of Xuan paper one by one for any flaws. The qualified products are packaged and stored in the warehouse in a certain quantity.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: 宣纸 Xuan paper #springonshorts
description:
Xuan paper originates from Jing County, Anhui Province, named after ancient Xuanzhou. It is a precious traditional Chinese painting and calligraphy paper with a thousand-year history. It took shape in the Tang Dynasty, was widely used in painting and calligraphy in the Song and Yuan dynasties, and matured in the Ming and Qing dynasties as a treasure among the Four Treasures of the Study.
Made of wingceltis bark and sandy-field rice straw via over a hundred manual procedures, it features tough texture, even ink absorption, insect resistance and durability, famed as "millennium-lasting paper".
Favored by ancient artists, it bears countless classic artworks. Ideal for calligraphy, painting, ancient book reproduction and seal mounting, its craftsmanship is now listed as intangible cultural heritage, an indispensable gem of oriental ink culture.

title: 新编粤剧《虎符》Cantonese Opera "Tiger Tally" 2021
description:
《虎符》之《窃符救赵》是新加坡华族戏曲研究会制作由刘满钻编剧的线上观赏版粤剧。本制作集合了新加坡粤剧界资深演员出演，由刘锦煌导演，戏曲导演为欧阳炳文，卢眉桦担任艺术顾问。本制作该是新加坡粤剧演员真人表演配以影视特效的首部数码粤剧片（附中英文字幕）。此剧由龚耀祥、刘满钻和陈福鸿领衔主演，张淑莲、巢紫贞和柳德衍主演。
Tiger Tally – Saving Zhao Kingdom is a digital production by Chinese Opera Society (Singapore) for webcast. An all-Singaporean cast performed in this Cantonese Opera which employed special effects and virtual backdrops to enhance viewers’ enjoyment without compromising the traditional artistry. This opera is written by Sally Low, a Singaporean playwright, and directed by Henry Lau. Chinese opera movements are directed by Aw-yeong Peng Mun and Cultural Medallion recipient, Lou Mee Wah is the Artistic Advisor. Singaporean veteran artistes, Gary Kong, Sally Low, Philip Chan, Jeslin Cheong, Cynthia Chow and James Lau star in this opera.

故事描述战国时代秦国围困赵国都城邯郸。赵国求救于魏国而魏王则担忧触怒秦国，只让主帅驻军邺城静观其变。魏王之弟信陵君魏无忌情急之下求得魏王姬妾如姬为其窃得兵符，夺取兵权遂出兵援赵。本剧主要描述兄弟间的猜忌，君臣间的矛盾以及宫廷内的权力斗争。
Based on a historical episode in the Warring States period (475-221 B.C.) in China, the opera tells of the distrust between brothers, conflicting stances between a king and his subject, territorial encroachment, court intrigues and power struggle. Wei Wuji, Lord Xinling of Wei Kingdom, decides to save Zhao Kingdom at the plea of his sister, Lady Pingyuan of Zhao. But the King of Wei fears incurring the wrath of the powerful invaders from Qin Kingdom. Wuji needs the “tiger tally”, a military token, so as to mobilize Wei troops…

title: 帝女花之香夭 Fragrant Sacrifice 任白名曲Yam Kim Fei and Bak Suit Sin
description:
This is probably the most well-known excerpt from the most-watched Cantonese Opera in the world.  I have supplied English subtitles here, hoping that those who are not familiar with this language can enjoy it too.

I have uploaded another version before, performed by the students of these two actresses.

帝女花 is a Cantonese Opera first made into a movie in 1959, starring Opera singers, Yam Kim Fei (任剑辉) and Bak Suit Sin (白雪仙), both ladies, but Yam impersonated a man in all her movies. 

The Emperor of the Ming dynasty had just approved of his future son-in-law who won the heart of his daughter with poetry. At that moment, The Ching army attacked and took over the palace. The Emperor committed suicide with his family but Princess Cheung Ping survived. To win the favour of his subjects, the new Emperor of the Ching dynasty adopted the Princess and proceeded with the grand wedding. To show their loyalty to the Ming emperor, the newly-weds take their own lives on their wedding night. The Cantonese Opera is too beautiful to be translated. Something will be "lost in translation." The Song here, 香夭 (Fragrant Sacrifice) is the grand finale of the opera, sung on the wedding night, the couple are dressed in exquisite finery. The Princess and her groom express their love for each other and their loyalty to the former dynasty, especially the late Emperor, before they take their own lives, drinking wine poisoned with Arsenic. 

I have added English subtitles for my friends here on youtube. Hope you will like this as much as I do. Regards, Jeremy

title: Traditional Cantonese opera.傳統粵劇。👍👍👍#taio#cantoneseopera#大澳#粵劇#戲曲#神功戲#福德宮寶誕#福德宮土地誕
description:
The Tai O Fuk Tak Kung Festival Committee in Hong Kong holds the To Tei Festival from the 19th to the 21st days of the first lunar month every year. The main events include celebrations held by fa pau (paper floral shrine) associations and thanksgiving Cantonese opera performances.

Cantonese opera was included in Unesco's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

香港大澳福德宮演戲值理會於每年農曆正月十九至二十一籌辦土地誕，儀式以花炮會賀誕為主，並上演粵劇神功戲。

粵劇為聯合國《人類非物質文化遺產》。

title: Keeping Chinese Opera alive in Hong Kong: Cantonese Opera, Peking Opera and Kunqu
description: There are more than 300 genres, or performing styles, in Chinese opera, but only three are on Unesco’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity: Kunqu, Peking Opera and Cantonese Opera. To celebrate the beauty and diversity of this traditional art form, Chinese opera veterans Liza Wang Ming-chun, Law Ka-ying and Tang Yuen-ha explain what makes each style so special and why Hong Kong continues to play an important role in their conservation and development. title: 【新編粵劇《竹林愛傳奇》(載譽重演) Cantonese Opera—𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 (Re-run)】
description:
【《竹林愛傳奇》原班人馬載譽重演 Re-run of the highly acclaimed Cantonese Opera—𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦】

2023年首演的新編粵劇《竹林愛傳奇》獲多方好評，其中有評論更譽之為「近年罕見的傑作」。徇眾要求，著名編劇、金像獎及金馬獎最佳編劇獎得主杜國威（杜Sir）將與原班人馬攜手，再度把這傑作搬上舞台，於今年10月12-14日帶來三場演出。

杜Sir筆下的《竹林愛傳奇》描繪兩位竹林七賢後人向沖及嵇旦之間的恩怨情仇，帶出愛情、親情、手足情、家國情等種種情感。是次重演，杜Sir將把劇本稍作調整，讓觀眾再次為當中的細膩情感及人性刻劃而感動！

此作由粵劇大老倌新劍郎擔任導演及參演，遵從傳統行當排場。而主演的藍天佑及鄭雅琪這對金童玉女，實力毋庸置疑，他們於香港文化中心的兩場首演獲得觀眾及評論熱烈讚賞。

香港藝術節新編粵劇《竹林愛傳奇》入選「國家藝術基金2024年度傳播交流推廣資助項目」，今次在香港的演出將為大灣區巡演打響頭炮，觀眾可先睹為快。
門票將於8月13日起於城市售票網公開發售，萬勿錯過！

Following widespread acclaim from audiences and critics for the premiere of the exquisite Cantonese opera 𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 in 2023, the HKAF will be presenting a highly anticipated re-run of the production in October.

Written by esteemed local playwright Raymond To Kwok-wai, who has received a string of awards and nominations from the Hong Kong Film Awards and Golden Horse Awards, 𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 depicts the lofty ideals and abiding love of the descendants of the famous Seven Sages. The virtuoso Sun Kim-Long directs and stars in the production, while the leading roles are played by one of the most notable partnerships in the scene, Lam Tin-yau and Cheng Nga-ki, who are among the most outstanding new-generation Cantonese opera artists working today.

Cantonese Opera—𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 has been selected for the China National Arts Fund 2024 (Cultural Exchange), which will help support a tour of Greater Bay Area cities. Before the tour, the audience in Hong Kong will be the first to watch the refined version of the work.

Tickets will be available for purchase via URBTIX from 13 August 2024. Don’t miss your chance to experience this acclaimed Cantonese opera!

◈◈◈◈◈◈

2024年10月12—14﹝六至一﹞

門票將於8月13日起於城市售票網公開發售

本節目門票於2024年8月13日起在城市售票網公開發售，但第五十三屆香港藝術節優先預訂並不包括此節目。

此製作將於10月17-22日於廣州、佛山及珠海巡演。此巡演獲國家藝術基金資助，而佛山及珠海演出獲香港特別行政區政府康樂及文化事務署資助，為第四屆「粵港澳大灣區文化藝術節」節目之一。此外，香港、廣州、佛山及珠海所有演出均獲香港藝術節基金會資助。

title: Gesar: Study Topics
description:
Gesar is a folk hero of Eastern Tibet and is predominantly known through literature and live performance. He is believed to have lived around the 10th century. The stories of Gesar, epic in size, are brought to life through dramatic performances, songs, and public readings of his many adventures. This Gesar can be termed Literary Gesar. It is quite possibly the longest epic poetry in the world. Despite the popularity of all of this there is relatively little found in the way of art: paintings, murals, and sculpture. What objects are known are also dated very late - 19th and 20th centuries. Primarily derived from the writings of Ju Mipham this later version of Gesar can be termed Religious Gesar. The general depiction of Gesar is of a Tibetan warrior, atop a horse, clad in armor, and wearing a helmet, with elaborate flag pennants and streamers, accompanied by either eight or more horsemen, generally up to thirty horsemen.
