title: Gesar epic tradition
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00204
Description: The ethnic Tibetan, Mongolian and Tu communities in western and northern China share the story of the ancient hero King Gesar, sent to heaven to vanquish monsters, depose the powerful, and aid the weak while unifying disparate tribes. The singers and storytellers who preserve the Gesar epic tradition perform episodes of the vast oral narrative (known as beads on a string) in alternating passages of prose and verse with numerous regional differences. Tibetan masters carry bronze mirrors and use facial expressions, sound effects and gestures to enhance their singing, while Mongolian performers are accompanied by fiddles and intersperse improvised, melodic singing with musical storytelling and oral narrative. Epic performances, often accompanied by rituals such as offerings and meditation, are embedded in the religious and daily lives of the community. For example, when a child is born, passages about King Gesars descent into the world are sung. The hundreds of myths, folktales, ballads and proverbs handed down as part of the tradition not only serve as a form of major entertainment in rural communities but also educate listeners in history, religion, custom, morality and science. A continuing inspiration for ''thangka'' painting, Tibetan opera and other art forms, the Gesar epic imbues audiences both young and old with a sense of cultural identity and historical continuity.
Country(ies): China

title: Gesar epic tradition
description:
The Epic of King Gesar (/ˈɡɛzər, ˈɡɛs-/ Tibetan, Bhutanese: གླིང་གེ་སར ། ), also spelled Geser (especially in Mongolian contexts) or Kesar (/ˈkɛzər, ˈkɛs-/), is an epic literature of Tibet and greater Central Asia. The Tibetan epic which is only written in Tibetan was formed between around 200 BC or 300 BC and 600 AD. In the later years, some folk balladeers continued to pass on the story orally; this enriched the plots and embellished the languages. The story had gradually become near perfect and very popular in the early 12th Century. The Epic relates the heroic deeds of the culture hero Gesar, the fearless lord of the legendary kingdom of Ling (Wylie: gling). It is recorded variously in poetry and prose, through oral poetry performance,  it is sung widely throughout Central Asia and North East of South Asia. Its classic version is to be found in central Tibet. Some 100 bards of this epic (Wylie: sgrung, "tale") are still active today in the Gesar belt of China. Tibetan, Mongolian, Buryat, Balti, Ladakhi and Monguor singers maintain the oral tradition and the epic has attracted intense scholarly curiosity as one of the few oral epic traditions to survive as a performing art. Besides stories conserved by such Chinese minorities as the Bai, Naxi, the Pumi (Boemi or Tibetans) , Lisu, Yugur and Salar, versions of the epic are also recorded among the Balti of Baltistan, the Burusho people of Hunza and Gilgit and the Kalmyk and Ladakhi peoples, in Sikkim, Bhutan, Nepal, and among various Tibeto-Burmese, Turkic, and Tungus tribes. The first printed version was a Mongolian text published in Beijing in 1716. 
The epic is composed of a very large body of versions, each with many variants, and is reputed by some to be the longest in the world. Although there is no one definitive text, the Chinese compilation so far of just its Tibetan versions has filled some 120 volumes, more than one million verses,[4] divided into 29 "chapters". Western calculations speak of more than 50 different books edited so far in China, India and Tibet. 
A Tibetan scholar has written:
Like the outstanding Greek epics, Indian epics and Kalevala, King Gesar is a brilliant pearl in the world's cultural treasure and is an important contribution made by our country to human civilization.

title: La pradera heroica de Gesar - Episodio 1: La pradera heroica | Documental
description:
Una voz ancestral ha resonado entre las montañas y aguas de la meseta Qinghai-Xizang durante miles de años. Ellos son pastores en el Techo del Mundo. Por generaciones, viven siguiendo al pasto y el agua. La riqueza de sus migraciones no solo incluye rebaños de ganado, sino también una historia. Vayan donde vayan, aquella historia antigua se siembra y fluye como semillas que germinan en la tierra y olas interminables sobre el río. El rey Gesar es el nombre de la historia. La extensa epopeya bajo el título El rey Gesar no solo es un retrato histórico de los tibetanos, sino también una enciclopedia de la sociedad tibetana. El rey Gesar parece estar incrustado en la tierra, existiendo en cada montaña, río, árbol y hierba. Aquí, los habitantes de la meseta dicen que las cumbres nevadas son su cuerpo, los ríos son su sangre y el viento es su respiración. #PanoramadeChina #Chinarama #CulturaChina #ElReyGesar #documental

title: Epic Dance Drama “Shi Jing · Gesar”: Fusion of China’s Ancient Poetry & Tibetan Heroic Legend
description:
Epic large-scale dance poetry drama “Shi Jing · Gesar” – a breathtaking fusion of China’s oldest poetry collection “The Book of Songs” (Shi Jing) and the world’s longest heroic epic “Epic of King Gesar” from Tibet.
Presented by Nagqu City, Tibet with support from Zhejiang Aid Tibet, this 2025 masterpiece blends Han and Tibetan music, dance, costumes, totems, and modern stage technology to tell a story of shared origins, journeys, and dreams between cultures.
Witness majestic group dances, heroic battles, poetic love stories, and the unity of Chinese civilizations across thousands of years.
Highlights include:• “Jian Jia Cang Cang” – inspired by The Book of Songs• “King Gesar” & “Hero’s Footprints” – powerful Tibetan heroic sequences• Stunning visual effects + live ethnic instruments
Premiered in Hangzhou 2025 → performed in Beijing & Lhasa
#汉藏融合 #EpicDance #TibetanCulture
大型舞蹈史诗剧《诗经·格萨尔》，首创将中国最古老的诗歌总集《诗经》与世界上最长的英雄史诗《格萨尔王传》深度融合，展现“同宗同源、同路同梦”的壮丽图景。由西藏那曲市策划、浙江援藏支持，2025年杭州首演后进京、拉萨巡演。融合汉藏歌舞、诗词、图腾、乐器与高科技舞台呈现，打造跨越千年的文化交响。包含《蒹葭苍苍》《格萨尔王》《英雄足迹》《凤凰于飞》等重磅舞段。

title: Ep93: The Epic of King Gesar - Amalia Rubin
description:
In this episode I am joined by Amalia Rubin - internationally known singer, Buddhist practitioner, 8 language polyglot, and scholar currently conducing her PhD research on ‘Gesar as an ongoing mode of reality in the lives of modern Tibetan youths in Eastern Tibet’ at the University of Leeds.

In this interview, Amalia reveals her unusual upbringing and subsequent life of adventure, intensive language immersion, life and work throughout Asia, and encounters with high Lamas including an ongoing friendship of the 17th Karmapa. 

We learn about Amalia’s research interests, including the shamanic healing practices of the Steppes, the babdrung trance-bards of the Gesar tradition, and the similarities between the modern revivals of traditional shamanism in Mongolia and the Gesar Ling traditions of Eastern Tibet,. 

Amalia also discusses her special relationship with Tana monastery, and how she became an internationally renowned singer and performer.

title: Introduction "King Gesar’s One Hundred Generals" Grand Thanka Project
description:
Epic of King Gesar is a breathtaking living heroic epic of the ancient Tibetan people. It is a magnificent piece of traditional cultural heritage, with an immense scope that spans multiple ethnic groups and languages. 

The epic is not only passed down in Tibetan regions such as Gansu, Qinghai, Sichuan, Yunnan, and Tibet, but is also widespread among other ethnic groups in neighboring provinces and regions, including Xinjiang, Ningxia, and Inner Mongolia. These groups include Tibetans, Mongols, Tu, Yugur, Salar, Pumi, Naxi, Bai, Lisu, and the Mosuo people.

Moreover, the epic has spread to countries and regions surrounding the "Roof of the World," such as Russia, Outer Mongolia, Pakistan, Sikkim, and Bhutan, making it a true treasure of the world’s cultural heritage. In 2009, the Epic of King Gesar was inscribed by UNESCO on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

As the longest epic in the world, the Epic of King Gesar is not merely a mythical tale, but a record of real historical events. It contains numerous proverbs, eulogies, and historical references that reflect every aspect of Tibetan culture—astronomy, geography, medicine, calendar calculation, philosophy, religion, law, customs, poetry, music and dance, craftsmanship, architecture, animal husbandry, and more can all trace their roots within the lines of this epic.

Traditionally, the epic was passed down orally by singer-storytellers. Some of these performers would put on the Gesar storytelling hat and unroll thangka paintings as they vividly recited and sang the many stories of King Gesar with deep emotion and expressive voices.

Compared to the abstract nature of written heritage, thangka, this unique form of painting, is more vivid and representative. For the vast Tibetan community, songs and thangka paintings are like visualized books—every verse, every symbol, every stroke of color holds irreplaceable meaning and conveys layers of deep, progressive information.

The King Gesar's One Hundred Generals Grand Thangka project is a monumental artistic achievement created over 17 years by Rongpo Rinpoche in collaboration with Master Lamu of the Karma Gadri painting school. 

This masterpiece brings to life the central characters of the Epic of King Gesar—King Gesar himself, his one hundred generals, his royal city, consorts, key events, and scenes. Every detail, from the color palette to the background, from personalities to costumes, armor, weapons, and steeds, is rendered through a fusion of traditional thangka painting and modern artistic techniques, combining Tibetan and Han Chinese artistic styles.

With deep respect for history and ethnic culture, and adherence to the core content of the epic, the creation involved extensive research and validation to ensure the most faithful representation of the epic’s characters and scenes. 

The resulting artworks showcase these legendary figures with exquisite brushwork and vibrant colors, immersing viewers in the sweeping grandeur of this historic saga.

Each thangka painting of the hundred generals is accompanied by the general’s original name and a poetic eulogy of their major accomplishments, seamlessly blending text and imagery. This album is not only a comprehensive, multi-dimensional study and portrayal of the main figures—King Gesar and his generals— but it also serves to fill historical gaps and clarify cultural origins.

It is a testament to the hope of ethnic cultural integration, development, and revival—a powerful bridge between the grandeur of ancient tradition and the progress of modern civilization. 

This work holds profound significance in expanding the cultural domain of the Epic of King Gesar, promoting comprehensive multi-ethnic research in Gesar studies, and contributing to the preservation and revitalization of outstanding traditional Chinese and ethnic cultures. It carries epoch-making historical value and far-reaching importance.

title: The traditional firing technology of Longquan celadon
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00205
Description: The city of Longquan in the coastal Chinese province of Zhejian is known for its celadon pottery and the traditional firing technology that imparts its distinctive glaze. Compounded from violet-golden clay and a mixture of burnt feldspar, limestone, quartz and plant ash, the glaze is prepared from recipes that have often been handed down for generations by teachers or within families. The glaze is applied to a fired stoneware vessel, which is then fired again in a repeated cycle of six stages of heating and cooling where precise temperatures matter a great deal: either over- or under-firing will spoil the effect. Experienced celadon artists carefully control each stage with a thermometer and by observing the colour of the flame, which reaches temperatures as high as 1310º C. The final product may take either of two styles: elder brother celadon has a black finish with a crackle effect, while the younger brother variety has a thick, lavender-grey and plum-green finish. With its underlying jade-like green colour, celadon fired by the family-oriented businesses of Longquan is prized as masterwork-quality art that can also serve as household ware. It is a proud symbol of the cultural heritage of the craftspeople, their city and the nation.
Country(ies): China

title: ice crack glaze of Longquan celadon Ge kiln #teaart #tea #teatime #ceramic #saanwu #pottery
description: The ice-crack glaze, also known as crackle glaze, refers to the natural network of cracks that form on the surface of porcelain during the firing process. It is not a deliberate decoration carved by hand, but rather the trace left by fire and water when exposed to intense heat and sudden cooling. The interlaced patterns resemble a sheet of fractured ice — layered, translucent, and full of dimension — earning it the name ice-crack glaze.

title: Introduction to Longquan Celadon
description:
This short video introduces the world-famous Longquan celadon. The Longquan Kilns in Zhejiang Province are world-famous for producing two types of celadon (a type of ceramic glaze that was historically jade green), each from a different kiln; the Ge (elder brother) kiln and the Di (younger brother) kiln. The Ge kiln produces a veined, cracked glaze which gives the porcelain a unique look, as if it is set with gold and iron wires, while the Di kiln’s glaze has no cracks and produces a sparkling, glittering finish like sapphire and emerald. While ancient celadon typically had the jade green colour, the Di kiln’s lavender-grey and plum green celadon has demonstrated the advancements in this traditional technique. 

This video is from the ‘Wonderful Time under the Moon’ series of the global event “Mid-Autumn Festival: A Moonmoment to Remember”.

project_name_en: Traditional firing technology of Longquan celadon
title: LONGQUAN CELADON POTTERY | Traditional Fern Root Glaze Making - UNESCO Heritage Craft
description:
Watch the COMPLETE creation of traditional Longquan celadon pottery (龙泉青瓷) using ancient fern root starch glaze - a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage technique since 2006.

This video documents every step of this intricate process:
• FERN HARVESTING - Wild bracken ferns gathered from mountain forests, the secret ingredient for celadon glaze
• STONE MILL GRINDING - Traditional stone mill extracts precious starch from fern roots, producing reddish-brown liquid
• STARCH REFINEMENT - Multiple washing and settling processes to purify the fern starch into glaze material (釉料)
• CLAY PREPARATION - Mountain clay wedged and prepared for wheel throwing
• WHEEL THROWING - Master potter shapes elegant tea cups on traditional kick wheel
• PATTERN CARVING - Delicate floral and lotus motifs carved into leather-hard clay
• GLAZE APPLICATION - Fern starch glaze carefully applied to bisqueware
• DRAGON KILN FIRING - Traditional wood-fired dragon kiln transforms pieces at 1300°C
• FINAL REVEAL - Stunning turquoise celadon emerges with jade-like translucency

The finished Longquan celadon (龙泉青瓷) represents over 1600 years of Chinese ceramic mastery - where forest meets fire in perfect harmony. No music, pure ASMR crafting sounds.

title: The Color of Sky After Rain: Ru Celadon雨过天青云破处-汝瓷#countryside #china #production #中国传统技艺
description: The phrase "The color of sky after rain, where clouds break apart" was Emperor Huizong of Song's poetic praise for Ru kiln porcelain. This line captures the essence of Ru ware's legendary celadon glaze—a tranquil, ethereal blue-green reminiscent of the heavens glimpsed through dispersing storm clouds. Symbolizing purity and harmony, it stands as the pinnacle of Song Dynasty aesthetics.  “雨过天青云破处”出自宋徽宗赵佶对汝窑瓷器的赞誉，描绘了汝窑天青色瓷器如雨后初晴、云层微裂时露出的天空之色，澄澈静谧。

heritage_item: 龙泉青瓷传统烧制技艺
project_name_en: Traditional firing technology of Longquan celadon
title: The Making of Longquan Celadon | Trailer
description:
The Making of Longquan Celadon -- http://youtu.be/vsP9HxgXPJI

Longquan is an ancient cultural city famous for making celadon in southwest Zhejiang Province, East China. Abundant in porcelain clay and pinewood, used for baking porcelain, over 360 sites are densely distributed throughout Longquan. All these sites historically belong to the ancient Longquan Kiln, which has the longest manufacturing history, widest site distribution, highest quality, largest celadon production and export scales in China's porcelain history.

There are roughly eight steps involved in making Longquan celadon, namely, throwing, trimming, biscuiting, printing, mixing glaze, glaze application, encastage,and burning.

The making of celadon is not only a skill but also an art. Superior celadon is reputed to be as beautiful as jade. Famous worldwide, Longquan celadon was not only used for every dynasty's royal courts in ancient China, but has been exported to many other countries and regions of Asia, Africa and Europe since the early Song Dynasty. Modern Longquan celadon still inherite features of the traditional Longquan Kiln and many celadon products made by local masters and craftsmen have won top honors and prizes in various pottery-making competitions.

The Longquan Celadon made by “Dun Tang” is one of the kilns, which is well-knowned for its generations of famous celadon craftsmen of wonderful workmanship.

Photographed and edited by Yameng Zhang

project_name_en: Traditional firing technology of Longquan celadon
title: Fantastic Longquan Celadon 龙泉青瓷 熠熠生辉｜Zhejiang｜浙江｜陶瓷｜瓷器｜龙泉｜Ceramics｜非遗
description:
Half of the history of Chinese ceramics took place in Zhejiang; half of the history of Zhejiang ceramics took place in Longquan. Longquan celadon began to appear in the Three Kingdoms period and the Western & Eastern Jin dynasties, and flourished during the Song and Yuan dynasties. Longquan got its name because of a sword, but earned its reputation with the ceramics produced here. These celadon makers have passed on the skill of making the unique celadon for a thousand years in the twinkling of an eye.
    All the three members of the family are celadon makers. They are confident, lovely, and respectable. They make azure and plum colors an impressive part of their works of art.

project_name_en: Traditional firing technology of Longquan celadon
title: The making of Longquan celadon is not only a skill but also an art.
description:
Longquan is an ancient cultural city famous for making celadon in southwest Zhejiang Province, East China. Abundant in porcelain clay and pinewood, used for baking porcelain, over 360 sites are densely distributed throughout Longquan. All these sites historically belong to the ancient Longquan Kiln, which has the longest manufacturing history, widest site distribution, highest quality, largest celadon production and export scales in China's porcelain history. 
There are roughly eight steps involved in making Longquan celadon, namely, throwing, trimming, biscuiting, printing, mixing glaze, glaze application, encastage and burning. 
The making of celadon is not only a skill but also an art. Superior celadon is reputed to be as beautiful as jade. Famous worldwide, Longquan celadon was not only used for every dynasty's royal courts in ancient China, but has been exported to many other countries and regions of Asia, Africa and Europe since the early Song Dynasty. Modern Longquan celadon still inherit features of the traditional Longquan Kiln and many celadon products made by local masters and craftsmen have won top honors and prizes in various pottery-making competitions.

title: Regong Art｜A Living Intangible Cultural Heritage from Qinghai, China
description:
Regong Art is a world-class intangible cultural heritage originating from Qinghai, China. It is more than a traditional art form, it is a spiritual language expressed through color, form, and craftsmanship.

Here, we introduce the cultural roots of Regong Art and its main artistic expressions, including thangka painting, clay sculpture, bronze carving, wood carving, and appliqué embroidery. For generations, these art forms have carried stories of belief, devotion, and cultural identity.

At the same time, we believe that heritage should not only be preserved in museums or temples, it can also live in everyday life.

Inspired by Regong Art, we create carefully crafted objects that translate traditional aesthetics into modern forms. Each piece is designed with respect for the original art, allowing cultural meaning to be experienced, touched, and appreciated in daily moments.

title: Unveiling China's Intangible Cultural Heritage: The Art of Hui Ink Production
description:
In this video, we delve into the intricate process of creating Hui Ink, an element of China's intangible cultural heritage. Originating from the Huizhou region in Anhui Province, Hui Ink is one of the four famous inks of China, renowned for its deep black color, enduring fragrance, and excellent water resistance.

We start with the collection of raw materials, explaining how soot is extracted from pine and oil smoke to form the base of the ink. We then demonstrate the process of mixing the ink base with animal glue to form ink sticks. A unique aspect of Hui Ink - its long natural drying and manual grinding process - is also highlighted, making each ink stick a unique piece of art.

Through this video, we hope to share and celebrate this traditional Chinese art and craft with a wider audience.

Don't forget to hit the subscribe button if you enjoyed this video and want to see more content like this. We appreciate your support and look forward to sharing more aspects of Chinese culture with you.

title: A Week at a Tibetan Art School in Labrang | CHINA OFF THE BEATEN PATH | Xiahe, Gansu, China
description:
A Week at a Tibetan Art School in Labrang - China off the beaten path.
In January 2018 I visited Snowland Art School in Xiahe, Gansu, during their annual Girl Power Camp. It was a fantastic experience that I will always remember. And I sure will be back!

SEE THE FULL VIDEO LIST FROM SNOWLAND ARTS SCHOOL HERE: https://bit.ly/2JzpB2r

If you are wondering what to do in Gansu, China, or have a couple of days to spare while traveling in Qinghai, Xiahe is only a 4 hour bus ride from Lanzhou and Xining respectively. It's the perfect place to visit where you can first see the beautiful Labrang Monastery, and then visit Snowland to meet the teacher (Tenzin) and all the students, plus see their art and handicrafts.

Follow Snowland Art School at their Facebook page: http://www.facebook.com/snowlandart

Snowland Art’s website: http://www.snowlandart.org/


› ABOUT THE SCHOOL
Snowland Art School was founded by Kristel Ouwehand (Tibetan name: Tenzin Dolma) in 2010. The purpose of the school is to preserve the old traditional style Thangka, where copying and tracing is not allowed. The students study at the school for 5 years, and along with the traditional Tibetan style art, they also learn western art with perspective and proportions. 

What I find extremely important with this school is the way they promote women in art. Nomadic Tibetan women often face pressure to marry early, and then a heavy work load as wife and mothers. Few get the opportunity to follow their passion, and many Thangka masters believe that women’s brains are too small to be able to learn something as complex as painting. What Tenzin does is therefore very important. Gender equality is part of daily life at the school – where the students are both boys and girls. Tenzin was thinking of starting a school only for girls, but decided against it – to change the circumstances for the girls, we also have to change the boys, she reasoned.

To start with, Tenzin was teaching in a small apartment in the city of Xining, and the students lived around the city. To provide a better environment and to get more space, Tenzin and her husband purchased a traditional style Tibetan house in Labrang/Xiahe – where Labrang monastery is located, famous for its Thangkas. Since the students now live at the school, it becomes more like a family – something I had the privilege to experience during my week there.

title: The History of Chinese Contemporary Art Melissa Chiu  | Big Think
description:
The History of Chinese Contemporary Art
New videos DAILY: https://bigth.ink/youtube
Join Big Think Edge for exclusive videos: https://bigth.ink/Edge
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
Melissa Chiu on Chinese art, before and after Tiananmen
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
Melissa Chiu:

Melissa Chiu, Museum Director and Curator for Contemporary Asian and Asian-American art at the Asia Society, has had a long involvement with Asian contemporary art and is recognized as a leading authority in the field. Prior to working at the Asia Society, she served as the founding Director of the Asia-Australia Arts Centre in Sydney, a non-profit contemporary art center devoted to promoting dialogue in the Asia-Pacific region among artists, writers, curators and filmmakers.

Additionally, Ms. Chiu has curated over thirty exhibitions with artists from Malaysia, Vietnam, China, Thailand and Japan, among others. She was a founding member of the Asian Contemporary Art Consortium and a driving force behind the establishment of Asian Contemporary Art Week, which will mark its sixth year in New York next Spring.

Melissa Chiu received her B.A. from the University of Western Sydney and her M.A. from the College of Fine Arts, University of South Wales. She holds a Ph.D. from the University of Western Sydney and has authored many artist monographs and conference papers and has published widely in journals, magazines and for exhibition catalogues. Ms. Chiu has been a faculty member of the Rhode Island School of Design where she taught Asian contemporary art and design. She has also served on a number of boards and grant panels, including the New York State Council on the Arts, Museums Grant Committee and the New York City Department of Cultural Affairs. 
 ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TRANSCRIPT:

Topic: The History of Chinese Contemporary Art 

Melissa Chiu: I think one of the most interesting things to say about Chinese contemporary art is that it really has this 30 year history. And when I talk about it, we . . . we often cite the most important kind of birth date, if you like, as 1979 when _______ declared his open door policy in China, which allowed the opening up of China. Artists in China at that time were allowed to engage with western art history in a way that they hadn’t been able to before. And there are these important moments within Chinese contemporary art that allow us to identify three main phases defined by decades. Mostly throughout the 1980s you saw this kind of frenetic energy and engagement with western ideas. And that is often cited as the kind of most experimental period. And then throughout the 1990s after Tieneman on June 4, 1989, we saw a restriction of activities that artists were able to stage. They were shut out of galleries . . . any galleries and museums. They couldn’t show their work in the public sphere. And during this decade we also saw the internationalization of Chinese contemporary art; that on the one had Chinese artists were prevented from showing their work inside China; but around the time of the mid-1990s, there was much curatorial interest, especially in Europe and places like Australia. And there were many international opportunities for Chinese artists to show their work outside. And then of course, everything changed in 2000. There was a real opening up of China. And the Chinese government itself had a different kind of relationship with experimental artists. They decided that it was okay for Chinese artists to show their work in museums. And we have since seen the Chinese government take on experimental artists at, you know, kind of national representation such as at the ______ premier . . . kind of a contemporary art event that happens every two years in Venice.

Question: What impact has China’s opening had on the country’s art world? 

Melissa Chiu: I’m not sure that it’s had a direct impact other than artists being able to show their work. I think that what has happened is that there is less of an inclination, I think now, to . . . to . . . for artists to produce works that directly critique famous political figures like Mao. We’re seeing a younger generation take a kind of different approach to creating the works that they do; but it’s very hard to generalize in the kind of . . . and identify a tangible trend since . . . since 2000. In fact, you might say that since 2000, you’ve . . . it’s much more difficult to categorize the Chinese art world in terms of movements. We’ve seen a real opening up all across the country towards different types of art being produced, different groups of artists, different . . . different kinds of exhibitions __________.

title: 3381: Chinese artist creates stunning chalk sketch of porcelain and hanfu girls
description:
On August 24, 2024, in Ningxia, a region in northwestern China, a video captured the creation of a delicate chalk sketch. In the video, an artist skillfully drew a beautiful blue-and-white porcelain vase.

Beside the vase, two girls dressed in traditional hanfu were also depicted. Their hair was elegantly styled, and their flowing robes made them appear as if they had stepped out of an ancient time.

The artwork, combining the elegance of the porcelain and the gracefulness of the hanfu girls, left Chinese viewers in awe with its beauty.

title: Some Chinese Japanese Art
description:
🌸 Are you a lover of Japanese culture and porcelain art? Then you'll definitely fall in love with the exquisite Japanese Lithophane Geisha Kutani Porcelain Tea Cup! 🍵✨

ℹ️ Lithophane is a unique technique used in Kutani porcelain, where thin translucent porcelain is carefully carved to create intricate designs that become visible when backlit. These porcelain tea cups depict stunning geisha portraits, capturing the grace and beauty of traditional Japanese culture. 😍🎎

⭐️ Made in the renowned Kutani region of Japan, these tea cups showcase exceptional craftsmanship and attention to detail. The combination of delicate lithophane carving and vibrant Kutani colors makes each tea cup a true work of art, perfect for your tea rituals or as a stunning display piece. 🎨⛩️

🌟 Interested in owning this beautiful piece of Japanese history? #KutaniPorcelain #GeishaTeaCup #JapaneseCulture #LithophaneArt

👉 Available in various sizes and designs, these tea cups are coveted collector's items. They make fantastic gifts for the tea enthusiasts in your life or anyone who appreciates elegant and unique home decor. 🎁🍵

✨ So, why not add a touch of enchantment and elegance to your tea-drinking experience with a Japanese Lithophane Geisha Kutani Porcelain Tea Cup? The timeless beauty of these cups will transport you to the captivating world of geishas and traditional Japanese art. 🌸🌟

title: UNESCO WORLD HERITAGE SITES - Zuojiang Huashan Rock Art, China 😍
description:
Located on the steep cliffs in the border regions of southwest China, these 38 sites of rock art illustrate the life and rituals of the Luoyue people. They date from the period around the 5th century BCE to the 2nd century CE. In a surrounding landscape of karst, rivers and plateaux, they depict ceremonies that have been interpreted as portraying the bronze drum culture once prevalent across southern China. This cultural landscape is the only remains of this culture today.

Source: https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1508

A thangka is a Tibetan hanging scroll, usually painted on cotton, and then placed in a silk brocade mount. Unlike other types of paintings, there are many rules when it comes to making a thangka. About the Rubin Museum of Art: 
The Rubin Museum of Art in Chelsea, New York City, explores and celebrates the diversity and uniqueness of Himalayan art, cultures, and ideas across history and into the present. With its globally renowned collection, largely centered around art from the Tibetan Plateau, the Rubin fosters understanding and appreciation of this region by relating its art and ideas to our shared human experience today. Inspired by the philosophical traditions of Buddhism, Hinduism, and indigenous religions, and aligned with ongoing research into learning, behavior, and the brain, the Rubin offers innovative exhibitions and programs that examine provocative ideas across the arts and explore the mind. Through its exhibitions, programs, and digital offerings, the Rubin Museum examines big questions about what it means to be human today, and serves as a space for reflection and personal transformation.

title: Tibetan Buddhist Thangka Paintings Documentary - Sand Mandala Lecture | Blanton Museum of Art
description:
On Saturday, Jan. 12, 2013, the Blanton invited monks from the Drepung Loseling Monastery to talk about the meaning of a sand mandala in a lecture titled "Symbolism of the Sand Mandala."

Sand mandalas are unique to Tibetan Buddhism and are created for the healing of living beings and the environment. Jan. 9 - 13 the visiting monks created a sand mandala in the Blanton's Rapoport Atrium as a special public program affiliated with the museum's "Into the Sacred City" exhibition of rare Tibetan works from the University of California, Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive collection.

In this 54-minute-long video with audio from the lecture, you'll learn how, by creating a sand mandala, we bring creative energy into our lives and attune ourselves to natural perfection.

title: Iconometry in Thangka Art
description:
Delve into the intricate world of Thangka Art, a sacred Tibetan Buddhist tradition. In this video, we explore the rules of iconometry, the ancient system governing the proportions of Buddhist deities and figures.


Discover how apprentices spend years mastering these sacred proportions, rooted in Sanskrit texts like the Manjushri Mulakalpa and Kalachakra Tantras.


Learn about:


The grid system used to create harmonious compositions
The two main units of measurement: cha chung (small) and cha chen (large)
The precise proportions for various deities, including Buddha and Vajrapani
The significance of adhering to iconometry rules for a "living" deity


Join us as we unravel the intricacies of Thangka Art's sacred geometry.

title: I paint a Tibetan Buddhist-style thangka in Acrylic on Canvas. #thangkapainting #thangka #thangkaart
description:
It is a common belief in many spiritual traditions that physical existence is temporary and the attachment to the body is illusory. The concept of impermanence and the transient nature of life can be difficult for some to accept, yet it is a fundamental truth. The dancing skull, often depicted in Tibetan Buddhism, serves as a powerful symbol of this impermanence. It serves as a reminder that death is an inevitable part of life and that all things, including our physical bodies, are temporary. This reminder can help shift our perspective from attachment to our physical form, to a deeper understanding of the interconnectedness and impermanence of all beings.
In the image of a hand holding a machete, one can see the symbolism of cutting off the negative traits that hinder our path towards liberation. The machete serves as a tool to sever ignorance, greed, attachment, ego, and hatred, which are the root causes of suffering and obstacles to enlightenment. It is a powerful representation of the courage and determination needed to confront and overcome these inner demons. As we wield the machete, we must approach each cut with precision and purpose, guided by wisdom and compassion. With each swipe, we let go of our attachments and ego, freeing ourselves from the cycle of suffering. This powerful image reminds us that the path towards liberation requires effort and determination, but the end result is worth the journey.

title: Thanka Painting and Wheel Of Life - Kathmandu Valley NEPAL
description:
Join us on a journey through the Kathmandu Valley in Nepal, where we explore the rich cultural tradition of thanka painting. Thankas are Buddhist paintings on canvas, often depicting deities and scenes from Buddhist mythology. In this video, we visit a thanka workshop and learn about the symbolism and meaning behind the "Wheel of Life," a common theme in thanka art.

Watch as skilled artists bring these intricate paintings to life, using a variety of techniques and materials. Discover the cultural significance of thankas and how they are used in Tibetan Buddhism.

The Thanka School we visited is called the Boudha Stupa Thanka Center, which is located near the famous Boudhanath Stupa in Kathmandu, Nepal. This center offers courses in traditional Thanka painting and is a great place to visit if you are interested in learning more about this art form. You can check out their website (https://bstcthanka.com) or social media pages for more information and to connect with them.

title: Nangsa Woebum Tibetan Opera from Tibet (Whole Show)
description:
This is an indigenous Tibetan story based on events that occurred around the eleventh century near the town of Gyangtsé, west of Lhasa. Nanga was a beautiful young woman who wanted to devote herself to the Dharma, but her beauty attracted the attention of the local governor, who was seeking a bride for his son. Her parents were in no position to resist the authority of the governor, and Nangsa was taken away to become a bride.  After seven years, she gave birth to a son. One day while she was working with others in the fields, two cotton-clad yogis appeared asking for alms.

This story is considered to be based on actual events, and the text of this volume mentions that while Nangsa was at the monastery, she meditated in a cave and that imprints of her cloak can still be seen on its walls “like impressions made in butter.” 

Nangsa is often cited as an example of the phenomenon of a death returner (’das log), one who returns to life after death. Many accounts of death-returners are found in Tibetan literature, but Nanga is the only one to have been made into an opera.

title: Tibetan Opera: A living fossil of Tibetan culture
description:
Tibetan Opera, often described as a "living fossil" of Tibetan culture, was inscribed in 2006 on China's first national list of Intangible Cultural Heritage, with approval from the State Council. In 2009, it was recognized by UNESCO and included on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. Rooted in centuries of history, Tibetan Opera is a medium through which the world can appreciate the enduring civilization of China's Xizang Autonomous Region.

title: Tibetan opera
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00208
Description: Tibetan opera, the most popular traditional opera of minority ethnic groups in China, is a comprehensive art combining folk song, dance, storytelling, chant, acrobatics and religious performance. Most popular in the Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau in western China, the performance begins with a prayer ceremony, including the cleansing of the stage by hunters and blessings by the elder, and concludes with another blessing. The heart of the opera is a drama narrated by a single speaker and enacted by performers supported by groups of singers, dancers and acrobats. Actors wear traditional masks of a variety of shapes and colours that contrast with their simple makeup. Performances may take place in public squares or temples (or, today, on stage), with the centre of the space marked by a tree placed on the ground, wrapped in colourful paper and surrounded by purified water and theatrical props. Rooted in Buddhist teachings, the stories told in Tibetan opera recount the triumph of good and the punishment of evil and therefore serve a social teaching function for the community. This multifaceted representative of Tibetan art and cultural heritage also acts as a bridge among Tibetans in different parts of the country, promoting ethnic unity and pride.
Country(ies): China

title: Tibetan Achi Lhamo Dance
description:
Lhamo, or Ache Lhamo, is a classical secular theatre of Tibet with music and dance that has been performed for centuries, whose nearest western equivalent is opera. Performances have a narrative and simple dialogue interspersed with comedy and satire; characters wear colorful masks. The core stories of these theatrical plays are drawn mostly from ancient Indian Buddhist folk tales, lives of important people and historical events from Tibetan civilization. However the ceremonial, dance and ritual spectacles strongly reflects the Tibetan Royal Dynastic period.

Very similar traditions are found in Bhutan and other regions influenced by Tibetan Buddhism and Tibetan culture

title: Tibetan Opera Nangsa woebum by TIPA (part 1)
description:
Tibetan Opera Nangsa woebum by TIPA
In the District of Gyantse, Tsang province, south-western Tibet lived a good peasant family of Phehue Nangba. The father of the family, Kusang Dechen and his wife Yansa Sedon were getting quite old, but continued to live pious lives, constantly praying to the Goddess Arya Tara and performing virtuous deeds. Their only regret about their lives was that they had not had any children. 

However, the Goddess Tara took pity on them and Yansa Sedon gave birth to a truly beautiful baby girl. She was named Nangsa Wobum, which means, "The One given to this earth, whose rays of light would liberate 100,000 sentient beings".

title: Tibetan opera
description:
Lhamo, or Ache Lhamo, is a classical secular theatre of Tibet with music and dance that has been performed for centuries, whose nearest western equivalent is opera. Performances have a narrative and simple dialogue interspersed with comedy and satire; characters wear colorful masks. The core stories of these theatrical plays are drawn mostly from ancient Indian Buddhist folk tales, lives of important people and historical events from Tibetan civilization. However the ceremonial, dance and ritual spectacles strongly reflects the Tibetan Royal Dynastic period.
Very similar traditions are found in Bhutan and other regions influenced by Tibetan Buddhism and Tibetan culture. There are at least three unique schools of Lhamo: Kyimulunga, Gyangara, and Chungba.

title: Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum | Standard vs. Tibetan #mantra #chant #spiritual
description:
Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum in two different ways.

Om Mani Padme Hum (Sanskrit-based transliteration):
This version uses Sanskrit or classical Romanized spelling.
Common in academic texts or Pan-Asian Buddhist contexts.
In actual Tibetan speech, it wouldn’t be pronounced exactly this way.

Om Mani Peme Hung (Tibetan phonetic spelling):
Reflects Tibetan pronunciation. Common in Tibetan-speaking areas and among practitioners aiming for more authentic spoken form.
Pronunciation:
Padme → Peme (དཔལ་མེད་)
Hum → Hung (ཧཱུྃ)

This ancient chant is said to hold infinite benefits—used for protection, purification, and higher manifestation.

“Om Mani Padme Hum” is recited to neutralize negative karma and dissolve spiritual obstacles.

Just listening to this mantra can shift your energy, raise your vibration, align you with the true essence of reality, attract higher experiences, and unlock hidden aspects of the self.

It is the most important mantra in Tibetan Buddhism. Each syllable purifies one of the six realms of existence and aligns body, speech, and mind with the path to enlightenment.

Sound, when repeated with intention, transforms.

title: Tibetan Opera to be Performed at Emory
description:
A Tibetan opera, "Sukyi Nyima" meaning "As Radiant As the Sun," will be performed in conjunction with Tibet Week at Emory (March 25-29, 2013).

Resident artist and Tibetan folksinger Techung will lead a cast of professionals and Emory students in the performance of the opera on Saturday, April 6, at 4 p.m. in Cannon Chapel.

Emory's performance of Sukyi Nyima is dedicated to Gen Norbu Tsering-la, who passed away on March 14, 2013, in Dharamsala, India.  Gen-la served as the opera master at the Tibetan Institute of Performing Arts (TIPA) from the late 60s until he retired in 1996.  He trained hundreds of artists during his tenure at the Institute, including our resident artist, Techung.  He will be lovingly remembered as a pivotal figure in preserving Ache Lhamo (Tibetan opera) for future generations.

The event is co-sponsored by the Emory-Tibet Partnership, the Shelley and Donald Rubin Foundation, the Department of Religion at Emory, among others.

title: World's longest epic represented in Tibetan Opera
description:
Of all China's ethnic minority folk operas, Tibetan opera probably has the longest history. It goes back to the Tang Dynasty in the 14th century when its Princess Wencheng married the Tibetan King and brought a new form of folk music to the Tibetan court. 
Yet, four-hundred years before her arrival in Tibet, the Epic of King Gesar, containing over 20 million words--the longest literary work in the world--was already widely popular and being passed down from generation to generation through song and dance. 
Fast forward to 2022, these two gems of Tibetan culture are finding their way to the stage in Beijing. Yet, can we still hear the words and emtions from so long ago? Let's find out!

title: Dancing with Tibetan Opera performers
description:
Tibetan Opera is an ancient form of drama that fuses dialogue, dances, chants, songs, acrobatics and religious rituals to tell a story. Masks are also an important element as their colors and motifs indicate the role of the characters. The art form made it to China's list of intangible cultural heritage in 2006, and in 2009 it was included in UNESCO'S list of intangible cultural heritage of humanity. The stories of the Tibetan Opera have a religious flavor as they are inspired by Buddhism and tackle devotion, love and the battle between good and evil.

CGTN reporter headed to Angren County, Shigatse City, Tibet Autonomous Region in southwest China to experience Qiongba, one of the four genres of Tibetan Opera.

title: Manas: The Kirgiz epic that echoes through generations
description:
For  more:
https://www.cgtn.com/video

The heroic epic of "Manas" is more than a story – it is a living cultural monument. As one of China's three great oral traditions, this extensive work spans over 230,000 lines across eight chapters, serving as an encyclopedia of Kirgiz history, culture, and daily life. It tells the saga of the hero Manas and his descendants over seven generations, highlighting a resilient spirit of unity, perseverance, and the fight against evil.

Meet Tuohenali, a dedicated heir to this tradition. A prefecture-level inheritor from Xinjiang's Akqi County, he began his journey at age three. As the great-grandson of the legendary Manas performer Jusup Mamay, Tuohenali has spent 28 years perfecting his art. Today, he is committed to ensuring his great-grandfather's powerful voice "reaches everyone's heart, like the wind," preserving the epic for generations to come.

title: Manas
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00209
Description: The Kirgiz ethnic minority in China, concentrated in the Xinjiang region in the west, pride themselves on their descent from the hero Manas, whose life and progeny are celebrated in one of the best-known elements of their oral tradition: the Manas epic. Traditionally sung by a Manaschi without musical accompaniment, epic performances takes place at social gatherings, community celebrations, ceremonies such as weddings and funerals and dedicated concerts. Regional variations abound, but all are characterized by pithy lyrics with phrases that now permeate the everyday language of the people, melodies adapted to the story and characters, and lively parables. The long epic records all the major historic events of greatest importance for the Kirgiz people and crystallizes their traditions and beliefs. The Kirgiz in China and the neighbouring Central Asian countries of Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan and Tajikistan regard the Manas as a key symbol of their cultural identity and the most important cultural form for public entertainment, the preservation of history, the transmission of knowledge to the young and the summoning of good fortune. One of the three major epics of China, it is both an outstanding artistic creation and an oral encyclopaedia of the Kirgiz people.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 Xinjiang Safeguarding and Research Center of Intangible Cultural Heritage

title: What Is The Epic of Manas?
description:
The Epic of Manas (Kyrgyz: Манас дастаны, Turkish: Manas Destanı) is a traditional epic poem of the Kyrgyz people. The monumental epic Manas is the most treasured expression of the national heritage of the Kyrgyz people. Composed and sung entirely in oral form by various singers throughout the centuries, Manas is regarded as the epitome of oral creativity. It is considered to be one of the greatest examples of epic poetry, whose importance is not inferior to that of the Homeric epic (Illiad and Odyssey. As nomads, the Kyrgyz had no written language. However, they excelled in oral composition, which they artistically employed in their traditional poetry and epic songs. As the internationally renowned Kyrgyz writer Chingiz Aitmatov notes: "If other peoples/nations displayed their past culture and history in written art, the sculpture, architecture, theatre and literature, Kyrgyz people expressed their worldview, pride and dignity, battles and their hope for the future in epic genre."

Today there are about sixty versions of the epic Manas recorded from various epic singers and oral poets. Its longest version, consisting of half a million poetic lines, was written down from one of the last master-manaschï (singers of Manas) Saiakbai Karalaev (1894-1971). The epic is indeed unique in its size. It is twenty times longer than the Homeric epics Iliad (15693) and Odyssey (12110) taken together and two and a half times the length of the Indian epic Mahabharata.

It is not known when and by whom the epic Manas was composed originally. People remembered deeds and kindness of certain historical personalities for a long time and their jomokchus, i.e., storytellers or epic singers, developed some of those major historical events into epic songs in which they glorified the life and the deeds of the hero. The singer named Ïrchï uul, who acts as one of the forty companions of the hero Manas in some episodes, is remembered among the Kyrgyz. According a legend, it was Ïrchï uul who composed the original version or the first lines of Manas in the form of a lament, glorifying the heroic deeds of Manas after his death. Later, all the laments were brought together by a legendary singer named Toktogul, who is believed to have lived about 500 years ago and created the epic Manas out of those separate songs. 

Manas is sung without an accompaniment of any musical instrument both by men and women, but traditionally male singers were more popular because they traveled more than women. Unlike other Kyrgyz epic songs, the epic Manas has a unique style of singing. It involves not only singing, but acting as well. The style of the song varies according to the nature of the stories. If the singer sings about a battle, he vividly recreates that scene for his audience. If he describes a tragic scene, e.g., death of a hero, he expresses that by singing laments and crying with actual tears. He does not just recite the epic, but acts it out by speaking the language of each character.

The epic singers were traditionally called jomokchu (derived from jomok, fairy-tale). The contemporary term manaschï, singer of the epic Manas, is a new term coined during the Soviet period and it refers only to those who recite Manas. Every singer of Manas had his own pupil, who learned the epic from the established master-singers. First they learned some episodes and then the main stories by heart. Later, if they possessed the gift of improvisation, they added their own innovations.

During the various stages of becoming masters of the epic, manaschïs were divided according to their poetic and improvisational skills into three categories: üyrönchük manashcï (new learner manaschï), chala manshï (not a true manaschï), chïnïgï manaschï ("true manaschï), and finally chong manaschï (great manaschï).

The epic tells the story of Manas, his descendants and his followers. Battles against Khitan and Oirat enemies form a central theme in the epic. The epic is divided into three parts, each consisting of a loose collection of episodic heroic events.

title: Epic of Manas - Manas Dastany
description:
#Her-Mannus #Er-Manas #Alp-Manash

The Epic of Manas is a traditional epic poem dating to the 18th century but claimed by the Kyrgyz people to be much older. The plot of Manas revolves around a series of events that coincide with the history of the region in the 17th century, primarily the interaction of the Turkic-speaking people from the mountains to the south of the Dasht-i Qipchaq and the Oirat Mongols from the bordering area of Jungaria.
In the 19th century versions, Manas is the leader of the Nogay people, while in versions dating after 1920, Manas is a Kyrgyz and a leader of the Kyrgyz.  
Attempts have been made to connect modern Kyrgyz with the Yenisei Kirghiz, today claimed by Kyrgyzstan to be the ancestors of modern Kyrgyz. Kazakh ethnographer and historian Shokan Shinghisuly Walikhanuli was unable to find evidence of folk-memory during his extended research in 19th-century Kyrgyzstan.
There are more than 65 written versions of parts of the epic. An English translation of the version of Sagimbai Orozbakov by Walter May was published in 1995, in commemoration of the presumed 1000th anniversary of Manas' birth, and re-issued in two volumes in 2004. Arthur Thomas Hatto has made English translations of the Manas tales recorded by Shokan Valikhanov and Vasily Radlov in the 19th century.

title: MANAS epic poem is the basis of Kyrgyz culture   #centralasia #silkroad #manas #kyrgyzstan
description:
The Epic of Manas is one of the world’s largest oral narratives and the foundational cultural text of the Kyrgyz people. More than just a heroic story, it is a living record of Kyrgyz history, worldview, and identity. Passed down through generations by manaschi — traditional storytellers — the epic has shaped Kyrgyz social memory for centuries and continues to play a central role in the nation’s cultural life.

At its core, Manas recounts the life and deeds of the legendary hero Manas, followed by the stories of his son Semetei and his grandson Seitek. Their struggles to unite the Kyrgyz tribes, defend the homeland, and preserve freedom form the backbone of the narrative. Though rooted in oral tradition rather than historical documentation, the epic preserves echoes of real historical processes: nomadic migrations, inter-tribal alliances, and conflicts that shaped the political landscape of Central Eurasia.

Scholars consider Manas an invaluable source for understanding traditional Kyrgyz society. The epic describes social structures, kinship ties, customary law, clan alliances, and codes of honor. It reflects the importance of hospitality, bravery, loyalty, and collective unity — values that remain central to Kyrgyz culture today. The text also preserves detailed knowledge about nomadic life: horsemanship, hunting practices, pastoral traditions, and the symbolic significance of the landscape.

One of the most remarkable aspects of the epic is its length. In its full recited form, Manas can exceed half a million lines, making it longer than the Mahabharata and The Iliad and The Odyssey combined. Throughout history, the epic was transmitted entirely orally, without a fixed written version. Each manaschi develops his own style, expanding or condensing episodes while preserving the core structure of the saga. This has allowed the epic to remain dynamic and adaptable, while maintaining continuity across centuries.

The role of the manaschi is central: they are not merely reciters but guardians of cultural memory. Traditionally, the ability to recite Manas is viewed as a calling or a gift, often revealed in dreams. Their performances are highly dramatic and rhythmic, combining deep knowledge of the text with improvisation. Through them, the epic has survived periods of political upheaval, from the era of nomadic khanates to Russian imperial rule and the Soviet period.

During the 20th century the epic was recorded, studied, and preserved by Kyrgyz scholars and ethnographers. Its significance was formally recognized in 1995 during the 1,000-year celebration of Manas — a major cultural event supported by UNESCO and attended by international researchers. In 2013, the Manas trilogy (Manas, Semetei, Seitek) was inscribed on UNESCO’s list of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

Today, the Epic of Manas remains a cornerstone of Kyrgyz national identity. It is taught in schools, performed at festivals, and studied by historians, linguists, and anthropologists. For many Kyrgyz people, Manas is not simply literature — it is a cultural map that links the past with the present, shaping how the nation understands its origins, values, and place within Central Asia.

title: Сhinese Artists Present ‘Manas’ Opera in Bishkek
description:
Chinese opera singers presented a new production of the famous ‘Manas’ epic in Kyrgyzstan for the first time. The premiere is a story of great victories and heroism of the Kyrgyz warriors, depicting their love for the homeland. The premiere was staged at the Maldybaev Kyrgyz National Academic Opera and Ballet Theater.

Each of the four scenes of the opera gave the audience not only aesthetic pleasure, but also the opportunity to plunge into the country’s rich national flavor. Chinese artists managed to convey the true image of their characters and the atmosphere of a distant time. The Chinese creative team visited the Kyzyl-Suu region to learn the Kyrgyz culture and mentality.

‘We have read almost all versions of the epos. The whole group went to Kyzyl Suu where Kyrgyz people reside. We have worked hard on this performance and I hope the audience will appreciate it,’ Zhumak Kadyr, adviser to the stage director said.

Turgunaaly Mamai, great-grandson of Zhusup Mamai was among the audience and spoke highly of the opera. His great-grandfather was a famous manaschy, the Manas expert who gave the world legendary tales resulting in 18 volumes of the epic in the original interpretation.

‘I have assessed the production not only as a descendant of Zhusup Mamai, but also as a performer. I want to thank the Chinese artists, who conveyed the images of our ancestors brilliantly,’ Mamai said.

Bright costumes, great stage props and professional performance of the actors surprised the audience, capturing their hearts. The original music written by the Chinese composer has fully conveyed the spirit of the Kyrgyz epic.

title: Mongolian Throat Singing | Khöömei | UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity
description:
Khoomei is a traditional Mongolian art of throat singing practiced by people in Mongolia, Inner Mongolia, Tuva and Siberia. The performer imitates sounds of nature, simultaneously emitting two distinct vocal sounds. Khoomei literally means pharynx, and is believed to have been learned from birds. It is inscribed in 2009 on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of UNESCO, under the name Mongolian art of singing, Khoomei.

ICCR - Indian Council for Cultural Relations in collaboration with Heritage Bengal presented this awesome Mongolian throat singing which is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

title: Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00210
Description: The Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei, or Hooliin Chor (throat harmony), is a style of singing in which a single performer produces a diversified harmony of multiple voice parts, including a continued bass element produced in the throat. These singers may perform alone or in groups. Khoomei is practised today among Mongolian communities in several countries, especially in Inner Mongolia in northern China, western Mongolia and the Tuva Republic of Russia. Traditionally performed on the occasion of ritual ceremonies, songs express respect and praise for the natural world, for the ancestors of the Mongolian people and for great heroes. The form is reserved for special events and group activities such as horse races, archery and wrestling tournaments, large banquets and sacrificial rituals. The timing and order of songs is often strictly regulated. Khoomei has long been regarded as a central element representing Mongolian culture and remains a strong symbol of national or ethnic identity. As a window into the philosophy and aesthetic values of the Mongol people, it has served as a kind of cultural emissary promoting understanding and friendship among China, Mongolia and Russia, and has attracted attention around the world as a unique form of musical expression.
Country(ies): China

title: Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum | Standard vs. Tibetan #mantra #chant #spiritual
description:
Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum in two different ways.

Om Mani Padme Hum (Sanskrit-based transliteration):
This version uses Sanskrit or classical Romanized spelling.
Common in academic texts or Pan-Asian Buddhist contexts.
In actual Tibetan speech, it wouldn’t be pronounced exactly this way.

Om Mani Peme Hung (Tibetan phonetic spelling):
Reflects Tibetan pronunciation. Common in Tibetan-speaking areas and among practitioners aiming for more authentic spoken form.
Pronunciation:
Padme → Peme (དཔལ་མེད་)
Hum → Hung (ཧཱུྃ)

This ancient chant is said to hold infinite benefits—used for protection, purification, and higher manifestation.

“Om Mani Padme Hum” is recited to neutralize negative karma and dissolve spiritual obstacles.

Just listening to this mantra can shift your energy, raise your vibration, align you with the true essence of reality, attract higher experiences, and unlock hidden aspects of the self.

It is the most important mantra in Tibetan Buddhism. Each syllable purifies one of the six realms of existence and aligns body, speech, and mind with the path to enlightenment.

Sound, when repeated with intention, transforms.

Share to multiply the energy of this chant

description:
throat-singing around the world 🌎 throat-singing originated amongst turko-mongol tribes of the altai and sayan mountains of southern siberia and western mongolia, but is also a practice in northern canada and south africa where it has taken on its own form.

to make this unique sound, a singer will produce two or more notes simultaneously by using precise movements in their lips, tongue, jaw, valum and larynx. this produces a distinct set of harmonies through the throat’s resonant qualities.

title: Mongolian Throat Singing | Khöömei | UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity
description:
Khoomei is a traditional Mongolian art of throat singing practiced by people in Mongolia, Inner Mongolia, Tuva and Siberia. The performer imitates sounds of nature, simultaneously emitting two distinct vocal sounds. Khoomei literally means pharynx, and is believed to have been learned from birds. It is inscribed in 2009 on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of UNESCO, under the name Mongolian art of singing, Khoomei.

ICCR - Indian Council for Cultural Relations in collaboration with Heritage Bengal presented this awesome Mongolian throat singing which is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

title: The Mongolian traditional art of Khöömei
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00396
Description: Khöömei is a form of singing originating in western Mongolia, in the Altai mountains. The performer imitates sounds of nature, simultaneously emitting two distinct vocal sounds: along with a continuous drone, the singer produces a melody of harmonics. Khöömei literally means pharynx, and it is believed to have been learned from birds, whose spirits are central to shamanic practices. The multitude of Khöömei techniques in Mongolia are grouped within two main styles: the ''kharkhiraa'' (deep Khöömei) and ''isgeree'' Khöömei (whistled Khöömei). In ''kharkhiraa'' the singer sings a drone in a normal voice, while emphasizing the undertone or subharmonic one octave below. In ''isgeree'' Khöömei, it is the overtones above the fundamental note of the drone that are emphasized, creating a higher-pitched whistle. In both cases, the drone is produced with very taut vocal cords, and the melody is created by modulating the size and shape of the mouth cavity, opening and closing the lips and moving the tongue. Khöömei is performed by Mongolian nomads in a variety of social occasions, from grand state ceremonies to festive household events. Khöömei is also sung during herding, and inside the yurt to lull babies to sleep. Traditionally, Khöömei is transmitted orally from bearer to learner, or via master-to-apprentice. 
Country(ies): Mongolia
© 2009 by Mongolian National Commission for UNESCO

title: Cântico gutural Tuvan - khoomei ( Huun-Huur-Tu - Live )
description:
Khoomei:
língua Tuva : Хөөмей ; mongol : , Baopei transliteração : kögemei , cirílico : Хөөмий

A arte da Mongólia, cantando: Khoomei, ou Hooliin Chor ("harmonia da garganta"), é um estilo de cantar no qual um único cantor produz uma harmonia diversificada de vários tons de voz, incluindo um elemento de baixo contínuo produzido na garganta. 
Esses cantores podem realizar sozinhos ou em grupos. Khoomei é praticada hoje entre as comunidades da Mongólia em vários países, especialmente no interior da Mongólia, no norte da China, Mongólia ocidental e na República de Tuva da Rússia.

Tradicionalmente realizada por ocasião de cerimoniais, rituais, canções onde se expressa o respeito e louvor para o mundo natural, para os antepassados do povo mongol e para grandes heróis. O formulário é reservado para eventos especiais e atividades em grupo, como corridas de cavalos, tiro com arco e luta livre em torneios, grandes banquetes e rituais de sacrifício. O calendário e a ordem das músicas é muitas vezes estritamente regulamentado. “Khoomei” tem sido considerado como um elemento central que representa a cultura mongol e continua a ser um forte símbolo de identidade nacional ou étnica. Como uma janela para a filosofia e os valores estéticos das comunidades mongóis, tem servido como uma espécie de emissário cultural em promover a compreensão e amizade entre China, Mongólia e Rússia, e tem atraído a atenção de todo o mundo como uma forma única de expressão musical.

Há muito tempo, a Mongólia incluiu o Khoomei como tesouro nacional, e Tuva colocou o Khoomei visto como a alma nacional de seu povo. E na China, juntamente com a ecologia original das canções folclóricas desde as escavações, também foi visto como uma prática folclórica chinesa.Atualmente, a República do Governo Popular da China tem um projeto, onde a UNESCO, declara e inclui o Khoomei como "obras-primas do Patrimônio Oral e Imaterial da Humanidade".

Em 2009, entrou oficialmente para o Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial Mundial destacando o Teatro chamado “Inner Mongolia Nacional de Canto e Dança”.

Critérios para inscrição na Lista Representativa

Em arquivos de nomeação, a (s) parte que apresente Estado (s) é (são) solicitado a demonstrar que um elemento proposto para inscrição na Lista Representativa do Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial da Humanidade satisfaz todos os critérios a seguir:

R.1 O elemento constitui patrimônio cultural imaterial, tal como definido no artigo 2 da Convenção.

R.2 Inscrição do elemento contribuirá para assegurar a visibilidade e conscientização da importância do patrimônio cultural intangível e de encorajar o diálogo, o que reflete a diversidade cultural no mundo inteiro e testemunhando a criatividade humana.

R.3 medidas de salvaguarda são elaborados que podem proteger e promover o elemento.

R.4 O elemento foi nomeado após a mais ampla participação possível da comunidade, grupo ou, se for o caso, os indivíduos em causa e com seu consentimento livre, prévio e informado.

R.5 O elemento é incluído em um inventário do patrimônio cultural imaterial existente no território (s) da apresentação de Estado (s) Parte (s), conforme definido no artigo 11 e artigo 12 da Convenção

title: How to Throat Sing With 3 Easy Steps (Kargyraa Style)
description:
Link to our Patreon:
https://www.patreon.com/Hachapuri69

If you have questions, you can check out this questions/answers video here:
https://youtu.be/WPlCMSGIQkc

Social Media platforms:

Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/Hachapuri69/
Discord: https://discord.gg/uvDBjtgc9k
Official Website: https://thefrogcult.com/
 
Hello! 

Recently on Reddit, I posted a poll about which video I should make: https://www.reddit.com/r/Hordecore/
Taking the votes into account, also interests of my friends who wanted to learn Mongolian throat singing, I present you "how to throat sing in 3 easy steps!" =)

It might take some dedication to get it better, it could be a bit challenging for girls but personally, I've seen a live show of a Tuvan woman, musician, and artist Sainkho, who can do a few forms of this culture. Another person was a female student I met a few years ago. She used to learn how to play morin khuur with a few boys when she was young, the boys were learning khoomei. They teased her that she can't sing it because she is a girl, so she got irritated and practiced morin khuur with khoomei at home. After some time, it seems she was able to sing as well as any other person. There is another woman who can sing it as well, she plays in Tuvan folk-rock band Yat-Kha :)

In any case, let me know in the comments below if you have questions or ideas. Oh, one detail I forgot to mention in the video is that this type of singing is specifically called "kargyraa". The other one is called "isgeree"(or sygyt in Tuvan) is quite difficult to learn, I still haven't mastered it fully. The intro music was done originally by ElectroKaplosion - coffin dance music with Mongolian theme, a super cool dude, check out his channel :D - https://www.youtube.com/user/ElectroKaplosion

If you liked the content and if you wish to support this channel for further video, please do give this man a helping hand :D

title: Throat Singing Around The World
description:
throat-singing around the world 🌎 throat-singing originated amongst turko-mongol tribes of the altai and sayan mountains of southern siberia and western mongolia, but is also a practice in northern canada and south africa where it has taken on its own form.

to make this unique sound, a singer will produce two or more notes simultaneously by using precise movements in their lips, tongue, jaw, valum and larynx. this produces a distinct set of harmonies through the throat’s resonant qualities.

title: Mongolian art of singing ,Khoomei——music in northern China
description: Khoomei is a unique singing art created by the Mongolians,which means that one singer sings two voice parts at the same time barele by this own vocal organ , listening carefully, you would find that the singer sends out both a continued thick bass, and a transparent and clear high pich,The bass of khoomei is a continuous one,the piches of which sometimes change,and the high part is a lumpy strain with no lyrics, While playing the Khoomei,singers should firstly loosen the vocal cords, and with the help of breath in the mouth, vibreat it to prouduce acoustical resonances to utter the bass notes, and then subtly adjust the apex gap and produce high notes by blowing a deep breath . A competent Khoomei player can play various types of musics with the method of Khoomei,in the last ,welcome to china for listening Mongolia music! and welcome to mobee bulid，ShangHai,China!

title: Throat Singing Around The World
description:
throat-singing around the world 🌎 throat-singing originated amongst turko-mongol tribes of the altai and sayan mountains of southern siberia and western mongolia, but is also a practice in northern canada and south africa where it has taken on its own form.

to make this unique sound, a singer will produce two or more notes simultaneously by using precise movements in their lips, tongue, jaw, valum and larynx. this produces a distinct set of harmonies through the throat’s resonant qualities.

title: Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum | Standard vs. Tibetan #mantra #chant #spiritual
description:
Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum in two different ways.

Om Mani Padme Hum (Sanskrit-based transliteration):
This version uses Sanskrit or classical Romanized spelling.
Common in academic texts or Pan-Asian Buddhist contexts.
In actual Tibetan speech, it wouldn’t be pronounced exactly this way.

Om Mani Peme Hung (Tibetan phonetic spelling):
Reflects Tibetan pronunciation. Common in Tibetan-speaking areas and among practitioners aiming for more authentic spoken form.
Pronunciation:
Padme → Peme (དཔལ་མེད་)
Hum → Hung (ཧཱུྃ)

This ancient chant is said to hold infinite benefits—used for protection, purification, and higher manifestation.

“Om Mani Padme Hum” is recited to neutralize negative karma and dissolve spiritual obstacles.

Just listening to this mantra can shift your energy, raise your vibration, align you with the true essence of reality, attract higher experiences, and unlock hidden aspects of the self.

It is the most important mantra in Tibetan Buddhism. Each syllable purifies one of the six realms of existence and aligns body, speech, and mind with the path to enlightenment.

Sound, when repeated with intention, transforms.

Share to multiply the energy of this chant

title: Hua'er: Grassroots folk songs from northwest China
description:
Hua’er is a genre of Chinese folk songs shared among nine ethnic groups in Gansu and Qinghai provinces of northwest China and throughout north-central China. With a soulful and exquisite melody, a long history and rich content, Hua'er boasts of intense ethnic flavor and the rustic style typical on the plateau. It is also called “the soul of the northwest” and was placed on UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2009.

Information of the Smithsonian Folklife Festival: http://www.festival.si.edu

Hua'er folksongs, often about love and longing, are popular in Gansu and Qinghai provinces, along China's Silk Road. Hailing from these regions, Kong Weifang, Cairang Zhuoma, and Suonan Sunbin demonstrated the genre at the 2014 Smithsonian Folklife Festival.

Videography: Ed Fry, David Barnes, Abby Sternberg
Editing: Charles Weber

[Catalog No. CFV10650; Copyright 2014 Smithsonian Institution]

title: Chinese folk music: "Hua'er Yu Shaonian"《花儿与少年》(Flowers and Youth)
description:
"Hua'er Yu Shaonian"《花儿与少年》(Flowers and Youth), a folk song from Qinghai province in northwest China, performed by the Cleveland Chinese Music Ensemble of Cleveland, Ohio, United States. The performance took place on Saturday, April 13, 2013 in the Community Room of the Laurel Lake Retirement Community in Hudson, Ohio, United States.

The arrangement of this piece, by David Badagnani of the Cleveland Chinese Music Ensemble, is based on the arrangement by the Kansas City Chinese Music Ensemble.

Personnel:
● Jay Xiao, dizi (笛子, bamboo transverse flute with buzzing membrane)
● Jiaqi Zheng, erhu (二胡, 2-stringed vertical fiddle with snakeskin-covered soundbox)
● Yan Ping Ye, pipa (琵琶, pear-shaped lute)
● Chaojun Gu, guzheng (古筝, bridge zither)
● David Badagnani, dahu (大胡, bass fiddle)
● Rob Hassing, percussion
● Courtney Lambert, percussion

title: Huaer
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity -2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00211
Description: In Gansu and Qinghai Provinces and throughout north-central China, people of nine different ethnic groups share a music tradition known as Huaer. The music is drawn from an extensive traditional repertoire named after ethnicities, towns or flowers (Tu Peoples ''ling,'' White Peony ''ling''), and lyrics are improvised in keeping with certain rules  for example, verses have three, four, five or six lines, each made up of seven syllables. Songs may tell of young love, the hard work and weariness of the farming life, the foibles of men and women or the joy of singing. The songs are also a vivid oral record of recent social developments in China as singers comment on the changes they observe around them. Huaer singers may have little schooling, but the most successful and widely respected singers today have become household names, performing widely and even creating their own institutes to pass on their art to apprentices. Whether it is being sung spontaneously by rural people working in the field or travelling or performed more formally at one of more than a hundred traditional Huaer festivals held annually in these provinces, Huaer is an important vehicle for expressing personal feelings in a social setting and cultural exchange across ethnicities, as well as a popular rural entertainment.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 Department of Culture of Gansu Province

title: Ningxia Hua'er Folk Music Documentary - Inheriting Classics: Ningxia Intangible Cultural Heritage
description: The Ningxia Shan Hua’er in the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity is a folk song coming from the Liupan Mountains area. Born in the lively work and life scenes, it has the countryside style and reflects the true feelings of ordinary people. With features such as a repeat in every three sentences and the fact that everyone sings along once someone else sings, the folk song is not only high-pitched and bold but also smooth and beautiful, demonstrating a delicate and lyrical charm. By singing Hua’er in the wild, beautiful longings and love thoughts will spread among the blue sky, white clouds, mountains and rivers!

title: Hua'er Folksong-
description: Hua'er is a form of folk song popular among several ethnic groups in northwestern China. Literally meaning "flower," it may have received its name from the image of a flower symbolizing one's beloved woman. Many hua'er songs begin with metaphoric and symbolic depictions of scenery, before developing into the real theme, which may be young love, the hard work and weariness of the farming life, and the foibles of men and women. The music is drawn from an extensive traditional repertoire named after ethnicities, towns, or flowers. The lyrics are improvised in keeping with certain rules.
