title: Hua’er, A Song Blooming on Barren Land - Inheriting Classics: Ningxia Intangible Cultural Heritage
description: Hua’er is a unique type of folk music. It’s known as the “soul of the northwest” and evokes a sense of romance that’s lost in bitterness. Ma Fengshan belongs to the post-70s generation. He has had a career in sales, served as a village head, and worked on construction sites, as a driver, and planting trees. For him, Hua’er is “Manhua’er”. Singers roam the mountains seeking inspiration. On this dry and cracked yellow land, a green hill can become the subject of a Manhua’er song. It’s something that blooms in a barren existence.

title: Singing Hua’er In The Hills & Clouds - Inheriting Classics: Ningxia Intangible Cultural Heritage
description:
The Ningxia Shan Hua’er in the UNESCO Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritages, the music is a unique type of folk music from Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region, with roots in the hardworking life of the people of Mount Liupan. The beautiful, exquisite melodies express heartfelt sentiments. Known as the ‘soul of China’s northwest’, Hua’er evokes a sense of romance. Its long history is a testament to its enduring popularity.

In this film, we meet Ma Yan, a young man who enjoys singing surrounded by the natural environment. His songs come from the heart and resemble poetry, he sings at the foot of the stunning Helan Mountain amongst the green hills and white clouds. ⛰️

title: Hua'er 花儿 from Ningxia 宁夏, northwest China
description:
A performance of hua'er (花儿), also called shan hua'er (山花儿), a genre of traditional unaccompanied vocal music from northwest China. Literally meaning "flower," the genre may have received its name from the image of a flower symbolizing one's beloved woman.

The performer, whose name is Ma Handong (马汉东, b. 1962), is a member of the Hui ethnic group (Hui zu, 回族) from Haiyuan County (海原县), Zhongwei (中卫市), Ningxia, northwest China.  In 2016 he was named a National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage Inheritor (国家级非物质文化遗产传承人) for hua'er.

The song Ma sings here is a famous hua'er song entitled "Shangqu Gao Shan Wangpingchuan" 《上去高山望平川》, which originates in Qinghai province, but which is also popular in Gansu and Ningxia.

From a CCTV production entitled "CCTV Western [Chinese] Folk Songs TV Competition" (CCTV西部民歌电视大赛), which was probably filmed in Beijing, China, which aired at the time of the Chinese New Year, from January 22 to 28, 2004.

In 2005, the tradition of shan hua'er, centering on Haiyuan County and Tongxin County, Ningxia, northwest China, was included in the First Batch of the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region Autonomous Region-Level List of Intangible Cultural Heritage Masterpieces (第一批宁夏回族自治区自治区级非物质文化遗产代表作名录).

In 2006, hua'er was additionally recognized by its inclusion in the first batch of 518 traditions inscribed in the National List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of China (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

In 2009, UNESCO inscribed hua'er on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (thanks to Meng Ren for this information).

title: 西北民歌花儿  Northwestern Region, China - Folk Song Hua'er
description:
credit tianyeyinxiang@163.com
 西北民歌“花儿对唱”
花儿产生于明代初年（公元1368年前后），是流传在中国西北部甘、青、宁三省（区）的汉、回、藏、东乡、保安、撒拉、土、裕固、蒙等民族中共创共享的民歌。因歌词中把女性比喻为花朵而得名。每逢农历4月26至28（俗称四月八），“花儿会”这天。男女老少就会背上干粮齐聚在松鸣岩，漫唱"花儿"，类似其他地方的赶庙或踏青，举行一年一度的松鸣岩"花儿"盛会。他们以歌会友，一问一答，互相对唱，不拘泥于任何形式，非常自由，看似男女之间散漫的对唱情歌，实则是充满思想激情的男女，用比、兴、赋的艺术手法即兴歌颂和追求纯真的爱，鲜明表达了自己对封建礼教，社会生活各方面的态度。有比较高的文学欣赏和研究价值。
非遗传统音乐

创作人 klose credit tianyeyinxiang@163.com
Original video was posted by a friend on Wechat, it is too precious to not keep a copy.  This video shows how the folk songs are spontaneous yet sophisticated, beautiful and skillful.  

Huaer was born in the early years of the Ming Dynasty (around 1368 AD). It was spread among the Han, Hui, Tibetan, Dongxiang, Baoan, Salar, Tu, Yugu, and Mongolian provinces (regions) in northwestern region of China. It is named "Huaer" (meaning flower) because of the comparison of women to flowers in the lyrics. Every April 26 to 28 in the lunar calendar (commonly known as April 8), the "Huaerhui" day (gathering for Hua'er folk songs). Men, women, old and young will gather at Songming Rock with food and sing "Hua'er", similar to village markets or outings in other places, and hold the annual Songming Rock "Hua'er" event. They meet friends with songs, ask and answer each other, and sing duet to each others. They are not limited to any form. They are very free style. The people sing love songs loosely between men and women. The songs are full of thought and passion, improvising with the artistic techniques of antithesis, affective image, and metaphor. By praising and pursuing pure love, the singers expresses their attitude towards feudal ethics and all aspects of social life. This form of folk songs holds a relatively high literary appreciation and research value.

title: Discover Linxia,Gansu,China: The Songs of Linxia Hua’er Echo Across Africa
description:
Recently, the Huaxing Art Troupe in Nigeria performed the traditional Linxia folk song “Hua’er,” gaining widespread popularity online.
Founded in 2017 by overseas Chinese entrepreneur Ni Mengxiao, who runs a shoe factory in Lagos, the troupe is composed of young Nigerian workers from the factory.
Today, the Huaxing Art Troupe has brought together nearly 50 Chinese performers and more than 60 Nigerian performers, and has staged multiple special performances featuring “Hua’er.”

马风山，作为一名70后，他干过销售、当过村长、当过建筑工人、跑过车、种过树。花儿对他而言，不叫“唱花儿”而叫“漫花儿”。一片绿山头，足以成为漫花儿的最佳场地，漫游山间，随心起调，在这片肆意的黄土地上漫开来。

🌷🌹💐''Inheriting Classics: Ningxia Intangible Cultural Heritage'' - A Micro-documentary on the Intangible Cultural Heritage - Hua'er🌷🌹💐
🌷🌹💐“传承经典·宁夏非遗”非遗微纪录片-花儿🌷🌹💐

The Ningxia Shan Hua'er in the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity is a folk song coming from the Liupan Mountains area. Born in the life work and life scenes, it has the countryside style and reflects the true feelings of ordinary people. With features such as a repeat in every three sentences and the fact that everyone sings along once someone else sings, the folk song is not only high-pitched and bold but also smooth and beautiful, demonstrating a delicate and lyrical charm. By singing Hua'er in the wild, beautiful longings and love thoughts will spread among the blue sky, white clouds, mountains, and rivers!
联合国教科文组织人类非物质文化遗产代表作名录项目——宁夏山花儿，从六盘山地区火热的生产生活场景中走来，带着山野泥土的芳香，打老百姓的心窝里漫出，三句一叠、一唱众和，既有高亢悠长、粗旷豪放的特点，又有流畅优美、细腻抒情的韵味。扯开嗓子漫上一曲“花儿”，美好憧憬和爱情相思便在蓝天白云、山涧河畔飘散开来！

title: Huaer
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity -2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00211
Description: In Gansu and Qinghai Provinces and throughout north-central China, people of nine different ethnic groups share a music tradition known as Huaer. The music is drawn from an extensive traditional repertoire named after ethnicities, towns or flowers (Tu Peoples ''ling,'' White Peony ''ling''), and lyrics are improvised in keeping with certain rules  for example, verses have three, four, five or six lines, each made up of seven syllables. Songs may tell of young love, the hard work and weariness of the farming life, the foibles of men and women or the joy of singing. The songs are also a vivid oral record of recent social developments in China as singers comment on the changes they observe around them. Huaer singers may have little schooling, but the most successful and widely respected singers today have become household names, performing widely and even creating their own institutes to pass on their art to apprentices. Whether it is being sung spontaneously by rural people working in the field or travelling or performed more formally at one of more than a hundred traditional Huaer festivals held annually in these provinces, Huaer is an important vehicle for expressing personal feelings in a social setting and cultural exchange across ethnicities, as well as a popular rural entertainment.
Country(ies): China

title: Hua'er: Grassroots folk songs from northwest China
description:
Hua’er is a genre of Chinese folk songs shared among nine ethnic groups in Gansu and Qinghai provinces of northwest China and throughout north-central China. With a soulful and exquisite melody, a long history and rich content, Hua'er boasts of intense ethnic flavor and the rustic style typical on the plateau. It is also called “the soul of the northwest” and was placed on UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2009.

title: Hua'er opera (花儿剧) play "Mansuer" (《曼苏尔》)
description:
Hua'er opera (花儿剧) play "Mansuer" (《曼苏尔》)  performed by Wuzhong City Hui Ethnicity Arts Troupe (吴忠市回族艺术团) of Ningxia.

Hua'er opera is an opera form that is mainly popular in the regions of Ningxia's (宁夏回族自治区) Xihaigu (西海固) and Yinchuan City (银川市). The opera form is created based on the foundation of Hua'er music (花儿), which is popular in the Hui communities of Ningxia, Gansu (甘肃), Qinghai (青海) and Xinjiang (新疆), and fused with Chinese opera elements.

description:
Merry Christmas Everyone 🎉🎊
Akhirnyaaa lagu favorite saya dalam Album Pertama Helen Huang launching juga. Memang saya menunggu moment Christmas ini di karnakan shooting di Gereja Tua peninggalan Perancis yang terletak di atas gunung di Vietnam.

Kebetulan saat itu cuaca sangat dingin, MINUS 4 DERAJAT, shooting sampai tengah malam dengan angin dingin super kencang yg menusuk tulang, apalagi outfit saya backless semua 😭 sehingga 'emosi' dan 'kesedihan akibat kehilangan' nya dapet banget. Daaan ternyata besoknya hujan badai selama 3 hari berturut-turut. Syukurlah lagu ini dapat di selesaikan dengan baik. Tuhan sangat memberkati kami.

Maka itu dukunglah karya dan perjuangan kami dengan membeli album asli, bukan bajakan melalui situs di bawah ini.

那片笑聲讓我想起我的那些花兒，在我生命每個角落靜靜為我開著，
我曾以為我會永遠守在他身旁，今天我們已經離去在人海茫茫。
他們都老了吧？他們在哪裡呀？我們就這樣各自奔天涯。
啦......想她。啦...她還在開嗎？
啦......去呀！她們已經被風吹走散落在天涯。

有些故事還沒講完那就算了吧！那些心情在歲月中已經難辨真假，
如今這裡荒草叢生沒有了鮮花，好在曾經擁有你們的春秋和冬夏。
他們都老了吧？他們在哪裡呀？我們就這樣各自奔天涯。
啦......想她。啦...她還在開嗎？
啦......去呀！她們已經被風吹走散落在天涯。

Where have all the flowers gone？ Where the flowers gone？
Where have all the young girls gone？ Where did they all gone？
Where have all the young men gone？ Where the soldiers gone？
Where have all the graveyards gone？ Where have all they gone？

他們都老了吧？他們在哪裡呀？我們就這樣各自奔天涯。

title: Huaer
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity -2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00211
Description: In Gansu and Qinghai Provinces and throughout north-central China, people of nine different ethnic groups share a music tradition known as Huaer. The music is drawn from an extensive traditional repertoire named after ethnicities, towns or flowers (Tu Peoples ''ling,'' White Peony ''ling''), and lyrics are improvised in keeping with certain rules  for example, verses have three, four, five or six lines, each made up of seven syllables. Songs may tell of young love, the hard work and weariness of the farming life, the foibles of men and women or the joy of singing. The songs are also a vivid oral record of recent social developments in China as singers comment on the changes they observe around them. Huaer singers may have little schooling, but the most successful and widely respected singers today have become household names, performing widely and even creating their own institutes to pass on their art to apprentices. Whether it is being sung spontaneously by rural people working in the field or travelling or performed more formally at one of more than a hundred traditional Huaer festivals held annually in these provinces, Huaer is an important vehicle for expressing personal feelings in a social setting and cultural exchange across ethnicities, as well as a popular rural entertainment.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 Department of Culture of Gansu Province

title: Hua'er: Grassroots folk songs from northwest China
description:
Hua’er is a genre of Chinese folk songs shared among nine ethnic groups in Gansu and Qinghai provinces of northwest China and throughout north-central China. With a soulful and exquisite melody, a long history and rich content, Hua'er boasts of intense ethnic flavor and the rustic style typical on the plateau. It is also called “the soul of the northwest” and was placed on UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2009.

Born in the lively work and life scenes, it has the countryside style and reflects the true feelings of ordinary people. description:
Recently, the Huaxing Art Troupe in Nigeria performed the traditional Linxia folk song “Hua’er,” gaining widespread popularity online.
Founded in 2017 by overseas Chinese entrepreneur Ni Mengxiao, who runs a shoe factory in Lagos, the troupe is composed of young Nigerian workers from the factory.
Today, the Huaxing Art Troupe has brought together nearly 50 Chinese performers and more than 60 Nigerian performers, and has staged multiple special performances featuring “Hua’er.”

title: Explore Linxia, ​​Gansu: The Unspoiled Grandeur of Northwest China
description:
Linxia, ​​Gansu is most notable for three layers of experience: the Buddhist rock carvings of Bingling Cave-Temple Complex within the UNESCO-listed Silk Roads; a unique urban-religious space with numerous mosques and mixed Sino-Islamic architecture in Linxia City; and the Linxia UNESCO Global Geopark, where UNESCO highlights the world's oldest recorded pterosaurs' footprints and the Hua'er folk villages of various ethnic communities. This area is the center of Linxia Hui Autonomous Prefecture, connected to the Hui community and other Muslim minority groups in Gansu.

Linxia, ​​Gansu, lies in the transitional zone between China's first and second topographic terraces, with an elevation difference of nearly 3,000 meters.

Here, the Yellow River leaves the sparsely populated Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, winding through mountain ranges and hillsides toward the more densely populated Loess Plateau.

You will find the wild grandeur of Northwest China, the poetic beauty of the Qinling Mountains, and the rugged, harsh landscapes of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau.

The Ningxia Shan Hua’er in the UNESCO Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritages, the music is a unique type of folk music from Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region, with roots in the hardworking life of the people of Mount Liupan. The beautiful, exquisite melodies express heartfelt sentiments. Known as the ‘soul of China’s northwest’, Hua’er evokes a sense of romance. Its long history is a testament to its enduring popularity.

In this film, we meet Ma Yan, a young man who enjoys singing surrounded by the natural environment. His songs come from the heart and resemble poetry, he sings at the foot of the stunning Helan Mountain amongst the green hills and white clouds. ⛰️

title: Gansu intangible cultural heritage
description:
Memory of Gansu Province: Short Video Display of Intangible Cultural Heritage

Gansu, the important birthplace of the Chinese nation and Chinese civilization, embraces the old Silk Road winding for more than 1,600 kilometers. Featuring diversified natural ecology and unique geological landforms, it has seven world cultural heritages. A number of world-class natural wonders such as Danxia landform in Zhangye, the Yellow River Stone Forest and the Crescent Spring in Dunhuang have won fame both at home and abroad. It is praised by global tourists as “birthplace of Chinese civilization, a museum of natural wonders, a showplace of ethnic customs and a destination of quality tourism”.

Three of over 27,000 intangible cultural heritage projects in Gansu have been included in the UNESCO’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, and 83 of them were recognized as national representative projects. Memory of Gansu Province: Short Video Display of Intangible Cultural Heritage visualizes the cultural charm on this land, which has been inherited to this day, through the rich and colorful folk culture, such as Tibetan Eagle Flute performance, Hua’er (folk song) Party, Loong-headed Lute playing and singing, Dunhuang dance and music, Guozhuang Dance, martial art of Kongtong School, Tibetan folk songs in Gannan, Duodi Dance, national costumes, Chinese shadow puppetry, paper cutting and so on.

title: 交响丝路，如意甘肃宣传片（Harmonized Silk Road，beautiful Gansu）
description:
Discover Gansu: Where the Silk Road Sings and Nature Paints Masterpieces
Imagine a land where the echoes of the ancient Silk Road blend with earth’s most vibrant colors—this is Gansu, China’s hidden gem waiting to ignite your wanderlust. From desert wonders to cultural treasures, here’s why Gansu should top your travel bucket list:
1. A Canvas of Natural Marvels
Rainbow Mountains: Witness the surreal Zhangye Danxia Landform, a geological masterpiece where layers of red, yellow, and white rock create a "rainbow painted by Earth itself"—a UNESCO-listed wonder that stuns every viewer810.
Desert Miracles: Walk the dunes of Singing Sand Mountain and discover the oasis of Crescent Moon Lake, a shimmering jewel nestled in the Gobi Desert, defying nature’s harshness89.
2. Timeless Treasures of the Silk Road
Caves of Enlightenment: Explore the Mogao Grottoes, a 1,600-meter-long gallery of Buddhist art with 735 caves and 45,000㎡ of murals. Here, celestial "Flying Apsaras" (divine dancers) from Tang Dynasty frescoes seem to leap off the walls, embodying 1,000 years of spiritual and artistic fusion910.
Guardians of History: Stand before Jiayuguan Pass, the "First and Greatest Pass Under Heaven," where the Great Wall’s western end once guarded empires. Its towering ramparts whisper tales of Silk Road caravans and frontier warriors8.
3. Adventures That Defy Boundaries
Sky-high Thrills: Soar above Yellow River Stone Forest—a 4-million-year-old labyrinth of cliffs and pillars—by paragliding or helicopter, capturing views that rival Mars10.
Nomadic Journeys: Trek through Hexi Corridor grasslands on horseback, retracing the footsteps of Marco Polo, or camp under starry skies in the Tibetan Plateau’s eastern edge9.
4. A Feast for the Senses
Culinary Poetry: Slurp Gansu’s iconic Lanzhou Beef Noodles—a symphony of hand-pulled noodles, clear broth, and chili oil—or savor tender Roasted Lamb seasoned with cumin, a taste of nomadic heritage10.
Living Traditions: Join locals in pounding Lanzhou Taiping Drums, their thunderous rhythms echoing ancient celebrations, or lose yourself in the haunting melodies of Hua’er folk songs9.
Why Now?
Gansu is no longer the "Jade Gate" of forgotten poems. Today, it’s a land reborn—where high-speed trains connect desert and metropolis, and eco-lodges blend luxury with wilderness. As UNESCO hails its cultural legacy and global travelers rave about its raw beauty, Gansu is stepping into the spotlight910.
Ready to answer the call of the Silk Road?
Let Gansu’s deserts, mountains, and millennia of stories rewrite your idea of adventure. “A thousand degrees of beauty await—find your own in Gansu.”

Hua'er opera (花儿剧) play "Mansuer" (《曼苏尔》)  performed by Wuzhong City Hui Ethnicity Arts Troupe (吴忠市回族艺术团) of Ningxia.

Hua'er opera is an opera form that is mainly popular in the regions of Ningxia's (宁夏回族自治区) Xihaigu (西海固) and Yinchuan City (银川市). The opera form is created based on the foundation of Hua'er music (花儿), which is popular in the Hui communities of Ningxia, Gansu (甘肃), Qinghai (青海) and Xinjiang (新疆), and fused with Chinese opera elements.

title: Hua'er
description:
Shan'ge (Chinese: 山歌; pinyin: shāngē) is a genre of Chinese folk song. They are commonly sung in rural provinces; the word "Shan'ge" means "mountain song".
A number of different subtypes are:
• Hua'er, a form popular in the Northwestern Chinese provinces such as Gansu, Ningxia and Qinghai, named to the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2009
• Xintianyou and Shanqu are popular in Shaanxi and Shanxi
• Zhengjinghong, from the Anhui province
• Xingguo, from the Jiangxi province
• Hengyang from the Hunan Province
• Hakka hill song (Kejia shan'ge) from the Guangdong Province
• Lalu, a Tibetan shan'ge
• Changdiao (aradun-urtu-yin-daguu), a Mongolian shan'ge
• Feige, a Miao shan'ge

title: Inheriting Classics: Ningxia Intangible Cultural Heritage - The Intangible Cultural Heritage Hua’er
description:
The folk song of Ningxia Shan Hua’er has been included in the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. It originates from the Liupan Mountains region and is characterized by its rural style, accurately embodying the sentiments of the common people. Its unique features, including a repeating pattern in every three lines and collective singing by those present, imbue the song with both a bold and high-pitched melody as well as a smooth and elegant lyrical quality. When performed in a natural setting, the song evokes feelings of longing and love that pervade the surrounding landscapes of sky, clouds, mountains, and rivers.

title: A hua'er népdal - Útjelzők a Selyemúton
description:
#KÍNA #NÉPMŰVÉSZET #SELYEMÚT

Gansu Kína északnyugati részén fekszik, változatos a terepe, tartomány nagyobb része dombos-hegyes. A hatalmas fennsíki területen népszerű a hua'er, magyarul virágocska nevű népdalstílus. Azért így nevezték el a népdalokat, mert a fiúk az éneklés során a szeretett lányt virágocskához szokták hasonlítani. A szerelem és a mindennapi élet témáin kívül a hua'er a munkát és az aktuális eseményeket szintén megénekli, ezért fontos orális művészetnek tekintik. A hua'er már több mint 400 éves történelemmel rendelkezik. Ennek folyamán annyira népszerűvé vált, minthogy az északnyugat-kínai nép lelkiségét tükrözi. 2009-ben a szellemi világörökségek része lett. A hua'ernek származási hely alapján három alstílusa van, a hódító ereje Nyugat-Kína több tartományát érinti, különösen a hui nemzetiségek lakóterületeit. A régen falusi, hegyvidéki területen állandóan hallott hua'er ma már kevesebbszer hangzik fel, a hua'er továbbörökítése nagy kihívással néz szembe. Ám a hua'er különböző módon befolyásolja a kínai slágereket. A "Virágocska és a legény" is egy hua'er stílusú modern sláger.

title: Hua'er 花儿 from Ningxia 宁夏, northwest China
description:
A performance of hua'er (花儿), also called shan hua'er (山花儿), a genre of traditional unaccompanied vocal music from northwest China. Literally meaning "flower," the genre may have received its name from the image of a flower symbolizing one's beloved woman.

The performer, whose name is Ma Handong (马汉东, b. 1962), is a member of the Hui ethnic group (Hui zu, 回族) from Haiyuan County (海原县), Zhongwei (中卫市), Ningxia, northwest China.  In 2016 he was named a National-Level Intangible Cultural Heritage Inheritor (国家级非物质文化遗产传承人) for hua'er.

The song Ma sings here is a famous hua'er song entitled "Shangqu Gao Shan Wangpingchuan" 《上去高山望平川》, which originates in Qinghai province, but which is also popular in Gansu and Ningxia.

From a CCTV production entitled "CCTV Western [Chinese] Folk Songs TV Competition" (CCTV西部民歌电视大赛), which was probably filmed in Beijing, China, which aired at the time of the Chinese New Year, from January 22 to 28, 2004.

In 2005, the tradition of shan hua'er, centering on Haiyuan County and Tongxin County, Ningxia, northwest China, was included in the First Batch of the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region Autonomous Region-Level List of Intangible Cultural Heritage Masterpieces (第一批宁夏回族自治区自治区级非物质文化遗产代表作名录).

In 2006, hua'er was additionally recognized by its inclusion in the first batch of 518 traditions inscribed in the National List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of China (第一批国家级非物质文化遗产名录) by China's Ministry of Culture (中华人民共和国文化部, known since March 2018 as the Ministry of Culture and Tourism, 中华人民共和国文化和旅游部). The sub-agencies responsible for maintaining these lists, both under the aegis of the aforementioned ministry, are the Department of Intangible Cultural Heritage (文化部非物质文化遗产司) and the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection Center (中国非物质文化遗产保护中心, CICHPC).

In 2009, UNESCO inscribed hua'er on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (thanks to Meng Ren for this information).

title: Trailer of " Huaer and waist sword"
description:
This is one of my documentary works - "Huaer and waist sword". The film records the security waist sword, one of China's famous swords. There is a nationality in Gansu, China. They thrive on their swords. They are the Baoan nationality, a unique famous nationality in Gansu. Therefore, the swords they make are also called Baoan waist swords. Baoan people have a history of smelting steel for more than 800 years, and Baoan waist sword has been born for more than 100 years.

In the film, the production process and technology of security waist sword are introduced in detail. The whole production process of waist sword is very complex, with more than 80 procedures, so it can be said that each waist sword is a unique art. Security waist sword has nearly 20 different series of products, among which the most representative is "flower folding sword". This kind of sword is famous for its complex process, hardness and softness, strange texture and sharp edge. This craft was lost in the 1950s and was not recovered until 2006 under the painstaking research of the fourth generation waist sword inheritors. In this century of inheritance, each generation of inheritors inherits the indomitable and excellence waist sword spirit, which is also the characteristic of the whole Baoan family. Therefore, it can be said that security waist sword and waist sword culture complement each other and achieve each other.

Western China continues to suffer from powerful earthquakes. Reports say the magnitude 6-point-6 quake struck Gansu province yesterday morning. State media says at least eighty people have been killed, as more casualties were still being counted. The quake struck a mountainous rural area, and triggered landslides. That's because of the difficulty in reaching communities that may initially be cut off from rock slides.

title: 甘肃的记忆——非遗短视频展播Memory of Gansu Province: Short Video Display of Intangible Cultural Heritage
description:
甘肃是中华民族和华夏文明的重要发祥地,古老的丝绸之路在甘肃境内蜿蜒1600余公里。甘肃自然生态多元,地质地貌独特，现有世界文化遗产7处，张掖丹霞、黄河石林、敦煌月牙泉等一批世界级的自然奇观驰名中外，被全球旅游者誉为“华夏文明的发源地、自然奇观的博物馆、民族风情的大观园、品质旅游的目的地”。
Gansu, the important birthplace of the Chinese nation and Chinese civilization, embraces the old Silk Road winding for more than 1,600 kilometers. Featuring diversified natural ecology and unique geological landforms, it has seven world cultural heritages. A number of world-class natural wonders such as Danxia landform in Zhangye, the Yellow River Stone Forest and the Crescent Spring in Dunhuang have won fame both at home and abroad. It is praised by global tourists as “birthplace of Chinese civilization, a museum of natural wonders, a showplace of ethnic customs and a destination of quality tourism”.

甘肃有2.7万余个非遗项目中，3项入选联合国教科文组织代表作名录，83项被国家公布为代表性项目。《甘肃的记忆—非遗短视频展播》通过藏鹰笛演奏技艺、花儿会、龙头琴弹唱、敦煌舞乐、锅庄舞、崆峒武术、甘南藏族民歌、多地舞、民族服饰、皮影戏、剪纸等丰富多彩的民俗文化，展现出这片土地传承至今的文化魅力。
Three of over 27,000 intangible cultural heritage projects in Gansu have been included in the UNESCO’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, and 83 of them were recognized as national representative projects. Memory of Gansu Province: Short Video Display of Intangible Cultural Heritage visualizes the cultural charm on this land, which has been inherited to this day, through the rich and colorful folk culture, such as Tibetan Eagle Flute performance, Hua’er (folk song) Party, Loong-headed Lute playing and singing, Dunhuang dance and music, Guozhuang Dance, martial art of Kongtong School, Tibetan folk songs in Gannan, Duodi Dance, national costumes, Chinese shadow puppetry, paper cutting and so on.

title: Farmers dance of Chinas Korean ethnic group
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00213
Description: Gathering in fields or villages during community festivals, members of the Korean ethnic group in Jilin and other provinces in north-eastern China offer a traditional sacrifice to the God of the Land to pay homage to nature and pray for good fortune and a plentiful harvest. This is the beginning of the farmers dance of Chinas Korean ethnic group, a popular folk practice passed on by senior members of a community to younger generations. Musicians play oboe-like ''suona,'' bell-shaped gongs and a variety of drums, while masked or unmasked dancers move farcically to the accompaniment. The dance is inspired by the motions of farming, which it imitates through gestures such as walking the field ridges. Spreading from its agricultural origins to Korean people of all walks of life in both urban and rural areas, the dance has evolved considerably since it was brought to China at the end of the nineteenth century. For example, the musical ensemble has been expanded to include wind instruments and the dancers costumes have been influenced by the clothes of other Chinese ethnic groups. As the product of accumulated labour and wisdom, the farmers dance remains an important expression of the cultural heritage of Chinas Korean ethnic group. 
Country(ies): China

title: Farmers' dance of China's Korean ethnic group
description:
The Farmer's Dance is a representative dance favored by the Korean ethnic group in northeast China and is especially prevalent in the Korean congregating area of Jilin, Heilongjiang and Liaoning provinces. It is characterized by "beating tambourines" and "swinging elephant caps". The dance is now performed mostly at special festivals and folk art competitions. It was inscribed on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity by the UNESCO in 2009.

title: Farmers' dance of China’s Korean ethnic group (Chosŏnjok nongak-mu)
description:
Pungmul (Korean: 풍물; Hanja: 風物; IPA: [pʰuːŋmul]) is a Korean folk music tradition that includes drumming, dancing, and singing. Most performances are outside, with dozens of players all in constant motion. Pungmul is rooted in the dure (collective labor) farming culture. It was originally played as part of farm work, on rural holidays, at other village community-building events, and to accompany shamanistic rituals, mask dance dramas, and other types of performance. During the late 1960s and 1970s it expanded in meaning and was actively used in political protest during the pro-democracy movement, although today it is most often seen as a performing art.
Older scholars often describe this tradition as nongak (Korean: [noŋak]), a term meaning "farmers' music" whose usage arose during the colonial era (1910–1945). The Cultural Heritage Administration of South Korea uses this term in designating the folk tradition as an Important Intangible Cultural Property. Opposition from performers and scholars toward its usage grew in the 1980s because colonial authorities attempted to limit the activity to farmers in order to suppress its use and meaning among the colonized. It is also known by many synonymous names throughout the peninsula.
Drumming is the central element of pungmul. Each group is led by a kkwaenggwari (RR- ggwaenggwari) (small handheld gong) player, and includes at least one person playing janggu (hourglass drum), buk (barrel drum), and jing (gong). Wind instruments (taepyeongso, also known as hojeok, senap, or nalari) sometimes play along with the drummers.
Following the drummers are dancers, who often play the sogo (a small drum without enough resonance to contribute to the soundscape significantly) and tend to have more elaborate—even acrobatic—choreography, particularly if the sogo-wielding dancers also manipulate the sangmo ribbon-hats. In some regional pungmul types, japsaek (actors) dressed as caricatures of traditional village roles wander around to engage spectators, blurring the boundary between performers and audience. Minyo (folksongs) and chants are sometimes included in pungmul, and audience members enthusiastically sing and dance along. Most minyo are set to drum beats in one of a few jangdan (rhythmic patterns) that are common to pungmul, sanjo, p'ansori (RR-pansori), and other traditional Korean musical genres.
Pungmul performers wear a variety of colorful costumes. A flowery version of the Buddhist gokkal is the most common head-dress. In an advanced troupe all performers may wear sangmo, which are hats with long ribbon attached to them that players can spin and flip in intricate patterns powered by knee bends.
Pungmul was added to the UNESCO intangible cultural heritage list as "Joseonjok Nongak" by China in 2009 and South Korea in November 2014.

title: Nongak (Korean Traditional Farmers Dance)
description: One of the oldest dance forms in Korea. Originating in the Three Kingdoms period (57 B.C.E. - 668 C.E.), the Farmer's Dance was traditionally performed during planting, harvesting, and other agricultural events. Early records mention Korean farmers working to the beat of percussion instruments. Until recently, the dance was performed in the rural areas, in particular during the planting and harvesting, to give encouragement to the farmers and to provide them with a beat to work by. It was also often performed as a part of shamanistic rituals to protect houses from thieves and fire, to purify the village, and to give thanks to the river or mountain spirits.

description:
Chinese classical dance need not require specifically training individual muscles instead of human body's natural ability, in which the muscle strength gained naturally through walking, running, jumping, etc., as well as the upper-body strength naturally built through physical labor.  Classical Chinese dance has also had a tremendous influence on athletics.  Before the 1970s, training in rhythmic gymnastics, the balance beam, and bars involved only simple ballet movements and physical training.  Then, China's gymnastics teams took classical Chinese dance techniques and flips, and used them on a large Scale in gymnastics events.  As soon as these techniques appeared in international competitions of the 1970s, gymnasts worldwide were astonished There were Chinese contestants who at one point managed to get five all-around medals Gymnasts around the world started learning the techniques and flips of classical Chinese dance and, overnight  gymnastics competition was pushed to a higherlevel of technical skill.
  In fact, many different dance forms, physical arts, and sports around the world have adopted the techniques and fips of classical Chinese dance.  Some copy themalittle better, while others are quite subpar, inaccurate, nonstandard, and even unsightly, and this has distorted the exquisite culture of five thousand years.

Alien registration cards issued to ethnic Koreans from China and Korean-Chinese will now show both their English and Korean names.

#korean #music #festival 
Art Introduction: K-POP_Korean traditional music 
Nongak(Korean Farmers' Music and Dance)is one of the popular performing arts derived from a sense of community and entertainment in rural society. Depending on the village and village, there are differences in the composition, style of performance, rhythm, and costume of the Nongak band. As shown by the fact that most Nongak bands use their hometowns or villages as they are, Nongak was highly regarded for maintaining a strong connection with the village community and having the same identity as members of the community, and was listed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2014. Even to this day, each village is maintained in a unique form.

title: The 5 Styles of Chinese Calligraphy Explained
description:
Have you ever been mesmerized by the elegant strokes of Chinese calligraphy but didn't know where to start? This video is your perfect introduction to one of the world's most ancient and beautiful art forms! You don’t need to know a single word of Chinese to appreciate the artistry, history, and emotion captured in each brushstroke.

Join us as we explore the five core styles of Chinese calligraphy, also known as “Wu Ti” (五體, wǔ tǐ). We’ll journey through their evolution, starting with the ancient, picture-like Seal Script (篆書, zhuànshū), moving to the wave-like rhythm of Clerical Script (隸書, lìshū), and then exploring the three modern siblings: the orderly Standard Script (楷書, kǎishū) you see in textbooks, the fluid Running Script (行書, xíngshū) used in daily handwriting, and the explosive, emotional Cursive Script (草書, cǎoshū). We use simple analogies, like comparing the styles to standing, walking, and running, to make them easy to understand and remember.

By the end of this video, you'll be able to distinguish between the major scripts and gain a deeper appreciation for the art you see in museums, books, and even on official seals.

00:00 - Start
00:55 - Style 1
02:18 - Style 2
04:15 - Style 3
06:08 - Style 4
07:00 - Style 5
08:39 - Ending

title: Living Heritage: Chinese calligraphy
description:
Chinese calligraphy is an artistic practice of writing Chinese characters, often with a brush and ink on "xuan" paper. The evolution of Chinese #calligraphy began alongside the earliest Chinese characters discovered to date - inscriptions on oracle bones from the Shang Dynasty (c.16th century-11th century BC) in Anyang, Henan province. Over time, calligraphy gradually took shape as a form of art rather than a mere means of record. The five major styles of script, running, cursive, official, seal and regular, were born from such calligraphy.

Calligraphy is a demanding and advanced #art. The type of brush, density of ink and texture of paper can all alter the output. From brush inclination and direction to speed of writing, every twist and turn of the wrist is also calculated. Structure of individual characters and spatial layout as a whole determine its quality. Moreover, it is said that the emotions and philosophy of the writer are directly reflected on calligraphy.

Calligraphy is refined art. "Lan Ting Xu" (The Orchid Pavilion Preface), created by Wang Xizhi during the Eastern Jin Dynasty (317-420), is one of the most celebrated masterpieces of Chinese calligraphy. Its elegance and expressive brushwork bestowed it both historical and cultural significance in Chinese literature. Calligraphy is also within reach. Like the spring festival couplets that adorn doors of everyday folks, calligraphy has always been the embodiment of aesthetic appreciation for the Chinese.

Where there is Chinese language, there is #Chinesecalligraphy, and where there is calligraphy, there is beauty. The artistry is still highly valued today, for it is more than just writing, it is a living heritage.

title: Classical Chinese poem | Spring Dawn / Mèng Hào rán | Chinese Calligraphy
description:
Meng Haoran (Chinese: 孟浩然; Wade–Giles: Meng Hao-jan; 689/691–740) was a major Tang dynasty poet, and a somewhat older contemporary of Wang Wei, Li Bai and Du Fu. Despite his brief pursuit of an official career, Meng Haoran mainly lived in and wrote about the area in which he was born and raised, in what is now Hubei province, China. Meng Haoran was a major influence on other contemporary and subsequent poets of the High Tang era because of his focus on nature as a main topic for poetry. Meng Haoran was also prominently featured in the Qing dynasty (and subsequently frequently republished) poetry anthology Three Hundred Tang Poems, having the fifth largest number of his poems included, for a total of fifteen, exceeded only by Du Fu, Li Bai, Wang Wei, and Li Shangyin. These poems of Meng Haoran were available in the English translations by Witter Bynner and Kiang Kanghu, by 1920, with the publication of The Jade Mountain. The Three Hundred Tang Poems also has two poems by Li Bai addressed to Meng Haoran, one in his praise and one written in farewell on the occasion of their parting company. Meng Haoran was also influential to Japanese poetry.

from Wikipedia
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meng_Haoran

春曉 孟浩然
Spring Dawn / Mèng Hào rán

春眠不覺曉
The sleep of spring night is so comfortable that I do not notice that the dawns

處處聞啼鳥
When I wake up, I hear the birds singing from everywhere

夜來風雨聲
Last night I heard the sound of wind and rain

花落知多少
How many flowers have fallen? (Maybe many flowers have fallen)

title: Grinding Ink and Chinese Calligraphy - ASMR Sleep Aid
description:
Relax as I grind some ink and practice some Chinese Calligraphy.
I recently acquired a stick of Chinese stick ink and a small ink stone in which to grind it. This is my first attempt using it as well as my first attempt at Chinese calligraphy.

In China, calligraphy is referred to as Shūfǎ which literally means the way, or method of writing. I certainly found the whole process of grinding the ink and then brushing it onto some paper, to be extremely relaxing. There is an order that the strokes are to made in and as a beginner, I have no idea what I'm doing. In the video, I am not actually writing, but rather drawing the symbols, attempting to make them look like the ones from a printed sheet just out of the camera's view. If I do a series of these videos perhaps I'll learn along the way and be able to memorize not only the symbols themselves, but also the order of the brush strokes used to create each. Nevertheless, I found this to be very relaxing and I hope you experience just a fraction of what I felt when you watch this video.

Congratulations! In reading the description, you found the secret bonus video!
https://youtu.be/1UV4VLdne4M

My videos are best enjoyed using headphones or earbuds.

The videos on my YouTube channel portray some of the typical day-to-day activities that I do for relaxation. This video was produced with relaxation in mind and hopefully has some ASMR qualities. Too much stress is harmful to your well-being and in today’s fast-paced world, stress management is more important than ever, and so I hope this ASMR video acts as a stress reliever for those watching and a sleep-aid for anyone having a difficult time falling asleep.

title: The art of Chinese seal engraving
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00217
Description: The art of seal engraving is a cornerstone of Chinese fine arts. The seal was originally used as a signature or sign of authority, but it came to be used by all social classes and in much of Asia. The Seal Engravers Society of Xiling in Zhejiang Province, central China, which was founded a century ago, preserves the art of seal engraving along with approximately a hundred other specialized institutions. The design is first sketched on paper, and then engraved on stone, in reverse, with a knife. In addition to mastery of traditional calligraphy, the art of engraving requires a high degree of virtuosity, since the artist works on a tiny surface area where every curve, every thickness of line counts. The very diverse motifs are the fruit of the artists imagination and culture. As an instrument of calligraphy and painting, the seal is a work of art in itself. It expresses an entire cultures ideas about humankind and nature. Today, seals continue to be used in official documents and private correspondence. Even though those understanding the complex characters are ever fewer, the art of seal engraving is still practised by both professionals and amateurs.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 骆芃芃 (LUO Peng Peng)

title: Chinese Seal Engraving
description:
As a cornerstone of Chinese fine arts, Chinese seal engraving manifests the beauty of Chinese characters to the utmost.    
        From the ancient imperial seals made of jade, to the more common seals used by all, history unfolds itself through these intricate curves and lines.
        The art of engraving requires mastery of calligraphy and a high degree of virtuosity.
        The imperial jade seals were used by the Chinese emperors as a sign of power and authority.
        Over 2000 years ago, China’s first imperial seal was created for China’s first emperor Qin Shi Huang. This Heirloom Seal of the Realm was carved out of Heshibi, a sacred piece of jade. 
        Later, the seals become more widely used in lieu of signatures and as artistic collections. 
        Today, Chinese seal engraving still enjoys great popularity.
        It helps improve one’s concentration and observation skills, also enabling the development of aesthetic taste.
        Consisting of the Chinese character “Jing” which also portrays a human figure running forward and embracing triumph, the emblem of the 2008 Beijing Olympic Games was also inspired by this art. 
        Chinese seal engraving is a combination of craftsman spirit and classical Chinese aesthetics, endowed with the wisdom of ancient Chinese people and the value of Chinese culture.
        Producer  Yu Lan
        Executive Producer  Wu Qingcai  Qi Bin
        Coordinator  Wang Kai  Zeng Nai

title: Immersive Oriental Heritage Craft: Engraving a Poetic "Joy of Fish" Leisure #seal #stonecarving #fyp
description:
Today, we invite you on a deeply immersive journey into the ancient art of Chinese seal carving as we create a poetic leisure seal inscribed with "鱼之乐" (Yú zhī lè), meaning "The Joy of Fish" ✨

This phrase, inspired by the carefree happiness of fish swimming in a pond, evokes a sense of peace and contentment. We begin by thoughtfully arranging the characters on a piece of beautiful green soapstone. With slow, deliberate, and precise knife strokes, we carve each character, focusing on the meditative rhythm of the process. The fine stone shavings and the gentle sound of the chisel create a soothing ASMR experience that helps relieve stress and calm the mind.

This hand-carved "Joy of Fish" seal is a perfect blend of art and philosophy. It’s ideal for stamping on calligraphy, paintings, journals, or personal letters, adding a touch of traditional Chinese culture and poetic charm.

What poetic phrase or idiom would you like to see carved into a leisure seal next? Share your thoughts in the comments! title: Immersive seal carving practice #asmr | Calming beginner oriental craft#stamp #stonecarving #seal
description:
Today we share a fully immersive relaxing session of seal carving practice, perfect for beginners learning the ancient Chinese intangible heritage craft of seal engraving ✨
We layout regular symmetrical seal script lines on smooth stone, then practice steady knife control to carve neat uniform strokes. Every slow, precise cut releases fine soft stone shavings, creating deeply hypnotic visual ASMR to ease anxiety and clear your mind.
This practice seal focuses on mastering even line thickness, balanced spacing and stable carving pressure — the core foundational skills for carving name seals, poetic leisure seals and portrait stamps later. Seal carving is a quiet meditative hobby that combines calligraphy, concentration and traditional oriental aesthetics.
Are you a new seal carving learner? Drop your biggest carving struggles in the comments! title: Satisfying #asmr #Seal Carving in Huang Mufu Style – The Square Seal "Qiao Shi"#stamp #sealcarving
description:
Today, we delve into the world of traditional Chinese seal carving by crafting a square seal inscribed with "Qiao Shi" (乔氏), meticulously executed in the iconic style of the late Qing Dynasty master, Huang Mufu (黄牧甫) ✨

Renowned for its bold, straight lines and clean, structured composition, the Huang Mufu style is a cornerstone of modern seal art. We begin by carefully arranging the characters on the stone, ensuring balanced spacing and a strong, geometric foundation. With precise and steady knife strokes, we carve deep, crisp lines, creating a striking contrast between the red characters and the white background. The process is deeply meditative, with the fine stone shavings and rhythmic carving motions offering a truly immersive ASMR experience.

This hand-carved "Qiao Shi" seal is not just a piece of art; it is a functional work of heritage, perfect for personal use, collection, or as a thoughtful, high-end gift. It embodies the timeless elegance and technical precision of the Huang Mufu school.

What name or phrase would you like to see carved in this distinctive style? Share your thoughts in the comments! Subscribe for more masterful seal carving sessions.##SealCarving #QiaoShi #ChineseSealArt #ASMRCarving #TraditionalChineseArt #IntangibleCulturalHeritage #StoneCarving #CustomSeal #OrientalCraftsmanship#seals#seallover

title: The art of Chinese seal engraving
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00217
Description: The art of seal engraving is a cornerstone of Chinese fine arts. The seal was originally used as a signature or sign of authority, but it came to be used by all social classes and in much of Asia. The Seal Engravers Society of Xiling in Zhejiang Province, central China, which was founded a century ago, preserves the art of seal engraving along with approximately a hundred other specialized institutions. The design is first sketched on paper, and then engraved on stone, in reverse, with a knife. In addition to mastery of traditional calligraphy, the art of engraving requires a high degree of virtuosity, since the artist works on a tiny surface area where every curve, every thickness of line counts. The very diverse motifs are the fruit of the artists imagination and culture. As an instrument of calligraphy and painting, the seal is a work of art in itself. It expresses an entire cultures ideas about humankind and nature. Today, seals continue to be used in official documents and private correspondence. Even though those understanding the complex characters are ever fewer, the art of seal engraving is still practised by both professionals and amateurs.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 骆芃芃 (LUO Peng Peng)

title: In the Hands of Japan’s Master Carvers — Wood, Lacquer, and Seal Engraving
description:
Japan is home to craft traditions that have been refined over generations. In this film, we visit three artisans whose work shows different approaches to carving, engraving, and working by hand.

◼️ Yutaka Mogami | Edo Sashimono — Tokyo
A third-generation Edo Sashimono craftsman, Yutaka Mogami works in a traditional style of Japanese woodworking known for its precise joinery, assembled without nails or adhesives. From his Tokyo workshop, he creates furniture and everyday objects with remarkable accuracy and restraint.

▼hands-on experiences
Woodworking Your Own Chopsticks at Mogami Kogei
 https://m.wabunka-lux.jp/4fUwLKt

◼️ Kyukei Goto | Kamakura-bori — Kamakura
Kyukei Goto is a Kamakura-bori artisan and the 29th-generation descendant of the Buddhist sculptor Unkei. His work combines deep wood carving with layers of urushi lacquer, a style closely associated with Kamakura’s temple culture and carving history. He works from a gallery studio near Tsurugaoka Hachimangu Shrine.

▼hands-on experiences
Japanese Lacquer-finished Kamakura-bori Wood Carving under Kyukei Goto
https://m.wabunka-lux.jp/4qcWWjr

◼️ Mitsuhiro & Chieko Tsukino | Hanko Seal Engraving — Kamakura
Mitsuhiro Tsukino is the third-generation owner of Kamakura Hanko, a specialty seal-making studio founded over 70 years ago. Together with his wife Chieko, both nationally certified seal engravers, they create hand-carved personal seals for individuals, temples, and shrines across Japan. Their work was also recognized with the top award at the Nippon Omiyage Award in 2022.

▼hands-on experiences
・Create a Personal Seal, Hand-finished by an Artisan at a Longstanding Kamakura Shop [ Basic Plan ]
https://m.wabunka-lux.jp/49MZsIw
・Create a Top Quality Mikkoku Seal at a Longstanding Specialty Shop in Kamakura [ Premium Plan ]
https://m.wabunka-lux.jp/4rsS23c

Wabunka works with artisans and local communities across Japan to help preserve cultural traditions. Through private cultural experiences and stays, travelers can meet artisans, learn directly from them, and gain deeper insight into Japanese culture and craftsmanship.

description:
Today we share a fully immersive relaxing session of seal carving practice, perfect for beginners learning the ancient Chinese intangible heritage craft of seal engraving ✨
We layout regular symmetrical seal script lines on smooth stone, then practice steady knife control to carve neat uniform strokes. Every slow, precise cut releases fine soft stone shavings, creating deeply hypnotic visual ASMR to ease anxiety and clear your mind.
This practice seal focuses on mastering even line thickness, balanced spacing and stable carving pressure — the core foundational skills for carving name seals, poetic leisure seals and portrait stamps later. Seal carving is a quiet meditative hobby that combines calligraphy, concentration and traditional oriental aesthetics.

title: Immersive Oriental Heritage Craft: Engraving a Poetic "Joy of Fish" Leisure #seal #stonecarving #fyp
description:
Today, we invite you on a deeply immersive journey into the ancient art of Chinese seal carving as we create a poetic leisure seal inscribed with "鱼之乐" (Yú zhī lè), meaning "The Joy of Fish" ✨

This phrase, inspired by the carefree happiness of fish swimming in a pond, evokes a sense of peace and contentment. We begin by thoughtfully arranging the characters on a piece of beautiful green soapstone. With slow, deliberate, and precise knife strokes, we carve each character, focusing on the meditative rhythm of the process. The fine stone shavings and the gentle sound of the chisel create a soothing ASMR experience that helps relieve stress and calm the mind.

This hand-carved "Joy of Fish" seal is a perfect blend of art and philosophy. It’s ideal for stamping on calligraphy, paintings, journals, or personal letters, adding a touch of traditional Chinese culture and poetic charm.

What poetic phrase or idiom would you like to see carved into a leisure seal next? Share your thoughts in the comments! title: Living Heritage: Paper-cutting
description:
A pair of scissors, a piece of paper, and a pair of dexterous hands. That's all that's needed to create delicate works of paper-cuttings.

Originating in the Southern and Northern Dynasties (420-581), this ancient form of art has decorated windows across #China to celebrate the #SpringFestival for generations. Patterns of whichever of the 12 Chinese zodiac animals the new year ushers forth, as well as festive scenes of family reunions and temple fairs, are still lighting up Chinese homes today.

Often created without any reference to a drawn draft, various patterns and images, such as animals and flowers, and mythological and historical figures, each with their own connotations, are cut and carved with scissors and burins.

This is the unrivaled skill of #papercutting, an art form closely related to the nation's original culture and religious beliefs, such as reproductive worship, praying for blessings and warding off disasters. It contains deep cultural value, as well as traditional Chinese philosophy and morals.

title: How To: Chinese Paper-Cutting | Lunar New Year with the Walters Art Museum
description:
This video was created for our 2021 Lunar New Year celebration and is now part of our Lunar New Year video archive. Celebrate Lunar New Year 2022 with the Walters Art Museum by enjoying the videos on this Lunar New Year playlist (https://bit.ly/3tulhEO) and the resources and activities available on our website (http://bit.ly/lnywalters).

Celebrate the Lunar New Year with artist Cheng Cao who will introduce you to Chinese paper-cutting, or jianzhi (剪紙), a folk art that originated in China around the 6th century CE. Learn how to craft a paper-cut ox. Materials needed: red paper, pencil, eraser, scissors. This video is made possible by the Towson Asian Art and Culture Center.

Cheng Cao is a Chinese artist working and living in Baltimore. Where some artists specialize in one genre of traditional Chinese painting, Cao has mastered many genres, including landscape, flower and bird, animals and portraiture. While concentrating on Chinese tradition, Cao always wants to combine modern elements in his art. Cao teaches Chinese painting at his studio in downtown Baltimore. Cao hopes his art lessons can lead the students to observe a deeper view of Chinese culture and art.

IMAGE DESCRIPTIONS
(0:29-0:34) Paper cut out of red paper featuring central Chinese character, lotus flower, lotus seed pods, and koi fish.

(0:42-0:47) Paper cut out of red paper featuring big central lotus flower with lotus bud on left            and lotus seed pod on right.

title: Simple Origami & Paper Cutting  - DIY Chinese New Year Decorations 🏮 - ‘春’ 剪纸，窗花步骤 - 过年啦， 剪个窗花吧
description:
Simple Origami &  Paper Cutting Designs - DIY Chinese New Year Decorations‘ 春’ 窗花剪纸步骤 - 过年手工

New Papercutting Video - Love Hearts ❤️ - https://youtu.be/1uXRCub1TjI

At New Year it is very popular in China to make paper-cuttings to decorate the home, and spread some festive cheer and positivity. The designs are usually displayed in the windows of the family home, or shop fronts to wish passers-by a happy new year, and to wish everyone good fortune for the year ahead. 

This video shows some basic designs for anyone to have a go at paper-cutting. I intend to make videos showing more complicated designs in the future, but this video is just to let you see how easy it is to get started.

Happy new year, hope you have a great Year of the Pig!

title: What Tools Do You Use For Jianzhi? - Art Across Cultures
description:
What Tools Do You Use For Jianzhi? In this informative video, we’ll take a closer look at the tools used in the fascinating art of Jianzhi, a traditional Chinese paper cutting technique. We’ll explore the essential materials needed to create stunning designs, starting with the types of paper that artists prefer. You’ll learn about the different cutting tools that can help achieve precise and intricate patterns, including specialized scissors and artwork knives. 

We’ll also discuss important accessories, such as cutting mats, that protect your workspace while ensuring your tools remain effective. Techniques like symmetrical cutting, single-sheet cutting, and layered cutting will be highlighted, showcasing the various methods artists employ to create their masterpieces. 

Additionally, we’ll touch on how modern technology is influencing Jianzhi through digital design tools, allowing artists to blend traditional practices with contemporary innovations. This art form is accessible to many, making it a wonderful way to engage with cultural traditions from around the world. 

Join us as we celebrate the beauty of Jianzhi and inspire you to gather your materials and start creating your own paper cuttings! Don’t forget to subscribe to our channel for more engaging content about art from different cultures.

If you want to choose a fine supplier, there are some items you need to pay attention to.
First, Zinc layer thickness, whether the coating is uniform. And the thickness of the inner wall.
Second, Inside design, whether has a special design to protect the inner wall.
Third, compare the length and angle of the threaded.
Then, the weight of the malleable iron pipe fittings.

title: Living Heritage: Paper-cutting
description:
A pair of scissors, a piece of paper, and a pair of dexterous hands. That's all that's needed to create delicate works of paper-cuttings.

Originating in the Southern and Northern Dynasties (420-581), this ancient form of art has decorated windows across #China to celebrate the #SpringFestival for generations. Patterns of whichever of the 12 Chinese zodiac animals the new year ushers forth, as well as festive scenes of family reunions and temple fairs, are still lighting up Chinese homes today.

Often created without any reference to a drawn draft, various patterns and images, such as animals and flowers, and mythological and historical figures, each with their own connotations, are cut and carved with scissors and burins.

This is the unrivaled skill of #papercutting, an art form closely related to the nation's original culture and religious beliefs, such as reproductive worship, praying for blessings and warding off disasters. It contains deep cultural value, as well as traditional Chinese philosophy and morals.

title: Jianzhi Brand Story
description:
Built to Make the World Safer

Either Do Your Best or Take a Rest.

Focused, dedicated, and specialized. High-quality products and a good reputation lay the foundation for an enterprise to grow and prosper. Jianzhi is dedicated to providing premium pipe connectors for customers all over the world.

—Chief Executive Officer, Jiuhong Ren

Focused, dedicated, and specialized. And continuously work in the professional field with a craftsman's spirit.
My name is Ren Jiuhong. I am the Chief Executive Officer of Hebei Jianzhi Casting Group Co., Ltd.
In 1982, I went into business and contracted a village run foundry and rented a production site. We could only produce one or two kinds of malleable iron pipe fittings at that time.
Relying on the new policy of the reform and opening-up, our small factory has excellent benefits.
In 1991, I founded Jianzhi Casting Group. At that time, we built the target of our factory. Better Quality is our forever target; reputation is our factory life. Implement total quality management and development in competition. Over the next 20 years, the construction support has been very stable. We cooperate with colleges and universities to cultivate technical talents and reward employees for innovation.
The Jianzhi pipe fittings have increased from several to more than 5000.
In 1998, the state issued regulations that drinking water pipelines could not use steel and zinc, which impacted the malleable iron fittings industry. From 1998 to 2002, half of the malleable iron and steel enterprises in China stopped production. Someone advised me not to work in this industry, and I can't leave the employees who have strived with me for so many years. That time was when we were most stressed. We visited and studied Bazhou, Shandong, Shanxi and other places to look for a market and a way out. At the same time, we also establish an enterprise product testing centre and technology R&D centre with relevant national departments.
Our company successfully reformed in 2000 and entered a period of rapid development.
2021 is the 100th anniversary of the founding of the Communist Party of China. As a leader of private enterprises growing with the new reform and opening-up policy. I will always keep in mind the original mission of a Communist Party of China with a modest attitude. "small pipe fittings" connect the "big world", we will try to create more value for society.

title: Unlock the Magic of Chinese Paper Cutting Jianzhi Art for Indian Art Lovers! 🇨🇳✂️🇮🇳
description:
"Namaste! Dive into the enchanting world of Chinese paper cutting, known as Jianzhi – an ancient art that's like our Indian rangoli or mehendi but with scissors and stories etched in paper. From its origins in the 2nd century CE with Cai Lun's paper invention to UNESCO recognition in 2009, this video explores techniques (scissors vs. knife cutting), regional styles (bold Northern, delicate Southern, vibrant Western), and its role in festivals for luck and prosperity.
I chat with Master Sue in Chengdu, a 30+ year expert whose 5-meter masterpiece has toured the world! Learn how it's empowering women, tying into UN Sustainable Development Goals, and getting government support through workshops and scholarships. Perfect for Indian art lovers – compare it to our embroidery or torans!

title: An overview of Chinese Timber structures
description:
中国木结构; 

Chinese timber structures, with a history spanning thousands of years, are renowned for their intricate design, sophisticated joinery, and symbolic architecture. These structures are characterized by:

1. Post and Lintel Construction

Chinese timber buildings rely on a post-and-lintel framework, where vertical posts support horizontal beams. This structure creates flexible, yet stable buildings that can resist earthquakes and environmental stresses.

2. Complex Joinery

Timber components are connected using interlocking joints, often without nails or metal fasteners. Mortise-and-tenon joints, dovetail joints, and brackets (dougong) are used to transfer loads efficiently while allowing for some movement, which makes these structures particularly earthquake-resistant.

3. Modular Design

Traditional Chinese timber structures follow a modular system, where buildings are composed of standardized components that can be easily replaced or expanded. This modularity allows for flexibility in design and easier repairs over time.

4. Decorative Elements

Roofs, beams, and columns are often elaborately decorated. The roof is especially important in Chinese architecture, with its overhanging eaves, curved lines, and use of glazed tiles. The dougong bracket system is both a structural and ornamental feature, serving as a transition between columns and roofs.

5. Symbolism

Many Chinese timber buildings were constructed with symbolic intent. Temples, palaces, and pavilions were often aligned according to principles of feng shui, reflecting balance and harmony between the built environment and nature. Colors, motifs, and roof styles all had cultural and political significance.

6. Sustainability and Longevity

The use of timber in these buildings promotes sustainability, as wood is a renewable resource. With proper maintenance, these structures can last for centuries, as seen in famous historical sites like the Forbidden City in Beijing and ancient temples.

Chinese timber architecture remains an important cultural heritage and continues to inspire modern sustainable building practices.

title: Traditional Chinese Timber Joinery (Mortise and Tenon) Construction
description: Craftsmanship is the soul of great architecture. In this video, a worker is assembling intricately carved wooden components of a traditional Chinese building. The magic lies in mortise and tenon joints—these are interlocking connections where one piece has a projection (tenon) that fits perfectly into a recess (mortise) of another piece, with no nails needed. As these pieces are joined, they create a sturdy and elegant structure that can withstand the test of time and even earthquakes, thanks to the flexibility and strength inherent in the joint design. This is vital because it preserves age-old Chinese building techniques, showing how ancient craftsmen used simple yet brilliant ideas to build durable structures without modern hardware. It’s awe-inspiring to see how this traditional method, relying on precise woodworking and natural interlocking, continues to bring historic architectural styles to life today. Ultimately, it reminds us that the wisdom of past artisans holds timeless value in creating beautiful, long-lasting buildings.

title: Traditional Chinese Douchuan Timber Frame Installation | Ancient Woodworking Technique
description: “Crafts preserve culture, and this video showcases the timeless art of Douchuan timber frame installation. In the video, artisans are assembling a traditional Chinese wooden structure using the Douchuan technique. This method involves connecting wooden columns with cross beams (chuanfang) and relying on mortise-and-tenon joints—no nails or screws are used. The artisans carefully fit each wooden piece, using tools like hammers to secure the joints. Through this process, they create a sturdy and durable structure that has been a cornerstone of Chinese architecture for centuries. The result is a building framework that can endure time and weather, all thanks to the precision of the joinery and the natural strength of the wood. This is important because it highlights an ancient building technique that is both sustainable and resilient, passing down invaluable knowledge from one generation to the next. It prompts us to reflect on how traditional craftsmanship might hold solutions for modern construction, emphasizing sustainability and the beauty of handcrafted work. In every joint and every piece of wood, there lies a story of human ingenuity that continues to inspire and inform building practices today.

heritage_item: 中国传统木结构建筑营造技艺
title: Living Heritage: Chinese traditional architectural craftsmanship for timber-framed structures
description:
Tight as a thread, seamless as a seal, and coupled like yin and yang. For centuries,sunmao structures have been providing sturdy and durable supports for Chinese wooden architecture.

Large components of traditional Chinese wooden architecture, including columns, beams, purlins and dougong (interlocking brackets), are often pieced together using sunmao structures. The convex sun (tenon joint) and the concave mao (mortise), when interlocked, both support and contain the adjoining parts. This augments stability, but also adds flexibility to the architecture, strong enough to resist earthquakes. Craftsmen can make and process the components in advance and assemble them quickly on site, which is simple and efficient

The Hemudu site in Yuyao, East #China’s Zhejiang province, dates back around 7,000 years.Hundreds of wooden components with sunmao structures have been unearthed from the remains of stilted houses there, the earliest known application of sunmaostructuresin China in wooden-frame buildings.

Since the Shang (c. 16th century-11th century BC) and Zhou (c. 11th century-256 BC) dynasties, generations of carpenters, with their ingeniousness, have produced more than a hundred types of sunmao structures. From such, a whole set of systematic methods was devised, from selecting locations and choosing materials, to structure, measurements, manufacture and assembly.

For the craftsmen who have preserved and guarded this heritage, and for the generations of people who have lived in and among the spaces defined by it across China, wood and wooden structureshave always been an important constituent of Chinese architecture.

Their styles may differ, but they all share the same root. Chinese people’s love of nature and pursuit of harmonious coexistence is also embedded within.

title: Chinese traditional architectural craftsmanship for timber-framed structures
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00223
Description: Standing as distinctive symbols of Chinese architectural culture, timber-framed structures are found throughout the country. The wooden components such as the columns, beams, purlins, lintel and bracket sets are connected by tenon joints in a flexible, earthquake-resistant way. The surprisingly strong frames can be installed quickly at the building site by assembling components manufactured in advanced. In addition to this structural carpentry, the architectural craft also encompasses decorative woodworking, tile roofing, stonework, decorative painting and other arts passed down from masters to apprentices through verbal and practical instruction. Each phase of the construction procedure demonstrates its unique and systematic methods and skills. Employed today mainly in the construction of structures in the traditional style and in restoring ancient timber-framed buildings, Chinese traditional architectural craftsmanship for timber-framed structures embodies a heritage of wisdom and craftsmanship and reflects an inherited understanding of nature and interpersonal relationships in traditional Chinese society. For the carpenters and artisans who preserve this architectural style, and for the people who have lived in and among the spaces defined by it for generations, it has become a central visual component of Chinese identity and an important representative of Asian architecture.

A 2025 peer-reviewed study in Buildings explores Cantonese palatial‑type timber halls (Diantang Shi) in Guangdong Province, from the Song to Yuan dynasties. These structures showcase modular design, artisan precision tenon‑and‑mortise joinery, and use of the cai‑fen system—an early carpentry standard. The analysis traces how southern and northern construction traditions merged into elegant palace-style timber architecture suited to Guangdong’s climate.
📖 Full study: DOI 10.3390/buildings15142447
📬 Weekly peer-reviewed archaeology & architectural science → https://crediblyweekly.org

heritage_item: 中国传统木结构建筑营造技艺
title: Chinese traditional architectural craftsmanship for timber-framed structures
description:
A fundamental achievement of Chinese wooden architecture is the load-bearing timber frame, a network of interlocking wooden supports forming the skeleton of the building. This is considered China's major contribution to worldwide architectural technology. However, it is not known how the builders got the huge wooden support columns into position.
Unlike western architecture, in ancient Chinese wooden architecture, the wall only defined an enclosure, and did not form a load-bearing element. Buildings in China have been supported by wooden frames for as long as seven millennia. The emergence of the characteristic articulated wooden Chinese frame emerged during the Neolithic period. Seven thousand years ago mortise and tenon joinery was used to build wood-framed houses. (The oldest are at Hemudu site at Zhejiang). Over a thousand of these sites have been identified, usually with circular, square or oblong shaped buildings. During the Yangshao culture in the Middle Neolithic, circular and rectangular semisubterranean structures are found with wooden beams and columns. Wooden beams or earth supported the roofs which were most likely thatched.[3] As the villages and towns grew they adhered to symmetrical shapes. Symmetry was also important in the layout of homes, altars, and villages.
Dougong is a unique structural element of interlocking wooden brackets, one of the most important elements in traditional Chinese architecture. It first appeared in buildings of the last centuries BC and evolved into a structural network that joined pillars and columns to the frame of the roof. Dougong was widely used in the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC) and developed into a complex set of interlocking parts by its peak in the Tang and Song periods. Since ancient times when the Chinese first began to use wood for building, joinery has been a major focus and craftsmen cut the wooden pieces to fit so perfectly that no glue or fasteners were necessary.

title: Why are they CURVED?  #history #ghibli #anime #architecture #shorts  #historicalarchitecture
description:
Have you ever wondered why Japanese temples have curved roofs? While many believe they were designed to ward off evil spirits, the real reason is rooted in functionality and architectural ingenuity. Traditional Japanese architecture, like many East Asian buildings, primarily used wood—a material highly susceptible to rot when exposed to heavy monsoon rains. To solve this, Japanese temples were built with large roof overhangs to protect the wood from getting wet.

However, these overhangs blocked sunlight from entering the building. The solution? Curving the roof plane. This innovative design not only allowed more sunlight but also helped snow and rainwater slide off easily. The curved roof follows a cycloid shape, which maximizes efficiency for shedding snow and water during the rainy and snowy seasons.

But there was another issue: the large roof cast shadows over the building's corners. To fix this, architects in Japan created "flying eaves," an architectural feature that extends the roof edges outward, allowing more natural light while also making the structure appear lighter and more aesthetically pleasing. For taller buildings, multiple tiers of roofs were added, creating the stunning, multi-layered look we often associate with traditional Japanese temples.

title: Traditional Chinese Timber Joinery (Mortise and Tenon) Construction
description: Craftsmanship is the soul of great architecture. In this video, a worker is assembling intricately carved wooden components of a traditional Chinese building. The magic lies in mortise and tenon joints—these are interlocking connections where one piece has a projection (tenon) that fits perfectly into a recess (mortise) of another piece, with no nails needed. As these pieces are joined, they create a sturdy and elegant structure that can withstand the test of time and even earthquakes, thanks to the flexibility and strength inherent in the joint design. This is vital because it preserves age-old Chinese building techniques, showing how ancient craftsmen used simple yet brilliant ideas to build durable structures without modern hardware. It’s awe-inspiring to see how this traditional method, relying on precise woodworking and natural interlocking, continues to bring historic architectural styles to life today. Ultimately, it reminds us that the wisdom of past artisans holds timeless value in creating beautiful, long-lasting buildings.

title: Traditional Chinese Joinery Crafting Ancient Wood
description: Craftsmanship is the language of the hands telling stories of the past. In this video, an artisan is meticulously crafting components for ancient Chinese architecture using traditional joinery (sunmao) techniques. He carefully carves and fits wooden pieces together, relying on the natural interlocking of tenons (protruding parts) and mortises (recessed slots) instead of nails or glue. This age-old method works because the precise connection of tenon and mortise distributes weight and stress evenly, making the wooden structure incredibly sturdy and durable—just like how ancient Chinese palaces, temples, and homes have stood the test of time for centuries. Successfully assembling these components results in a robust framework that blends engineering ingenuity with artistic beauty. This craft is vital not only as a building technique but also as a piece of cultural heritage, showcasing how our ancestors used simple tools and deep knowledge to create lasting works of art from wood. It makes us reflect on the value of traditional skills in an era of modern machinery, reminding us that such craftsmanship carries the soul of history and the brilliance of human creativity. In the end, watching this artisan at work is a reminder that traditional Chinese joinery is more than a construction method—it’s a bridge between the past and present, preserving the wisdom and artistry that define our cultural roots.
