title: 10 Architectural Wonders From Ancient China #architecture #ancienthistory #china
description:
10 Architectural Wonders From Ancient China

✨The Great Wall of China: Constructed over several dynasties, the Great Wall is an iconic defensive structure built to protect against invasions by nomadic tribes. It stretches over 13,000 miles and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

✨The Forbidden City (Imperial Palace): Located in the heart of Beijing, the Forbidden City served as the imperial palace for Chinese emperors during the Ming and Qing dynasties. It is an extensive complex with intricate architecture and traditional Chinese design.

✨Terracotta Army (Qin Shi Huang's Mausoleum): Discovered in the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the Terracotta Army in Xi'an consists of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers, horses, and chariots. It was created to accompany the emperor in the afterlife.

✨Longmen Grottoes: Carved into limestone cliffs along the Yi River, the Longmen Grottoes in Luoyang feature thousands of Buddhist statues and inscriptions. The site spans several dynasties and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

✨The Summer Palace: Situated in Beijing, the Summer Palace is a vast imperial garden complex with palaces, temples, and a large lake. It served as a retreat for emperors during the hot summer months.

✨Hanging Monastery (Xuankong Temple): Located near Datong, the Hanging Monastery is a unique architectural marvel built into the side of a cliff. It combines elements of Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism.

✨Leshan Giant Buddha: Carved into a cliff face near Chengdu, the Leshan Giant Buddha is a colossal statue of Maitreya, standing at over 230 feet tall. It is the largest stone Buddha statue in the world.

✨Mogao Caves (Thousand Buddha Grottoes): Situated along the Silk Road near Dunhuang, the Mogao Caves house a vast collection of Buddhist art, including murals, sculptures, and manuscripts.

✨Pingyao Ancient City: A well-preserved ancient city, Pingyao in Shanxi province retains its traditional layout and architectural features from the Ming and Qing dynasties, offering a glimpse into China's historical urban planning.

✨The Temple of Heaven (Tiantan): Located in Beijing, the Temple of Heaven is a complex of religious buildings where emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties performed ceremonies to ensure a good harvest. It is known for its unique architecture and symbolism.

title: A City Built on History: Exploring the Ancient Chinese Architecture of Kaifeng | 4K China
description:
📜 [Intro]
1. Daxiangguo Temple (大相国寺)
Daxiangguo Temple is one of the most renowned Buddhist temples in China, located in the heart of Kaifeng. Originally built during the Northern Wei Dynasty (early 6th century), it has a history spanning over 1,400 years. The temple is famous for its grand architectural design and historical significance, as it was once a prominent center for Buddhist learning and cultural exchange. Visitors can admire its ancient wooden structures, exquisite carvings, and beautiful gardens. The temple is also known for housing numerous cultural relics, including ancient Buddhist scriptures and statues.

2. Shan, Shan, and Gan Guild Hall (山陕甘会馆)
The Shan, Shan, and Gan Guild Hall, also known as the Shanxi, Shaanxi, and Gansu Guild Hall, is an impressive historical building that once served as a meeting place for merchants from these regions. Located near the Kaifeng Ancient City Wall, the guild hall is a fine example of traditional Chinese architecture, showcasing intricate woodwork, carved beams, and painted ceilings. It reflects the vibrant commercial life of Kaifeng during the Song Dynasty when it was a bustling trade hub. The hall also features a rich collection of artifacts that offer a glimpse into the merchant culture of the time.

3. Longting Park (龙亭公园)
Longting Park, or Dragon Pavilion Park, is one of Kaifeng’s most famous scenic spots, known for its serene beauty and historical importance. The park is home to the Longting Pavilion, a magnificent structure originally built during the Song Dynasty as a royal palace. The pavilion has been reconstructed over the years, but it still retains its grandeur and historical charm. Surrounded by tranquil lakes and lush greenery, Longting Park is a perfect place to explore the fusion of nature and ancient Chinese architecture. It also houses several other historical buildings and cultural relics, making it a must-visit for anyone interested in Chinese history.

4. Chinese Han Garden (中国翰园)
The Chinese Han Garden, or Zhongguo Han Yuan, is a unique cultural and architectural park dedicated to showcasing the history and achievements of the Han Dynasty. Located in Kaifeng, this garden beautifully blends traditional Chinese landscape design with ancient architectural styles. It features a collection of pavilions, bridges, and pathways that recreate the atmosphere of the Han Dynasty. The park is home to numerous stone tablets inscribed with famous Chinese calligraphy and poetry, providing visitors with an insight into the intellectual and artistic accomplishments of the era. The tranquil surroundings and impressive structures make it a peaceful retreat for history enthusiasts.

These landmarks offer a deep dive into the rich history and architectural grandeur of Kaifeng, making them essential stops for any traveler exploring the city’s cultural heritage.

title: Who says no Tang wooden buildings survived in China? | China Documentary
description:
#china #hebei #northernchina #history #culture 
In 1933, groundbreaking Chinese architect and scholar Liang Sicheng measured the bell tower at Kaiyuan Temple in Zhengding, Hebei Province and refuted scurrilous Japanese claims that no Tang wooden buildings survived in China. More importantly, Liang’s endeavour sparked a wave of architectural research in Hebei revealing many of the 291 state-protected heritage sites, spanning over 1,000 years from the Tang to the Qing dynasties, that stand to this day as architectural embodiments of China’s elegance and resilience.

title: Mortise and tenon joint structural of ancient Chinese architecture
description:
Mortise and tenon joint (sǔn mǎo) is the main structural method of ancient Chinese architecture, furniture and other instruments. It is a connection method that combines concave and convex parts on two components. The protruding part is called tenon; the concave part is called mortise.
It is characterized by not using nails on objects, but using mortise and tenon to reinforce objects, reflecting ancient Chinese culture and wisdom.

title: Festive China: Dragon Boat Festival
description:
The #DragonBoatFestival is the fifth day of the fifth month on the Chinese lunar calendar.

There are many legends about the 2,000-year-old festival, but the most famous one is commemorating the death of #QuYuan, a patriotic poet from the State of Chu during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC).

Qu Yuan is said to have been loyal and patriotic his whole life. When he realized the decline of Chu was beyond recovery, his remorse knowing he could no longer save it grew stronger and stronger. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, he threw himself into the river and died for his beloved homeland.   
 
Watch this video to learn more about the legend, as well as traditions and festive foods related to the festival. 

Festive China is a series of short clips focusing on traditional Chinese festivals and festivities, the cultural connotations of traditional holidays, their development and changes, and how they manifest in today's China.

title: Dragon Boat Races Celebrate China's Ancient Past | National Geographic
description:
Near the summer solstice, communities across China gather to race boats, many adorned with painted carvings of dragons.
➡ Subscribe: https://on.natgeo.com/4p5A0D6

About National Geographic:
National Geographic is the world's premium destination for science, exploration, and adventure. Through their world-class scientists, photographers, journalists, and filmmakers, Nat Geo gets you closer to the stories that matter and past the edge of what's possible.

Get More National Geographic:

Instagram: https://instagram.com/natgeo

Threads: https://threads.com/@natgeo
X: https://x.com/NatGeo
LinkedIn: https://linkedin.com/company/national-geographic
TikTok: https://tiktok.com/@natgeo
Reddit: https://reddit.com/user/nationalgeographic
Pinterest: https://pinterest.com/natgeo

It’s clear to see how Duanwu Jie, China’s summer solstice holiday, got its English-language name—the Dragon Boat Festival. Around the fifth day of the fifth month on the traditional Chinese calendar, people gather to race boats adorned with brightly-painted carvings of dragons. Designs and types of contest vary widely. Some races are intense athletic challenges, while other events are more of a community get-together. Stories link the festival with the poet Qu Yuan, born in the late fourth century B.C. Said to be an able minister of the Chu state who was banished from the court, Qu Yuan despaired at his rivals’ corruption, and at last drowned himself in a river. By legend, an ensuing search for Qu by boat inspired the day’s races.

Read more in "Go Inside China's Lively Dragon Boat Festival Celebrations"

title: Living Heritage: Duanwu - Dragon Boat Festival
description:
Beating drums, synchronous strokes and an exhilarated crowd. Such spectacles of excitement are no rare scene across #China on #Duanwu, or #DragonBoatFestival, which falls on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month.

Although tales of its origin differ, the two-millennia-old festival is widely celebrated in memory of Qu Yuan, a patriotic poet during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC). When Qu realized his country's decline was beyond recovery, his remorse grew so strong that he committed suicide, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month. Legend has it that locals rowed boats to save the poet and threw zongzi, or sticky rice dumplings, into the river to keep fish from devouring his body. This tradition was passed down in China, even travelling across borders, carrying China’s respect for a patriot to all parts of the world.

A host of other practices are also associated with Duanwu. The fifth day of the fifth month is considered unlucky and poisonous. To rid themselves of the misfortune, many people, especially those in South China, take herbal baths, hang moxa and calamus plants over their doors and wear sachets stuffed with Chinese herbs, all with strong scents, to do away with venomous insects. Realgar wine, a common antidote against venom, is often enjoyed along with meals. Five-colored thread bracelets are also worn to obviate evil spirits. 

Duanwu began as a day to remember a poet steeped with love for his country. His patriotism is still celebrated today, with rich and fun customs that unite hearts across the globe, in methods that hail to the harmony between nature and man.

title: What is the Chinese Dragon Boat Festival (Duanwu) ?
description:
With a history over 2,000 years old, the Dragon Boat Festival is steeped in tradition and legend. It commemorates Qu Yuan, a Chinese poet and patriot. He lived 340-278 BC. 

Qu Yuan committed suicide by drowning himself in the Miluo River because of his despair over the king allying with the state of Qin.

The Dragon Boat Festival, also known as Duanwu, is celebrated annually on the fifth day of the fifth month of the Chinese lunar calendar. It falls on June 20 this year.

For thousands of years, the festival has been celebrated by eating zongzi and holding dragon boat races.

Zongzi, is a glutinous rice dumpling which is wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves and filled with other ingredients. Traditions of making the special dish vary across different parts of China.

The Dragon Boat Festival is also celebrated by many ethnic minority groups across China.

Folks here at CCTV-America help explain what it's all about.

title: Duanwu Festival
description:
The Dragon Boat Festival, also often known as the Tuen Ng or Duanwu Festival, is a traditional and statutory holiday originating in China.


The festival now occurs on the 5th day of the 5th month of the traditional lunar calendar, the source of its alternative name, the Double Fifth Festival. The Chinese calendar is lunisolar, so the date varies from year to year on the Gregorian calendar. In 2012, it fell on June 23; in 2013, on June 12; and in 2014, it occurred on June 2. The focus of most celebrations involves eating zongzi, drinking realgar wine, and racing dragon boats.


The sun is considered to be at its strongest around the time of summer solstice, as the daylight in the northern hemisphere is the longest. The sun, like the Chinese dragon, traditionally represents masculine energy, whereas the moon, like the phoenix, traditionally represents feminine energy. The summer solstice is considered the annual peak of male energy while the winter solstice, the longest night of the year, represents the annual peak of feminine energy. The masculine image of the dragon was thus naturally associated with Duanwu.


The usual English name for the holiday, 'Dragon Boat Festival,' directly translates into two alternative Chinese names for the holiday, Lóngchuánjié and Lóngzhōujié.


The official Chinese name of the festival is 端午节 on the mainland and 端午節 on Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore and Macao. This is pronounced variously in different Chinese dialects. In Mandarin, it is romanized as Duānwǔjié on the mainland and Taiwan; in Cantonese, it is romanized as Tuen1 Ng5 Jit3 on Hong Kong and Tung1 Ng5 Jit3 on Macao. All of these names refer to its original position as the first seventh-day in the fifth month of the traditional Chinese calendar, which was also known as 午. Both the People's Republic and the Republic of China use 'Dragon Boat Festival' as the official English translation of the holiday, while Hong Kong calls it the 'Tuen Ng Festival' and Macao calls it 'Dragon Boat Festival ' in English and Festividade do Barco-Dragí in Portuguese.


In Indonesian, the festival is known as 'Peh Cun', which is derived from the Hokkien phrase 扒船.


The story best known in modern China holds that the festival commemorates the death of the poet and minister Qu Yuan of the ancient state of Chu during the Warring States period of the Zhou Dynasty. A cadet member of the Chu royal house, Qu served in high offices. However, when the king decided to ally with the increasingly powerful state of Qin, Qu was banished for opposing the alliance and even accused of treason. During his exile, Qu Yuan wrote a great deal of poetry. Twenty-eight years later, Qin captured Ying, the Chu capital. In despair, Qu Yuan committed suicide by drowning himself in the Miluo River.


It is said that the local people, who admired him, raced out in their boats to save him or at least retrieve his body. This is said to have been the origin of dragon boat races. When his body could not be found, they dropped balls of sticky rice into the river so that the fish would eat them instead of Qu Yuan's body. This is said to be the origin of zongzi.


Despite the modern popularity of the Qu Yuan origin theory, in the former territory of the state of Wu, the festival commemorated Wu Zixu. Wu Zixu was a loyal advisor whose advice was ignored by the king to the detriment of the kingdom. Wu Zixu was forced to commit suicide by the king Fuchai, with his body thrown into the river on the fifth day of the fifth month. After his death, in places such as Suzhou, Wu Zixu is remembered during the Duanwu Festival to this day.

title: Festive China: Dragon Boat Festival
description:
The #DragonBoatFestival is the fifth day of the fifth month on the Chinese lunar calendar.

There are many legends about the 2,000-year-old festival, but the most famous one is commemorating the death of #QuYuan, a patriotic poet from the State of Chu during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC).

Qu Yuan is said to have been loyal and patriotic his whole life. When he realized the decline of Chu was beyond recovery, his remorse knowing he could no longer save it grew stronger and stronger. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, he threw himself into the river and died for his beloved homeland.   
 
Watch this video to learn more about the legend, as well as traditions and festive foods related to the festival. 

Festive China is a series of short clips focusing on traditional Chinese festivals and festivities, the cultural connotations of traditional holidays, their development and changes, and how they manifest in today's China.

title: Dragon Boat Races Celebrate China's Ancient Past | National Geographic
description:
Near the summer solstice, communities across China gather to race boats, many adorned with painted carvings of dragons.
➡ Subscribe: https://on.natgeo.com/4p5A0D6

About National Geographic:
National Geographic is the world's premium destination for science, exploration, and adventure. Through their world-class scientists, photographers, journalists, and filmmakers, Nat Geo gets you closer to the stories that matter and past the edge of what's possible.

It’s clear to see how Duanwu Jie, China’s summer solstice holiday, got its English-language name—the Dragon Boat Festival. Around the fifth day of the fifth month on the traditional Chinese calendar, people gather to race boats adorned with brightly-painted carvings of dragons. Designs and types of contest vary widely. Some races are intense athletic challenges, while other events are more of a community get-together. Stories link the festival with the poet Qu Yuan, born in the late fourth century B.C. Said to be an able minister of the Chu state who was banished from the court, Qu Yuan despaired at his rivals’ corruption, and at last drowned himself in a river. By legend, an ensuing search for Qu by boat inspired the day’s races.

title: What’s a Zongzi? Dragon Boat Festival Explained
description:
Dragon Boat Festival is a summer holiday usually celebrated in early June. Zongzi, typically translated as rice dumplings, are a staple on the festival table. In Taiwan, these savory sticky rice snacks are stuffed with goodies like braised pork, mushrooms, chestnuts, or salted egg yolk. But there’s quite the debate when it comes to cooking. Northern style zongzi are stir-fried before being wrapped, then steamed. They're firmer, drier, and packed like a savory rice bento.
 
Meanwhile, down south, Southern style skip the stir-fry. The raw rice and filling go straight into the leaf wrapper, and then get boiled all together, creating a softer, almost gooey texture. What’s your favorite kind of zongzi? Sound off in the comments below. Remember to reply at your own risk!

title: Dragon Boat Festival Feast: Use natural containers like leaves and bamboo to create delicious dishes
description:
※Click "cc" on the lower right menu to choose your subtitle language.

In Yunnan cuisine, there is a category of dishes that need to be wrapped in natural containers. Zongzi leaves and bamboo shoot husks are used to wrap zongzi, banana leaves are used for roasted dishes, lotus leaves can be used for lotus leaf chicken and sticky rice, and bamboo tubes can be used to steam and roast all kinds of food. The amazing thing is that whatever is used to wrap the food imparts its flavor to it, enhancing the richness and deliciousness of the dishes!

The day we had our meal, we were really lucky because there were flying ants everywhere. Actually, our family had postponed the meal for two days, but it was quite unfortunate that during the Dragon Boat Festival, there were flying ants every day. These insects are attracted to light and are quite common in the village during this season. Usually, when there are flying ants at home, we turn off all the lights. However, for filming, we couldn’t do that. I included a text warning in the video where the flying ants appear, so viewers can watch with caution. For us, flying ants are a good omen, indicating a bountiful harvest of wild mushrooms and plentiful rain. They are also edible insects. My family seemed quite happy about it. We're used to seeing them, so we don't find it uncomfortable. I also advised viewers in the video to skip this part if they don’t like it.

Finally, I wish everyone happiness and safety every day!

Hello, everyone! I am Dianxi Xiaoge, a Yunnan native. If you enjoy my videos, please keep subscribing to my channel. I'll continue to share my life and Yunnan-styled food in my uploads. Wish you have a happy life and enjoy tasty food every day

title: The Dragon Boat festival
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00225
Description: Beginning on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, people of several ethnic groups throughout China and the world celebrate the Dragon Boat festival, especially in the middle and lower reaches of the Yangtze River. The festivities vary from region to region, but they usually share several features. A memorial ceremony offering sacrifices to a local hero is combined with sporting events such as dragon races, dragon boating and willow shooting; feasts of rice dumplings, eggs and ruby sulphur wine; and folk entertainments including opera, song and unicorn dances. The hero who is celebrated varies by region: the romantic poet Qu Yuan is venerated in Hubei and Hunan Provinces, Wu Zixu (an old man said to have died while slaying a dragon in Guizhou Province) in South China, and Yan Hongwo in Yunnan Province among the Dai community. Participants also ward off evil during the festival by bathing in flower-scented water, wearing five-colour silk, hanging plants such as moxa and calamus over their doors, and pasting paper cut-outs in their windows. The Dragon Boat festival strengthens bonds within families and establishes a harmonious relationship between humanity and nature. It also encourages the expression of imagination and creativity, contributing to a vivid sense of cultural identity.

In this video, we’ll explore the story behind the Dragon Boat Festival and what makes this ancient celebration, which began in China, so unique.

We’ll be introduced to Qu Yuan, a famous and loyal poet who was banished by King Huai of Chu. We’ll then learn about the sad events that took place, leading Qu Yuan to take his life. And people reacted when they heard the news about Qu Yuan.

There’s also an explanation why people race dragon boats, eat rice dumplings, and drink a certain wine during this event!

01:01 - Who is Qu Yuan?
01:42 - Why was Qu Yuan banished by King Huai of Chu?
02:19 - How did Qu Yuan feel about being banished?
02:39 - Tragedy for the State of Chu and Qu Yuan’s sad decision.
03:04 - How did people react when they heard Qu Yuan took his life?
03:40 - The Dragon Boat Festival is an important traditional Chinese event.
03:59 - Why dragon boats, rice dumplings, and realgar wine are associated with the Dragon Boat Festival.

The Dragon Boat Festival is one of the oldest festivals, spanning a history of more than 2,000 years. It is an annual celebration to commemorate the life and death of Qu Yuan, as well as the attempt at saving him. In 2009, it was added to the UNESCO World Intangible Cultural Heritage List.

description:
The Amazon Dragons Paddling Club raced two dragon boats in the Seattle Dragon Boat Festival, an event hosted by the Seattle Flying Dragons. This race took place at Lake Union Park in Seattle, Washington, on June 13th, 2026. Amazon Warriors took 2nd place in Women's B Division, while Amazon Rebels took 2nd place in Women's A Division.

==========
The Amazon Dragons Paddling Club is an organization which provides lesbians and queer women with camaraderie, fitness, and physical strength through competitive dragon boat racing. Dragon boat racing has been a traditional Chinese paddled watercraft activity for over 2000 years and began as a modern international sport in Hong Kong in 1976.

title: Mazu e (Abbé Karol Abessolo) — Le Chœur des Piccoli (Version acoustique) [paroles + traduction]
description:
Mazu e est un chant camerounais de l'abbe Karol Mario Abessolo (de regrettée mémoire) en langue ewondo et repris ici en version acoustique par le Choeur des Piccoli. Au delà de la musique c'est une vraie prière chantée. 

Ces paroles profondes écrites par l'abbé Karol Mario Abessolo, ont rendu la chanson célèbre dans le répertoire du chant choral en langue beti/ewondo. Cette interprétation, à laquelle nous avons ajouté les paroles et la traduction, privilégie la chaleur des voix et l’intimité du message. Le Chant Mazu e Il est utilisé dans les liturgies catholiques camerounaises surtout comme chant d'entrée, et est repris non seulement par les chorales Ewondo mais aussi les chorales dans d'autres langues camerounaises.

==============
Compositeur : Karol Mario Abessolo.
Lead: Raphaël (Le Choeur des Piccoli)
Choeurs: Anne Sophie, Roxane, Raphaël (Le Choeur des Piccoli)
Piano: Alexis BAKOND
Mix et Master: Kisito Sound Studio
Langue : beti / ewondo (utilisée au Cameroun). 
Transcription des paroles et traduction: Georges Eric Mbah

===============
Paroles:

Refrain: Mazu e, mazu ée a Nti mazu, mezu dzali nyian(e) woé, mazu éé, a Nti, Mazu éé, mazu ée a Nti mazu, mezu dzali nyian(e) woé, mazu éé, a Nti mazu.

1. A mindzug miama menga yanga Nti o me loege nye o a Nti, (mazu). Ndo Nti a ke alô abe etuga dzie a wog(e) ntad wom oo , (mazu), anga dudu ma edog ebe o a tele ma mekol a tsa(mazu), owe ma yebe nloé Nti o, owe a Nti me so ya.

2. Ndo Nti asô me kalan mpkaman dzia, menga tag ai nyé oo, (mazu), dzia mefon, dzia’olugu o, asu Zamba wom oo (mazu), eban oyeng olugu ai duma, bibô ai Ntondobe oo, (mazu), owe, matag ai Nti Zamba wam(e), Tara Nti mafon ai wa.

3. A Nti wa ndiki diñ zeze metunga, ai zeze bifae (mazu), ve da’a Nti onga yiè ma melô ne me wog ebug dzoé a (mazu), onga teb minkpel biyem o mena menyô mesô ya (m’azu), owe matuni wa mamen a Nti, Tara Nti m’atuni wa enyiñ dzama a.

4. Ongá dudu ma edog ebe o a Nti Ntondobe oo (m’azu), be bonde bama ai bemvoé bama b'ayem na meke ya a (m’azu). Ebá bësë b'ayén ma a Nti, b'ayén ve awu aa (m’azu), m'etam etam o, okòn wama m'ayem na m'eńiñ aa (m’azu), a Nti, zëzë nsem minsëm o, m'ave wa ntud ngañ aa.

===============

title: Mazu: The Chinese Sea Goddess of the Ocean
description:
Mazu: The Chinese Sea Goddess of the Ocean

Meet Mazu, the Sea Goddess of Southern China, a legendary figure with over a thousand years of history. This YouTube Short uncovers the captivating story of Lin Mo Niang, a girl born with mystical powers on Meizhou Island who became a divine protector of the seas.

Learn how she fell into a trance to save her family from a storm and how, after her death, she became a glowing figure guiding ships to safety. We explore her rise to becoming the "Heavenly Empress" and how her worship has spread to over 200 million people across China, Taiwan, and Southeast Asia.

Discover the cultural significance of her temples, filled with incense and prayers from fishermen, merchants, and families. This video is a journey into Chinese mythology, folklore, and the enduring power of faith.

title: The Journey of the Goddess | Capturing the spirit of Mazu
description:
For  more:
https://www.cgtn.com/video

Wang Wen-hsien is a photographer from Beigang Town on the island of Taiwan. For the past three years, he's been traveling regularly across the Taiwan Strait to Meizhou Island in Fujian Province, southeastern China. His purpose is to photograph a series of events related to Mazu, the goddess of the sea who's revered across southern China and beyond. Tune in to the CGTN documentary "The Journey of the Goddess" on February 3, and discover what it is about Mazu culture on Meizhou that so fascinates this photographer from Taiwan.

《妈祖》
主演：刘涛、严屹宽、林心如

东海龙王次子睚眦，凶残好斗、野心勃勃、报复心强，他与其爪牙常在海面兴风作浪，伤及生灵。赤脚大仙发现，奏明玉帝，在王母娘娘的支持下，准许他在凡间物色、培育一位统辖海上救援诸事的海神苗子。岛上渔民林愿夫人去观音庙求子，得观音赐孕，十四月迟迟不生，睚眦唆使部下变化成道人造谣，以至于蒙蔽村民，险些驱逐林家离岛。婴儿出生后，沉默不语，取名默娘，默娘有慧根与善心，观音菩萨与赤脚大仙暗地收她为徒。

Mazu, born Lin Mo during the Song Dynasty (960-1279), is cherished for protecting sailors and calming storms.
April 20 marks Mazu’s 1,065th birthday, drawing global devotees to honor her legacy. 🌊🙏

title: The Tale of the Goddess Mazu - The Protector of Sailors in Chinese Mythology
description:
Lin Moniang, a young woman with a deep connection to the sea, foresees a storm that threatens her family. Using her mystical powers, she calms the waves and saves her relatives. The news of her miraculous deed spreads, and she becomes a guide for fishermen and sailors. However, the spiritual effort leaves her ill, and she passes away. In her honor, she is transformed into the goddess Mazu, the protector of sailors, and temples are erected in her name. To this day, Mazu remains a central figure in Chinese culture, inspiring faith and hope.

title: The Legend of Mazu 妈祖的传说 | Animated Comic Short Film | Chinese Goddess Lianhuanhua-style comic book
description:
Mazu, the beloved goddess and protector of the seas, began her life as a mortal girl in the coastal town of Meizhou. With extraordinary abilities and compassion, she defeated evil demons, saved countless lives, and ascended into immortality. Tales of her virtue, piety, and miraculous deeds spread throughout China and–over centuries–the entire world.

In this bilingual lianhuanhua-style 连环画 comic book, Mazu's adventures are brought to life with stunning illustrations by artist Koh Chin Tong. Be mesmerised by her mythology and gain new insight into the culture of overseas Chinese.

title: World Mazu Returns to Her Birthplace: Thousands Gather at Meizhou Island to Honor Tradition
description: 🌏On the first day of the third lunar month, over 100 Mazu statues from around the world were accompanied by nearly ten thousand devotees at Meizhou Island in #Putian. According to tradition, Mazu was born on the 23rd day of the third lunar month. 🙏Each year, Mazu statues from various locations are taken back to the ancestral Mazu Temple in Meizhou to pay homage and offer incense, an event affectionately known as "World Mazu Returns to Her Birthplace". 💞🤝This practice not only demonstrates a deep commitment to the faith but also symbolizes its devotees' heartfelt connection to their cultural roots. #MazuCulture

title: Witness the Fascinating Dajia Mazu Pilgrimage  in Taiwan, and Everything I Ate For FREE!!?
description:
In this video, we will be joining the fascinating Dajia Mazu Pilgrimage in Taiwan.  It's one of the world's biggest religious events, something everyone has to experience at least once in life.  

People take part and support this event by simply giving, like free food, free drinks, rest areas, bathrooms, free rides, etc., selflessness, that's everytihng about the Mazu culture!

Not only did I feel loved and protected by Mazu, I was also touched by everyone around me being so helpful and loving, watch this video to learn more about this pilgrimage!

title: Worshippers deeply moved in Baishatun Mazu’s grace｜Taiwan News
description:
Bilingual text here: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026414W02EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description
雙語文字版: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026414W02EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description

Our official website: ➔ https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/

Follow us on
Facebook: ➔ https://www.facebook.com/ftvformosanews
X (Twitter): ➔ https://x.com/FTV_Taiwan_News
The journey continues in the footsteps of the much-revered Baishatun Mazu. Early Tuesday morning, FTV news anchor Peter Ho and videographer arrived in Dadu District, Taichung, where Mazu has decided as her next stop. Along the way, the FTV team was deeply moved by the touching stories in all corners of society. No matter what challenges life throws at them, people could always find solace and strength under Mazu’s wing.

Worshippers readily lay prostrate for their beloved goddess’s arrival. On Day 2 of the Baishatun Mazu Pilgrimage, the sea goddess’s pink palanquin arrived at the border of Taichung’s Dadu and Longjin districts.

A woman stands out among the crowd outside the government-owned Dadu Market. She’s in tears, begging for Mazu to bring her mother-in-law home.

- Miss, what’s wrong?
- I need to find my mother-in-law. She’s been missing since April 11. I can’t find her anywhere. I’ve called the police. I really don’t know what to do.

Later, Mazu wills her pilgrims to stop the palanquin in front of the woman and offer support. 

Our team also noticed this man in crutches. He identifies himself as Mr. Hsiang from Sanchong, New Taipei. It’s the first time he’s following in Baishatun Mazu’s footsteps, even with limited mobility after he’s diagnosed with Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy, or CIDP. It’s a rare disease that causes the patient to progressively develop muscle weakness and numbness from the limbs.

Mr. Hsiang
Worshipper
This period over the past two days was the first time for strangers to cheer me on ever since I got my diagnosis. It’s also the most enthusiastic. It’s really taxing and exhausting. I thought of giving up, but then I thought, if even strangers were willing to cheer us on, why not try a little harder?

Once again, the FTV team was lucky to have a chance to receive blessings, by laying prostrate for the palanquin to pass over. But don’t worry if you can’t actually make it in person. You can always ask family members to carry your clothing in a backpack for Mazu to charge it with good fortune and wear later, like many worshippers did today.

- I can’t describe it. It’s so moving on a visceral level. I’m so moved this very instance.
- You’ll be deeply moved simply by following her. I feel like I might cry.

Every worshipper following Mazu has a story to tell, but they all come together as one in a profoundly affective experience, by the grace of her boundless mercy.

title: Living Heritage: Woodblock Printing
description:
Delicate strokes, carved onto wood, with ultimate precision. This is #woodblockprinting, an ancient #printing technique that propelled human civilization.

As Buddhism prevailed in China during the Tang Dynasty (618-907), there was a strong need to produce a large amount of Buddhist scriptures, and copying by hand could not meet the rising demand. Ancient Chinese craftsmen thus came up with a novel way to mass produce printed works. Hence came the prototype of woodblock printing.

Traditional woodblock printing can be divided into four major steps: writing, engraving, printing, and binding. With each step then sub-divided into several procedures, it takes roughly 30 steps to produce a woodblock print.

Carving lies at the center of woodblock printing as this arduous step can make or break the final print. Characters and images are carved to produce raised areas or lines that will eventually apply ink to paper. It calls for a pair of skilled hands.

A five-meter-scroll of the Buddhist scripture Diamond Sutra, printed in 868, is the "earliest dated printed book" in the words of the British Library where it is stored. It is just one example of ancient works of art that not only tell the wisdoms of our predecessors, but also is a testimony to the pursuit of beauty by Chinese craftsmen throughout centuries.

The invention of writing gave life to great thoughts, but it is the invention of printing that made knowledge a shareable fruit for all humankind. #Chineseculture

title: Revealing the untold story of Chinese woodblock prints
description:
For  more:

#Chinese woodblock prints can be traced back to ancient times and are believed to have influenced Japanese ukiyo-e print works, known as "pictures of the floating world." title: China engraved block printing technique
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00229
Description: The traditional China engraved block printing technique requires the collaboration of half a dozen craftspeople possessed of printing expertise, dexterity and team spirit. The blocks themselves, made from the fine-grained wood of pear or jujube trees, are cut to a thickness of two centimetres and polished with sandpaper to prepare them for engraving. Drafts of the desired images are brushed onto extremely thin paper and scrutinized for errors before they are transferred onto blocks. The inked designs provide a guide for the artisan who cuts the picture or design into the wood, producing raised characters that will eventually apply ink to paper. First, though, the blocks are tested with red and then blue ink and corrections are made to the carving. Finally, when the block is ready to be used, it is covered with ink and pressed by hand onto paper to print the final image. Block engraving may be used to print books in a variety of traditional styles, to create modern books with conventional binding, or to reproduce ancient Chinese books. A number of printing workshops continue this handicraft today thanks to the knowledge and skills of the expert artisans. 
Country(ies): China
© 2008 by YZYQ

title: Woodblock Printing Process ... in 3-D spatial audio
description:
In this video, Tokyo-based woodblock printmaker Dave Bull shows the entire process of making a batch of prints (8 sheets) from blank paper to finished print - in POV style (from the printer's Point Of View) - with each of the colour impressions happening in an uncut, unedited, uninterrupted flow. The viewers can get a very good feeling for the rhythm of the process, and can see just how the pigment/paste balance is controlled along the way.

As a kind of 'bonus', the entire video was recorded using binaural microphones embedded in Dave's ears - you hear _exactly_ what he heard, at every step of the way. (Use headphones!) The recording of each segment was done starting around 3:00 am, when Asakusa is as quiet as possible, but even then, there are residual sounds interrupting along the way (the occasional truck passing by, some rainfall for a few of the impressions, and unavoidably a large air conditioner on a building a few blocks away …)

The print used for this demonstration is ‘The Demon King’, which was designed for us by Jed Henry, creator of the famous Ukiyo-e Heroes prints. It is available as part of our 'Portraits Set #1’ subscription series; information here: https://mokuhankan.com/subscriptions/portraits.html

[Note: there is no narration included in this video, in an attempt to bring you into the undisturbed 'world' of a printer's work. If there is enough interest, we could perhaps upload a second 'cut' of this video, with Dave explaining things along the way ...]

title: Movable-Type Printing | The Timeless Innovation of Ancient China
description:
✨ Movable-type printing, one of China's Four Great Inventions, revolutionized traditional printing methods and opened new avenues for knowledge dissemination. Invented by Bi Sheng during the 11th century in the Northern Song Dynasty, this groundbreaking technique laid the foundation for modern printing and left an indelible mark on human civilization.

Unique Features of Movable-Type Printing

🌟 Diverse Materials
Early types were made of ceramic, later evolving into wood and metal, catering to various printing needs.

🌟 Modular Design
Each character was carved on individual blocks, allowing flexibility in arrangement and reuse, enhancing efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

🌟 Efficiency
Compared to block printing, movable type dramatically increased production speed and reduced costs, fostering widespread access to literature.

🌟 Versatile Applications
From classic texts to business documents, movable type provided a practical solution for mass text reproduction.

Cultural and Historical Significance
🌟 Knowledge Accessibility
By making books cheaper and faster to produce, movable-type printing enabled broader access to knowledge and culture.

🌟 Global Influence
This invention served as a vital medium for spreading ideas, technologies, and cultural values worldwide.

🌟 A Foundational Contribution
Movable-type printing provided the technical groundwork for modern printing, continuing to inspire innovation.

Modern-Day Relevance
Despite the rise of advanced printing technologies, movable-type printing remains a cherished intangible cultural heritage, celebrated for its:

✨ Cultural Showcase: As an educational tool, it offers hands-on experiences in traditional craftsmanship.
✨ Skill Preservation: Teaching and demonstration ensure that this ancient art is passed down to future generations.
✨ Artistic Value: Many artists incorporate movable type into contemporary designs, breathing new life into this timeless craft.

A Legacy of Innovation
Movable-type printing is not just a technique but a legacy of creativity, intellectual pursuit, and cultural exchange. It continues to inspire us to value knowledge, cherish tradition, and innovate for the future.

title: ANCIENT PRINTING: The 1,000-Year-Old Art Of Woodblock Printing
description:
Step back in time and witness the mesmerizing process of Woodblock New Year Prints (Nian Hua), a cornerstone of Chinese folk art. Unlike modern digital printing, this ancient "Water-based Woodblock" technique relies entirely on the artisan's touch and natural materials. Every masterpiece begins with a series of intricately carved blocks, typically made from durable pearwood or jujube wood. Each block represents a single color layer, requiring absolute precision in alignment—a process known as "color registration"—to ensure that the reds, greens, and yellows perfectly fit within the initial black outlines.

In this video, we showcase the delicate balance of pressure and pigment. The artisan applies natural mineral colors to the woodblock with a brush, then lays down a sheet of handmade Xuan paper. Using a specialized palm-fiber tool (Zong Pa), they rub the back of the paper with rhythmic pressure, forcing the vibrant colors to seep deep into the fibers. This creates a rich, textured finish that never fades and possesses a "soul" that machines simply cannot replicate. Discover the patience, skill, and cultural heritage behind every stroke of this living history, turning a simple piece of wood and paper into a festive symbol of luck and joy.

title: Woodcut classic records origins of rural China's transformation
description:
For  more:
https://news.cgtn.com/news/2022-12-23/Woodcut-classic-records-origins-of-rural-China-s-transformation-1fZFQWVmtig/index.html

Under the artist's carving blade, a scene springs to life showing people's anger boiling over at a rally called to reduce farmers' rent, as they struggle against their landlords and demand that excessive rent payments they have made are returned. Some 80 years ago, when the transformation of rural #China first unfolded, a woodcut artwork recorded this historic moment. Created by Gu Yuan in 1943, "Rent Reducing Rally" marks a representative work of the "Yan'an Woodcuts School." In the #artwork, Gu Yuan broke new ground by blending European woodcut techniques with China's traditional woodblock prints, presenting the dawn of a new epoch while at the same time ushering in a fresh artistic style in the country's woodcuts and engravings. China Media Group's documentary series "Fine Arts in China" explores this more.

title: Living Heritage: Woodblock Printing
description:
Delicate strokes, carved onto wood, with ultimate precision. This is #woodblockprinting, an ancient #printing technique that propelled human civilization.

As Buddhism prevailed in China during the Tang Dynasty (618-907), there was a strong need to produce a large amount of Buddhist scriptures, and copying by hand could not meet the rising demand. Ancient Chinese craftsmen thus came up with a novel way to mass produce printed works. Hence came the prototype of woodblock printing.

Traditional woodblock printing can be divided into four major steps: writing, engraving, printing, and binding. With each step then sub-divided into several procedures, it takes roughly 30 steps to produce a woodblock print.

Carving lies at the center of woodblock printing as this arduous step can make or break the final print. Characters and images are carved to produce raised areas or lines that will eventually apply ink to paper. It calls for a pair of skilled hands.

A five-meter-scroll of the Buddhist scripture Diamond Sutra, printed in 868, is the "earliest dated printed book" in the words of the British Library where it is stored. It is just one example of ancient works of art that not only tell the wisdoms of our predecessors, but also is a testimony to the pursuit of beauty by Chinese craftsmen throughout centuries.

The invention of writing gave life to great thoughts, but it is the invention of printing that made knowledge a shareable fruit for all humankind. #Chineseculture

title: China engraved block printing technique
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00229
Description: The traditional China engraved block printing technique requires the collaboration of half a dozen craftspeople possessed of printing expertise, dexterity and team spirit. The blocks themselves, made from the fine-grained wood of pear or jujube trees, are cut to a thickness of two centimetres and polished with sandpaper to prepare them for engraving. Drafts of the desired images are brushed onto extremely thin paper and scrutinized for errors before they are transferred onto blocks. The inked designs provide a guide for the artisan who cuts the picture or design into the wood, producing raised characters that will eventually apply ink to paper. First, though, the blocks are tested with red and then blue ink and corrections are made to the carving. Finally, when the block is ready to be used, it is covered with ink and pressed by hand onto paper to print the final image. Block engraving may be used to print books in a variety of traditional styles, to create modern books with conventional binding, or to reproduce ancient Chinese books. A number of printing workshops continue this handicraft today thanks to the knowledge and skills of the expert artisans. 
Country(ies): China

title: Movable-Type Printing | The Timeless Innovation of Ancient China
description:
✨ Movable-type printing, one of China's Four Great Inventions, revolutionized traditional printing methods and opened new avenues for knowledge dissemination. Invented by Bi Sheng during the 11th century in the Northern Song Dynasty, this groundbreaking technique laid the foundation for modern printing and left an indelible mark on human civilization.

Unique Features of Movable-Type Printing

🌟 Diverse Materials
Early types were made of ceramic, later evolving into wood and metal, catering to various printing needs.

🌟 Modular Design
Each character was carved on individual blocks, allowing flexibility in arrangement and reuse, enhancing efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

🌟 Efficiency
Compared to block printing, movable type dramatically increased production speed and reduced costs, fostering widespread access to literature.

🌟 Versatile Applications
From classic texts to business documents, movable type provided a practical solution for mass text reproduction.

Cultural and Historical Significance
🌟 Knowledge Accessibility
By making books cheaper and faster to produce, movable-type printing enabled broader access to knowledge and culture.

🌟 Global Influence
This invention served as a vital medium for spreading ideas, technologies, and cultural values worldwide.

🌟 A Foundational Contribution
Movable-type printing provided the technical groundwork for modern printing, continuing to inspire innovation.

Modern-Day Relevance
Despite the rise of advanced printing technologies, movable-type printing remains a cherished intangible cultural heritage, celebrated for its:

✨ Cultural Showcase: As an educational tool, it offers hands-on experiences in traditional craftsmanship.
✨ Skill Preservation: Teaching and demonstration ensure that this ancient art is passed down to future generations.
✨ Artistic Value: Many artists incorporate movable type into contemporary designs, breathing new life into this timeless craft.

A Legacy of Innovation
Movable-type printing is not just a technique but a legacy of creativity, intellectual pursuit, and cultural exchange. It continues to inspire us to value knowledge, cherish tradition, and innovate for the future.

description:
Uncover the ancient craft of Woodblock Door God Prints — vibrant artworks traditionally placed on doors to ward off evil and welcome good fortune during the Lunar New Year. ✨

🔸 Highlights of the Craft
✔️ Intricately carved woodblocks
✔️ Color separated printing
✔️ 100% handmade process

title: Woodcut classic records origins of rural China's transformation
description:
For  more:
https://news.cgtn.com/news/2022-12-23/Woodcut-classic-records-origins-of-rural-China-s-transformation-1fZFQWVmtig/index.html

Under the artist's carving blade, a scene springs to life showing people's anger boiling over at a rally called to reduce farmers' rent, as they struggle against their landlords and demand that excessive rent payments they have made are returned. Some 80 years ago, when the transformation of rural #China first unfolded, a woodcut artwork recorded this historic moment. Created by Gu Yuan in 1943, "Rent Reducing Rally" marks a representative work of the "Yan'an Woodcuts School." In the #artwork, Gu Yuan broke new ground by blending European woodcut techniques with China's traditional woodblock prints, presenting the dawn of a new epoch while at the same time ushering in a fresh artistic style in the country's woodcuts and engravings. China Media Group's documentary series "Fine Arts in China" explores this more.

title: Learn how to make a two and three-colour linocut print, using the multi-block technique.
description:
How to make a multi-block linocut print with professional artist Michelle Hughes. 

I will show you how to create a two and three-colour linocut print at home, using the multi-block technique.

Learn how to:
-Create a multi-colour linocut print using the multi-block technique. 
-Plan a design for multi-block printing.
-Make a registration device or jig.
-Accurately register each lino block so that each colour lines up in a print.
-Print your design by hand, using water-based or oil-based inks.  
-Create a linocut print that you can print as an edition. 
 
The course includes:
-Online self-paced learning.
-Step-by-step guides and instructional videos.
-Demonstrations of two multi-block print designs from start to finish.
-Tools and equipment advice as downloadable PDFs.
-Hints and tips.
-Creative community and support.

See website for details. https://www.michellehughesdesign.com/linocut-workshops-york




See examples of my work at..
https://www.michellehughes.co.uk

Follow me on social media for latest updates at...
https://www.instagram.com/michellehughesdesign
https://www.facebook.com/michellehughesdesign/
https://twitter.com/Design_York
https://www.pinterest.co.uk/michellehughesdesign

About the artist
Michelle Hughes is a printmaker and designer, based in York, UK. Her linocut prints are inspired by nature; her love of gardening and the great British countryside.
 
Michelle loves exploring the countryside by bike or on foot, camera in hand, capturing ideas for her next prints. Back in her garden studio, Michelle creates simple but stylised silhouettes based on her photographs, and cuts these shapes into lino. She hand prints with an etching press, using oil-based inks to create tonal blocks of colour. Michelle’s prints are limited editions.

title: ANCIENT PRINTING: The 1,000-Year-Old Art Of Woodblock Printing
description:
Step back in time and witness the mesmerizing process of Woodblock New Year Prints (Nian Hua), a cornerstone of Chinese folk art. Unlike modern digital printing, this ancient "Water-based Woodblock" technique relies entirely on the artisan's touch and natural materials. Every masterpiece begins with a series of intricately carved blocks, typically made from durable pearwood or jujube wood. Each block represents a single color layer, requiring absolute precision in alignment—a process known as "color registration"—to ensure that the reds, greens, and yellows perfectly fit within the initial black outlines.

In this video, we showcase the delicate balance of pressure and pigment. The artisan applies natural mineral colors to the woodblock with a brush, then lays down a sheet of handmade Xuan paper. Using a specialized palm-fiber tool (Zong Pa), they rub the back of the paper with rhythmic pressure, forcing the vibrant colors to seep deep into the fibers. This creates a rich, textured finish that never fades and possesses a "soul" that machines simply cannot replicate. Discover the patience, skill, and cultural heritage behind every stroke of this living history, turning a simple piece of wood and paper into a festive symbol of luck and joy.

title: Kunqu Opera: "The Peony Pavilion"
description:
#KunquOpera #ShanghaiKunquOperaTroupe #ThePeonyPavilion

“The Peony Pavilion” is the most classic play in the repertoire of Kunqu Opera. The story focuses on the romance between DU Liniang and LIU Mengmei It was written by TANG Xianzu, a Ming dynasty playwright, nicknamed “Shakespeare of the East”. The play can be compared to William SHAKESPEARE’s “Romeo and Juliet”, as both are immortalized masterpieces about the tragedy of love.

Now, “The Peony Pavilion” is also available online! The video below is a rendition of one of the 55 scenes in the play, “A Vow”.

title: Kunqu Opera: An orchid in bloom
description:
Kunqu Opera, originated in the late Yuan (1271-1368) and early Ming (1368-1644) Dynasties, is one of China’s oldest operas to have defied time and pulled through till today. It first emerged as a local melody in the eastern Chinese city of Suzhou.

Kunqu Opera, like all other Chinese operas, fuses various art forms including songs, narration, dance, martial arts and even acrobatics. The astonishing makeup, embroidered costumes and accessories and stage arrangements add to its elegance.

title: "The lute: Cai Bojie" by Suzhou Kunqu Opera Theater
description:
Presented by Suzhou Kunqu Opera Theater of Jiangsu Province, China
Event Date: April 13, 2019
Michigan Theater 

Synopsis:
Authored by Gao Ming (fl. 1340s), The Lute is a centuries-old masterpiece of Chinese drama/opera that tells a moral story about a man, Cai Bojie, and his two wives, Woman Zhao (Zhao Wuniang) and Lady Niu (Niu Xiaoji), whom he has sequentially married. Acquiescing to his parents’ demands, Cai goes to the capital to pursue fame and wealth, leaving his father, mother, and new-wed wife behind at home. In the capital, Cai becomes successful but is cut off from his family because of natural disasters; then accepting an imperial order, he marries Lady Niu.  At home, famine has Cao’s parents starved to death, even though Woman Zhao tries her best to take care of them. After their death–triggered by their finding out that Woman Zhao eats husk so that they can eat rice, Woman Zhao goes to the capital to look for Cai, bringing a portrait of the deceased couple,  and supporting herself on the road as an itinerary lute-playing balladeer. On the road, she visits a Buddhist temple;  as she exits, leaves the portrait of her in-laws behind. Coincidentally, Cai visits the same temple; they do not meet though. Depressed by the lack of communication from home, Cai goes there to pray for the safety of his parents and Woman Zhao. There he finds the portrait Woman Zhao has left behind and taken it home because it reminds him of his parents. Informed by a monk at the temple that Cai has taken the portrait home, Woman Zhao comes to his mansion and finds his new wife, Lady Niu. Not knowing if Cai still loves her, and how she can compete with Lady Niu, Woman Zhao tells Lady Niu that she would go home alone. Realizing the dilemma Woman Zhao faces, Lady Niu asks her to stay so that they can find out why Cai has not contacted home. Thus, they have Woman Zhao sing a ballad telling the tragedy that Cai’s parents faced. Listening to the song, Cai can no longer hold his yearnings for his parents and Woman Zhao, and reveals the reasons why he has not contacted him–he did, but his communications were stopped by Lady Niu’s father. Realizing that Cai is neither an unfilial son nor disloyal husband, the two woman embrace Cai, and then the three live as a happy family.

title: 《玉簪记・琴挑·懒画眉》施夏明X自得琴社｜Courtship Through Song: A Classic Kunqu Love Story
description:
月明云淡，寂寂空庭。 
小明老师与琴社再度携手，带来全新编曲的「懒画眉」。 
昆腔、古琴、竹笛、手碟——四艺相融，在微黄的卷轴里，重述那段以琴传情的月下相逢。

【懒画眉】
【Lan Huamei】
月明云淡露华浓
A clear night – the moonshines brightly, the cloudsfew, and the flowers aremoist with dew.
欹枕愁听四壁蛩
Sleepless, melancholy,I listen to the cricket’s call.
伤秋宋玉赋西风
The autumn wind hasinspired great elegies.
落叶惊残梦
The leaves fall, and I amawakened from my drowsyreverie.
小生对此 溶溶夜月 悄悄闲庭
In the vastness of themoonlight, I stand alonein the tranquil courtyard.
背井离乡 孤衾独枕好生烦闷
Far away from my home, Isleep alone. What misery!
哦 不免到白云楼下 闲步一回 多少是好
Ah! Why not stroll by theWhite Cloud Pavilion?Indeed a fine idea.
闲步芳尘数落红
I saunter down the gardenpaths, covered in fallenpetals.

这支【懒画眉】出自明代高濂的传奇《玉簪记》。高濂是钱塘（今杭州）人，这部作品创作于隆庆年间，在昆山腔鼎盛的万历年间广为流传，被誉为 “中国十大古典喜剧” 之一。

故事以南宋动荡为背景：少女陈娇莲（即陈妙常）因金兵南侵与家人失散，不得已寄居金陵女贞观为道姑。观主的内侄潘必正落第后羞于回乡，借住观中读书。一段因战火而错位的人生，在道观清规之下悄然展开。

「琴挑」就是全剧最动人的折子。

所谓 「琴挑」，即以琴为媒、挑动情思。明代文人笔下，最美的邂逅不是四目相对，而是一阵琴声。这是一个知识青年特有的浪漫 —— 不直接说 “我喜欢你”，而是借着谈论琴曲、借着弦外之音，让情感在旋律中流转。两人分唱四支〖懒画眉〗、交替对唱四支〖朝元歌〗，词句清丽典雅，唱腔交织徘徊，把那种欲言又止、欲进还退的青涩试探刻画得层次分明。

这一折也因唱做规范严谨，是昆曲小生的开蒙戏之一。明清以来，这支〖懒画眉〗被数十种戏曲文献收录。

Under the soft moon and sparse clouds, silence fills the empty courtyard.
Kunqu artist Shi Xiaming joins Zi De Guqin Studio once again for a brand-new arrangement of the classic aria Lan Huamei . Blending Kunqu opera vocals, guqin, bamboo flute, and handpan, the piece reimagines the iconic moonlit encounter of two souls connecting through music, framed like an unfolding ancient scroll.

🎋 About the Aria & The Story
This aria is from The Jade Hairpin (Yuzan Ji), a legendary Ming-dynasty chuanqi play written by Gao Lian, a native of Qiantang (modern-day Hangzhou). Composed during the Longqing era and widely popularized in the golden age of Kunqu opera during the Wanli period, it is widely recognized as one of "China’s Ten Great Classical Comedies."
Set amid the turmoil of the Southern Song dynasty, the story follows Chen Miaochang (born Chen Jiaolian), a young woman displaced by invading Jin armies. Separated from her family, she takes refuge as a Taoist nun at the Nüzhen Temple in Jinling. Pan Bizheng, a scholar who failed the imperial examination and is too ashamed to return home, comes to stay at the temple to study. Their lives, upended by war, slowly intertwine beneath the quiet rules of the Taoist temple.
The scene Qin Tiao (The Zither Courtship) is the most beloved act of the entire play.
As the name suggests, "Qin Tiao" tells of a romance kindled through the guqin (Chinese zither). For Ming-dynasty literati, the most beautiful encounter was not a shared glance, but the sound of a zither drifting through the night. It is a uniquely scholarly romance: instead of speaking their feelings outright, they exchange thoughts through music, letting unspoken longing linger between the notes.
The two characters each sing two stanzas of Lan Huamei, then alternate through four stanzas of Chaoyuan Ge (Song of Taoist Worship). With elegant verses and interwoven melodies, the scene perfectly captures the tender hesitation, shy curiosity, and unspoken affection of a budding romance.
This scene is also a core introductory piece for Kunqu’s young male role type (xiaosheng), celebrated for its rigorous standards of singing and performance. Since the Ming and Qing dynasties, this classic version of Lan Huamei has been anthologized in dozens of traditional opera scores and collections.

💡 For attentive viewers: keep an eye out for the traditional gongche music notation alongside the lyrics!

title: Chinese Guqin  : Flowing water Guqin 流水 古琴曲
description:
Guqin song "running water" has a long history. In the Ming Dynasty Zhu Quan's "magic secret manual", the caption of "high mountain" and "running water" recorded that "high mountain" and "running water" had only one song. At the beginning, ambition cared about the high mountain and said that benevolent people liked the mountain. Later, ambition cared about the running water and said that wise people liked the water. In the Tang Dynasty, it was divided into two songs, with no number of segments. In the Song Dynasty, it was divided into four segments of "high mountain" and eight segments of "running water". Since then, there have been more than 30 kinds of musical scores published in running water. Their basic content and spirit come down in one continuous line, and they basically maintain the structure of 8 paragraphs. During the period from Xianfeng to Guangxu of the Qing Dynasty, Zhang Kongshan, a Taoist from Qingcheng, a Sichuan pianist, added a paragraph between the original fifth and sixth paragraphs to become the ninth paragraph

In the first paragraph, the whole song starts to flow, which vaguely implies the theme tone of the whole song. 2、 The third paragraph uses overtone to write all kinds of spring sounds of mountains, streams and waterfalls. 4、 The fifth section shows that the spring of thousands of gullies flows out of the mountain and into the flood, and gradually has a turbulent trend. Since the sixth section, the water flows into the vast ocean, and the rapids cross the gorge and beach, forming a momentum of rough waves and surging unstoppable, conveying the character of fearing difficulties and dangers and forging ahead bravely. 7、 The eighth segment is the afterwave after the climax, which is slow and urgent, released and closed from time to time, and gradually calmed down. The ninth paragraph is the finale of the passing weather. This piano song is full of the harmony between man and nature, and exudes the atmosphere of the harmony of nature, earth and people. This song has both lyricism and simulation, the combination of virtual and real, the integration of scenes and the lofty atmosphere.

On August 20, 1977, the Voyager spacecraft launched by NASA into outer space carried a gold-plated record that is said to have been preserved for 1 billion years, including the song "running water" played by Mr. Guan Pinghu, a famous pianist in China.
古琴曲《流水》历史悠久。明代朱权《神奇秘谱》中《高山》、《流水》的题解记载：“《高山》、《流水》二曲本只一曲。初，志在乎高山，言仁者乐山之意。后，志在乎流水，言智者乐水之意。至唐分为两曲，不分段数，至宋分《高山》为四段，《流水》为八段”。此后刊载《流水》的琴谱多达30多种，它们的基本内容和精神一脉相承，也基本保持了8段的结构。清咸丰至光绪年间川派琴家青城道士张孔山在原第五、六段之间加了一段，成为9段
        首段，起全曲水流之势，隐约暗示全曲的主题音调。二、三段用泛音写出山涧小溪潺潺、瀑布飞溅的各种泉声。四、五段表现万壑之泉由细流出山汇入洪流，并渐有汹涌之势。自六段起，水流汇入浩瀚汪洋，急流穿峡过滩，形成惊涛骇浪、奔腾难挡的气势，传达了不畏艰险、勇往直前的品格。七、八段为高潮之后的余波，忽缓忽急，时放时收，渐渐平复。第九段以杳渺徐逝的气象终曲。 这首琴曲充满着人与自然的和谐之音，散发了天籁、地籁、人籁相知相合、浑然一体的气象。此曲兼有抒情性和模拟性，虚实结合、情景相融、气象高远。
        1977年8月20日，美国宇航局向外太空发射的“旅行者”号宇宙飞船上搭载了一张据称能保存10亿年之久的镀金唱片，其中就有我国著名琴家管平湖先生演奏的《流水》一曲。

title: 【古琴Guqin筝笛巫毒】《醉醉渔，唱唱晚》Drunken fishermen singing in the sunset宋代装束Costumes and Armor of Song Dynasty
description:
《醉醉渔，唱唱晚》见名思义  ，改编自古琴曲《醉渔唱晚》，原曲最早见于明代的《西麓堂琴统》(共十段)，据解题：“陆鲁望（龟蒙）与皮袭美（日休）泛舟松江，见渔父醉歌，遂写此曲。西塞山前，桃花流水，其兴致恐不相下也”来看，编者认为此曲作者乃是唐代诗人陆龟蒙与皮日休，此说虽不可尽信，但据该本的旋法与用音上看，确应是前朝遗响。
 现今流传的《醉渔唱晚》，源自清代川派的《天闻阁琴谱》，该本应是张孔山改编整理而来，与其余诸谱版本音调迥异，乃同名异曲，此本体裁短小，曲调精炼，形象鲜明，在此本的诸多演奏版本之中，犹以卫仲乐先生的演绎精妙绝冠，卫先生此曲时而跌宕疏放，时而潇洒豪放，实有“啸傲烟云，醉乡酣美”之意趣。
《醉醉渔，唱唱晚》即是唐彬老师，根据卫先生演奏本重新改编创作而成，此曲中运用现代的作曲手法，给古琴配上了多种乐器，对原曲进行了解构重述。
全曲分为三个段落：
第一个段落为“晨兴”，此段曲速悠缓，黎明船舷攒碰，波尚轻，鱼尚眠，琴声（re-mi-re～）的核心动机似渔翁，在做“起床否”的思想斗争。
第二个段落为“鸣榔”，此时曲速忽快，古筝如风浪暗涌，巫毒鼓如盘涡潆洄，笛声似鳞耀鱼翔，小打似喧歌鸣号，一时齐作，渔翁举网鸣榔，罟鱼欢歌，船随浪摇，人随波动。
第三个段落为“唱晚”，曲速又归于平缓，惬意的和声取自于法国作曲家埃里克·萨蒂的《吉姆诺佩蒂舞曲》，灯曳酒酣，人醉船懒“明月太虚同一照，浮家泛宅忌昏晓”。
原曲：《醉渔唱晚》《吉姆诺佩蒂舞曲》（裸体歌舞）
改编/作曲：唐彬 
古琴：白无瑕
古筝：蔡珊

title: Sunset, Guqin, and a Can of Coke | A Nostalgic Cover of "Anhe Qiao"
description:
A sound of nostalgia that everyone understands, with a little twist you've never seen before.

This is our Guqin cover of the legendary modern Chinese folk song 'Anhe Qiao' (安和桥) by Song Dongye, a song about lost childhood, fading memories, and the bittersweet feeling of looking back.

To capture a unique sound, we used a Coca-Cola can as a slide, blending the ancient soul of the Guqin with a modern touch. Performed at sunset, overlooking the mountains. This is a soundtrack for the end of a summer day.

What memories does this song bring back for you?

title: Guqin- Traditional Chinese Music
description:
Guqin: Li Xiangting (Ancient Rhyme of Yuan Song) "Purple Flower"

Guqin "Purple Flower" is taken from Mr. Li Xiangting's Guqin and Xiao improvisation album "Yuanqu Ancient Rhyme".
Following "The Poetic Flavor of the Tang Dynasty" and "The Poetic Meaning of the Song Dynasty", Mr. Li Xiangting's "The Ancient Rhyme of Yuan Opera" once again interprets the unique artistic charm of Yuan Opera with the guqin and Xiao. 
Either long-distance, sincere feelings, sadness and grudges, or beautiful and charming, Mr. Li Xiangting is inspired and inspiring.
 His mellow improvisations and unvoiced rhymes create a transcendence of time and space.
The background of the music is based on the Yuan Dynasty poet Wang Shifu's Yuetiao "Purple Flower Preface". Yuan man Wang Shifu used to be an official in the court, and in his later years he chose to be like Fan Li, sailing on the five lakes, free and easy, with a song "Purple Flower", telling his ideal.

title: 古琴《禅定》: 李祥霆 / Chinese Traditional Music, Guqin “Chan Ding (Buddhist Meditation)”: LI Xiang Ting
description:
古琴曲《禅定》选自李祥霆先生的专辑《心》(风华国韵系列) © 2009 龙源音乐。
“禅”是外不着相，不执着一切境界相是为禅；“定”是内不动心。潘国静禅师曰: “禅者藏也，未见此物，不知何物，只有依悟，明心见性。” 由「凡人」到「成佛」整个过程中，起关键性作用的是「禅定」，没有它发挥积极作用，「凡人」是无法达到「成佛」的境界。
独处之时，听一曲李祥霆先生的《禅定》，悠然的琴声犹如一缕佛香，让心底的一抹浮躁烟消云散。故而在纷繁世事中寻得一片安逸，静坐敛心，专注一境，思行悟禅，忘言息虑。

图片来源: 薛大庸人物画 龙牙居遁禅师《学道》
https://www.yczihua.com/goods-6110.html
薛大庸，字兰石，1966年出生于河南。中国书画家杂志社艺术总监。作品多次在东南亚及日本、韩国展出，部分作品被中国当代书画院永久收藏。

title: 'Drunken Fisherman Singing in the Sunset': One of the Top Ten Guqin Songs in China | Musical Moments
description:
Title: "Drunken Fisherman Singing in the Sunset"
Guqin performance: Ziying Zhao
Music Recording: Joseph Ma
Mixing: Joseph Ma

"Drunken Fisherman Singing in the Sunset" is one of the top ten guqin songs in China. In "Xilutang Qintong," it is recorded that Lu Guimeng and Pi Rixiu were boating on the Songjiang River, and they saw a fisherman singing while drunk, so they wrote this song. This song is about laughing proudly at the clouds, enjoying the beauty of drunkenness. It was composed for those who are seeking a reclusive life. "The universe in the urn is large, and the sun and the moon in the pot are long."

title: 【古琴Guqin】《碧涧流泉》荡迹山林，意趣盎然Guqin music in the Song Dynasty describing clear springs
description:
据传为宋人朱紫阳所作。盖其寓情山水，结盟泉石。曲调畅达萦回，动静相宜，弹之则有山幽涧深、枕流漱石之想。
起(第一段)音清意静。有远山寒烟，幽壑松风之想也。 
承(第二三段)音畅意达，初微渐甚，娓娓成韵。泉动而不撼静，石静而不碍动，泠泠碧涧，溯源徂流也。 
转(第四五段)声疾指快，洋洋乎盈耳。浮玉飞花，萦崖抱壑，情之至也。 
合(第六段)曲尽意犹，水远而情留。回首烟霞处，玉浪堆中得自由。

title: How to craft ancient Chinese musical instrument Guqin?
description:
It takes at least 18 months to make a Guqin, a 7-stringed zither that dates back over 3,000 years. 
The process has more than 100 steps, including crushing pearls and gemstones and mix them with raw lacquer.
The instrument has been hailed as the "father of Chinese music."

★ Hello People of all over The World! Welcome to my Channel!
★ I Hope everyone feel comfortable with all of music in my channel !

🎵Introduction---The  Chinese  guqin: 古琴) is a plucked seven-string Chinese musical instrument of the zither family.It has been played since ancient times, and has traditionally been favoured by scholars and literati as an instrument of great subtlety and refinement, as highlighted by the quote "a gentleman does not part with his qin or se without good reason," as well as being associated with the ancient Chinese philosopher Confucius.

 It is sometimes referred to by the Chinese as "the father of Chinese music" or "the instrument of the sages". The guqin is not to be confused with the guzheng, another Chinese long zither also without frets, but with moveable bridges under each string.

title: Guqin: “The Moon Over the Mountain Guan,” performed by Jiaoyue Lyu
description:
Jiaoyue Lyu performs “The Moon Over the Mountain Guan”. Filmed in The Astor Chinese Garden Court, at The Metropolitan Museum of Art, April 13, 2016
 
This well-known example of Qin music from 1931 has a title identical to that of Li Bai’s poem, also performed to this tune. When interpreted by excellent musicians, the audience feels and understands the profound longing of soldiers on the frontier for the comforts and familiarity of home and family.
 
Jiaoyue Lyu’s guqin is hand-crafted by Ni Shiyun in Jiangsu Province, China.

title: 【古琴Guqin】弦上有舟《欸乃》｜The guqin lets you appreciate the timeless beauty of Chinese rivers and mountains.
description:
本曲以柳宗元“欸乃一声山水绿”为题，一叶扁舟，悠游隐逸于江湖，到有风波处寻无波。曲调清新隽永，静中有动，有脱尘寓江海河汉之游，物外烟霞之思。   

渔翁  
唐·柳宗元  
渔翁夜傍西岩宿，晓汲清湘燃楚竹。 
烟销日出不见人，欸乃一声山水绿。  
回看天际下中流，岩上无心云相逐。  

据《天闻阁琴谱》管平湖打谱 
演奏：白无瑕  
录音、混音：唐彬 
出品：自得琴社
Ai Nai (The Creak of Oars‘Ao Ai’)
Inspired by Liu Zongyuan’s line — “At the creak of oars, mountains and waters gleam green” — this piece paints a solitary boat adrift in mist-clad rivers, where the seeker journeys through winds and waves toward inner stillness.

The melody flows with serene vitality: stillness breathes into motion, transcendence awakens amid river mists and starlit seas—a soul wandering beyond the mundane, dreaming among clouds and radiance.

The Fisherman
by Liu Zongyuan, Tang Dynasty
Nightlong, the fisherman moors by western cliffs;
At dawn, he draws clear Xiang water, lights bamboo with Chu fire.
Mists fade, sun rises—yet no soul is seen,
At the creak of oars, mountains and waters gleam green.
Turning, he gazes skyward, down mid-current drifting;
Above the cliffs, untroubled clouds chase one another in ease.

Credits

Score interpreted from Tian Wen Ge Qin Pu by Guan Pinghu
Performed by Bai Wuxia
Recording & Mixing Tang Bin
Presented by Zi De Guqin Studio

title: Water Tranquility 水境 中國風音樂 | Ancient Chinese Guqin & Xiao Flute | Zen Meditation & Relaxation Music
description:
霧深之處，天地無形。
音起之時，心念自止。

一人立於舟上，
不語，不動，
只是讓簫聲穿過濃霧，
落入水面與夜色之中。

此刻無需追尋、無需理解，
讓所有未說完的心事化入夜波，
讓未安定的念頭沉入霧後。

在聲音之外，
有更安靜的地方；
在呼吸之內，
有更柔軟的自己。

願你靜下來，
不求證悟，
只願安住。



When the mist grows deeper,
the world becomes softer.

When the Xiao begins,
the mind grows still.

Stand within the silence,
and let every breath
return to where it first began.

Here, nothing must be solved,
nothing must be reached.
Only stay—

title: Chinese Guqin  : Flowing water Guqin 流水 古琴曲
description:
Guqin song "running water" has a long history. In the Ming Dynasty Zhu Quan's "magic secret manual", the caption of "high mountain" and "running water" recorded that "high mountain" and "running water" had only one song. At the beginning, ambition cared about the high mountain and said that benevolent people liked the mountain. Later, ambition cared about the running water and said that wise people liked the water. In the Tang Dynasty, it was divided into two songs, with no number of segments. In the Song Dynasty, it was divided into four segments of "high mountain" and eight segments of "running water". Since then, there have been more than 30 kinds of musical scores published in running water. Their basic content and spirit come down in one continuous line, and they basically maintain the structure of 8 paragraphs. During the period from Xianfeng to Guangxu of the Qing Dynasty, Zhang Kongshan, a Taoist from Qingcheng, a Sichuan pianist, added a paragraph between the original fifth and sixth paragraphs to become the ninth paragraph

In the first paragraph, the whole song starts to flow, which vaguely implies the theme tone of the whole song. 2、 The third paragraph uses overtone to write all kinds of spring sounds of mountains, streams and waterfalls. 4、 The fifth section shows that the spring of thousands of gullies flows out of the mountain and into the flood, and gradually has a turbulent trend. Since the sixth section, the water flows into the vast ocean, and the rapids cross the gorge and beach, forming a momentum of rough waves and surging unstoppable, conveying the character of fearing difficulties and dangers and forging ahead bravely. 7、 The eighth segment is the afterwave after the climax, which is slow and urgent, released and closed from time to time, and gradually calmed down. The ninth paragraph is the finale of the passing weather. This piano song is full of the harmony between man and nature, and exudes the atmosphere of the harmony of nature, earth and people. This song has both lyricism and simulation, the combination of virtual and real, the integration of scenes and the lofty atmosphere.

On August 20, 1977, the Voyager spacecraft launched by NASA into outer space carried a gold-plated record that is said to have been preserved for 1 billion years, including the song "running water" played by Mr. Guan Pinghu, a famous pianist in China.
古琴曲《流水》历史悠久。明代朱权《神奇秘谱》中《高山》、《流水》的题解记载：“《高山》、《流水》二曲本只一曲。初，志在乎高山，言仁者乐山之意。后，志在乎流水，言智者乐水之意。至唐分为两曲，不分段数，至宋分《高山》为四段，《流水》为八段”。此后刊载《流水》的琴谱多达30多种，它们的基本内容和精神一脉相承，也基本保持了8段的结构。清咸丰至光绪年间川派琴家青城道士张孔山在原第五、六段之间加了一段，成为9段
        首段，起全曲水流之势，隐约暗示全曲的主题音调。二、三段用泛音写出山涧小溪潺潺、瀑布飞溅的各种泉声。四、五段表现万壑之泉由细流出山汇入洪流，并渐有汹涌之势。自六段起，水流汇入浩瀚汪洋，急流穿峡过滩，形成惊涛骇浪、奔腾难挡的气势，传达了不畏艰险、勇往直前的品格。七、八段为高潮之后的余波，忽缓忽急，时放时收，渐渐平复。第九段以杳渺徐逝的气象终曲。 这首琴曲充满着人与自然的和谐之音，散发了天籁、地籁、人籁相知相合、浑然一体的气象。此曲兼有抒情性和模拟性，虚实结合、情景相融、气象高远。
        1977年8月20日，美国宇航局向外太空发射的“旅行者”号宇宙飞船上搭载了一张据称能保存10亿年之久的镀金唱片，其中就有我国著名琴家管平湖先生演奏的《流水》一曲。

description:
★ Hello People of all over The World! Welcome to my Channel!
★ I Hope everyone feel comfortable with all of music in my channel !

🎵Introduction---The  Chinese  guqin: 古琴) is a plucked seven-string Chinese musical instrument of the zither family.It has been played since ancient times, and has traditionally been favoured by scholars and literati as an instrument of great subtlety and refinement, as highlighted by the quote "a gentleman does not part with his qin or se without good reason," as well as being associated with the ancient Chinese philosopher Confucius.

 It is sometimes referred to by the Chinese as "the father of Chinese music" or "the instrument of the sages". The guqin is not to be confused with the guzheng, another Chinese long zither also without frets, but with moveable bridges under each string.

title: Guqin- Traditional Chinese Music
description:
Guqin: Li Xiangting (Ancient Rhyme of Yuan Song) "Purple Flower"

Guqin "Purple Flower" is taken from Mr. Li Xiangting's Guqin and Xiao improvisation album "Yuanqu Ancient Rhyme".
Following "The Poetic Flavor of the Tang Dynasty" and "The Poetic Meaning of the Song Dynasty", Mr. Li Xiangting's "The Ancient Rhyme of Yuan Opera" once again interprets the unique artistic charm of Yuan Opera with the guqin and Xiao. 
Either long-distance, sincere feelings, sadness and grudges, or beautiful and charming, Mr. Li Xiangting is inspired and inspiring.
 His mellow improvisations and unvoiced rhymes create a transcendence of time and space.
The background of the music is based on the Yuan Dynasty poet Wang Shifu's Yuetiao "Purple Flower Preface". Yuan man Wang Shifu used to be an official in the court, and in his later years he chose to be like Fan Li, sailing on the five lakes, free and easy, with a song "Purple Flower", telling his ideal.

The instrument has been hailed as the "father of Chinese music."

Specially curated to soothe your mood, heal your heart organ, and uplift your soul, this video is the perfect companion for relaxation, meditation, and self-reflection.

title: The Eagles - Hotel California - Reimagined on the Traditional Chinese Guzheng | Moyun
description:
This time I will bring you a fun loop form! Different from the previous multi-part ensemble, it is possible to complete a piece of music in one go, and the process is quite interesting~

The use of guitar in Hotel California can be regarded as one of the classic arrangements in history, especially the 1994 live version. So I made some deletions in the vocal part, focusing on the two guitar solos of the prelude and the ending. Hope you like it!

The costume this time is Wei Jin style wide-sleeved cross-collared Hanfu. For the convenience of plucking the instrument, panbo are added. (襻膊, pàn bó, originated from the Song Dynasty, a tool used to roll up the sleeves for easy operation), the hat is a hand-woven straw hat, the overall mix and match looks relatively "plain", and it feels like the rice planting will start in the next second hahahahahahaha~

Hope you like it! Remember to subscribe to this channel!

title: 古琴《禅定》: 李祥霆 / Chinese Traditional Music, Guqin “Chan Ding (Buddhist Meditation)”: LI Xiang Ting
description:
古琴曲《禅定》选自李祥霆先生的专辑《心》(风华国韵系列) © 2009 龙源音乐。
“禅”是外不着相，不执着一切境界相是为禅；“定”是内不动心。潘国静禅师曰: “禅者藏也，未见此物，不知何物，只有依悟，明心见性。” 由「凡人」到「成佛」整个过程中，起关键性作用的是「禅定」，没有它发挥积极作用，「凡人」是无法达到「成佛」的境界。
独处之时，听一曲李祥霆先生的《禅定》，悠然的琴声犹如一缕佛香，让心底的一抹浮躁烟消云散。故而在纷繁世事中寻得一片安逸，静坐敛心，专注一境，思行悟禅，忘言息虑。

图片来源: 薛大庸人物画 龙牙居遁禅师《学道》
https://www.yczihua.com/goods-6110.html
薛大庸，字兰石，1966年出生于河南。中国书画家杂志社艺术总监。作品多次在东南亚及日本、韩国展出，部分作品被中国当代书画院永久收藏。

description:
Another masterpeace from my collection. 

Traditional Sichuan folk music played by very famous and honourable local musician WANG Hua-de. Played by Qin (Guqin) made by LEI Wei in Tang Dynasty (618-907 A.D.)

Photos made by famous Sichuan photographer Chen Jin. Photos taken from his book "Teahouse". 

"Chengdu, the capital city of Sichuan province, is really famous for traditional teahouses around the streets . If you like tea and chinese tea culture, you totally need to go to Sichuan.  This book is work of the famous photographer Chen Jin who look into the everyday events in the tearoom during thirty years."

title: 古琴《面壁》：李祥霆 / Chinese Music, Guqin “Facing the Wall”: LI Xiang Ting
description:
古琴曲《面壁》选自中国古琴大师北派代表李祥霆录制的即兴佛乐发烧专辑《心》(风华国韵系列 © 2009 龙源音乐) 。

专辑中使用的传世唐朝古琴“九霄环佩”，被视为“鼎鼎唐物”，声音尽善尽美。此琴是唐玄宗第三子李亨即帝位的至德丙申年(西元756年) 宫廷作品，琴韵独特，空灵苍远，浑厚恬静，古朴幽深，行家誉为琴中“仙品”。专辑《心》是在李祥霆所理解之佛家精神所感觉之佛家气象前提下选定十个题目即兴演奏录制而成。

古琴曲《面壁》，其意至深，其理至微，其音至乐，是明心见性之正音。以正音得正悟，在证道后恢复本真的“心”，永不退转，方可得大智慧究竟圆满。

背景图片: 卢志军《红衣达摩》

description:
Played since ancient times, the Guqin is a seven-string Chinese musical instrument. It is considered an instrument of great subtlety and refinement.

(Ancient poem By Li Bai)
-Question and Answer on the Mountain-

Ask me what reason stay green mountain
Smile but not answer heart self idle
Peach blossom flow water far go
Apart have heaven earth in human world

You ask for what reason I stay on the green mountain,
I smile, but do not answer, my heart is at leisure.
Peach blossom is carried far off by flowing water,
Apart, I have heaven and earth in the human world

title: Water Tranquility 水境 中國風音樂 | Ancient Chinese Guqin & Xiao Flute | Zen Meditation & Relaxation Music
description:
霧深之處，天地無形。
音起之時，心念自止。

一人立於舟上，
不語，不動，
只是讓簫聲穿過濃霧，
落入水面與夜色之中。

此刻無需追尋、無需理解，
讓所有未說完的心事化入夜波，
讓未安定的念頭沉入霧後。

在聲音之外，
有更安靜的地方；
在呼吸之內，
有更柔軟的自己。

願你靜下來，
不求證悟，
只願安住。



When the mist grows deeper,
the world becomes softer.

When the Xiao begins,
the mind grows still.

Stand within the silence,
and let every breath
return to where it first began.

Here, nothing must be solved,
nothing must be reached.
Only stay—
and peace unveils itself.

墨韵：《权御天下》筝鼓合奏版（又名《墨韵第二号古筝练习曲》233）该来的终于来了！久等！这次的作品有着空前的合作阵容，多数是幕后的，但都为之付出了很多心血，参与拍摄的除了司鼓君都不是专业人士，还请多指教。此曲古筝部分的改编力度很大，因为我想尽量呈现一个丰富而有层次的演奏，开头致敬经典筝鼓合奏曲目——常静大神的《篆音》。感谢作者乌龟的授权，希望大家能喜欢！【UP主着装：对襟襦裙加半臂，请勿掐架】
